topics.name: Some Branches of Biology

subtopics.definition_content: knowledge gained by actual observation, found correct on verification and put in a systematic manner
branch of science which deals with the study of life; combination of Greek words bios (life) and logos (study)
science of plants; technically called Phytology (Gk. phyto = plants; logos = study)
study of animals (Gk. zoon = animals; logos = study)
branch of biology which deals with different aspects of microorganisms

key_concepts: Father of biology and zoology: Aristotle (384-322 B.C.)
definition biology given by Lamarck (French) and Gottfried Treviramus of Germany (1744-1829)
Father of botany: Theophrastus (370-287 B.C.)
Father of microbiology: Leeuwenhoek (1632-1723)

topics.name: Specified Branches of Biology
key_concepts: Includes Acarology, Bacteriology, Cardiology, Molecular biology, Oncology, Ornithology, Virology, Zymology and many other specialised fields

topics.name: Fathers of Various Sciences
key_concepts: Father of Genetics: G.J. Mendel
Father of Evolutionary ideas: Empedocles
Father of Eugenics: Francis Galton
Father of Mutation: Hugo de Vries
Father of Modern Embryology: Karl Ernst Von Baer
Father of Palaeontology: Leonardo da Vinci

topics.name: Scope of Biology
key_concepts: Biology helps understand ourselves, inter-relationships, resources, literature, ecosystem, and medicinal aspects
Biotechnologists produce genetically modified (GM) crops
Biology solves population growth, pollution, conservation problems

topics.name: Basic Properties of Living Organisms
key_concepts: Living organisms classified into five kingdoms: Monera, Protista, Fungi, Plantae, Animalia
Properties: definite organisation, cellular nature, homeostasis, metabolism, intussusceptional growth, adaptations, reproductive powers, variations

topics.name: Levels of Biological Organization
key_concepts: Levels common in living and non-living: Atomic → Molecular
Levels only in living: Cellular → Tissue → Organ → Organ system → Individual
Levels beyond individual: Population → Community → Ecosystem → Biosphere
All living organisms made of four elements: C, H, O, N

topics.name: Building blocks of life and their function
key_concepts: Water forms 70-90% of cellular pool; 65% of human body; called elixir or cradle of life
Proteins polymer of amino acids linked by peptide bonds; Magic-20: 20 biologically important amino acids
Nucleic acids: polymers of nucleotides linked by phosphodiester bonds (polynucleotides)
Each nucleotide: pentose sugar + phosphate group + nitrogen base

topics.name: Matter and Energy

subtopics.definition_content: a measure of the disorder or randomness within a system
system that can exchange energy but not matter with surroundings
system that can exchange matter and energy with surroundings; all living systems are open systems

key_concepts: E = mc^2 (Albert Einstein): energy equals mass times square of speed of light
Matter is the physical material of the universe; energy is the capacity to do work

subtopics.definition_content: the energy of movement; includes movement of large objects, electrical energy (movement of electrons) and heat (movement of atoms and molecules)
stored energy that can be released as kinetic energy under right conditions

topics.name: Laws of Thermodynamics

subtopics.definition_content: energy can neither be created nor destroyed; but energy can change from one form to another; also called law of conservation of energy

the amount of useful energy always decreases when energy is converted from one form to another; every transfer or transformation of energy makes the universe disordered; no physical process or chemical reaction is 100 per cent efficient

key_concepts: Thermodynamics deals with isolated systems — parts of the universe that cannot exchange matter or energy with any other parts

topics.name: Energy flow

subtopics.definition_content: phenomenon in which one form of energy is changed into another form; e.g., in photosynthesis radiant energy of sunlight converted to chemical energy of glucose
movement of energy from one source or area or substance to another in the same form; e.g., in cell respiration oxidative breakdown of glucose occurs inside mitochondria

key_concepts: About 686 kcal of energy released per mole of glucose during cell respiration
Energy transformation examples: Chemical→electrical (nerve cell), Light→electrical (retina), Chemical→mechanical (muscles), Chemical→light (bioluminescence)

topics.name: Homeostasis

subtopics.definition_content: maintenance of a constant internal environment or steady state (blood plasma, haemolymph, extracellular fluid, etc.); Gk. homoios = same; stasis = standing

key_concepts: Claude Bernard (1857) recognized importance of stability in internal environment (milieu interieur)
definition coined by American physiologist Walter Bradford Cannon in 1929
Homeostasis is a fundamental property of life and a good sign of life

subtopics.definition_content: Regulation of body heat in response to the internal and external environment of the body
having a variable temperature; cold-blooded; body temperature changes with fluctuations in environmental temperature
having the same temperature; warm-blooded; body temperature is independent of environmental temperature; includes mammals, birds and a few fishes like tuna fish and sword fish

topics.name: Growth

subtopics.definition_content: permanent increase in size or weight or volume of an organism or its body parts
process in which reserve food material is utilized and exhibited negative growth

key_concepts: Growth at molecular level: increase in cell size, addition of non-living aprotoplasmic materials, increase in number of cells by cell division
Growth is result of greater anabolic over catabolic processes

topics.name: Development

subtopics.definition_content: generation of form and structure during development of an individual organism; produces new forms by involving cell movements

key_concepts: Growth involves cell proliferation, cell enlargement and secretion of extracellular matrix
Differentiation results in increasing diversity of cells
Morphogenetic movements particularly noticed during gastrulation

topics.name: Reproduction
key_concepts: Reproduction is the only way that living things can perpetuate themselves
Sexual reproduction involves two organisms and their sex cells
Asexual reproduction produces identical copies of themselves

topics.name: Adaptation

subtopics.definition_content: any characteristic of an organism which makes the organism better suited to its environment; improves performance and survival in environment

key_concepts: Types: short range or temporary adaptation vs long range or permanent adaptation
Individuals with favourable characteristics replace those with less favourable characteristics

subtopics.definition_content: average longevity of an organisms from birth to death; different in different living organisms

topics.name: Death

subtopics.definition_content: permanent breakdown and finally stopping of vital functions of body, especially heart beat and respiration; last event in the degenerative processes of ageing

key_concepts: Death involves widespread cell breakdown and cell death
No natural death in some protists e.g. Amoeba and monerans like bacteria

formulas.description: Einstein's equation: energy equals mass times the square of the speed of light

topics.name: Taxonomy — Introduction

subtopics.definition_content: art of identifying distinctions among organisms and placing them into groups that reflect their most significant features and relationship; Gk. Taxis = arrangement; nomos = law
Taxonomy based on all available information and attempting to classify organisms, according to their origin, evolution and variations

key_concepts: Scientists who study classification are called systematists or taxonomists
References of classification in Upanishads and Vedas: 740 plants and 250 animals recorded

topics.name: History of classification
key_concepts: Chandyogya upanishad: animals classified into viviparous Jivaja, oviparous Andaja and minute Udbhija
Susruta samhita: sthavara (immobile, e.g. plants) and jangama (mobile, e.g. animals)
Parasara: angiosperms classified into dvimatruka (dicotyledons) and ekamatruka (monocotyledons)
Hippocrates and Aristotle: classified animals into insects, birds, fishes and whales in Scala naturae

topics.name: Nomenclature

subtopics.definition_content: name of a plant or animal composed of two Latin (or Latinised) words — first is generic name (begins with capital letter), second is specific epithet (begins with small letter, printed in italics); introduced by Carolus Linnaeus in Species Plantarum (1753)
three-word name including genus, species, and subspecies or variety; e.g., Homo sapiens europeus
when a single species is described under different names by different authors; name under which species is first described is valid

key_concepts: Binomial system: credit goes to Carolus Linnaeus; proposed by Bauhin (1623), elaborated by Linnaeus (1753)
Codes: ICBN (plants), ICZN (animals), ICBN (bacteria), ICVN (viruses), ICNCP (cultivated plants)
Generic name always begins with capital letter; specific name with small letter, printed in italics

topics.name: Valid name
key_concepts: Not a tautonym (same generic and specific name)
Given according to binomial nomenclature with abbreviation of author's name
Type specimen described in Latin and kept in recognised herbarium/collection
Published in widely circulated scientific journal
All names previously given to organism must be indicated

subtopics.definition_content: The original type specimen from which the description of a new species is established
Duplicate of holotype, as another branch of the same tree
Specimen selected from original material to serve as nomenclatural type where there is no holotype
New nomenclatural type when the original type is missing
Any other specimen described along with holotype
Any of two or more specimens cited by an author when there is no holotype

topics.name: The taxonomic hierarchy

subtopics.definition_content: any unit of classification; may be large (e.g. Plant Kingdom) or small (e.g. a single species); definition introduced by ICBN during 1956, defined by Mayr (1964)

key_concepts: Hierarchy first given by Linnaeus with five categories; now seven obligate categories: Kingdom, Division/Phylum, Class, Order, Family, Genus, Species
Kingdom is highest; Species is lowest and basic unit
Division suffix: -phyta; Class suffix: -ae; Order suffix: -ales; Family suffix: -aceae; Subfamily suffix: -oideae
Species coined by John Ray (1693); Class and Order by Linnaeus (1735)
Indefinitionediate categories: subkingdom, superphylum, subdivision, superclass, subclass, superorder, suborder, superfamily, subfamily, tribe, subspecies

topics.name: Species concept

subtopics.definition_content: Species inhabiting the same geographical area (identical or overlapping)
Species inhabiting different geographical areas
Related species which are reproductively isolated but morphologically similar
Species that contain two or more subspecies
Species that are not sub-divided into subspecies

key_concepts: Modern biological species concept by Ernst Mayr (1942): groups of interbreeding natural populations reproductively isolated from each other
Traditional morphological (typological) concept by Linnaeus in Systema naturae
Genetic species concept by Lotsy (1918): group of genetically identical individuals
Species is the basic unit of taxonomy; species is the smallest rank and basic unit of taxonomic classification

topics.name: System of Biological Classification
key_concepts: Three basic categories: artificial systems, natural systems, phylogenetic systems
Artificial system: based on gross morphology; advocates: Aristotle, Theophrastus, John Ray, Carolus Linnaeus
Natural system: based on many taxonomic characters; Bentham & Hooker (1862-83) Genera plantarum — still considered best from practical viewpoint
Phylogenetic system: based on evolutionary relationships; A.W. Eichler (1883), Engler & Prantl, Bessey, Hutchinson (1884-1972), Takhtajan (1980), Cronquist (1981)

topics.name: New Systematics or Biosystematics

subtopics.definition_content: definition proposed by Sir Julian Huxley in 1940; species considered related to one another, mutable and the work of gradual modification; in conformity with facts of evolution

key_concepts: Forms: Morphotaxonomy (structural features), Cytotaxonomy (cytological information, chromosome number and structure), Karyotaxonomy (banding pattern of chromosome), Biochemical taxonomy/Chemotaxonomy (protein and serum analysis), Numerical taxonomy (quantitative assessment), Experimental taxonomy (genetic relationship by experiments)

topics.name: Modern trends in systematics
key_concepts: Two kingdom (Carolus Linnaeus 1758): Plantae and Animalia
Three kingdom (Ernst Haeckel 1866): added Protista for unicellular organisms and multicellular without tissue
Four kingdom (Copeland 1956): added Monera (bacteria and blue green algae) and Protista
Five kingdom (R.H. Whittaker 1969): Monera, Protista, Fungi, Plantae, Animalia; based on cell complexity, body complexity, nutrition mode, phylogenetic relationship, ecological life style
In five kingdom classification eukaryotes assigned to only four of the five kingdoms; Monera includes prokaryotes
Monera: prokaryotic organisms, autotrophic or heterotrophic, rigid cell wall; includes Bacteria, Actinomycetes, Cyanobacteria
Protista: mostly aquatic unicellular eukaryotes, cilia or flagella; e.g. Euglena
Fungi: eukaryotic heterotrophic, filamentous mycelium, saprophytes or parasites; moulds, mushrooms, puff balls
Plantae: multicellular photosynthetic plants; Animalia: multicellular eukaryotic, holozoic nutrition, nervous system

topics.name: Taxonomic Aids

subtopics.definition_content: book or essay giving comprehensive account of all available information about a genus, family or higher category at the time of publication; highly useful taxonomic aid
collections of dry, pressed and preserved plant specimens mounted on sheets; some plant parts kept in preservative liquids if they cannot be dried and pressed

taxonomical aid for identification of plants and animals based on similarities and dissimilarities; based on contrasting characters in pairs called couplet; each statement called a lead; two types: bracketed key and indented (yolked) key

key_concepts: Famous botanical gardens: Kew (England), Indian Botanical Garden Howrah (India), NBRI Lucknow (India)
Largest museum in India: Indian Museum at Kolkata
Insects preserved in insect boxes; larger animals stuffed and preserved

subtopics.definition_content: The coloration of bacteria by applying a single solution of stain to a fixed smear; the cells usually stain uniformly.

Technique introduced by Hans Christian Gram (1884) to classify bacteria into Gram +ve and Gram -ve using crystal violet, iodine solution, and decolouration with acetone/ethyl alcohol; bacteria retaining purple stain are Gram +ve; those decolourised are Gram -ve.

Slimy layer outside the cell wall in many bacteria, composed of polysaccharides and nitrogenous substances; when thick, forms a capsule; bacteria bearing a capsule are called capsulated or virulent bacteria.

Circular coiled bodies on the plasma membrane that are infoldings of the plasma membrane; contain respiratory enzymes (oxidases and dehydrogenases); known as 'mitochondria of bacterial cell' or chondrioides; more prominent in Gram +ve bacteria.
Also known as metachromatic granules; composed of polyphosphate; serve as reserve source of phosphate; first reported in Spirillum volutans.
Also known as genophore, naked nucleus, incipient nucleus; contains double helical circular DNA surrounded by polyamine proteins (not histones).

Small circular double stranded extra-chromosomal DNA molecules in bacteria that replicate independently and maintain independent identity; definition coined by Lederberg (1952); episomes are plasmids that integrate into bacterial DNA chromosome.
A type of plasmid responsible for transfer of genetic material between bacteria.
A type of plasmid that provides resistance against drugs/antibiotics.
A type of plasmid that produces Colicines, which kill other bacteria (other than the bacteria that produce these colicines).

Process in which one bacterium is transformed into another by transfer of DNA from capsulated to non-capsulated bacterium; first reported by Griffith (1928) in mice; studied in detail by Avery, McLeod and McCarty (1944) in Diplococcus pneumoniae.

Process in which DNA of a bacterial cell is transferred to another bacterium through a bacteriophage (virus parasitic on bacteria); first reported by Zinder and Lederberg (1952) in Salmonella typhimurium.

Process in which genetic material from a male (donor) bacterium is transferred to a female (recipient) bacterium through a conjugation tube formed by sex pili; first reported by Lederberg and Tatum in E. coli K12 (1946).
The recipient bacterium during conjugation that contains both the endogenote (its own genetic segment) and integrated exogenote (transferred donor segment); also called partial zygote.
Donor strain formed when the F+ plasmid attaches to the nucleoid and becomes an episome; transfers a few nucleoid genes at high frequency (1:100) to F- recipient.
Bacteria that grow exclusively in the presence of molecular oxygen and fail to survive in its absence; e.g., Bacillus subtilis, Azotobacter, Mycobacterium.
Aerobic bacteria that can also survive in the absence of oxygen; e.g., Aerobacter, Klebsiella, Pseudomonas.
Bacteria that grow and multiply only in the absence of free oxygen and fail to survive under aerobic conditions; e.g., Clostridium botulinum.

Mostly anaerobic bacteria that use sunlight as energy source; possess bacteriochlorophyll; perform anoxygenic photosynthesis; includes green sulphur (Thiothrix/Chlorobium) and purple sulphur (Chromatium) bacteria.

Bacteria that manufacture organic matter from inorganic raw materials using energy from oxidation of inorganic substances; e.g., sulphur bacteria, iron bacteria, hydrogen bacteria, nitrifying bacteria.
Majority of bacteria that cannot synthesize their own food and depend on external organic materials; types include parasites, saprotrophs, and symbionts.
Strict anaerobic archaebacteria found in muddy areas and stomach of cattle; produce methane (CH4) from CO2 or formic acid; e.g., Methanobacterium, Methanobacillus, Methanosarcina, Methanococcus.

Anaerobic archaebacteria that occur in extreme saline conditions (up to 35% NaCl); develop a purple pigmented membrane containing bacteriorhodopsin in sunlight for metabolic activities; e.g., Holobacterium, Halococcus.
Aerobic archaebacteria found in hot sulphur springs (up to 80°C) capable of oxidizing sulphur to H2SO4 at high temperature and high acidity (pH 2.0); e.g., Sulfobolus, Thermoplasma, Thermoproteus.
Food poisoning caused by Clostridium botulinum; main symptoms are vomiting followed by paralysis and death.
Spoilage of protein in the absence of O2 by putrefying bacteria such as Proteus and Mycoides.

Simplest and smallest unicellular non-motile aerobic prokaryotes without cell wall; size 0.1–0.15 μm; highly elastic and variable in shape (pleomorphism); covered by three-layered plasma membrane; gram-negative; sensitive to tetracycline and resistant to penicillin.
The highly variable and irregular shape exhibited by mycoplasma cells due to the absence of a rigid cell wall.
Designation for mycoplasmas first isolated from bovine sheep with pleuropneumonia by Nocard and Roux (1898); later placed under genus Mycoplasma by Nowak (1929).

Group of ancient Gram negative, photosynthetic prokaryotes; also called blue-green algae or myxophyceae/cyanophyceae; have survived for about 3 billion years; possess chlorophyll a, β carotene, myxoxanthophyll, C-phycocyanin (blue) and C-phycoerythrin (red); photosynthetic pigments present in thylakoids; food stored as cyanophycean starch; sexual reproduction totally absent.
Specialised cells in certain cyanobacteria (e.g., Nostoc, Anabaena) that are sites of nitrogen fixation; enzyme nitrogenase present in heterocysts; airtight anaerobic bodies.
Smaller pieces formed when Nostoc trichome breaks at points of death/decay of ordinary cells; slip out of mucilage sheath and grow into new plants; frequently break near heterocysts.
Resting spores in Nostoc; formed when vegetative cells enlarge, accumulate food material, and develop thick walls under unfavourable conditions; germinate after a period of rest.
The photosynthetic, floating protists collectively called phytoplankton.
The free-floating, holozoic protozoans collectively definitioned zooplankton.
The cell wall of dinoflagellates, composed of cellulose plates, called theca or lorica; contains two grooves — longitudinal sulcus and transverse girdle (annulus).
The relatively large and prominent nucleus of dinoflagellates; DNA is without association with histone proteins.
An osmoregulatory organelle in dinoflagellates that superficially resembles a contractile vacuole.
Rod-like or spindle-shaped ejective structures occurring just below the cell membrane of dinoflagellates.

The cells of diatoms; microscopic, unicellular photosynthetic organisms whose cell wall is composed of cellulose impregnated with silica, in two overlapping halves fitting like two parts of a soap box.
The upper half (lid) of the diatom cell wall (frustule).
The lower half (case) of the diatom cell wall (frustule).
A polysaccharide (also called leucosin) serving as the reserve food material in diatoms, along with oil.

A thin, flexible covering of the euglenoid body (absent cellulose cell wall), composed of fibrous elastic protein and small amounts of lipid or carbohydrates; shows oblique parallel stripes called myonemes.
The creeping movement through expansion and contraction of the body exhibited by euglenoids.
The product of photosynthesis in euglenoids, stored as paramylum granules in paramylum bodies in the cytoplasm.
A combined mode of nutrition — holotrophic (photosynthetic) and saprobic — seen in euglenoids that can also absorb organic compounds from the surrounding medium.
A wriggling movement of Euglena achieved by contraction and expansion of the body (metaboly).
The somatic phase of acellular slime moulds; a free-living, multinucleated, diploid protoplasm that slowly streams or glides over decaying organic matter showing amoeboid movement.
Haploid, uninucleated, naked cells (no cell wall) covered only by plasma membrane; the motile somatic stage of cellular slime moulds that move by amoeboid movement and phagotrophic nutrition.
A rigid cellulose-walled resting structure formed by a myxamoeba under unfavourable conditions, serving as a means of perennation and dispersal.

The aggregated mass of non-fused amoeboid cells in cellular slime moulds formed when food supply is exhausted, stimulated by release of cyclic AMP; a sort of community association, hence called communal slime moulds.

Protozoans without definite cell wall or pellicle, with no definite shape, using pseudopodia as locomotory organs; divided into orders Lobosa, Filosa, Foraminifera, Heliozoa and Radiolaria. Examples: Amoeba, Entamoeba histolytica, Globigerina.

Protozoans with body covered by thin pellicle or cuticle; locomotory organs are flagella; chloroplasts present in some forms; free living or parasitic. Examples: Volvox, Noctiluca, Trypanosoma, Leishmania.

Exclusively endoparasitic protozoans, body covered by pellicle, reproduction by spore formation. Divided into Telosporidia (spores without polar capsules; examples: Plasmodium, Monocystis) and Neosporidia (trophozoite amoeboid multinucleated; examples: Nosema).

Protozoans with body covered by thin pellicle, fixed permanent shape, locomotory organs are cilia, with two kinds of nuclei (micronucleus and meganucleus). Divided into Ciliata (cilia present throughout life) and Suctoria (cilia only in young; adults bear tentacles). Examples: Paramecium, Vorticella, Acineta.
The trilaminar, selectively permeable membrane covering the body of Amoeba; serves as excretory surface (ammonia diffuses out) and respiratory surface (diffusion of O2 and CO2).
The type of pseudopodium in Amoeba proteus; short, blunt, thick finger-like outgrowth composed of both ectoplasm and endoplasm; stabilised at the forward end by a hyaline cap of ectoplasm.
Cylindrical thread-like pseudopodia formed entirely of ectoplasm, radiating from the body in all directions e.g., Euglypha, Radiolaria.
Filamentous pseudopodia that form a network, e.g., Polystomella, Chlamydophrys.
Long, stiff, semi-transparent extensions of cytoplasm with pointed distal ends, e.g., Actinophrys.
Disease caused by Entamoeba histolytica residing in the upper part of the large intestine in humans; the magna (trophozoite) form is pathogenic, found in mucosa and sub-mucosa forming ulcers.
The mature, infective cyst of Entamoeba histolytica, containing four nuclei and two chromatoid bodies; infection is oral via contaminated food and water; produces eight amoebulae on excystation.
An enzyme secreted by Entamoeba histolytica that damages the intestinal mucosa.
Deadly disease caused by Trypanosoma gambiense (a parasitic zooflagellate), transmitted by the tse-tse fly (Glossina palpalis); parasite found in blood, finally invading cerebrospinal fluid.
A membrane formed along the body of Trypanosoma by its flagellum, which arises from the posterior end and runs anteriorly.
A distinct organelle associated with the basal granule (blepharoplast) of the flagellum in Trypanosoma.

The spindle-shaped infective stage of Plasmodium introduced into humans by female Anopheles mosquito along with anticoagulant saliva (anophilin) during blood-sucking; enters liver cells to begin the life cycle.
The spherical form that a sporozoite assumes after entering a liver cell; undergoes pre-erythrocytic cycle (completed in 10 days) by schizogony, producing cryptomerozoites.
The ring-like appearance of Plasmodium inside RBCs when, after entering as a rounded disc-like form, it develops a vacuole giving it a ring appearance.
Asexual multiple fission of Plasmodium occurring in liver cells (pre-erythrocytic and exo-erythrocytic) and in RBCs (erythrocytic schizogony) of the human host.
The motile, elongate diploid zygote formed after fusion of macrogamete and microgamete in the mosquito stomach; pierces the stomach wall and forms an oocyst on its outer surface.
The process in the mosquito by which oocysts divide to produce sporoblasts and ultimately sporozoites, which migrate to salivary glands for transmission.
Female gametocytes of Plasmodium; large cells laden with food material with nucleus at one end.
Male gametocytes of Plasmodium; small cells with centrally placed nucleus; produce 4–8 motile microgametes by exflagellation.
Peculiar bottle-shaped organelles present in the ectoplasm of Paramecium, serving as organelles of offence and defence.
The large, kidney-shaped nucleus in Paramecium controlling vegetative (metabolic) functions; divides amitotically during binary fission.
The small nuclei in Paramecium concerned only with reproduction; divides mitotically; number varies by species — 1 (P. caudatum), 2 (P. aurelia), several (P. multimicronucleatum).
The diploid nucleus formed by fusion of the stationary (female) and migratory (male) pronuclei during conjugation in Paramecium; divides thrice to form eight nuclei.
A process of self-fertilisation in a single Paramecium aurelia, resulting in the production of two daughter paramecia.
An asexual form of nuclear reorganisation in Paramecium aurelia where one individual produces four daughter paramecia.
A sexual process in Paramecium caudatum where two cytogamonts come together but do not exchange male pronuclei.

topics.name: Cell wall composition

subtopics.definition_content: Main component of fungal cell wall; polymer of N-acetyl glucosamine; chemical formula (C22H54N4O21).

key_concepts: Cell wall composition varies by group: cellulose-glucan (Oomycetes), chitin-chitosan (Zygomycetes), mannan-glucan (Ascomycetes), chitin-mannan (Basidiomycetes), chitin-glucan (some Ascomycetes/Basidiomycetes/Deuteromycetes)
Plasma membrane in fungi bears coiled membrane outgrowths called lomasomes (Moore and McAlear, 1961)

topics.name: Eukaryotic cell structure

subtopics.definition_content: Coiled membrane outgrowths of the plasma membrane in fungi; first described by Moore and McAlear (1961).
Nuclear division in fungi where the nuclear envelope persists during mitosis and meiosis (Moore, 1965).

key_concepts: Fungi are eukaryotic, possessing mitochondria, ER, ribosomes, microbodies, lysosomes, vacuoles
Fungi cells lack chloroplast; genetic material is DNA
Spindle poles contain centrioles or spindle pole bodies (SPB) but lack microtubular organisation
Reserve food: glycogen and lipid

subtopics.definition_content: Loosely or compactly woven tissue-like structure formed by organised mycelium in higher fungi.
Plectenchyma comprising loosely woven hyphae lying almost parallel to each other.
Plectenchyma where hyphae are closely interwoven, looking like parenchyma in cross-section.
Root or string-like elongated structure of closely packed and interwoven hyphae; may have a compact growing point.
Structures where hyphae are interwoven forming pseudoparenchyma with external hyphae thickened to prevent desiccation; persist for several years.
Thick mattress of compact hyphae associated with fruiting bodies.

topics.name: Thallus organization — Introduction

subtopics.definition_content: Plant body of true fungi (Eumycota); may be non-mycelial or mycelial.
False mycelium formed by non-mycelial unicellular fungi through budding.
Thread-like structures constituting the mycelium of fungal plant body (sing. hypha).
Non-septate and multinucleate mycelium found in lower fungi e.g., Phycomycetes.
Organisation where plant body is unicelled at one stage and mycelial at another.
Thallus in which the entire mycelium is converted into reproductive structure.
Thallus in which only a part of the mycelium becomes reproductive.

key_concepts: Non-mycelial forms are unicellular and may form pseudomycelium by budding
Aseptate mycelium in lower fungi (Phycomycetes); septate in Ascomycetes, Basidiomycetes, Deuteromycetes

subtopics.definition_content: Parasites that thrive on a living host throughout their entire life.
Actually saprophytes that have secondarily become parasitic.
Remains saprophytic throughout its life.
Actually a parasite which has secondarily become saprophytic.

topics.name: Symbionts

subtopics.definition_content: Symbiotic association between fungal forms (mycobiont) and green or blue-green algae (phycobiont).
Symbiotic association between fungal forms and roots of higher plants.

key_concepts: Fungi are achlorophyllous and hence heterotrophic; cannot prepare their own food

topics.name: Vegetative reproduction
key_concepts: Fragmentation: breakage of mycelium in Ascomycotina and Basidiomycotina
Budding: bud arises as papilla on parent cell
Fission: few unicelled forms like yeasts and slime moulds

topics.name: Asexual reproduction

subtopics.definition_content: Cells formed when mycelial thallus breaks into component cells, each rounds up; they germinate to form new mycelium e.g., Rhizopus.
Thick-walled fungal cells formed as resting spores; intercalary in position; e.g., Rhizopus, Ustilago.
Thin-walled, non-motile spores formed in a sporangium; also called aplanospores; e.g., Rhizopus, Mucor.
Thin-walled flagellate spores occurring in aquatic fungi; formed in zoosporangium.
Spores borne freely on tips of special branches called conidiophores; not formed inside a sporangium; e.g., Penicillium.
Special branches on tips of which conidia are borne.

key_concepts: Zoospore types: uniflagellate whiplash (Allomyces), uniflagellate tinsel (Rhizidiomyces), biflagellate tinsel+whiplash (Saprolegnia), biflagellate two whiplash (Plasmodiophora)

topics.name: Sexual reproduction

subtopics.definition_content: Fusion of two protoplasts during sexual reproduction in fungi.
Fusion of two nuclei during sexual reproduction in fungi.
Haploid (n) mycelial phase resulting from meiosis.
Mycelial phase (n+n) resulting from plasmogamy.
Mycelial phase (2n) resulting from karyogamy.

key_concepts: Sexual methods: Planogametic copulation (Iso-, Aniso-, Ooplanogametic), Gametangial contact, Gametangial copulation, Spermatization, Somatogamy
Deuteromycetes lack sexual reproduction
Isoplanogametic copulation: both gametes similar and flagellate e.g., Synchytrium
Anisoplanogametic copulation: flagellate gametes similar in structure but different in size e.g., Allomyces
Ooplanogametic copulation: small flagellate male + large non-flagellate female e.g., Monoblepharella

subtopics.definition_content: Mode of dikaryotic cell division in Basidiomycetes; lateral pouch-like outgrowth projects downward like a hook; first observed by Hoffman (1856) who named it Schnallenzellen (buckle-joints).

topics.name: Heterothallism

subtopics.definition_content: Sexual union between two hyphae of the same mycelium; self-fertile species (Blakeslee, 1904).
Sexual union between two hyphae derived from different spores; self-sterile species. Designated as + and - strains (Blakeslee, 1904).
Found in Mucor and Rhizopus; two mating types + and -.

key_concepts: Blakeslee (1904) working with Mucor sp. described homothallism and heterothallism

topics.name: Phycomycetes (Oomycetes/Algal fungi)
key_concepts: Also called lower fungi; coenocytic mycelium
Hyphal wall: chitin or cellulose (e.g., Phytophthora)
Asexual reproduction: conidio-sporangia; zoospores under wet conditions (heterokont flagellation), sporangia germinate as conidia under dry conditions
Sexual reproduction: oogamous by gametangial contact; oospore formed
Examples: Saprolegnia, Albugo (Cystopus), Phytophthora, Pythium, Sclerospora, Peronospora, Plasmopara

topics.name: Zygomycetes (Conjugation fungi)

subtopics.definition_content: Resting diploid spore produced by gametangial copulation in Zygomycetes; forms germ sporangium on germination.

key_concepts: Coenocytic mycelium; hyphal wall has chitin or fungal cellulose
Motile stage absent; sporangiospores/aplanospores borne inside sporangia
Sexual reproduction: fusion of coenogametes through conjugation (gametangial copulation)
Examples: Mucor, Rhizopus, Pilobolus, Abscidia, Saksenea

topics.name: Ascomycetes (Sac fungi)

subtopics.definition_content: Sac-like structure in Ascomycetes in which ascospores are formed endogenously (pl. asci).
Fruiting body of Ascomycetes; four types: Cleistothecium, Perithecium, Apothecium, Ascostroma.
Ovoid or spherical fruiting body completely closed; e.g., Aspergillus, Penicillium.
Flask-shaped fruiting body opening by a single pore called ostiole; e.g., Neurospora.
Saucer-shaped fruiting body; asci constitute the fertile zone called hymenium; e.g., Peziza.
Not a distinct fruiting body; asci arise directly within a cavity (locule) of stroma; lacks own well-defined wall; e.g., Mycosphaerella.

key_concepts: Unicellular or multicellular (septate mycelium in latter)
Asexual spores: conidia formed in chains, exogenously outside sporangium
Sexual spores: ascospores formed endogenously inside ascus
Gametes: nonmotile compatible, represented as + and -
Examples: Erysiphe, Claviceps, Yeast (Saccharomyces), Neurospora, Penicillium

topics.name: Basidiomycetes (Club fungi)

subtopics.definition_content: Club-shaped structure at end of mycelium in Basidiomycetes on which basidiospores are produced.
Multicellular fruiting body containing basidia in Basidiomycetes.
Sexual spores of Basidiomycetes; generally four in number; produced exogenously outside the body.
Central pore in septa of Basidiomycetes hyphae; septa possess central dolipores and lateral clamp connections.

key_concepts: Most advanced fungi and best decomposers of wood
Club fungi; septate multinucleated mycelium
Two compatible nuclei fuse to form zygote; undergoes meiosis to form four basidiospores
Common members: Agaricus (edible mushroom), Ustilago (smut), Puccinia (rust)

topics.name: Deuteromycetes (Fungi imperfecti)
key_concepts: Sexual or perfect stage unknown/absent
Septate hyphae; asexual reproduction by conidia
Examples: Alternaria solani, Fusarium, Gibberella, Colletotrichum falcatum, Helminthosporium oryzae, Cercospora personata

topics.name: Harmful aspects

subtopics.definition_content: Poisoning caused by alkaloids of Claviceps purpurea (ergot fungus of rye); acute condition called St. Anthony's fire.
Carcinogenic toxins produced mainly by Aspergillus flavus and A. parasiticus; e.g., Aflatoxin B1, B2, M1, M2, G1, G2.
Hallucinogenic drug (Lysergic acid diethylamide) extracted from Claviceps purpurea and Inocybe.

key_concepts: Important fungal crop diseases (table): White rust of crucifers (Albugo candida), Early blight of potato (Alternaria solani), Ergot of rye (Claviceps purpurea), Red rot of sugarcane (Colletotrichum falcatum), Powdery mildew of wheat (Erysiphe graminis), Late blight of potato (Phytophthora infestans), Black rust of wheat (Puccinia graminis-tritici)
Bengal famine 1943: caused by Helminthosporium oryzae (brown leaf spot of rice)
Ireland famine 1845: associated with Phytophthora infestans (late blight of potato)
Human fungal diseases: Athletes foot (Epidermophyton floccosum), Ringworm (Trichophyton sp.), Moniliasis (Candida albicans), Aspergillosis (Aspergillus niger), Torulosis (Cryptococcus neoformans)
Poisonous fungi: Amanita phalloides (death cup) produces alpha-amanitin and phalloidin

topics.name: Useful aspects

subtopics.definition_content: Protein substitute food obtained from yeasts, Penicillium, Fusarium.
First known antibiotic extracted from Penicillium notatum by A. Fleming (1944); also from P. chrysogenum (Raper, 1952).

key_concepts: Edible fungi: Agaricus bisporus, A. campestris, Morchella esculenta, Lentinus edodes, Volvariella volvacea
Organic acids: Citric acid (Aspergillus niger), Gluconic acid (A. niger/Penicillium purpurogenum), Fumaric acid (Rhizopus stolonifer/Mucor sp.), Lactic acid (Rhizopus nodosus)
Antibiotics: Griseofulvin (Penicillium griseofulvum), Cephalosporin (Acremonium sp.)
Plant hormones: Gibberellins from Gibberella fujikuroi; trisporic acid from Mucor mucedo
Vitamins: Vitamin A (Rhodotorula gracilis), Riboflavin B2 (Eremothecium ashbyii), Thiamine B1 (Saccharomyces cerevisiae)

topics.name: Rhizopus/Mucor

subtopics.definition_content: Hyphae of Rhizopus that grow horizontally on substrate surface; relatively stout and less branched; nodes give out rhizoids and sporangiophores.
Hyphae arising in clusters from lower side of each node; penetrate substratum for anchorage and food absorption; secrete amylase and maltase.
Erect, aerial, unbranched reproductive hyphae arising from upper side of each node; each bearing a single definitioninal sporangium.
Budded condition of oidia of Rhizopus/Mucor formed in liquid, sugary, acidic medium; takes part in alcoholic fermentation.
Mycelial branches of Rhizopus/Mucor growing towards each other during sexual reproduction.
Dark-coloured thick-walled resting spore formed after plasmogamy and karyogamy in Rhizopus/Mucor; diploid.
Zygospore formed by parthenogenous development due to failure of gametangial copulation in Rhizopus.

key_concepts: Rhizopus stolonifer: black bread mold (cosmopolitan saprophyte on moist bread)
Mucor: dung mold; both called black mold or pin mold
Mycelium: aseptate, branched, multinucleate (coenocytic)
Asexual: sporangiospores (aplanospores), chlamydospores, oidia
Sexual: heterothallic (R. stolonifer) or homothallic (R. sexualis); gametangial copulation; haplobiontic life cycle
Mucormycosis: Mucor pusillus and M. ramosissimus attack internal human organs

topics.name: Yeast

subtopics.definition_content: Extracellular enzyme secreted by yeast cells that converts complex sugars into simple soluble sugars; used in alcoholic fermentation.
Sexual reproduction where entire vegetative thallus is transformed into reproductive body; the best example being sexual fusion in yeast.
Phenomenon in Schizosaccharomyces pombe where two adjoining sister cells fuse.
Phenomenon in Debaryomyces where mother and daughter cells fuse to form zygote.

key_concepts: First described by Anton Van Leeuwenhoek in 1680
Unicellular, non-mycelial; facultative aerobe
Cell wall: proteins (7.8%), lipids (8.5-13.5%), chitin (1-2%), glucan (30-35%), mannan (~30%)
Reproduction: vegetative (budding - Saccharomyces; fission - Schizosaccharomyces), sexual (ascospore formation)
Life cycle types (Guilliermond, 1940): Haplobiontic (Schizosaccharomyces octosporous), Diplobiontic (Saccharomyces ludwigii), Haplodiplobiontic (Saccharomyces cerevisiae)
Alcoholic fermentation: C6H12O6 → C2H5OH + 2CO2 (yeast)

topics.name: Albugo

subtopics.definition_content: Disease caused by Albugo (Phycomycetes) on members of Cruciferae family; common species: Albugo candida.
Finger-like or globular structures produced by Albugo mycelium that enter host cells to absorb food material.
Diploid zygote with warty wall formed after fertilisation of oosphere by male nucleus in Albugo; diploid nucleus undergoes meiosis on germination.
Structure developing on one side of oogonium in Albugo through which fertilisation tube enters.

key_concepts: Member of Phycomycetes; obligate parasite (intercellular mycelium)
Mycelium: aseptate, multinucleate (coenocytic); cell wall: cellulose-glucan
Asexual: sporangiophores cut sporangia in basipetal manner; zoospores (kidney-shaped, laterally biflagellate) or direct germ tube germination
Sexual: oogamous; antheridia (club-shaped) + oogonia (globular); gametangial contact
Life cycle: mostly gametophytic; sporophytic phase limited to oospore stage

topics.name: Aspergillus (Psalliota)

subtopics.definition_content: Carcinogenic toxin in food produced by Aspergillus flavus; serious health hazard.

key_concepts: A. oryzae: prepares alcohol by saccharification of rice starch; produces various enzymes
A. niger: produces citric acid from molasses; also produces various enzymes
A. flavus: carcinogenic aflatoxin in contaminated food
Some species cause aspergillosis in humans and animals

topics.name: Agaricus

subtopics.definition_content: Above-ground fruiting body of Agaricus; divided into stipe (stalk) and pileus (cap).
Basal cylindrical fleshy stalk of Agaricus sporophore; pinkish white colour.
Umbrella-shaped cap of Agaricus sporophore; bears hymenial layers of gills on ventral side.
Fertile layers bearing basidia in Agaricus gills; undergoes fertilisation for spore production.
Four finger-like projections on basidium of Agaricus into which nuclei are pushed to form basidiospores.

key_concepts: Macrofungi; commonly called mushrooms; grow in rich organic soil
Only type of reproduction: somatogamy (+ and - hyphae)
Vegetative mycelium: dikaryotic rhizomorph (perrenating stage after somatogamy)
Gill internal layers: Trama (central), Sub-hymenium (sterile, on both sides), Hymenium (fertile, bears basidia)
Agaricus bisporus and A. campestris are edible

topics.name: Introduction and Classification

subtopics.definition_content: Structurally organised entity consisting of permanent association of a fungus (mycobiont) and an alga (phycobiont); definition first given by Theophrastus.
Fungal component of a lichen; mostly Ascomycetes.
Algal component of a lichen; mostly blue-green alga.
Phenomenon of controlled parasitism in lichen where fungal partner has the upper hand; Crombie's (1885) master and slave hypothesis.
Study of lichen.
Lichens that grow on stones e.g., Dermatocarpon.
Lichens that grow on barks of trees e.g., Usnea, Parmelia, Graphis.
Lichens developing on wood e.g., Cyphelium.
Lichens growing on soil e.g., Cladonia, Lecidea.

key_concepts: Dual nature of lichen studied by Schwendener (1897)
Ahmadjian (1963): considers fungus to be a controlled parasite
Classification by Hole (1967): Ascolichens, Basidiolichen, Lichen Imperfecti
Lichens grow very slowly; some arctic lichens believed to be 4500 years old
Do not grow near smoky industrial areas (SO2 sensitive)

topics.name: Structure

subtopics.definition_content: Lichens occurring as crust over rocks, soil or tree barks e.g., Graphis, Haematomma.
Leaf-like lobed lichens attached to substratum by rhizoid-like organs e.g., Parmelia, Peltigera.
Branched shrubby lichens with small base e.g., Cladonia, Usnea.
Lichen thalli where algal cells and fungal hyphae are uniformly dispersed throughout e.g., Collema.
Lichen thalli where algal cells are restricted to algal zone only; four distinct zones: upper cortex, algal layer, medulla, lower cortex.
Gall-like outgrowths on upper surface of lichen thallus; meant for retaining moisture e.g., Peltigera.
Coral-like outgrowths on upper surface of lichen thallus; help in photosynthesis and vegetative propagation e.g., Parmelia, Peltigera.
Powdery mass comprising both algal and fungal components; formed in pustule-like soralia; e.g., Physia, Parmelia.
Loosely arranged areas in lower cortex of lichen; help in gaseous exchange; analogous to stomata of higher plants.

key_concepts: Four zones in heteromerous thallus: upper cortex, algal (gonidial) layer, medulla, lower cortex
Algal layer constitutes about 5% of lichen body
Cladonia rangiferina (reindeer-moss): grows in tundra region, food for reindeer and caribou

topics.name: Reproduction

subtopics.definition_content: Unique phenomenon in Cladonia where older parts of thallus die while young branches continue to grow.
Conidia formed in flask-shaped pycnidia embedded in lichen thallus; form new fungal mycelium.

key_concepts: Asexual: fragmentation, rejuvenation, conidia, pycniospores (soredia, isidia as vegetative propagules)
Sexual: performed mainly by fungal component; structure of reproductive organs depends on fungal partner type

topics.name: Economic importance

subtopics.definition_content: Acid-base indicator obtained from Roccella montagnei and Lasallia pustulata.
Purified dye from orchill dye (Roccella/Lecanara); used as a biological stain.
Broad spectrum antibiotic obtained from Usnea and Cladonia sp.; effective against gram-positive bacteria.

key_concepts: Pioneer of vegetation: soil builders on rocks
Food: Cladonia rangiferina (reindeer food), Cetraria icelandica (Iceland moss), Umbillicaria (rock tripe)
Indicators of air SO2 pollution: Usnea (sensitive to SO2)
In perfumery: Ramalina, Evernia prunastri, Lobaria pulmonaria
Tanning/dying: Cetraria icelandica; Parmelia sp. (brown dye); Ochrolechia sp. (red dye)
Brewing: Cetraria islandica, Cladonia rangiferina (yield up to 66% polysaccharides) for alcohol in Sweden and Russia

topics.name: Types of mycorrhizae

subtopics.definition_content: Association between a fungus and the root of a higher plant e.g., Pine, Birch, Eucalyptus, Ficus; definition coined by Frank (1885).

Fungus completely encloses rootlet in a sheath/mantle of compact hyphal cells; penetrates between cortex cells only (no root hair, root cap); occurs in ~3% of plant species, mostly forest trees; fungal partner commonly Basidiomycetes.
Fungus lives within the root without forming external mantle; three subtypes: ericaceous, orchidaceous, and vesicular-arbuscular (VAM).
Type of endomycorrhiza where fungi (mostly Zygomycetes) develop vesicles and arbuscules within root cortical cells; significant in agriculture.
Mycorrhiza sharing characteristics of both ecto and endotrophic types; forms hyphal mantle and Hartig net, and also establishes haustoria and hyphae coils in cells; e.g., Monotrapa indica.

key_concepts: Mycorrhiza: symbiosis/mutualism between fungus and root of higher plant
Ericaceous mycorrhizae: fungus forms dense intracellular coils in outer cortical cells
Orchidaceous mycorrhizae: associated with orchid roots from seed germination

topics.name: Advantages of mycorrhizal association
key_concepts: Increases plant uptake of phosphorus, copper, zinc, nitrogen and potassium from soil
Fungal hyphae permeate soil and help absorption of water by host
Mycorrhizal plants need less fertilizer; can grow on infertile soils
Fungi produce growth-promoting substances
Develops resistance to soil-borne diseases (phytolaxins), drought, salinity, pH and temperature extremes

topics.name: Important discovery of virus

subtopics.definition_content: Sub-microscopic entities capable of being introduced into specific living cells and reproducing inside such cells only. The definition 'virus' is derived from Latin, meaning poison or venom or viscous fluid. They remain inactive outside a living host but become active inside the host and multiply in it. They represent a transitional form of life between non-living and living world.
A single virus particle. Most plant viruses are RNA viruses; most animal viruses are DNA viruses.

key_concepts: Carolous causius (1576) recorded first viral disease in tulips
A. Mayer (1886) found tobacco mosaic disease in tobacco caused by virus
D. Ivanowski (1892), a Russian Botanist, discovered the infectious nature of viruses
B. Beijerinck (1898) called viruses 'Cotagium virum fluidum' (living infectious fluid)
W. Twort (1915) and D. Herelle (1917) discovered bacteriophages
W. M. Stanley (1935) first isolated tobacco mosaic virus (TMV) in crystalline form; showed crystals were made of proteins; awarded Nobel Prize

topics.name: Nature of viruses
key_concepts: Non-living characters: no complete cellular structure, no cell membrane or cell wall, no cellular metabolism, no respiration, high specific gravity, active only inside host, cannot grow in vitro (in-vitro), postulates of Robert Koch not applicable, absence of growth and division

Living characters: definite shape and morphology, possess genetic material (DNA or RNA) passed generation to generation, obligate intracellular parasites with host specificity, show mutation, show irritability to heat/UV rays/humidity/alcohol, can multiply enormously inside host

topics.name: Chemical composition
key_concepts: Viruses are nucleoproteins: central core of nucleic acid (either DNA or RNA, never both in typical viruses)
Nucleic acid represents genetic characters of virus
TMV has RNA; influenza virus has 10% RNA and 90% protein
PSTV (Potato Spindle Tuber Viroid) has RNA but lacks capsid
Plant viruses contain RNA; cauliflower mosaic virus is exception with DNA
Bacteriophages contain DNA; almost half animal viruses contain RNA and half contain DNA
Cancer-causing reovirus contains both RNA and DNA
Enzymes in viruses: Lysozyme in bacteriophages, transcriptase in vaccinia virus, reverse transcriptase and DNA/RNA polymerase in retroviruses
DNA virus examples: Adenoviruses (ds), Bacteriophage phi-X-174 (ss), Coliphage lambda (ds), Coliphage T2/T4/T6 (ds), Pox virus (ds), Herpes virus (ds)
RNA virus examples: Avian leukemia virus (ss), Influenza virus (ss), Poliomyelitis virus (ss), TMV (ss), Reovirus (ds), Rice dwarf virus (ds)

topics.name: Uses of viruses
key_concepts: Specific viral strains are cultured and attenuated to be used as vaccines against specific diseases
Cyanophages LPP-1 and SM-1 are useful in controlling water blooms
Bacteriophage was used by Hershey and Chase to prove that DNA is the chemical basis of heredity
Bacteriophages bring about transduction in genetics
Water of river Ganga believed to have phages which destroy bacteria, keeping water unspoiled

topics.name: General Structure of Virus — Introduction

subtopics.definition_content: Outer thin loose covering present in some viruses. Composed of proteins (from virus), lipids and carbohydrates (both from host). Found in some animal viruses e.g., Herpes virus, HIV. Viruses without envelope are called naked.
Smaller subunits of the viral envelope.

Protein coat that surrounds the central portion of nucleoid and enzymes (if present). Consists of a specific number and arrangement of small sub-units called capsomeres. Capsomeres possess antigenic properties.
Small sub-units that make up the capsid and possess antigenic properties.
The nucleic acid present in the virus. It is the infective part of virus which utilizes the metabolic machinery of the host cell for synthesis and assembly of viral components.

key_concepts: Viruses are structurally made up of envelope, capsid, nucleoid and occasionally one or two enzymes

Genetic material types: Double stranded DNA (ds DNA) e.g., Herpes virus, Hepatitis B virus; Single stranded DNA (ss DNA) e.g., Coliphage; Double stranded RNA (ds RNA) e.g., Wound tumour virus; Single stranded RNA (ss RNA) e.g., Retrovirus, TMV, Polio virus

topics.name: Life cycle
key_concepts: Viruses do not reproduce; they divide by a special mechanism
Attachment: Bacteriophage attaches to bacterial cell wall with the help of caudal fibres
Penetration: Bacteriophage dissolves bacterial wall by enzyme Lysozyme; DNA molecule enters through pore; entire protein coat remains outside
Latent period: Phage DNA controls host cellular machinery; phage protein formation begins; cellular DNA and RNA broken down; phage DNA forms inside
Maturation: Young virion is changed into an adult virus
Release: Cell wall weakened by lysozyme; host cell bursts; viruses released for next infection

topics.name: Shape
key_concepts: Straight rigid rods with helical architecture: TMV, Barley stripe mosaic virus (BSMV)
Long flexous thread-like rods: Potato latent mosaic, Wheat streak mosaic virus
Polyhedral virions: Turnip yellow mosaic, Tobacco ring spot virus
Tadpole-like: Bacteriophages
Spherical: Influenza virus

topics.name: Size
key_concepts: Viruses range from 10 mu to more than 300 mu in size
Virus of foot and mouth disease (FMD) is smaller than the largest protein molecule
Largest virus: smallpox virus - variola (250 mu)

chapters.name: Classification of Animal Viruses and Disease Tables

topics.name: Families of animal viruses grouped by type of nucleic acid
key_concepts: dsDNA - Papova virus: Naked polyhedral, 40-57nm; Papilloma (human warts, cervical cancer), polyoma
dsDNA - Adeno virus: Naked polyhedral, 70-80nm; respiratory disease, tumors
dsDNA - Herpes virus: Enveloped polyhedral, 150-250nm; herpes simplex I (cold sores), herpes simplex II (genital), varicella zoster (chicken pox), Epstein-Barr (mononucleosis, Burkitt's lymphoma)
dsDNA - Pox virus: Enveloped complex, 200-350nm; variola (smallpox), vaccinia, cowpox
ssDNA - Parvo-virus: Naked polyhedral, 18-26nm
ssRNA+ - Picorna virus: Naked polyhedral, 18-38nm; poliovirus, rhinovirus (common cold), enteric viruses
ssRNA+ - Toga virus: Enveloped polyhedral, 40-60nm; rubella, yellow fever, encephalitis
ssRNA+ - Retrovirus: Enveloped polyhedral, 100-120nm; RNA tumor viruses, AIDS
ssRNA- - Rhabdovirus: Enveloped helical, 70-180nm; rabies
ssRNA- - Paramyxovirus: Enveloped helical, 150-300nm; measles, mumps
ssRNA- - Orthomyxovirus: Enveloped helical (RNA in 8 segments), 80-200nm; influenza
dsRNA - Reovirus: Naked polyhedral (RNA in 10 segments), 60-80nm; diarrhoea

topics.name: Important plant diseases caused by viruses
key_concepts: Abutilon mosaic - Abutilon mosaic virus
Bunchy top of banana - Banana bunchy top virus
Cucumber mosaic - Cucumber mosaic virus
Little leaf of brinjal - Brinjal little leaf virus
Little leaf of cotton - Cotton little leaf virus
Papaya mosaic - Papaya mosaic virus
Potato leaf roll - Potato leaf roll virus
Potato mild mosaic - Potato virus X
Potato rugose mosaic - Potato virus X and Y
Rosette of groundnut - Groundnut mosaic virus
Sugarcane mosaic - Sugarcane virus I
Tobacco mosaic - Tobacco mosaic virus
Tomato leaf curl - Tomato curl virus
Tristeza of citrus - Citrus Tristeza virus

topics.name: Important human diseases caused by viruses
key_concepts: Encephalitis - Encephalitis virus
Infectious hepatitis - Hepatitis virus
Herpetic Keratitis - Herpes virus
Influenza - Influenza virus-a
Measles - Measles virus
Viral bronchitis - Parainfluenza virus
Poliomyelitis - Polio virus (children)
Small Pox - Pox virus
Common cold - Rhino virus
Yellow fever - Yellow fever virus

topics.name: Interferons — Introduction

subtopics.definition_content: Phenomenon in which the cell infected with one type of virus becomes resistant to super infection by other viruses. Reported by G.M. Findley and McCallum (1937).

Chemical substances responsible for viral interference. Protein molecules or polypeptides of low molecular weight which prevent viral multiplication. Produced by cells in mammals, rodents, birds, etc. definition given by Alliac Issacs and Lindeman (1957).

key_concepts: Interferons are low molecular weight proteins/polypeptides that inhibit viral multiplication
Produced by cells in mammals, rodents, birds and provide resistance against viruses

topics.name: Prions — Introduction

subtopics.definition_content: Proteinaceous infectious particles discovered by Stanley B. Prusiner (1982) as human disease causal agents. Also called slow viruses. Made of protein molecules only; genetic material (DNA and RNA) is absent. Thought to cause a number of slow virus diseases. Kuru, a disease of central nervous system found in few cannibalistic tribes of New Guinea, is caused by prions.

key_concepts: Prions are proteinaceous particles with no genetic material (DNA or RNA)
Also called slow viruses
Kuru (CNS disease) caused by prions

topics.name: Tobacco mosaic virus (TMV)

subtopics.definition_content: Rod-shaped virus discovered by Russian worker D. Ivanowski. Has a central core of RNA surrounded by protein coat (capsid) forming the nucleocapsid. Ultrastructure described by Franklin et al (1957). The protein coat (capsid) consists of 2130 identical subunits (capsomeres). Nucleocapsid may be naked or surrounded by a loose membranous envelope.

key_concepts: TMV is rod-shaped with central RNA core surrounded by capsid
Capsid consists of 2130 identical capsomeres

topics.name: Bacteriophage

subtopics.definition_content: Viruses which attack bacteria. In outline they look like tadpole or sperm. Body divided into a hexagonal head, neck and tail. Hexagonal head has central core of double-helix DNA surrounded by protein coat. Cylindrical tail is hollow and entirely made of proteins. Six long threads called tail fibres or caudal fibres at the end help the virus attach to bacteria. Contains lysozyme enzyme.

key_concepts: Bacteriophage structure: hexagonal head, neck, hollow protein tail, six caudal fibres
Double helix DNA in head; entire protein coat remains outside host during infection
Contains lysozyme enzyme for dissolving bacterial cell wall

subtopics.definition_content: Viruses that attack blue-green algae. Sofferman and Morris (1963) reported viruses attacking filamentous forms of blue-green algae (Lyngbya, Plectonema and Phormidium - hence called LPP-1). Cyanophages contain DNA as their genetic material and resemble bacteriophages in morphology and behaviour.
Viruses that infect fungi such as Mushrooms, Penicillium, etc. They are isometric in shape and contain double stranded RNA.
Viruses which attack algae.

topics.name: Viroids — Introduction

subtopics.definition_content: Very simple smallest infectious agents discovered by Diener and Raymer (1967). Consist of RNA only; capsid is lacking. Contain only very low molecular weight RNA. Viroids are single-stranded, covalently closed circular as well as linear RNA molecules. Transmission is mechanical. T.O. Diener (1971) definitioned it viroid. Causal agent of potato spindle tuber disease is a free RNA - no viral nucleoprotein particles present in infected tissue.

key_concepts: Viroids are smallest infectious agents with no capsid (protein coat)
Consist solely of low molecular weight single-stranded RNA
Diseases caused by viroids: Potato spindle tuber disease, Cadang Cadang of coconut, Cucumber pale fruit, Chrysanthemum stunt, Avacado sunblotch
Symptoms on host plants are almost similar to those caused by viruses

subtopics.definition_content: Branch of botany dealing with the study of algae.
Algal forms remaining attached to the bottom of water bodies or to submerged objects.
Algae growing on rocks or stones.
Plants growing on snow or ice, e.g., Chlamydomonas nivalis (red), Scotiella nivalis (black).
Plants growing in hot water, e.g., Oscillatoria brevis grows at ~70°C.
Algal forms growing on other plants (algae, angiosperms), e.g., Oedogonium, Cladophora, Vaucheria.
Blue-green algae growing inside other plants, e.g., Anabaena inside leaf of Azolla; Nostoc inside thallus of Anthoceros.
Algae growing on the bodies of animals, e.g., Cladophora crispata on snail shell.
Algae growing inside the body of animals, e.g., Chlorella inside tissue of Hydra.
Blue-green algae growing endozoically inside protozoans.
A colony having a fixed number of cells and division of labour, e.g., Volvox.
Vegetative cells surrounded by a mucilaginous matrix, e.g., Tetraspora, Aphanotheca.
Colony appearing like a microscopic tree with secretion of mucilage from polar end, e.g., Ecballocystis.
Distinction of a prostrate system and an erect system in filamentous algal forms, e.g., Stigeoclonium.
An aseptate, multinucleate (coenocytic) condition of a filament or thallus, e.g., Vaucheria.
Cells transformed through deposition of food and wall thickening; germinate under favourable conditions, e.g., Cladophora, Ulothrix, Nostoc.
Thin-walled motile spores produced in sporangia during asexual reproduction in algae.
Thin-walled, non-motile spores formed commonly in Chlamydomonas, Ulothrix.
Thin-walled, non-motile spores resembling the parent cell, e.g., Chlorella.
Thick-walled non-motile spores formed to tide over unfavourable conditions, e.g., Chlamydomonas, Ulothrix.
In dry conditions, zoospores or aplanospores surrounded by mucilaginous sheath form a colony; each gives rise to new plant under favourable conditions, e.g., Chlamydomonas.
Fusion of gametes that are morphologically and physiologically similar (isogametes), e.g., Chlamydomonas eugametos.
Fusion of morphologically dissimilar gametes (morphological anisogamy) or morphologically similar but behaviourally dissimilar gametes (physiological anisogamy).
Fusion of flagellate antherozoids with non-motile egg (oosphere); male organ is antheridium, female is oogonium.

topics.name: Important features of some selected classes

subtopics.definition_content: Fresh water or marine; pigments: chlorophyll a, b; reserve food: starch; flagella identical; reproduction isogamous, anisogamous or oogamous, e.g., Chlamydomonas, Spirogyra.
Fresh water; pigments: chlorophyll a, e; reserve food: chrysolaminarin and oils; flagella non-identical (unequal), e.g., Vaucheria.
Marine; pigments: chlorophyll a, c; fucoxanthin (brown colour); reserve food: laminarin, mannitol; flagella unequal, e.g., Fucus, Dictyota.
Generally marine; pigments: chlorophyll a, d; r-phycoerythrin (red); reserve food: floridean starch; no zoospores; male gametes non-flagellate, e.g., Polysiphonia, Porphyra.
Prokaryotic cells; pigments: chlorophyll a, c-phycocyanin, c-phycoerythrin; reserve food: cyanophycean starch; no flagellate bodies; sexual reproduction absent, e.g., Nostoc, Anabaena.
Non-nitrogenous carbohydrate (agarose + agaropectin) obtained from red algae (Gracilaria, Gelidium, Gigartina, Chondrus); used as culture media base.
Alginic acid polymer from cell walls of Laminaria, Fucus etc.; used in pharmaceuticals, soups, cosmetics.
Polysaccharide colloid (phycocolloid) from red algae Chondrus crispus and Gigartina stellata; used in food industry and pharmaceuticals.
First algal antibiotic extracted from Chlorella; used against Gram +ve and Gram -ve bacteria.
Abundant algal growth floating on water surface in nutrient-rich water reservoirs, looking like foam or soap lather, e.g., Microcystis, Anabaena, Oscillatoria.
Floating masses of Spirogyra filaments supported by bubbles of oxygen in stagnant fresh water; also called water silk or mermaid's tresses.
Protein core surrounded by starch plate or starch grains; centre of starch formation in Spirogyra chloroplasts.
Most common type of conjugation in Spirogyra involving two filaments; conjugation canals connect paired cells forming a ladder-like structure.
Conjugation between two nearest cells of the same filament (homothallic) in Spirogyra.
Result of gamete fusion in Spirogyra; three-layered: outer exospore (thin), middle mesospore (thick, chitinized), inner endospore (thin cellulose).
Gametes that fail to fuse due to sudden environmental change and each functions independently as a parthenospore.
Life cycle in Spirogyra where dominant phase is haploid (n); diploid phase represented only by zygospore which undergoes zygotic meiosis (R.D.).
Elongated, colourless basal cell of Ulothrix filament; attaches the filament to a substratum.
Large zoospores with 4 flagella; usually 4 produced per cell in Ulothrix.
Lower plants in which sex organs are hidden and seeds and flowers absent; includes Thallophytes, Bryophytes, Pteridophytes. (Gr. Cryptos = hidden; gamos = marriage)
Higher plants in which sex organs are evident; seeds present; includes Gymnosperms and Angiosperms. (Gr. Phaneros = visible; gamos = marriage)
Plant body thallus-like with no embryo formation; includes Algae, Fungi, Bacteria, Lichens. definition coined by Endlicher (1836).
Plants in which zygote develops into multicellular embryo.
Science connected with the study of bryophytes. S.R. Kashyap is father of Indian Bryology.
Unicellular or multicellular thread-like structures replacing true roots in bryophytes; absorb water and fix the plant.
Multicelled vegetative reproductive structures produced in gemma cups, e.g., Marchantia, Lunularia.
Branched filamentous stage produced by germination of spores in mosses; ephemeral; produces gametophores.
Protonema arising from rhizoids, primary protonema or injured sporophyte in mosses; produces buds giving rise to new gametophores.
Male sex organ in bryophytes; club-shaped with stalk and body; jacket of sterile cells enclosing androgonial cells that produce biflagellate antherozoids.
Female sex organ in bryophytes; flask-shaped with neck and swollen venter; contains egg and ventral canal cell.
Sporophytic plant body of bryophytes differentiated into foot, seta and capsule; dependent on gametophyte for water, minerals and often organic nutrition.
Protective covering formed from the venter wall surrounding the developing sporogonium.
Class of bryophytes (liverworts); gametophyte dorsiventral, thallose or foliose; rhizoids unicellular; chlorophyll cells contain many chloroplasts.
Class of bryophytes; thalloid gametophyte, lobed, dorsiventral; each cell with single large chloroplast with central pyrenoid; antheridia endogenous, borne in closed cavities.
Mosses; gametophyte in two stages: prostrate protonema and erect leafy shoot; rhizoids multicellular with oblique septa; sex organs from superficial cells.
Dead compressed remains of Sphagnum plants in acidic marshes; rich in carbon; formed by weight and compression of dead remains.
Formation of gametophyte-like plant from diploid sporophyte cells without reduction division; cells have diploid chromosome set (2n).
Rosette of leaves surrounding the antheridial cluster in Funaria.
Club-shaped green multicelled sterile hair present in between antheridia in Funaria.
Green photosynthetic basal sterile portion of the Funaria capsule in continuation with seta; contains primitive stomata.
Fertile middle zone of Funaria capsule between apophysis and operculum; contains spore sac with spore mother cells.
Central sterile portion of the theca in Funaria capsule.
Two whorls of 16 peristomial teeth each in Funaria capsule; outer exostome (hygroscopic) and inner endostome (non-hygroscopic); aids spore dispersal.
Upper cap-like portion of Funaria capsule that covers the peristome; separated from theca by annulus and rim.
Two morphologically distinct generations following each other in regular sequence; gametophytic (n) independent and complex; sporophytic (2n) partially dependent.
Patches formed by dichotomously branched, dorsiventral thalli of Riccia.
One of the two types of rhizoids in Riccia; helps in fixation.
Small leaves in pteridophytes, e.g., Lycopodium, Equisetum.
Large leaves (fronds) in pteridophytes, e.g., Pteridium, Pteris and other ferns.
Sorus where all sporangia mature at the same time.
Sorus where oldest sporangium lies in the centre and younger stages are on either side.
Sorus showing mixed arrangement of younger and older sporangia.
Sporangium developing from a group of superficial initials; outer cells form wall, inner give rise to sporogenous tissue, e.g., Selaginella, Equisetum, Lycopodium.
Sporangium arising from a single superficial initial; inner cell forms stalk, outer gives rise to sporangium proper, e.g., Salvinia, Azolla.
Innermost nutritive wall layer of sporangium that degenerates at maturity.
Production of only one type of spores of almost the same size, e.g., Lycopodium, Pteridium.
Production of two types of spores—smaller microspores and larger megaspores, e.g., Selaginella, Marsilea.
Malic acid secreted by degeneration of neck canal cells and venter canal cells attracting antherozoids towards archegonia in pteridophytes.
Proposed by Van Tiegham and Douliot (1886); classifies steles in pteridophytes.
Solid core of xylem surrounded by phloem, pericycle and endodermis.
Protostele with central smooth core of xylem, e.g., Selaginella sp., Lygodium.
Protostele with star-shaped xylem core with radiating ribs, e.g., Psilotum, Lycopodium serratum.
Protostele with xylem broken into parallel plates alternating with phloem, e.g., Lycopodium clavatum.
Stele with central pith; formed by medullation in protostele.
Siphonostele perforated by several overlapping leaf gaps; each separate strand called meristele, e.g., Dryopteris, Pteridium, Pteris.
Much dissected siphonostele with vascular strands separated by parenchyma, e.g., Equisetum.
Formation of sporophyte from a gametophytic cell other than egg without fertilization; definition coined by de Bary (1878); first observed by Farlow (1874) in Pteris cretica.
Formation of gametophyte from a sporophytic cell without meiosis; first observed by Druery (1884) in Athyrium filix-femina.
Formation of sporophyte from egg without fertilization.
Selaginella species (e.g., S. lepidophylla, S. pilifera) that roll into compact ball in dry conditions and revive when moisture is available.
Flap-like outgrowth at the base of leaf on adaxial side; may be fan-shaped, tongue-shaped, lobed or fringed.
Leafless, colourless, positively geotropic, cylindrical structure in Selaginella growing downward; differs from root by lacking root cap.
Sheath of elongated secretory cells at the base of the ligule in Selaginella.
Sessile reproductive structure at definitioninal ends of branches; bears ligulate sporophylls with microsporangia and megasporangia.
Borne on megasporophylls; four-lobed with 2-layered jacket; one functional megaspore mother cell produces 4 megaspores by meiosis.
Borne on microsporophylls; contains 400–2000 microspores; development eusporangiate type.
Underground stem of fern; sparingly branched in Dryopteris, moderately branched in Pteris and Adiantum, profusely branched in Pteridium.
Young fronds of ferns rolled like a spring from apex downwards.
Brown hairy structures surrounding younger parts of leaves and rhizome in Dryopteris.
Kidney-shaped covering around each sorus in Dryopteris (true, arising from placenta); false indusium in Pteris formed by leaf margins.
Green, dorsiventral, heart-shaped fern gametophyte; monoecious but protandrous (antheridia mature first).
Fern life cycle with heteromorphic alternation of generations; sporophyte initially dependent on gametophyte but becomes independent.
Apogeotropic, dichotomously branched, bluish-green roots with blue-green algae (Anabaena, Nostoc) in cortex for nitrogen fixation.
Symbiotic association of fungal hyphae with roots of gymnosperms like Pinus for water and nutrient absorption.
Wood formed in one ring due to persistent cambium; cortex and pith broad; wood soft and commercially useless, e.g., Cycas.
Wood formed in more than one ring due to ephemeral cambium; wood hard, compact and commercially most important, e.g., Pinus.
Special leaf-like structures bearing microsporangia (pollen sacs) in gymnosperms.
Special leaf-like structures bearing megasporangia (ovules) in gymnosperms.
Compact structures formed by aggregation of sporophylls; generally unisexual in gymnosperms.
Wind pollination in gymnosperms; pollination drop secreted by micropyle captures pollen grains and draws them into pollen chamber.
Fertilization where male gametes are carried to archegonia through pollen tube, e.g., Pinus.
Fertilization by flagellate gametes, e.g., Cycas where pollen tube acts as haustorium.
Only a part of the oospore is involved in formation of the embryo, e.g., Pinus.
Persistent nucellus forming a cap-like structure in gymnosperm seeds.
Parent sporophyte (integument and nucellus) + female gametophyte (endosperm) + new sporophyte (embryo).
Group represented by a single genus/species while the rest have become extinct and fossilized, e.g., Ginkgo biloba. Cycas is also regarded as a living fossil.
Alkaloid obtained from species of Ephedra; used for treatment of cough, asthma and bronchitis.
Branches of unlimited growth in Pinus with apical buds; grow in whorls; bear scale leaves.
Branches of limited growth in Pinus lacking apical buds; arise in axil of scale leaves; bear foliage (needle) leaves.
Symbiotic association of fungal hyphae with ultimate branches of Pinus roots.
Scale leaves on dwarf shoots in Pinus; possess distinct midrib.
Compact and hard wood formed by long-lived cambium in Pinus; shows well-marked annual rings.
Annual ring zone formed during spring; cells thinner, large and polygonal. Also called early wood.
Annual ring zone formed during autumn; cells smaller, squarish and thick. Also called late wood.
Phenomenon where large numbers of winged pollen grains released from male cones of Pinus appear as a pale-yellow cloud.
Cellular female gametophyte of Pinus formed before fertilization; haploid gametophytic tissue that serves as endosperm.
Type of polyembryony in Pinus where cells of embryonal tier separate and each develops into an individual embryo.
Germination in Pinus where hypocotyl elongates pushing cotyledons and plumule in air above soil.
Dichotomously branched, apogeotropic, bluish-green lateral roots of Cycas harboring Anabaena cycadacearum and Nostoc in middle cortex (algal zone); help in nitrogen fixation.
Indirect leaf traces in Cycas that arise from the stele on the opposite side and make a semicircle before entering the leaf.
Loose wood of Cycas due to alternating rings of xylem and thin-walled phloem tissue; polyxylic; resembles some dicots with abnormal secondary growth.
Oval or elliptical shortly stalked compact cone of Cycas measuring 40–60 cm; bears microsporophylls spirally arranged; sympodial growth of male plant.
Modified foliage leaf bearing 1–6 pairs of ovules; loosely arranged (no female cone formation); monopodial growth of female plant.
Tiny space between nucellus and female gametophyte, formed by degeneration of nucellar cells; archegonia develop here.
Beak-like process at apex of Cycas nucellus projecting into micropyle; disorganizes to form pollination drop.
Male gametes of Cycas measure 300μ; largest in plant kingdom, visible to naked eye; oval, top-shaped, with thousands of small cilia.
Starch obtained from pith of Cycas revoluta; source of the name sago palm.
Germination in Cycas where cotyledons remain underground in endosperm while plumule grows upward.
Unique characteristic of angiosperms: one male gamete fuses with egg (diploid zygote → embryo) and another fuses with polar nuclei (triple fusion → triploid endosperm).
Hollow structure enclosing ovules in angiosperms, forming the definition 'enclosed seed' (angiosperm).
Smallest angiosperm; tiny flat oval green stem (phylloclade) about 1 mm diameter; free-floating aquatic.
Tallest angiosperm; trees may attain height up to 100 metres or more.
Plants living only for a few weeks (very short growing season), e.g., Arabidopsis (20–28 days life span).
Plants completing life-cycle in a single favourable season, e.g., wheat, rice, maize.
Plants completing life-cycle in two favourable seasons (two years), e.g., sugar beet.
Plants living for more than two years; may be monocarpic or polycarpic.
Perennial plants that produce flowers and seeds only once in lifetime, e.g., Agave, Bamboos.
Plants that produce flowers and fruits every year after maturity, e.g., Mango, Lemon, Apple.
Angiosperms with two cotyledons; tap roots; reticulate venation; tetramerous or pentamerous flowers; open vascular bundles in ring arrangement.
Angiosperms with one cotyledon; adventitious roots; parallel venation; trimerous flowers; scattered, closed vascular bundles, e.g., cereals, bamboos, palms.

chapters.name: Plant Life Cycles and Alternation of Generations

subtopics.definition_content: Dominant photosynthetic phase is free-living haploid gametophyte; sporophytic generation represented only by one-celled zygote; zygotic meiosis; e.g., Spirogyra, some Chlamydomonas species, Volvox.

Dominant photosynthetic independent phase is the diploid sporophyte; gametophytic phase reduced to single to few-celled haploid gametophyte; all seed-bearing plants (gymnosperms and angiosperms) follow this pattern.

Both phases are multicellular; in bryophytes dominant phase is haploid gametophyte (sporophyte short-lived, dependent); in pteridophytes dominant phase is diploid sporophyte (gametophyte short-lived, independent).
Regular alternation between gamete-producing haploid gametophyte and spore-producing diploid sporophyte in plant life cycles.
Animals without red blood e.g., sponges, cnidaria, mollusca, arthropoda, echinodermata
Animals with red blood e.g., vertebrates
Animals which give birth to young ones e.g., man, dogs, cows
Animals which lay eggs e.g., frogs, toads, lizards, snakes, birds
Vertebrates without embryonic membranes e.g., fishes, amphibians
Vertebrates with embryonic membranes (chorion, amnion, allantois, yolk sac) e.g., reptiles, birds, mammals
Animals with notochord, dorsal tubular nerve cord, paired pharyngeal gill slits; all urochordates, cephalochordates and vertebrates
Animals without notochord; Phylum Porifera to phylum Hemichordata
Animals without vertebral column; all nonchordates, urochordates and cephalochordates collectively
Present in protozoans; no cell division of labour
Body consists of many cells with minor division of labour; distinct tissues not formed; e.g., sponges
Cells form poorly defined tissues; two distinct cell layers; e.g., coelenterates
Cells organised into tissues, tissues into organs, organs into organ systems; all animals except sponges and coelenterates
Body consists of a cluster of cells without distinct tissues or organs; found in sponges
Single cavity functioning as both digestive tract and coelom; one opening (mouth=anus); found in coelenterates and flatworms
Two tubes: outer body wall and inner digestive tract with two openings (mouth and anus); coelom between them; found in Aschelminthes to chordates
Any plane through centre divides body into equivalent halves; e.g., Volvox, Heliozoa, Radiolaria
Body can be divided into equal halves by any plane through centre from top to bottom; e.g., some sponges (Sycon), coelenterates, echinoderms
Only two planes through longitudinal axis divide body into similar halves; e.g., Ctenophora, sea anemones (Anthozoa)
Body divided into two equal halves by median longitudinal (sagittal) plane only; found in many invertebrates and all vertebrates
Embryos with two germ layers (ectoderm and endoderm); e.g., poriferans and coelenterates
