Embryos with three germ layers (ectoderm, mesoderm, endoderm); from Platyhelminthes to Chordata
Segmentation where external divisions correspond to internal divisions; body divided into metameres; e.g., annelids. Occurs in Annelida, Arthropoda and Chordata
Segmentation in tapeworms where proglottides are budded from neck; not of embryonic origin; differs from true segmentation
Fluid-filled space between the gut and the outer body wall of an animal; contains the major internal organs
Animals lacking a coelom; e.g., sponges, coelenterates, ctenophorans and flat worms
Animals with false coelom (pseudocoel) derived from embryonic blastocoel; e.g., round worms (Nemathelminthes)
Animals with true coelom arising as cavity in embryonic mesoderm; lined by coelomic epithelium (peritoneum)
Coelom developing as a split in the mesoderm sheet; found in annelids, arthropods, molluscs
Coelom formed as hollow outgrowths from wall of embryonic gut (archenteron); found in echinoderms and chordates
Primary body cavity (blastocoel) persisting as blood-containing space; found in Mollusca and Arthropoda; animals called haemocoelomates
Mouth forms from first embryonic opening (blastopore); dedefinitioninate and spiral cleavage; e.g., Platyhelminthes, Aschelminthes, Annelida, Arthropoda, Mollusca
Mouth does not form from blastopore (blastopore gives rise to anus); indedefinitioninate and radial cleavage; e.g., Echinodermata and Chordata

chapters.name: Phylum Porifera : The sponges (pore bearing animals)

subtopics.definition_content: Characteristic cells present only in sponges; form the endoderm (choanoderm); responsible for water current and intracellular digestion
Undifferentiated totipotent cells in sponge mesenchyme; develop into other cell types; gametes derived from these
Numerous minute pores on sponge body through which water enters
Large central cavity in sponges into which ostia open
Large opening through which spongocoel opens to outside; water exits through osculum
Internal buds containing archaeocytes; mostly in freshwater sponges; concerned with asexual reproduction; survive unfavourable conditions
Most characteristic feature of coelenterates; present in cnidoblasts; found mainly on tentacles in epidermis; used for locomotion, food capture, offence and defence; 'independent effectors'
Central cavity in coelenterates comparable to gut; digestion both extracellular and intracellular; absent anus (blind sac)
Dimorphism with polypoid (asexual) and medusoid (sexual) stages in coelenterates
Non-cellular gelatinous layer between ectoderm and endoderm in coelenterates; may become cellular in Anthozoa
Structure on which mouth is situated in Hydra; most sensitive part of the body

subtopics.definition: Four types of nematocysts in Hydra
subtopics.definition_content: Penetrants/stenoteles (largest), valvents (smallest), stereoline glutinants (small atrichous), streptoline glutinants (large holotrichous)
Special adhesive cells present in all ctenophores; used to capture prey; absent nematocysts
Larval form in ctenophores during indirect development
Excretory system in Platyhelminthes
False segmentation in cestodes (tapeworms); proglottides budded from neck
Cells in planarians responsible for remarkable regeneration power
Miracidium → Sporocyst → Redia → Cercaria → Metacercaria; miracidium and cercaria free-swimming; redia and sporocyst in snail (Limnaea); metacercaria is infective to primary host (sheep)
Groove formed by ventral folding of male Schistosoma body in which female individual lives
Disease caused by blood fluke Schistosoma; primary host is man, secondary host is snail
Head of tapeworm with rostellum (two circles of chitinous hooks) and four suckers for attachment
Long tapering part of tapeworm containing proglottids (young, mature, gravid)
Process of separation of gravid proglottids from the strobila
Embryo with six hooks in shell (onchosphere); infective stage to indefinitionediate host (pig)
Larval stage of Taenia in pig muscles; infective stage to man through measly pork
Disease caused by bladderworm of Taenia; more dangerous than taeniasis; may cause necrosis of brain and epilepsy
False body cavity in nematodes derived from embryonic blastocoel; not lined by coelomic epithelium

topics.name: Class 1. Phasmidia or Secernentea or Rhabditea

subtopics.definition_content: Pair of unicellular pouch-like sense organs near hind end of body; present in parasitic nematodes (Phasmidia)

topics.name: Class 2. Aphasmidia or Adenophorea or Enoplea

subtopics.definition_content: First stage juvenile developed inside Ascaris egg in soil; second stage rhabditiform larva is infective stage to man
Disease caused by Ascaris lumbricoides; symptoms include abdominal discomfort, nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea, colic pain
Larvae of Wuchereria bancrofti; appear in cutaneous blood at midnight; transmitted by Culex/Aedes mosquitoes
Disease caused by Wuchereria blocking lymph glands and lymph vessels; swelling of arms, scrotum, mammary glands
Segmentally arranged excretory organs in annelids; analogous to vertebrate kidney; ectodermal in origin
Free-swimming larval form in indirect development of annelids
Division of body into numerous similar segments (metameres / somites) as in annelids
Lateral fleshy projections of body wall bearing setae in polychaetes; primary locomotory organs; respiratory in some
Glandular region (segments 14-16) in oligochaetes; secretes cocoons for egg deposition; development direct within cocoons
Brown pigment in circular muscle layer of earthworm body wall; protects from injurious effects of bright light
Highly glandular vascular longitudinal ridge in earthworm intestine increasing surface area for absorption
Star-shaped yellow cells in earthworm coelom; involved in storage of reserve food, deamination of proteins, formation of urea; analogous to liver of vertebrates
Arrangement of setae (80-120 per segment) encircling the segment in earthworm
Anti-coagulant in leech saliva preventing clotting of blood during blood meal
Phenomenon of transformation of Nereis into Heteronereis (reproductive phase with epitoke and atoke regions) for spawning

chapters.name: Phylum Arthropoda : The animals with jointed feet

subtopics.definition_content: Persistent blastocoel forming blood-containing space in arthropods; basis of open blood vascular system
Blood filling haemocoel in arthropods; may contain haemocyanin (copper-based respiratory pigment)
Excretory organs of arthropods; excretory product is uric acid (uricotelism)
Living connecting link between Annelida and Arthropoda; terrestrial walking worm with unjointed legs
First pair of wings (mesothoracic) in cockroach; thick, hard, leathery; protective in function; second pair is thin membranous metathoracic
Functional unit of compound eye in cockroach; about 2000 per compound eye
Vision in cockroach; due to non-contractile pigment sheath of ommatidia; capable only of mosaic (apposition) vision even in dim light
Egg case of cockroach containing 16 fertilized eggs in two rows (8+8); formed of protein secreted by collateral glands
Endocrine organ attached to brain in cockroach; secretes juvenile hormone (neotinin) regulating metamorphosis
Moulting hormone secreted by prothoracic glands; stimulated by brain hormone (intercerebral glands); regulates moulting
Incomplete metamorphosis in cockroach; nymph (not larva) emerges from ootheca
Modified metathoracic wings in mosquito (and housefly); balancing and sound-producing structures
Free-swimming active aquatic larva of mosquito performing wriggling movements; undergoes 4 moults (5 instars)
Comma-shaped pupa of mosquito; non-feeding; has pair of respiratory trumpets
Larval stage of housefly living in decaying organic matter; undergoes 2 moults (3 instars); holometabolous (complete) metamorphosis

chapters.name: Phylum Mollusca : The soft bodied animals

subtopics.definition_content: Fleshy fold of body wall covering the soft body of molluscs; may secrete shell
Rasping/scraping organ in mollusc digestive system used to obtain food; absent in Pelecypoda
Chemoreceptor sense organ in molluscs to test chemical nature of water
Kidney (excretory organ) in molluscs
Copper-containing respiratory pigment dissolved in mollusc blood plasma; gives blue colour to blood when oxygenated

topics.name: Class 6. Pelecypoda or Bivalvia or Lamellibranchiata

subtopics.definition_content: Larval form in Pelecypoda (bivalves) during indirect development
Gland present in cephalopods; produces ink used for defence (ink cloud to confuse predators)

chapters.name: Phylum Echinodermata : The spiny skinned animals

subtopics.definition_content: System of water-filled canals with tube-feet in echinoderms; used for locomotion, feeding and respiration
Small pincer-like appendages on echinoderm skin; present in asteroids and echinoids; function in defence and cleaning body surface
Biting and chewing apparatus made of five strong sharp teeth covering the mouth in Echinoidea (sea urchins)

topics.name: Class 1. Crinoidea (sea lilies / feather stars) [Subphylum Pelmatozoa]

subtopics.definition_content: Skeletal rod present at some stage in chordate life cycle; replaced by vertebral column in vertebrates
Openings in pharynx present at some stage in chordates; may or may not be functional in adults
Metamorphosis in Herdmania (sea squirt) where free-swimming tadpole larva regresses to sessile adult losing notochord and nerve cord
Type of scales in cartilaginous fishes made of dentine covered by enamel; teeth are modified placoid scales
Asymmetrical tail fin in cartilaginous and primitive bony fishes where upper lobe is longer
Structure in intestine of cartilaginous fishes increasing surface area for absorption
Symmetrical tail fin characteristic of most modern bony fishes (Teleostei)
Bony flap covering gills on each side in bony fishes; absent in cartilaginous fishes
Hydrostatic organ in bony fishes helping maintain buoyancy; lung-like in Dipnoi (lungfishes)
Skull with two occipital condyles characteristic of amphibians and mammals
Larva of Ambystoma (tiger salamander); shows neoteny/paedogenesis
Retention of larval features in sexually mature organism; e.g., axolotl larva of Ambystoma
Skull with single occipital condyle; characteristic of reptiles and birds
Chemoreceptor structures present in roof of mouth in reptiles; detect chemical stimuli
Excreting uric acid as nitrogenous waste; characteristic of reptiles, birds and insects; conserves water on land
V-shaped bone formed by fusion of both clavicles and single interclavicle in birds
Sound box in birds at or near junction of trachea and bronchi; produces voice (larynx lacks vocal cords)
Regulated constant body temperature; birds are first vertebrates to have warm blood
Extinct Jurassic bird (155 million years ago); connecting link between reptiles and birds; had teeth, scale-like feathers, clawed wings
Glands producing milk for suckling young; characteristic of all female mammals; defines the class
Teeth of several types (incisors, canines, premolars, molars) in mammals
Dentition with two sets of teeth (milk/deciduous and permanent) in mammals
Band of nervous tissue connecting both cerebral hemispheres in mammals; absent in monotremes (Prototheria) and marsupials
Abdominal pouch in marsupial (Metatheria) mammals where immature young complete development attached to teats
Skull with two occipital condyles; each half of lower jaw made of a single bone (dentary) articulating with squamosal

topics.name: Characteristics of the root
key_concepts: Positively geotropic and negatively phototropic
Non-green or brown in colour
No nodes, internodes, leaves or true buds
Tip protected by root cap
Bears unicellular root hairs
Lateral roots are endogenous in origin (arise from pericycle)

subtopics.definition_content: The tip of the root, called calyptra or root cap, formed from meristem called calyptrogen, protects the root tip against injury. Pandanus is the only plant with multiple root caps. In aquatic plants like Pistia, Lemna and Eichhornia, root pockets replace root caps. Root caps are absent in parasites and mycorrhizal roots.
Region of cell division lying protected below the root cap; comprises closely arranged, small, thin-walled and isodiamatric cells with dense protoplasm; vacuoles are reduced or absent.
Lies behind the growing point; cells elongate due to vacuolation (formation of vacuoles); chiefly concerns absorption of minerals along with some water.

subtopics.definition: Region of cell differentiation (Root hair zone)
subtopics.definition_content: Region where elongated cells differentiate into permanent tissues; oudefinitionost layer cells develop root hairs; most water absorption occurs through this region.
Primary root which develops from the elongation of the radicle of embryo and persists throughout the life of plant. It forms secondary and tertiary roots forming tap root system.
Root system that develops from any part of the plant body other than the radicle. Mostly seen in monocotyledonous plants. Grasses possess fibrous root system.
Swollen root broad at the base and tapering gradually towards the apex giving a cone shape, e.g., Carrot.
Root swollen in the middle and narrow towards both its ends, e.g., Radish (Raphanus sativus).

Nearly globular or spherical root; basal portion much swollen which suddenly tapers towards the apex giving a top-shaped appearance, e.g., Turnip (Brassica napus, vern. Shalgam) and Beet (Beta vulgaris, vern. Chukandar).
Storage root having no definite shape, e.g., Mirabilis jalapa (4 O'clock plant).

Root nodules on leguminous plants (sub-family Papilionatae, family Leguminosae). Red due to leghaemoglobin. Inhabited by nitrogen-fixing bacteria Rhizobium leguminosarum which converts atmospheric nitrogen into nitrates.

Aerial, negatively geotropic roots of mangrove plants (saline marshes) bearing minute pores called pneumathodes (lenticels) for gaseous exchange, e.g., Sonneratia, Heritiera, Rhizophora, Avicennia and Ceriops; found in Sundarbans of West Bengal.
Adventitious roots swollen without any definite shape for food storage, e.g., Ipomoea batata (Sweet potato).
Tuberous roots arising in cluster from the base of the stem, e.g., Dahlia, Ruellia (Menow weed), Asparagus.
Roots that become swollen at their tips due to accumulation of food, e.g., Maranta sp. (Arrowroot), Curcuma amanda (Mango-ginger).
Adventitious roots swollen at frequent intervals giving the root a beaded appearance, e.g., Portulaca (Rose moss), Momordica (Bitter gourd), Cyperus (Guinea rush).
In Orchis, a pair of succulent tuberous roots; one perishes every year while a new one forms beside it. May be palmate in shape.
Roots of Cephaelis ipecacuanha (Ipecac) that look like discs placed one above another; yields emetine.
Roots in orchid epiphytes hanging freely in air; absorb moisture with velamen (sponge-like tissue, modification of epidermis), e.g., Vanda, Dendrobium.
Special sponge-like tissue (modification of epidermis) in epiphytic orchid roots that absorbs moisture from the atmosphere.
Roots of parasitic plants that penetrate into host tissues to absorb nourishment, e.g., Cuscuta (Dodder, vern. Amarbel).
Roots associated with fungal hyphae superficially (ectomycorrhizae) or internally (endomycorrhizae) for absorption of water and minerals, e.g., Monotropa and Sarcodes.

Green aerial adventitious roots which prepare food by photosynthesis, e.g., Taeniophyllum, Trapa and Tinospora. In Taeniophyllum the entire plant is represented by thin green ribbon-like roots containing velamen.
Fleshy adventitious roots that develop buds which grow into new plants, serving as means of vegetative propagation, e.g., Sweet potato, Dahlia.

Aerial adventitious obliquely growing roots from lower nodes of stem for additional support, bearing multiple root caps, e.g., Sugarcane, Pandanus, Rhizophora, Sorghum and Maize. Pandanus (screw pine) is a common sea shore plant.

Adventitious aerial roots arising from horizontal aerial branches (e.g., Ficus benghalensis/Banyan), growing vertically downward into soil, becoming thick and pillar-shaped to support spreading branches. Possess lenticels for aeration.
Horizontal plank-like aerial adventitious roots at base of stem for additional support, e.g., definitioninalia and Salmalia.

Aerial adventitious roots from nodes/internodes of weak-stemmed plants to climb support, e.g., Pothos, Piper betel, Vanilla and Hedera. In Vanilla single tendril-like roots (tendrillar roots) arise at each node. In Piper betel, many short branched (clinging roots) arise at each node.
Roots from nodes of floating aquatic plants (e.g., Jussiaea/Ludwigia) that store air, become inflated and spongy, keep plant light and function as floats.
Roots of plants with underground stems that contract or swell to keep aerial shoots at proper depth in the soil, e.g., Canna, Crocus, Allium, Lilium, Freesia.
Adventitious roots that become hard and pointed in aroids like Pothos and palms (Acanthorhiza and Iriartea).

topics.name: Characteristics of stem
key_concepts: Ascending axis of the plant, develops from plumule and epicotyl
Negatively geotropic and positively phototropic
Bears definitioninal bud at growing apex
Differentiated into nodes and internodes
Lateral organs (leaves and branches) are exogenous in origin
Young stem is green and photosynthetic
Hairs, if present, are generally multicellular

subtopics.definition_content: Long, slender, flexible, sensitive stems that twin or coil around an upright support due to nutation; sinistrorse (anticlockwise, e.g., Convolvulus, Ipomoea quamoclit) or dextrorse (clockwise, e.g., Lablab).
Tendrils are thread-like green structures helping in climbing; may be modified stem (Vitis), stem branches (Passiflora) or inflorescence (Antigonon).
Woody perennial climbers found in deep forests; initially ordinary twiners that become woody on reaching sunlight, e.g., Tinospora, Ficus, Bauhinia, Bignonia.
Weak stems creeping on the ground without roots arising at nodes. Types: Procumbent (e.g., Tribulus, Basella), Decumbent (e.g., Portulaca, Tridax), Diffuse (e.g., Boerhaavia).
Prostrate aerial stem with long internodes creeping horizontally; axillary buds form aerial shoots and roots at nodes, e.g., Cynodon (doob grass) and Oxalis.
Special runners that initially grow upward like ordinary branches then arch down to develop new daughter plants on soil contact, e.g., Strawberry (Fragaria vesica), Colocasia, Peppermint, Jasminum.
Weak elongated horizontal branch of one internode arising in axil of leaf; at tip produces cluster of leaves above and tuft of roots below; mainly in aquatic plants, e.g., Eichhornia, Pistia, Agave.

Sub-aerial branch arising from the main stem; grows horizontally below soil surface then obliquely upward; shorter and stouter than runners, e.g., Mentha arvensis (mint, vern. Podina) and Chrysanthemum.

Tuberous tip of an underground branch (stolon) swollen due to accumulation of starch and proteins, e.g., Solanum tuberosum (Potato). 'Eyes' on corky surface represent nodes with axillary buds in pits.

Thickened underground dorsiventral stem growing horizontally at particular depth; brown, with nodes, internodes, definitioninal bud, axillary bud and scale leaves. Types: Rootstock (e.g., Alocasia, Banana) and Straggling/horizontal (e.g., Lotus, Ginger, Turmeric).

Underground modification of main stem with more diameter than length; grows vertically; non-green; bears scale leaves at nodes with axillary buds; propagates by daughter corms, e.g., Amorphophallus, Gladiolus, Colocasia and Crocus (Saffron).

Specialized underground stem with reduced discoid stem bearing adventitious roots below and rosette of fleshy leaf bases or fleshy scales above storing carbohydrates and water, e.g., Allium cepa (Onion).

Bulb with fleshy leaf bases arranged concentrically, covered by peripheral dry membranous leaf bases (tunics), e.g., Allium cepa (Onion), Narcissus, Tulip. Compound tunicated bulbs as in Allium sativum (garlic).
Bulb with fleshy scale leaves arranged loosely overlapping one another, not covered by any tunics (naked bulb), e.g., Lilium bulbifera (Lily).

Thin, wiry, leafless and spirally coiled branches formed from axillary or definitioninal buds; help weak stems climb support. Types: Axillary (e.g., Passiflora), Extra-axillary (e.g., Luffa, Cucurbita, Lagenaria), Apical bud (e.g., Vitis vinifera/Grape Vine), Floral bud/inflorescence (e.g., Antigonon).

Axillary buds modified into stiff, sharp, pointed structures with vascular connections; reduce transpiration and protect from browsing animals, e.g., Citrus, Duranta, Bougainvillea, Pomegranate, Aegle marmelos.

Special modified photosynthetic stem mostly in xerophytes; green, flattened or cylindrical with distinct nodes and internodes; retains water as mucilage, e.g., Opuntia (leaves modified into spines), Casuarina, Cocoloba and Ruscus.

Modifications of stem and branches of limited growth with only one internode; green, flat or cylindrical, leaf-like photosynthetic structures, e.g., Asparagus (leaves reduced to curved spines), Ruscus aculeatus.
Thalamus of a flower is a modified stem apex bearing floral parts; may be convex (Ranunculus), concave (Lathyrus) or flask-shaped (Rosa).
definitioninal bud grows indefinitely; axillary buds grow into lateral branches in acropetal succession; shoot system appears conical, e.g., Eucalyptus, Polyalthia (Ashoka tree).

definitioninal bud active for short period then modified into permanent structure (tendril, thorn, flower); further growth by axillary buds. Types: Uniparous/Monochasial (Helicoid e.g., Saraca, Canna; Scorpioid e.g., Cissus, Gossypium), Biparous/Dichasial (e.g., Viscum, Silene, Mirabilis jalapa), Multiparous/Polychasial (e.g., Euphorbia tirucalli, Croton, Nerium).
Buds developing in the axil of leaves to produce branches.
Additional buds at leaf bases. Collateral buds: present on side of axillary bud (e.g., lilies). Superposed buds: present above the axillary bud (e.g., Aristolochia).

Buds growing from positions other than normal. Foliar buds: on leaves (e.g., Bryophyllum, Begonia, Kalanchoe). Radical buds: on roots (e.g., sweet potato, Dalbergia). Cauline buds: on stem (e.g., Rose).

Modification of whole buds into swollen structures due to food storage for vegetative propagation, e.g., Lilium bulbiferum and Dioscorea bulbifera (axil of leaves), Agave (floral buds of inflorescence), Oxalis (above swollen roots).

topics.name: Characteristics of leaf
key_concepts: Lateral dissimilar appendage of the stem
Always borne at the node of stem
Growth of leaf is limited
No apical bud or regular growing point

subtopics.definition_content: Swollen leaf base in members of Leguminosae helping in seismonastic movements (e.g., Mimosa pudica) and nyctinastic movements (e.g., Enterobium, Arachis, Bean).
Broad leaf base surrounding the stem as an envelope in grasses and many monocots; protects the intercalary meristem in Sorghum and Wheat.
Leaf base that completely encircles the stem, e.g., Polygonum.
Additional outgrowth present at junction of leaf sheath and leaf lamina in some grasses. Leaves with ligules are called ligulate.

Small lateral appendages on either side of the leaf base for protection of young leaf or leaf primordia. Types: Free lateral (e.g., Hibiscus), Adnate (e.g., Rose), Interpetiolar (e.g., Ixora), Axillary/Intrapetiolar (e.g., Gardenia), Ochraceous (e.g., Polygonum), Foliaceous/Assimilatory (e.g., Pisum sativum, Lathyrus aphaca), Spinous (e.g., Acacia arabica, Zizyphus), Convolute/Bud scales (e.g., Artocarpus).
Modified petiole that is flat, green and lamina-like; photosynthetic organ, e.g., Acacia auriculaeformis (Australian babool).
Green, flattened petiole, e.g., Citrus and Dionaea.
Petioles modified into tendrils for climbing, e.g., Clematis and Tropaeolum.
Petiole becomes swollen and spongy due to aerenchyma development, helping plant to float; hydrophytic adaptation, e.g., Trapa bispinosa and Eichhornia.

topics.name: Shape of lamina
key_concepts: Acicular: needle-shaped (e.g., Allium cepa, Pinus)
Linear: long and slightly broader (e.g., Triticum/wheat)
Lanceolate: lance-shaped (e.g., Nerium, Polyalthaea)
Orbicular/Rotund: more or less circular (e.g., Lotus, Nasturtium)
Elliptical oval (e.g., Psidium/Guava)
Ovate: egg/top-shaped (e.g., China rose); Obovate: inverted egg-shaped (e.g., Walnut)
Oblong: rectangular (e.g., Banana)
Reniform: kidney-shaped (e.g., Hydrocotyle)
Cordate: heart-shaped with deep notch at base (e.g., Betel, Banyan)
Sagittate: arrow head-shaped (e.g., Sagittaria)
Hastate: sagittate with basal lobes directed outwards (e.g., Ipomoea)
Lyrate: lyre-shaped (e.g., Raphanus sativus, Brassica campestris)
Centric: hollow and cylindrical (e.g., Onion)

topics.name: Margin, Apex and Surface of lamina
key_concepts: Margin types: Entire (Mango), Serrate (Hibiscus), Repand (Polyalthia), Dentate (Aloe), Crenate (Bryophyllum), Spiny (Argemone), Biserate (Ricinus)

Apex types: Acute (Mango), Acuminate (Ficus religiosa), Obtuse (Banyan), Mucronate (Vinca), Cuspidate/Spiny (Date palm), Tendrillar (Gloriosa), Truncate (Paris polyphylla), Emarginate (Bauhinia, Oxalis)
Surface: Glabrous (smooth, e.g., Mangifera), Glaucus (waxy, e.g., Calotropis), Scabrous (rough, e.g., Ficus), Pubescent (soft wooly hair, e.g., Tomato), Hispid (long rigid hair, e.g., Cucurbita)
Texture: Herbaceous (thin, soft), Coriaceous (leathery), Succulent (thick, soft, juicy), Hygrophytic (thin, membranous, spongy)

topics.name: Life Span, Leaf Insertion and Types of leaves

subtopics.definition_content: Leaves falling down soon after their appearance, e.g., Opuntia.
Leaves falling almost simultaneously at the end of growing season (leaf fall), e.g., Mulberry, Poplar.
Leaves living for more than one season, falling individually at different times, e.g., Pinus, Eucalyptus, Mango, Oleander.
Unipinnate compound leaf with even number of leaflets borne in pairs, e.g., Tamarindus indica (Imli), Cassia.
Unipinnate compound leaf with odd number of leaflets; rachis definitioninated by single unpaired leaflet, e.g., Neem, Rose, Murraya.
Primary rachis divided once producing secondary rachis bearing leaflets, e.g., Delonix, Acacia, Mimosa pudica, Albizzia.
Primary rachis divides many times without definite order; lamina dissected into many units, e.g., Coriandrum, Carrot.
Palmately compound leaf with three definitioninal leaflets, e.g., Aegle marmelos (Bael), Oxalis corniculata, Trifolium (Clover).
Palmately compound leaf with five or more definitioninal leaflets, e.g., Bombax malabarica, Cleome viscosa.
The arrangement of leaves on the stem. Angular divergence = (No. of circles / Orthostichies) of 360°. Phyllotaxy is written as number of spirals/number of leaves.

One leaf at each node; leaves at successive nodes alternate with each other. Types based on orthostichies: Distichous (1/2, 180°), Tristichous (1/3, 120°), Pentastichous (2/5, 144° e.g., China rose), Octastichous (3/8, 135° e.g., Carica papaya). Schimper-Brown Series.
All pairs of leaves arise in same plane forming only two vertical rows, e.g., Jamun, Guava.
Pair of leaves at one node at right angles to next upper or lower pair forming four vertical rows, e.g., Calotropis, Zinnia, Tulsi, Quisqualis.
More than two leaves at a node as whorl (cyclic or verticellate phyllotaxy), e.g., Nerium, Hydrilla and Alstonia.

Special arrangement where older leaves at lower nodes have longer petioles with bigger lamina; young leaves at upper nodes have shorter petioles with smaller lamina, e.g., Begonia, Acalypha and Sycamore.

Arrangement of leaves in bud condition. Types: Imbricate (irregular overlapping), Contorted (twisted, regular overlapping), Induplicate (margin bent inwardly), Equitant (conduplicate in two series, one overlapping other), Supervolute (convolute leaves, one rolled over other).

Occurrence of more than one type of leaves on the same plant. Types: (1) Adaptive heterophylly - submerged leaves different from floating/emerged leaves (e.g., Sagittaria, Ranunculus aquatilis); (2) Environmental heterophylly (e.g., Sagittaria); (3) Developmental heterophylly - young leaves different from mature (e.g., Eucalyptus); (4) Habitual heterophylly - different leaf shapes at same time (e.g., Artocarpus heterophyllus/Jack fruit, Ficus heterophylla).

Leaves modified into slender, wiry, coiled structures for climbing. Entire leaf (Lathyrus aphaca), definitioninal leaflets (Pisum sativum, Lathyrus odoratus, Narvella), leaf tip (Gloriosa), petiole (Clematis), stipule (Smilax), midrib (Nepenthes).

Entire leaf or part modified into pointed spines. In Opuntia: leaves of axillary branches; Berberis: entire leaf into three spines; Phoenix: leaf tip; Citrus: first leaf of axillary branch; Argemone: leaf margin; Perkinsonia/Acacia/Zizyphus: stipules.
Foliage leaves reduced to thin, membranous, dry, small, sessile, colourless structures in xerophytes, e.g., Casuarina, Orobanche and Balanophora.
Leaves becoming fleshy due to storage of water or food; found in succulent plants, e.g., Aloe, Allium, Kalanchoe and Peperomia.
Leaves of plants in nitrogen-deficient soils that capture and digest insects; modified to attract, capture, kill and digest insects. Plants have poorly developed root system, e.g., Nepenthes.
Leaf segments of aquatic insectivore Utricularia modified into bladders for trapping small aquatic animals like Cyclops.

Lamina (e.g., Nepenthes) or whole leaf (e.g., Dischidia, Sarracenia) modified into pitcher with lid for catching and digesting small animals. In Dischidia the pitcher stores rain water. In Sarracenia takes part in digesting small animals.

Shedding of leaves; a special layer of parenchymatous cells (abscission or separation layer) appears at leaf base; below it a protective layer of suberised cork cells forms; cells of abscission layer gelatinise and leaf falls leaving a scar.

Lateral veins divide and redivide forming net-like pattern (reticulum); characteristic of dicotyledons. Exceptions with reticulate venation in monocots: Smilax, Alocasia, Dioscorea. Types: Unicostate/Pinnate (single strong midrib, e.g., Ficus, Mangifera); Multicostate/Palmate - Convergent (prominent veins converge at apex, e.g., Zizyphus, Cinnamomum camphora) or Divergent (veins spread to margins, e.g., Papaya, Ricinus, Cucurbita).

Veins and veinlets run parallel; characteristic of monocotyledons. Exceptions with parallel venation in dicots: Calophyllum, Eryngium. Types: Unicostate/Pinnate (e.g., Banana, Canna, Curcuma); Multicostate/Palmate - Convergent (e.g., Sugarcane, Maize, Wheat, Bamboos) or Divergent (e.g., Fan palm).

Folding of lamina in bud condition. Types: Circinate (rolled from apex to base e.g., fern), Plicate (plaited/folded lengthwise e.g., Fan Palm), Conduplicate (folded over midrib e.g., Bauhinia, Guava), Reclinate (upper half bent over lower half e.g., Loquat), Involute (margins rolled on upper surface e.g., Colocasia), Revolute (margins rolled on lower surface e.g., Oleander).
Single definitioninal flower developing on tip of main stem and its branches, e.g., Poppy.
Flower occurring singly in the axil of a leaf (e.g., Petunia) or tip of a peduncle (e.g., China Rose = Hibiscus rosa-sinensis).
Unbranched elongated peduncle bearing pedicellate or stalked flowers acropetally (oldest at base, youngest at apex), e.g., Delphinium (Larkspur), Raphanus (Radish), Lupinus.
All acropetally arranged flowers at same level due to shortening of peduncle and elongation of pedicels of lower flowers, e.g., Iberis amara (Candytuft).
Like a corymb near growing point and raceme lower down; pedicels of lower flowers are longer, e.g., Brassica campestris (Mustard).
Pedicellate flowers arranged centripetally around an extremely reduced peduncle with an involucre below, e.g., Centella (= Hydrocotyle) asiatica (Brahmi Booti), Androsace.
Sessile (without stalk) flowers borne acropetally, e.g., Callistemon (Bottle Brush), Amaranthus, Achryanthes, Adhatoda.
Compact spike having a few flowers on axis (rachilla) surrounded by two scales (glumes); characteristic of grasses, e.g., Wheat, Oat, Sorghum, Grass.
Spike having persistent and membranous bracts, e.g., Humulus (Hop).
Compact unisexual spike often hanging, e.g., Morus (Mulberry), Salix (Willow), Populus (Poplar), Betula (Birch), Acalypha, Quercus (Oak).
Fleshy spike covered with spathe, e.g., Maize, Banana.

Peduncle flattened into receptacle bearing centripetally arranged sessile florets surrounded by involucre of bracts. Homogamous (one type of florets e.g., Sonchus, Vernonia) or heterogamous (two types, e.g., Sunflower/Helianthus annuus with ligulate female ray florets and tubular intersexual disc florets), e.g., Cosmos, Zinnia, Tagetes, Chrysanthemum.
Racemes borne acropetally on a raceme (raceme of racemes), e.g., Cassia fistula, Delonix regia, Yucca, Asparagus, Margosa.
Axis bearing a number of corymbs in corymbose fashion, e.g., Pyrus, Cauliflower (Brassica oleracea var. botrytis).
Many umbels from a common point in umbellate fashion; characteristic of family Umbelliferae; involucre below mother umbel and involucels below each umbellule, e.g., Coriander, Fennel, Carrot, Cumin.
Multiple spadices, e.g., Date Palm, Coconut.
More or less flat-topped, broad inflorescence of dedefinitioninate growth where central flower opens first (most mature). Main axis definitioninates in a flower.
Flowers borne on one side; can be drepanium (flowers in one plane) or bostryx (flowers in different planes), e.g., Begonia, Drosera.
Flowers borne on both sides alternately, e.g., Tecoma, Freesia, Heliotropium. Rhipidium: scorpioid cyme with all flowers in one plane (e.g., Solanum nigrum). Cincinnus: flowers in different planes.
Growth continued by two branches when growing point changes into flower, e.g., Dianthus (Pink), Silene, Nyctanthes, Jasminum, Clerodendron, Bougainvillea, Teak.
More than two branches continue growth when parent axis becomes a flower, e.g., Calotropis, Hamelia, Asclepias.
Centrifugally arranged flowers around a receptacle, e.g., Anthocephalus cadamba (Kadam).
Cymose clusters arranged acropetally, e.g., Vitis vinifera (Grape Vine).
Spadices having cymose inflorescence arranged acropetally on fleshy axis, e.g., Banana.

Flask-shaped fleshy receptacle with a pore or ostiole lined by scales and a short canal bearing hair; internally: male flowers near ostiole, female flowers towards base, sterile female flowers between the two, e.g., Ficus (Peepal, Banyan, Fig).
Saucer-shaped receptacle with upturned margin bearing florets like hypanthodium, e.g., Dorstena.
Characteristic inflorescence of Ocimum (Tulsi) of family Lamiaceae or Labiatae; flowers arranged in two opposite cymose groups on each node.
Characteristic inflorescence of Euphorbia: single female flower in centre, number of male flowers around it, all inside a cup-shaped involucre (whorl of bracts).
Modified dwarf shoot meant for sexual reproduction; characteristic feature of angiosperm; consists of four floral parts: calyx, corolla, androecium and gynoecium.
Presence of male and female flowers on the same plant, e.g., Acalypha, Cocos and Ricinus. In Maize flowers are unisexual but plant is monoecious.
Presence of male and female flowers on different plants (male plants and female plants), e.g., Cycas, Carica papaya and Vallisneria.
Presence of unisexual and bisexual flowers on the same plant, e.g., Mangifera and Polygonum.
Flowers divisible by any vertical plane through center into two equal and similar halves, e.g., Mustard, Brinjal, Catharanthus roseus.
Flowers divisible into two equal halves by only one vertical plane, e.g., Pea, Larkspur, Ocimum.
Flowers that cannot be divided into two equal halves by any vertical division, e.g., Canna, Orchids.
Thalamus conical or convex with floral parts spirally arranged, e.g., Water lily and Magnolia.
Floral organs arranged in regular whorls at the nodes of thalamus, e.g., Hibiscus and Datura.
Some floral parts (sepals and petals) in regular whorls and remaining parts (stamens and carpels) arranged spirally, e.g., Annona and Polyalthia.
Two floral parts in each whorl, e.g., Poppy.
Three floral parts in each whorl; characteristic of monocot flowers (Liliaceae).
Four floral parts in each whorl, e.g., Mustard.
Five floral parts in each whorl, e.g., Solanum.
Leaf-like, expanded green bract, e.g., Pisum, Lathyrus, Adhatoda and Gynandropsis.
Large modified bract enclosing spadix inflorescence totally or partially; leathery or woody, e.g., Alocasia, Cocoa, Musa and Typhonium.
Brightly coloured petal-like bract, e.g., Bougainvillea, Poinsettia and Euphorbia.
One or more whorls of green bracts protecting young inflorescence, e.g., Coriandrum, Tagetes and Heracleum.
Whorl of bracteoles present below the calyx or outside the calyx, e.g., Hibiscus rosa-sinensis and Malvaviscus arborcus.
Elongated first internode between the calyx and corolla, e.g., Silene, Pennsylvania and Lychnis.
Elongated second internode between corolla and androecium, e.g., Gynandropsis.
Elongated third internode between androecium and gynoecium, e.g., Capparis and Gynandropsis.
Elongated stalk-like part between non-essential and essential organs of the flower; equivalent to androphore, e.g., Passiflora.
Stalk-like connection between two carpels formed by expansion of thalamus between the carpels, e.g., Coriandrum and Foeniculum.
When androphore and gynophore are present simultaneously, e.g., Cleome gynandra syn. Gynandropsis gynandra.

Non-essential organs calyx and corolla together; protects stamens and carpels. Achlamydeous (absent, e.g., Euphorbia). Chlamydeous (present): Monochlamydeous (one whorl, e.g., Amaranthus, Ricinus); Dichlamydeous (two whorls): Homochlamydeous (two similar whorls, e.g., Michelia) or Heterochlamydeous (two dissimilar whorls, e.g., Datura, Hibiscus).
Perianth units when both sepals and petals are similar, e.g., most monocots.
Calyx with free sepals, e.g., Annona, Tomato.
Calyx with united sepals, e.g., Datura and Hibiscus.
Persistent calyx that does not show any growth after fertilization, e.g., Brinjal, Chilies.
Persistent calyx showing continuous growth even after fertilization, e.g., Physalis and Shorea.
Calyx modified into hairs, e.g., Sonchus, Tridax (Asteraceae/Compositae).
Special appendages (scales, hairs) developing from the corolla, e.g., Passiflora, Oleander and Nerium.
Four free clawed petals arranged in the form of a cross, e.g., Mustard and Radish.
Five free sessile petals with lobes spreading outwards, e.g., Rose, Hibiscus.
Five free unequal petals: posterior petal (standard/vexillum) is largest, two lateral petals (wings), two anterior petals united into boat-shaped keel; characteristic of family Papilionaceae.
Five united petals forming a cylindrical tubular structure, e.g., disc florets of Asteraceae.
Funnel-shaped corolla, e.g., Datura.
Bell-shaped corolla, e.g., Thevetia.
Short tubular corolla with spread-out lobes appearing like a wheel, e.g., Brinjal.
Salver-shaped corolla with elongated narrow tube and lobes at top at right angles, e.g., Vinca.
Short tube drawn out into a tongue-shaped (strap-shaped) structure, e.g., ray florets of Asteraceae.
Irregular gamopetalous corolla appearing two-lipped; characteristic corolla of Labiatae, e.g., Leucas.
Arrangement of sepals and petals in bud condition of the flower.
Margins of perianth members in a whorl are free with wide gap between them, e.g., sepals of Mustard.
Edges of perianth members in a whorl nearly touching but not overlapping, e.g., calyx and corolla of Annona.
Each perianth member has one edge outside and one edge inside, regularly overlapping neighbouring members on one side, e.g., corolla of Hibiscus.

One perianth member completely inside, another completely outside, remaining show partial overlapping. Descending imbricate: odd petal posterior and completely outside (e.g., Tephrosia, Crotalaria). Ascending imbricate: odd petal posterior and completely inside, characteristic of Caesalpiniaceae (e.g., Cassia, Delonix).
In pentamerous flowers: two perianth members completely outside, two completely inside, one with one edge outside and one-edge inside, e.g., sepals of Ipomoea, Vinca and Thevetia.
Same as papilionaceous corolla aestivation; characteristic of Papilionaceae.

Third whorl of a flower consisting of stamens or microsporophylls; fertile stamens produce pollen grains. Staminodes are sterile stamens. Petaloid stamens are brightly coloured appearing like petals, e.g., Canna.
Structure connecting the filament of stamen to the anther. Anther may be monothecous (one sac, bilocular e.g., Hibiscus) or dithecous (two sacs, tetralocular e.g., Datura).
Face of anther towards the centre of flower, e.g., Tomato.
Face of anther towards the periphery, e.g., Ranunculus.
Filament attached to the total length of the anther on the back, e.g., Michelia (Champa).
Filament attached to the base of the anther, e.g., Datura, Mustard, Radish.
Filament attached to the anther on the dorsal side at middle portion, e.g., Passiflora.
Filament attached to the anther at a point so that anther can swing freely in all directions, e.g., Grasses.
Four stamens in a flower of which two are long and two are short, e.g., Ocimum.
Six stamens in a flower of which four are long and two are short, e.g., Raphanus and Brassica.
Stamens form a single whorl with number equal to sepals and petals, e.g., Solanum.
Two whorls of stamens: first alternating with petals (antisepalous) and second alternating with sepals (antipetalous), e.g., Cassia.
First whorl antipetalous and second whorl antisepalous, e.g., Dianthus.
All filaments unite to form a single bundle with anthers remaining free, e.g., family Malvaceae (Hibiscus).
Filaments unite to form two bundles, e.g., family Papilionaceae (Pisum, Sesbania, Tephrosia); (9) + 1.
Filaments unite to form many bundles, e.g., family Rutaceae (Citrus, Melaleuca).
Anthers of filaments are united while filaments remain free, e.g., Tridax, Sunflower.
All stamens of a flower united completely to form a single structure, e.g., family Cucurbitaceae.
Stamens united with perianth, e.g., Onion.
Stamens united with sepals, e.g., Prunus (Peach).
Stamens united with petals, e.g., Datura.
Stamens united with gynoecium (also called gynandrium or gynostegium), e.g., Calotropis.

Fourth essential whorl of female reproductive part of the flower made up of one or more carpels (megasporophylls). Each carpel has three distinct parts: ovary (lower most swollen fertile part enclosing ovules), style and stigma.
Sterile pistil.
Gynoecium with a single carpel, e.g., Bean.
Gynoecium with two carpels, e.g., Helianthus.
Gynoecium with three carpels, e.g., Cocos.
Gynoecium with four carpels, e.g., Cotton.
Gynoecium with five carpels, e.g., Hibiscus.
Gynoecium with many carpels, e.g., Annona.
Fruit is defined as fertilized ovary. The ovary develops into fruit. The ovary wall at maturity forms the wall of the fruit, which is known as pericarp.

Sometimes, other parts of flower such as tepals (e.g., Morus), bracts (e.g., Ananas) or thalamus (e.g., Pyrus) are also involved in the formation of fruit and such fruits are called false fruits or pseudocarps.
They are formed from mono- or polycarpellary but syncarpous ovary. They may be dry or fleshy.
It is characteristic of the family leguminosae; developed from monocarpellary unilocular superior ovary with marginal placentation. It can open or dehisces by both ventral and dorsal sutures.
It is very much resembles the legume but on ripening it opens generally along the ventral suture. e.g., Calotropis, Larkspur, etc.

The fruit is developed from bicarpellary, syncarpous and superior ovary which bears ovules on two parietal placenta. The ovary is unilocular but later becomes bilocular due to the development of a false partition wall called replum. It dehisces from the base towards the apex by both the sutures.
It is flattened and short in length from siliqua type, found in Iberis (Candytuft) and Capsella bursa (Shepherd's purse).
It is small, dry one seeded fruit which develops from a superior or inferior monocarpellary ovary. In this type, the pericarp is tough but thin and free from the seed coat.
It is very small, dry and one seeded fruit which develops from a superior monocarpellary ovary. Here the pericarp is closely fused with seed coat. It is the characteristic of family gramineae.

It is dry, one seeded fruit which develops from an inferior, bicarpellary ovary. Here the pericarp is free from seed coat but the thalamus is fused with pericarp. The fruit is provided with a crown of hairs at the top called pappus.
In schizocarpic fruits, the fruit breaks into one seeded segments known as mericarps.

It is a dry, many seeded fruit which develops from a monocarpellary, superior, unilocular ovary with marginal placentation. The fruit arises just like a legume but when ripened it becomes partitioned between seeds into single seeded mericarps.

It is a fleshy fruit formed from mono or poly carpellary superior ovary, where one or more ovules may develop into seeds. Here the epicarp is thin and leathery. The mesocarp is thick, fleshy, juicy and edible in Mangifera (Mango) and fibrous in Cocos (Coconut). The endocarp is hard and stony in both the cases.

It is usually many seeded fleshy fruit develops from polycarpellary, syncarpous, superior ovary. Rarely it is single seeded as in Borassus (Palm). Here the epicarp remains as the skin of the fruit. The mesocarp and endocarp are fused together to form the pulp of the fruit.

It is a special type of berry. Here the epicarp and thalamus form the outer ring of the fruit. The mesocarp, endocarp and placentae are fused to form pulp which is edible; seeds are many. It is characteristics fruit of family cucurbitaceae.

The fruit develops from inferior, pentacarpel ovary. The fruit is covered by the fleshy thalamus, which is fused with the pericarp and edible. The outer part again encloses the inner stiff and membranous portion enclosing the seeds.

It is another type of berry; it develops from a polycarpellary, syncarpous, superior ovary with many seeds. Here the outer skin is thick and leathery that represents the epicarp, which contains oil glands. The fibrous portion fused with epicarp is the mesocarp. The endocarp consists of many chambers with juicy glands.

This is many chambered, many seeded fruit developing from a multicarpellary, syncarpous but inferior ovary. The pericarp of balausta is leathery or tough. The carpels are arranged in two rows. Calyx is persistent. The seeds have succulent seed coat (testa) which form the edible part.
In aggregate fruits formed from polycarpellary, apocarpous ovary, each ripened carpel is called fruitlet or etaerio.
Composite fruit developing from spike or spadix inflorescence. e.g., Pineapple, Jackfruit, Mulberry, etc.
Composite fruit developing from hypanthodium inflorescence. e.g., Ficus carica (banyan).
Morphologically, ripened ovule is known as seed. In other words, seed is mature integumented megasporangium.
The nucellus is generally used up during the development of embryo but in some cases it remains outside the endosperm in the form of a thin layer, called perisperm.
A scar is usually visible on one side of the outer seed coat. It is known as hilum and marks the point of attachment to the stalk.

In exalbuminous seeds endosperm is completely consumed by the developing embryo, and the mature seeds are without endosperm. The food is stored in cotyledons. e.g., Gram, Pea, Bean, Mustard, Mango, Groundnut.
In albuminous seeds, embryo does not consume all endosperm. So it persists in the mature seed. e.g., Castor, Poppy, Artabotrys, Custard apple.
Membranous covering of radicle in monocot seed.
Membranous covering of plumule in monocot seed.
The process by which the dormant embryo of the seed resumes active growth and grows into a new plant is known as germination.
In this type of germination, the cotyledons come above the surface of the soil into the air and light due to the rapid growth and elongation of the hypocotyl. e.g., Bean, Gourd, Castor, Cotton.
In this type of germination, the cotyledons remain in the soil or just above the surface. In this case epicotyl elongates pushing the plumule upwards. e.g., Pea, Mango, Groundnut.

This is a special type of germination found in mangrove plants. The embryo grows not only out of the seed but also out of the fruit and projects from it in the form of a green seedling displaying root and hypocotyl. e.g., Rhizophora and Sonneratia.
In some plants seeds are incapable of germination because of some inhibitory factors. Such seeds are unable to germinate even under suitable conditions. This is called seed dormancy.
Germinating ability of the seed is called its viability. Only viable seeds are able to germinate. As the viability expires, the embryo dies and seed looses its capacity of germination.
One or more tufts of hairs are attached on seeds constitute coma. e.g., Calotropis, Cotton etc.

In plants like Antirrhinum, Aristolochia, Papaver, Argemone mexicana, Nigella, the fruit is a capsule. At maturity it ruptures but the seeds do not come out. However, when the capsule is shaken violently by the wind, the seeds are scattered in all directions.
In members of family Asteraceae (Compositae), sepals are modified into tufts of hairs called pappus. It is persistent and acts like a parachute allowing wind to carry seeds to great distances.

In some species plants dry out after bearing fruits and seeds. The entire plant breaks off at the base and rolls over the ground shedding seeds along the way. Such rolling plants are called tumble weeds.

topics.name: Dispersal by explosive or Spring like mechanism (Autochory)

subtopics.definition_content: Division: Angiospermae, Class: Monocotyledonae, Series: Coronarieae, Order: Liliales, Family: Liliaceae.
Stamens attached to tepals (perianth segments) in Liliaceae; anthers may be basifixed, dorsifixed or versatile.

An alkaloid obtained from seeds and corms of Colchicum luteum and C. autumnale, used in cure of rheumatism and liver disorders, and also for inducing polyploidy in plant breeding by arresting spindle formation.
Modified stem resembling a leaf; found in Asparagus in Liliaceae.
Division: Angiospermae, Class: Dicotyledonae, Subclass: Polypetalae, Series: Thalamiflorae, Order: Parietales, Family: Cruciferae (Brassicaceae).
Four petals spread out in the form of a Greek cross; characteristic arrangement of petals in family Cruciferae.
Androecium condition in Brassicaceae: Stamens 6, tetradynamous, arranged in two whorls — outer of two short lateral stamens and inner whorl of 4 long stamens arranged in two median pairs.
A false septum called replum in the ovary of Brassicaceae; the parietal ovary becomes bilocular due to the presence of a false septum called replum.
Presence of myrosin enzyme is chief feature of family Brassicaceae.
Division: Angiospermae, Class: Dicotyledonae, Subclass: Polypetalae, Series: Calyciflorae, Order: Rosales, Family: Leguminosae.

Corolla of subfamily Papilionatae: Petals 5, polypetalous, papilionaceous, descending imbricate aestivation, one posterior long standard, two lateral short wings, two anterior petals jointed to each other forming keel.
Androecium condition in Papilionatae: stamens usually diadelphous (9+1 in Lathyrus). Filaments of 9 are fused and 1 (posterior) is free.
Corolla aestivation of subfamily Caesalpinoideae: Petals 5, polypetalous, ascending imbricate aestivation.
Androecium in Mimosoideae: In most members stamens are indefinite and polyandrous; filaments long, usually connate at the base.
Division: Angiospermae, Class: Dicotyledonae, Subclass: Gamopetalae, Series: Bicarpellatae, Order: Polimoniales, Family: Solanaceae.
Calyx that enlarges in fruit; e.g., Physalis, Withania in Solanaceae.
Drug obtained from dried leaves and flowering tops of Datura stramonium, used in treatment of asthma. Contains alkaloids atropine, hyoscyamine and hyocine.
Alkaloid obtained from roots of Atropa belladona (Belladona).
Drug obtained from roots of Withania somnifera (Asgandha), used in rheumatism, female troubles and cough.
Division: Angiospermae, Class: Dicotyledonae, Subclass: Polypetalae, Series: Thalamiflorae, Order: Malvales, Family: Malvaceae.
A whorl of bracteoles present below the calyx in Malvaceae; the number of bracteoles varies from 3 to many.
Indefinite stamens monoadelphous in Malvaceae; filaments united to form a long staminal tube or staminal column which encloses the style. Basal part of staminal tube fused with petals (epipetalous).
Anthers of Malvaceae: monothecous, reniform, transversely attached to the filament and extrorse.
Division: Angiospermae, Class: Dicotyledonae, Subclass: Polypetalae, Series: Calyciflorae, Order: Passiflorales, Family: Cucurbitaceae.

Flowers are bracteate or ebracteate, pedicellate, unisexual, incomplete actinomorphic, pentamerous and epigynous. Most members are monoecious; a few are dioecious (e.g., Coccinia cordifolia, Trichosanthes dioica).
Characteristic fruit of family Cucurbitaceae; a variation of berry where epicarp and thalamus form the outer ring, and mesocarp, endocarp and placentae are fused to form pulp.
Division: Angiospermae, Class: Dicotyledonae, Subclass: Gamopetalae, Series: Inferae, Order: Asterales, Family: Compositae (Asteraceae). Largest family among the angiosperms.
Inflorescence of Compositae; a head with an involucre of bracts at its base. The number of flowers varies from 1000 (in Helianthus) to 1 (in Echinops). Peduncle flat on which florets are attached.
Florets towards the periphery of the capitulum head; sessile bracteate, pistillate or neutral, zygomorphic, ligulate, epigynous.
Florets in the centre of the capitulum head; bracteate, bisexual, actinomorphic, tubular, pentamerous, epigynous. Stamens 5, epipetalous, syngenesious, dithecous.
Florets in which androecium and gynoecium both are absent; remaining structures similar to ray floret and disc florets.
Characteristic fruit of Compositae (Asteraceae); dry, one seeded, inferior ovary, pericarp fused with thalamus.
Commercial insecticide obtained from flower heads of Chrysanthemum marschallii and C. cinerariaefolium (Guldaodi).
A wormicide (anthelmintic) obtained from dried unopened flower heads of Artemisia cina or A. maritima, used as vermifuge for expelling intestinal worms.
Stem of grasses having marked solid nodes and hollow internodes; stem is generally circular and hollow.
A membranous outgrowth present at the junction of leaf sheath and leaf lamina in Gramineae.
Unit of inflorescence in Gramineae; having 1–5 flowers on a reduced axis bearing two leaf like structures (glumes) at base.
A bract-like structure (bract) in the axil of which each flower of a spikelet is borne in Gramineae.
A bracteole-like structure present on the flower axis above the lemma in Gramineae.
Two scale-like structures above the palea in Gramineae; represent the reduced perianth.
Characteristic fruit of Gramineae; single seeded indehiscent fruit in which seed coat fuses with fruit wall to form husk.

topics.name: Types of meristems

subtopics.definition_content: A group of similar or dissimilar cells having common origin and performing specific function.
A group of cells which are much active and capable of showing continuous divisions and redivisions.

The promeristem originates from embryo and therefore, called primordial or embryonic meristem. A group of initial cells that lay down the foundation of an organ or a plant part, is called promeristem. It occupies a small area at the tips of stem and root.

A primary meristem originates from promeristem and retains its meristematic activity. It is located in the apices of roots, stems and the leaf primordia. Primary meristem gives rise to the primary permanent tissue.

They always arise in permanent tissues and have no typical promeristem. Some living permanent cells may regain the meristematic nature. The secondary meristems produce secondary tissues in the plant body.
The process in which permanent tissue regains meristematic nature.
This meristem is located at the growing apices of main and lateral shoots and roots. These cells are responsible for linear growth of an organ.

These are the portions of apical meristems which are separated from the apex during the growth of axis and formation of permanent tissues. Present mostly at the base of node (e.g., Mentha viridis), base of internode (e.g., stem of monocots viz., Wheat, Paddy) or at the base of the leaf (e.g., Pinus).

These meristems occur laterally in the axis, parallel to the sides of stems and roots. This meristem consists of initials which divide mainly in one plane (periclinal) and results in increase in the diameter of an organ.
It is the oudefinitionost layer of the apical meristem which develops into the epidermis or epidermal tissue system.
It occurs inside the protoderm. Some of the cells of young growing region which by their elongation and differentiation give rise to primary vascular tissue, constitute the procambium.
It constitutes the major part of the apical meristem which develops ground tissues like hypodermis, cortex, endodermis, pericycle, pith and medullary rays.
The cells divide anticlinally in all planes, so mass of cells is formed. e.g., formation of spores, cortex, pith, endosperm.
The cells divide anticlinally in two planes, so plate like area increased. e.g., formation of epidermis and lamina of leaves.
The cells divide anticlinally in one plane, so row or column of cells is formed. e.g., formation of lateral root.

key_concepts: meristem definition introduced by Nageli (1858)
classification by origin: promeristem, primary, secondary
classification by position: apical, intercalary, lateral
classification by function (Haberlandt 1890): protoderm, procambium, ground meristem
classification by plane of division: mass, plate, rib meristem
phellogen (cork cambium) is example of secondary meristem
cambium of vascular bundles belongs to lateral meristem category

topics.name: Structure and organisation of apical meristem

subtopics.definition_content: Proposed by Nageli (1858). Shoot apical meristem consists of single apical cell. Applicable in higher algae, bryophytes and many pteridophytes but not in higher plants (gymnosperms and angiosperms).

Proposed by Hanstein (1870). Shoot apical meristem consists of three distinct meristematic zones: (a) Dermatogen - oudefinitionost layer forms epidermis, (b) Periblem - middle layer forms cortex and endodermis, (c) Plerome - innermost layer forms pith and stele.

Proposed by Schmidt (1924). Shoot apex consists of two distinct zones: (a) Tunica - mostly single layered, shows only anticlinal division, responsible for surface growth; (b) Corpus - central core with larger cells, divisions in all planes, responsible for volume growth.

A group of initial cells, present at the subdefinitioninal region of the growing root tip, which is protected by a root cap, is called root apical meristem or root apex. Embryonic in origin, formed from radicle part of embryo.
Proposed by Schuepp (1917). Comparable with tunica-corpus theory of shoot apex. Korper means body and Kappe means cap.

Proposed by Clowes (1961) in maize. In addition to actively dividing cells, a zone of inactive cells is present in the central part of the root apex called quiescent centre. Cells have light cytoplasm, small nuclei, lower concentration of DNA, RNA and protein.
Fourth meristem (fourth Hanstein zone) present only in monocots that forms root cap.
During reproductive phase, vegetative apices are converted into reproductive apices. The apex may develop into a flower or an inflorescence.

key_concepts: Popham and Chan (1950): mantle = tunica, core = corpus
root apex is subdefinitioninal due to root cap
shoot apex is definitioninal / dome-shaped
reproductive apex is elongated in Sagittaria; 400 times broad in Chrysanthemum
tunica: anticlinal division only (surface growth); corpus: all plane divisions (volume growth)

topics.name: Simple permanent tissues

subtopics.definition_content: Permanent tissues are made up of mature cells which have lost the capacity to divide and have attained a permanent shape, size and function due to division and differentiation in meristematic tissues.
Simple tissues are a group of cells which are all alike in origin, form and function.

Most simple and unspecialized tissue concerned mainly with vegetative activities. Cells are isodiametric, living, thin walled, soft, possess a distinct nucleus, having well developed intercellular spaces, vacuolated cytoplasm and cellulosic cell wall.
Parenchyma cells that enclose large air spaces; help in floating of aquatic plants (hydrophytes) and gaseous exchange.
Parenchyma cells that develop chlorophyll; help in photosynthesis.
Elongated parenchyma cells with tapering ends.
Parenchyma cells that store secretory substances (ergastic substance) such as tannins, resins and gums.

definition coined by Schleiden (1839). Tissue of primary body. Cells contain protoplasm and are living without intercellular spaces. Cell walls are thickened at the corners and are made up of cellulose, hemicellulose and pectin. Elastic and extensible.
Thickening of the cells is confined to the corners of the cells (e.g., Tagetes, Tomato, Datura, Potato).
Thickenings are present in the tangential walls (e.g., hypodermis of sunflower stem).
Thickened cell wall associated with intercellular spaces of adjacent cells (e.g., leaf petioles of Compositae, hypodermis of Cucurbita stem, Salvia, Malva).
Discovered and coined by Mettenius (1805). Consists of thick-walled dead cells. Due to deposition of impermeable secondary walls (lignin) cells become dead, thick and hard.

Greatly elongated and tapering at both ends. Fully developed fibre cells are always dead. Polygonal in transverse section, walls highly lignified. Intercellular spaces absent, lumen highly obliterated.
Fibres present in pericycle (e.g., Cannabis sativa/Hemp) and phloem (e.g., Corchorus capsularis/Jute, Hibiscus cannabinus, Calotropis).
Fibres associated with wood or xylem. Thick-walled with simple pits are called libriform fibres; thin-walled with bordered pits are called fibre-tracheids.
Fibres present over surface of plant organs (e.g., Cotton fibres in testa of seeds, mesocarp fibres of Coconut).
Lignified, extremely thick-walled with lumen almost obliterated. May be spherical, oval, cylindrical, T-shaped or stellate. Found in hard parts e.g., endocarp of Walnut and Coconut.
Small and more or less isodiametric sclereids. Occur in cortex, pith, phloem, and pulp of fruits (e.g., Pyrus).
Rod-shaped elongated sclereids usually found in leaves, cortex of stem and outer seed coats.
Bone or barrel-shaped sclereids dilated at their ends (e.g., leaf of Hakea).
Star-shaped sclereids with extreme lobes or arms (e.g., leaf of Nymphaea).
Hair-like sclereids found in the intercellular spaces in leaves and stem of some hydrophytes.

key_concepts: parenchyma functions: storage, photosynthesis (chlorenchyma), flotation (aerenchyma), rigidity (turgid state), healing of wounds
collenchyma absent in monocots and roots; present in hypodermis of herbaceous dicot stems
types of collenchyma (Majumdar 1941): angular, plate/lamellar, lacunar/tubular
two types of sclerenchyma: fibres (elongated) and sclereids/stone cells
five types of sclereids: brachy-, macro-, osteo-, astro-, trichosclereids
sclerenchyma fibres in pericycle of many dicots; cotton and jute are surface fibres and bast fibres respectively

topics.name: Complex permanent tissues

subtopics.definition_content: A group of more than one type of cells having common origin and working together as a unit. Important complex tissues are xylem and phloem, together called vascular tissue.
definition introduced by Nageli (1858). Conducting tissue which conducts water and mineral nutrients upwards from root to leaves. Also known as hadrome (Haberlandt).
Derived from procambium during primary growth. Consists of protoxylem and metaxylem.
Formed from vascular cambium during secondary growth.

definition given by Sanio (1863). Elongated tubelike cells with tapering or rounded or oval ends with hard and lignified walls. Dead on maturity. Possess bordered pits. Present in all vascular plants.

Rows of elongated tube-like cells, placed end to end with their end walls dissolved. Multicellular with wide lumen. Absent in pteridophytes and gymnosperms (except Ephedra, Gnetum, Selaginella, Pteridium). Always present in angiosperms.
Vessels of same size uniformly distributed throughout the growth or annual ring (e.g., Pyrus, Azadirachta, Eucalyptus, Mangifera). Characteristic of plants growing in tropical region.
Large vessels formed in early wood and small vessels in late wood (e.g., Quercus, Morus, Cassia, Dalbergia).
Living parenchymatous cells found associated with xylem. Serve for storage of reserve food and help in conduction of water upwards through tracheids and vessels.
Long, slender, pointed, dead and sclerenchymatous cells associated with xylem. Aid the mechanical strength of xylem and various organs.
definition given by Nageli. Main function is the transport of organic food materials from leaves to stem and roots in a downward direction. Also known as leptome.
Normal type, present outside the xylem (e.g., mostly angiosperms and gymnosperms).
Originates from procambium, occurs on innerside of primary xylem. Primary anomalous structure (e.g., Apocynaceae, Asclepiadaceae, Convolvulaceae, Solanaceae).
Originates from cambium, secondary phloem which occurs in groups within the secondary xylem. Secondary anomalous structure (e.g., Leptadaenia, Salvadora, Chenopodium, Boerhaavia, Amaranthus).
Formed by procambium during primary growth. May show differentiation into protophloem and metaphloem.
Process where protophloem elements are crushed by surrounding tissues and disappear during primary growth.
Long tube-like cells placed end to end forming continuous channel. Cell wall made of cellulose; transverse wall perforated like sieve. Nucleus not found in mature cells.
Substance (soluble) that plugs sieve pores in winter, retarding food transport. Gets dissolved in spring and summer for rapid transport.
P-protein is found in sieve tubes.
Thin-walled, elongated living cells connected with sieve tube through sieve pore. Contain nucleus. Found only in angiosperms; absent in pteridophytes and gymnosperms.
Living parenchyma associated with phloem. Present in pteridophytes and most dicotyledonous angiosperms. Absent in monocots and few dicots like Ranunculus.
Sclerenchymatous fibres associated with phloem. Elongated lignified cells with tapering ends and simple pits. Non-living, provide mechanical support.

key_concepts: xylem vessels absent in Winteraceae, Trochodendraceae, Tepacenpaceae (homoxylous wood)
albuminous cells in gymnosperms instead of companion cells (Strasburger)
metaphloem: sieve elements + parenchyma + fibres; protophloem: sieve elements + parenchyma
commercial jute fibres from phloem fibres of Corchorus
diffuse porous wood is primitive; ring porous wood is advanced

topics.name: Laticiferous tissues

subtopics.definition_content: Highly branched cell with long slender processes ramifying in all directions in the ground tissue. Do not fuse and do not form network. Plants having such tissues are called simple or non-articulated laticifers (e.g., Calotropis, Nerium, Vinca, Euphorbia, Ficus).

Formed due to fusion of cells and form network like structure in all directions. Form highly ramifying system of channels full of latex. Plants called compound or articulated laticifers (e.g., Argemone, Papaver, Sonchus, Hevea, Manihot).

key_concepts: non-articulated laticifers (no fusion): Calotropis, Euphorbia, Ficus
articulated laticifers (fusion of cells): Argemone, Hevea brasiliensis (rubber), Papaver (opium)

topics.name: Glandular tissue

subtopics.definition_content: Highly specialized tissue consisting of glands, discharging diverse functions including secretory and excretory. Glands may be external or internal.
Glands which secrete essential oil.
Water secreting glands present at tip of leaves (e.g., Colocasia) or along margin (e.g., Tropaeoleum).

key_concepts: external glands: glandular hair (Plumbago, Boerhaavia), stinging hair (Urtica dioica), nectar glands (Rutaceae, Euphorbiaceae), digestive enzymes (Drosera, Nepenthes)
internal glands: oil glands (Citrus, Eucalyptus), resin ducts in Pinus (schizogenous), mucilage canals in Cycas

topics.name: Epidermal tissue system

subtopics.definition_content: A collection of tissues performing the same general function. According to Sachs (1975) there are three major tissue systems: epidermal, ground/fundamental, and vascular.
Composed of single layer of cells. Cells are living, parenchymatous, compactly arranged without intercellular spaces. May be multilayered (e.g., Ficus, Nerium, Peperomia, Begonia).
Multiple epidermal cells in aerial roots modified to absorb water from atmosphere (e.g., Orchids).
Comparatively very large cells in leaves of grasses. Hygroscopic, thin-walled with big central vacuoles filled with water. Play important role in folding and unfolding of leaves (e.g., Ammophila).
Layer formed by cutin, a waxy substance secreted by epidermal cells, within and on the outer surface of walls. Helps in reducing loss of water by evaporation.
Minute apertures in the epidermis. Each aperture is bounded by two kidney shaped cells, called guard cells. Absent in roots. Sunken in grooves in xerophytes (e.g., Nerium).
Large air cavity below each stomatal aperture.
Cells surrounding guard cells that differ morphologically and ontogenetically from other epidermal cells. In monocots, subsidiary cells and guard cells originate from same cell.
Epidermal outgrowths present temporarily or permanently on almost all plant parts. May be unicellular or multicellular. Serve for checking excess loss of water and for protection.
Enlargements of special epiblema cells called trichoblasts, occur in root hair zone. Specialised to absorb water from soil crevices. Also hold soil particles.

key_concepts: multilayered epidermis: Ficus, Nerium, Peperomia, Begonia
guard cells are dumb bell shaped in monocots (maize, grasses)
stomata scattered in dicot leaves but arranged in rows in monocots
cystoliths: CaCO3 crystals (Ficus); Druse and Raphides: CaOC3 crystals (Pistia)
trichome types: stellate hair, glandular hair, floccose hair, urticating hair, stinging hair

topics.name: Ground or Fundamental tissue system

subtopics.definition_content: Includes all tissues of plant body except epidermal tissue system and vascular tissues. Forms the bulk of body. Mainly originates from ground meristem.
Lies between epidermis and the pericycle. Distinct in dicotyledons but not in monocotyledons where there is no clear demarcation between cortex and pith.
Collenchymatous in dicot stem and sclerenchymatous in monocot stem. Provides strength.
Consists of parenchymatous cells. Main function is storage of food.
Mostly single layered, made up of parenchymatous barrel shaped compactly arranged cells. Inner and radial or transverse walls have casparian strips of suberin.
Strips of suberin present on inner and radial/transverse walls of endodermal cells. Act as water tight dam and air dam to check entry of air in xylem elements.
Thin-walled endodermal cells present just outside the protoxylem patches in roots. Absent in woody dicot stem, monocot stem and leaves.
Single layered or multilayered cylinder of thin-walled or thick-walled cells present between the endodermis and vascular tissues. In roots gives rise to lateral roots and cork cambium.
Occupies the central part in dicot stem, and monocot root. Mostly made up of parenchymatous cells. In dicot root pith is completely obliterated by metaxylem elements.
Pith cells between vascular bundles that become radially elongated. Help in lateral translocation.

key_concepts: endodermis with casparian bands absent in woody dicot stem, monocot stem and leaves of angiosperms
endodermis acts as internal protective tissue
lateral roots arise endogenously from pericycle cells
cork cambium and part of vascular cambium originate from pericycle in dicot roots
in dicot stem: pith cells between VBs = primary medullary rays / pith rays

topics.name: Vascular tissue system

subtopics.definition_content: The central cylinder of the shoot or root surrounded by cortex. Includes vascular tissues and ground tissue like pericycle and pith, when present.
Xylem and phloem strands alternate with each other separated by parenchymatous cells. Found mainly in roots.
A vascular bundle having both xylem and phloem together, in the same radius. Occur in stems.
A vascular bundle in which the phloem lies towards outer side and xylem towards inner side (e.g., Sunflower).
Collateral bundle having a cambium between xylem and phloem (e.g., Dicot stem).
Collateral bundle lacking a cambium between xylem and phloem (e.g., Monocot stem).
A vascular bundle having the phloem strands on both outer and inner side of xylem (e.g., Cucurbita, Lagenaria). Found in Cucurbitaceae, Solanaceae and Myrtaceae.
A vascular bundle in which one tissue is completely surrounded by the other.
Phloem lies in the centre and remains completely surrounded by xylem (e.g., Dracaena, Yucca).
Xylem lies in the centre and remains completely surrounded by phloem (e.g., Ferns).

key_concepts: Kranz anatomy: characteristic of C4 plants - mesophyll undifferentiated, concentric around VBs, large chloroplasts in bundle sheath
xylem exarch (centripetal maturation) in roots; xylem endarch (centrifugal) in stems
origin of lateral roots: endogenous from pericycle

chapters.name: Internal structure of root, stem and leaf

topics.name: Difference between internal structure of root and stem

subtopics.definition_content: Protoxylem towards the outerside. Found in roots. Also called centripetal because differentiation of xylem proceeds from outside to inside.
Protoxylem towards innerside of metaxylem. Found in stems. Also called centrifugal as xylem matures from inside to outside.
Protoxylem surrounded by metaxylem.
Protoxylem in the centre of metaxylem.

key_concepts: root: epiblema/piliferous layer without cuticle; stem: epidermis with cuticle
root: unicellular hairs; stem: usually multicellular hairs
root: hypodermis absent; stem: hypodermis present (collenchymatous or sclerenchymatous)
root: endodermis very distinct; stem: endodermis poorly developed or absent
root: vascular bundles radial; stem: conjoint collateral/bicollateral/concentric

topics.name: Difference between dicot and monocot leaf

subtopics.definition_content: Dicot leaf type. Stomata usually more on lower epidermis. Mesophyll differentiated into palisade parenchyma and spongy parenchyma with large intercellular spaces.
Monocot leaf type. Equal stomata on lower and upper epidermis (amphistomatic). Only spongy parenchyma present with very small intercellular spaces.
Characteristic feature of C4 plants. Mesophyll is undifferentiated and occurs in concentric layers around vascular bundles. Cells of bundle sheath possess large chloroplasts.

key_concepts: monocot bundle sheath: sclerenchymatous cells upto epidermis
dicot bundle sheath: parenchymatous or collenchymatous cells
bulliform cells present in monocot upper epidermis (absent in dicot)
Kranz anatomy occurs in both monocot and dicot leaves of tropical/arid C4 plants

topics.name: Difference between dicot and monocot stem
key_concepts: monocot stem: sclerenchymatous hypodermis, ground tissue (no distinct cortex), scattered VBs, conjoint collateral closed VBs, no pith, VBs larger towards centre, xylem V/Y-shaped, no phloem parenchyma
dicot stem: collenchymatous hypodermis, parenchymatous cortex, ring of VBs, conjoint collateral open VBs, pith abundant, all VBs same size, radial xylem, phloem parenchyma present

topics.name: Difference between dicot and monocot root

subtopics.definition_content: Dicot root has 2-4 (diarch to hierarch) vascular bundles. Pericycle gives rise to secondary roots and lateral meristem. Secondary growth takes place. Pith absent or poorly developed.
Monocot root has more than 6 vascular bundles (hexarch to polyarch). No cambium, no secondary growth. Pith abundant and fully developed.

key_concepts: dicot root: narrow cortex, endodermis less thickened, casparian strips prominent
monocot root: wide cortex, endodermal cells highly thickened later

topics.name: Types of steles

subtopics.definition_content: Proposed by Van Tieghem and Douliot (1886). Primary body of root and stem are basically alike anatomically; each consists of a central stele surrounded by cortex.
Simplest and most primitive type of stele in which central core of xylem is surrounded by phloem. definition given by Jeffrey.
Smooth core of xylem surrounded by a ring of phloem (e.g., Rhynia, Selaginella, Lycopodium).
Protostele having star shaped xylem core with many radiating arms (e.g., Psilotum, Lycopodium).
A protostele in which xylem core broken into a number of parallel plates (e.g., Lycopodium clavatum).
A protostele in which xylem is broken into small groups or patches (e.g., Lycopodium cernuum, Hymenophyllum demissum).
A protostele with central pith. Considered derived phylogenetically from protostele, represents an advanced form.
When phloem occurs on the outer side of xylem (e.g., Osmunda, Equisetum).
When phloem is present on both external and internal sides of the xylem (e.g., Marsilea, Adiantum).
A siphonostele with non-overlapping leaf gaps. May be ectophloic or amphiphloic.
A siphonostele with overlapping leaf gaps. Has many scattered vascular strands called meristeles (e.g., Dryopteris, Pteris, Ophioglossum).
Vascular tissue present in the form of two or more concentric cylinders (e.g., Pteridium aquilinum, Marattia). May be polycyclic solenostele or polycyclic dictyostele.
More than one stele present in the axis of some pteridophytes (e.g., 2 steles in Selaginella kraussiana, 16 steles in S. laevigata).
Scattered vascular strands present in dictyostele (e.g., Dryopteris, Pteris).

key_concepts: stele evolution: protostele → siphonostele
polystele: Selaginella (2 steles), S. laevigata (16 steles)
siphonostele derived from protostele - advanced form due to pith formation

topics.name: Secondary growth in stem

subtopics.definition_content: The increase in thickness or girth due to the activity of the cambium and the cork cambium is known as secondary growth.
Vascular cambium in between xylem and phloem, primary in origin.
Parenchymatous cells of medullary rays between vascular bundles become meristematic to form strip of cambium. Secondary in origin.
Both inter- and intra-fascicular cambium join together to form cambium ring, partly primary and partly secondary in origin. Produces secondary xylem on innerside and phloem on outerside.
Elongated cambium cells that form fibres, sieve cells, sieve tubes, tracheids.
Cambium cells that produce parenchyma cells of the rays in wood and phloem.
Narrow bands of living parenchyma cells passing through secondary xylem and secondary phloem. Provide radial conduction of food from phloem, and water and mineral salts from xylem.
Wood formed in spring with greater number of vessels with wider cavities. Light in colour.
Wood formed in dry summer or cold winter with narrower and smaller vessels. Dark in colour.
Both autumn and spring wood constitute a growth or annual ring. In one year only one growth ring is formed.
Develops from outer layer of cortex. Produces secondary cortex (phelloderm) on innerside and cork (phellem) on outerside.
Secondary cortex produced by cork cambium on innerside. Cells are thin-walled, living and store food.
Produced by cork cambium on outerside. Cells are dead, suberized and impervious to water.
