Phellem, phellogen and phelloderm collectively. Secondary protective tissue replaces epidermis after secondary growth.
All dead tissues lying outside the active cork-cambium are collectively known as bark. Includes ruptured epidermis, hypodermis and cork.
When cork-cambium appears in the form of a complete ring (e.g., Betula/Bhojpatra).
When cork cambium occurs as separate strips, bark appears in form of scales (e.g., Eucalyptus, Psidium guava).
The oudefinitionost layer of bark; dead.
Aerating pores formed in the cork through which gaseous exchange takes place. Found in old dicot stem. Consist of radial row of thin-walled complementary cells or filling tissue.

Consists of dead cells with thick walls heavily impregnated with suberin. Compactly arranged in radial rows without intercellular spaces. Impervious to water. Commercial cork from Quercus suber (Oak).
Centrally situated darker and harder wood. Physiologically inactive (almost dead). Dark colour due to deposition of tannins, resins, gums, essential oils in cell walls and cavities.
Outer light coloured zone. Physiologically active. Water conduction takes place through sap wood.
Balloon like structures that develop from xylem parenchyma. Block the passage of xylem vessels (tracheal plug) during conversion of sap wood to heartwood.

key_concepts: secondary xylem produced 8-10 times greater than secondary phloem
heartwood absent in Populus and Salix; well developed in Morus alba
as tree grows older: heartwood thickness increases, sap wood remains same
heartwood vs sap wood: sap wood is physiologically active, lighter; heartwood is inactive, darker
periderm is secondary protective tissue; replaces epidermis
lightest wood: Ochroma pyramidate; heaviest wood: Guaiacum officinale; heaviest in India: Acacia sundra
most durable soft wood: Cedrus deodara; Tectona grandis is definitionite resistant
reaction wood: compression wood (conifers, lower side) vs tension wood (dicots, upper side)
cavity types: schizogenous (intercellular spaces, e.g., oil cavities of Sunflower), lysigenous (degeneration of cells, e.g., Citrus), schizolysigenous (e.g., protoxylem cavity)
sequence from outside: Cork → Cork cambium → Secondary cortex → Primary phloem → Secondary phloem → Cambium → Secondary xylem → Primary xylem → Pith

topics.name: Secondary growth in dicot roots

subtopics.definition_content: Parenchyma cells lying just below each phloem strand in dicot root, from which vascular cambium strips are formed secondarily.

key_concepts: vascular bundles in dicot roots: radial, exarch, mostly tetrarch
cambium strips equal in number to phloem strands initially
pericycle cells outside protoxylem also become meristematic to join cambium
complete cambium ring: wavy outline initially, becomes circular later
growth rings not distinct in roots (no seasonal variation underground)
phellogen arises from outer pericycle layers in roots
monocots showing secondary growth: Yucca, Dracaena

subtopics.definition_content: A group of one or more types of cells having a similar origin and specialized for a specific function or functions along with the intercellular material.
Branch of biology dealing with the study of tissue; definition coined by Mayer; founder of histology is Marcello Malpighi.

A tissue composed of one or more layers of cells that cover the body surface and lines its various cavities; serves for protection, secretion and excretion; introduced by Ruysch; evolved first in animal kingdom; originates from all three primary germ layers.
Non-living membrane on which lowermost epithelial cells rest; formed of mucopolysaccharides, glycoprotein and collagen or reticular fibres; made of no cell product of epithelial tissue.
Simple and minute cytoplasmic processes arising from free exposed surfaces of the cell; absorb material. e.g. Intestine.
Junctions in epithelial tissue consisting of thickened area and fine tonofibrils extending from plasma membrane into cytoplasm; provide physical support. e.g. Vagina, Urinary bladder.

topics.name: Functions
key_concepts: Protection from mechanical, chemical injury, and bacteria
Sensory functions in skin, nose, eye and ear
Glandular epithelium secretes hormones, mucous, digestive juices, sweat
Absorption of nutrients from gut lining
Excretion and concentration of products in kidney tubules
Ciliated epithelium moves fluid and mucus
Germinal epithelium produces gametes
Gas exchange across alveolar epithelium

subtopics.definition_content: Single layer of flat, scale-like polygonal cells closely fitted together; also known as pavement epithelium. Found in blood vessels, lymph vessels, heart, peritoneum, Bowman's capsule, lung alveoli.
One layer of cuboidal-shaped cells with centrally situated nuclei resting on a basement membrane. Found in PCT, DCT, ovaries, seminiferous tubules, thyroid vesicles, sweat glands.
Single layer of columnar cells; common modifications are goblet cells, cilia and microvilli. Found in stomach, intestine, gall bladder and bile duct.

Single layer of irregularly shaped columnar cells all touching the basement membrane but appearing multilayered because nuclei are at different heights. Found in trachea, bronchi, epididymis, vasa deferentia.

Multiple layers of cells with flattened squamous cells at outer surface; keratin makes it waterproof and dead, protecting underlying tissues from abrasion. Found in epidermis of skin of land vertebrates.
Free surface is moist; outer epithelial cells do not contain keratin; serves a protective function. Found in oral cavity, pharynx, oesophagus, anal canal, lower urethra, vocal cords, vagina, cornea.
Often ten or more layers thick; lacks germinative layer and basement membrane; found in urinary bladder, ureter and renal pelvis; protects organ wall from tearing when subjected to stress.

Olfactory mucosa (Schneiderian membrane), retina of eyes and epithelial covering of tongue containing taste buds; sensory cells bear slender sensory hairs at free ends connected by synapses with sensory nerve fibrils.
Specialized cuboidal cells in gonads capable of producing gametes — ova (female gametes) and sperms (male gametes).

A cell, tissue or organ which secretes a useful chemical material; made up of cuboidal epithelial cells; all glands arise as folding of epithelia; most are merocrine type; originate from all three germinal layers.
Unicellular gland cells that secrete mucous; found in mucosa of intestine and stomach; life span about 2-3 days.
Glands which discharge secretory products into ducts (glands of external secretion). e.g. Salivary glands, Mammary glands, Tear glands.
Glands that discharge secretory products (hormones) directly into the blood. e.g. Pituitary gland, thyroid, parathyroid and adrenal glands.
Glands that are partly endocrine and partly exocrine in function. e.g. Pancreas.

Glands that collect secretory products near the apex of the cell and release by pinching off the distended end, resulting in some loss of cytoplasm and damage to the cell. e.g. Mammary glands (modified sweat gland).
Glands that collect secretory products inside the cell and then rupture completely to release them; cells self-destruct. e.g. Sebaceous glands.

Glands that discharge secretory product directly through the cell or plasma membrane, without injury to the cell wall and without loss of cytoplasm. e.g. Sweat glands, salivary glands, intestinal glands.

A tissue made up of matrix (abundant intercellular substance or ground substance) and living cells that connects and supports different tissues; the cellular element is usually scanty; all connective tissues are formed from mesoderm.
Amorphous, transparent material filling intercellular spaces; insoluble protein fibres lie in it; deterioration in connective tissues is associated with ageing.

topics.name: Types of connective tissues (connective tissue proper)

subtopics.definition_content: Loose connective tissue with transparent gelatinous, highly vascular and sticky matrix containing various cells and fibres; allows movement of connected parts; acts as packing material in various organs.

Most abundant cells of areolar tissue; produce fibres; synthesize proteins (Collagen, elastin and reticulin); undifferentiated mesenchyme stem cells capable of giving rise to other connective tissue cells.
Polymorphic, amoeboid cells; main phagocytes of connective tissue; have most active lysosomes; phagocytise dead cells and pathogens; called scavenger cells.
Large, irregular ovoid cells in areolar tissue discovered by Paul Ehrlich; secrete histamine (vasodilator), serotonin (vasoconstrictor) and heparin (anticoagulant); number increases during allergies.

Most abundant fibrous elements; long, unbranched fibres of soluble shining collagen protein (tropocollagen); provide maximum tensile strength; collagen constitutes 25% of total body protein; stained by eosin.
Formed of elastin protein; less numerous, thinner, branched, anastomosing, pale yellow; very elastic; stained by orcein (orceinophilic); most resistant protein to chemical changes.
Delicate, freely branching and inelastic fibres of reticulin protein; form networks; argentophillic (stained with AgBr and AgNO3); abundant in embryos, lymphoid tissues, liver, pancreas stroma.
Non-elastic but flexible tissue of parallel bundles of collagenous fibres with rows of fibroblasts; joins muscles to bones; also forms chordae tendinae.
Modified yellow elastic fibrous tissue; connects bone to bone.

Modified areolar tissue made up of large spherical fat cells (adipocytes); acts as food reserves (fat depots), heat insulator and pressure absorber. Unilocular adipocyte = white adipose tissue (common body fat); Multilocular adipocyte = brown fat (more mitochondria, found in hibernating mammals and newborn humans).

Modified areolar tissue with fluidy matrix and large stellate-shaped reticular cells with protoplasmic processes; found in spleen, thymus, tonsils, lymph glands, liver, bone marrow; reticular cells act as phagocytes.
Modification of reticular tissue; ground substance is plasma; heavy network of reticular fibres; active cells are myeloblasts; found in red bone marrow (haemopoietic tissue).
Solid but semi-rigid and flexible non-vascular connective tissue; cells embedded in resilient matrix of chondrin (protein of cartilage); mesodermal in origin; regeneration occurs from perichondrium.

Most primitive and glass-like cartilage; matrix transparent, homogenous, pearly white or bluish green; slightly elastic; also called articular cartilage. Found in trachea, larynx, bronchi, ribs, nasal septum.

Toughest and least flexible cartilage; small amount of matrix packed with bundles of thick white collagen fibres. Found in intervertebral discs (shock absorber) and pubic symphysis (helps in parturition).
Matrix packed with yellow elastic fibres running in all directions; very flexible, gives recoiling power. Found in mammalian pinna, pharyngotympanic tube, epiglottis.
Modified hyaline cartilage; hard and non-elastic due to deposition of calcium salt (hydroxy appetite) in matrix. Found in pubis of old frog, shark vertebrae, ends of long bones in man.
Membrane forming an envelope around bone; outer layer of white fibrous connective tissue; inner layer of osteoblasts (bone forming cells).
Bone forming cells (spider-like); synthesize and secrete osteoid; processes called canaliculi; produce new bone materials.
Mature, non-dividing osteoblast surrounded by matrix and lying within lacunae.
Bone destroying cells; take part in reabsorption of bones; contain large amount of acid phosphatase enzyme.
A Haversian canal, its lamellae and osteocytes; Haversian canals contain artery, veins, osteoblasts in areolar tissue, nerves and lymph; interconnected by transverse Volkmann's canals.
Red due to many blood vessels; present in ribs, sternum, clavicles, vertebrae, scapula, pelvis in adults; produces RBCs, WBCs, platelets, granular leucocytes.
Much fatty (adipose) tissue; present in shaft of long bones; produces blood cells only in emergency (excessive blood loss).
Rod-like structure found in all chordates; made up of chordal cells; replaced by vertebral column in vertebrates.

Red coloured liquid connective tissue originating from mesoderm; mobile, fibre-free fluid matrix; circulates regularly; viscosity = 4.7, pH = 7.4, specific gravity = 1.04–1.07, volume = 5–6 litres/70 kg.
Matrix of blood; slightly alkaline and transparent; forms 55–60% by volume of blood; contains Water (91–92%), Solid (8–9%); maintains osmotic pressure and pH.

Iron-containing respiratory pigment of RBCs; purple coloured; consists of haem (5%) and globin (95%); 4 polypeptide chains (2α with 141 amino acids, 2β with 146 amino acids each); 1g haemoglobin binds 1.34 ml oxygen.
Bursting of plasma membrane of RBCs allowing haemoglobin to escape; occurs when RBCs are placed in hypotonic solution; caused by fat solvents and snake venom.

Process of blood clot formation; normal time 3–8 minutes. Stage I: damaged platelets/tissue release thromboplastin; Stage II: Prothrombin → Thrombin (Ca²⁺); Stage III: Fibrinogen → Fibrin (Ca²⁺); Stage IV: Fibrin + cells → Clot.

Rate of sinking/settling of RBCs in plasma to form rouleaux; measured by Wintrobe's or Westergren's tube; normal male ~5 mm, female ~10 mm/first hour; rise indicates infective/destructive/inflammatory diseases.

WBCs with stainable granules and multi-lobed nucleus; develop from myeloblast in red bone marrow; polymorphonuclear leucocytes. Types: Neutrophils (60–70%, phagocytic, 3–5 lobes), Eosinophils (2–4%, bilobed), Basophils (0.5–1%, nonphagocytic).
Protoplasmic discs found only in mammalian blood; arise as detached tips of megakaryocytes; non-nucleated; approximately 300,000 per cubic mm; life span 5–9 days; involved in blood coagulation.

Most muscles of body, bringing voluntary movements under conscious control; inserted at both ends upon bones; show transverse striation of alternate dark A (anisotropic) and light I (isotropic) bands.
Segment of a myofibril between two adjacent Z-bands (Krause's membrane); 2.3 μm long in uncontracted mammalian fibres; ultimate unit of contraction.
Dark band containing ~120Å thick and 1.8 μm long myosin filaments; contains H zone in middle (lighter region) and M/Hansen's line.
Light band containing ~60Å thick and 1.0 μm long actin filaments; bisected by Z-band.

Proposed by H.E. Huxley and A.F. Huxley (1954); shortening of fibrils during contraction brought about by sliding movement of actin filaments over myosin filaments towards M line by rapidly forming and breaking cross bridges (rachets) at myosin spurs.
Third protein component of actin filaments (along with actin and tropomyosin); involved in regulation of muscle contraction.

Lack striations; occur in walls of hollow internal organs (alimentary canal, blood vessels, uterus etc.); also in iris and ciliary body of eyes; no connection with bones; involuntary under control of ANS.
Unbranched, goose-spindle shaped, uninucleated; no sarcolemma; contraction is slow and involuntary.

Heart wall (myocardium) made of cardiac muscles; structurally resemble striated muscles but involuntary; cells comparatively shorter and thicker, cylindrical, mostly uninucleate with central nucleus, somewhat branched, covered by sarcolemma; unfatiguable.
Sustained contraction evoked by a series of nerve impulses reaching muscles in rapid succession; different from the disease tetanus (lockjaw) or tetany (parathyroid deficiency).
State of mild sustained partial contraction of striated muscles at rest to maintain body posture; a mild state of tetanus.
Rigidity that develops in muscles after death due to permanent irreversible contraction, establishment of permanent link between actin and myosin, and fall in ATP concentration.
Condition during active work where oxygen supply falls short of muscular requirement, causing lactic acid accumulation and gradual increase in breathing to enhance O2 intake.
Lactic acid is transported by blood to liver where it is converted back to glycogen.
Muscles put to excessive work become thick and strong (hypertrophy); muscles not used for long period become thin and weak (atrophy / disuse atrophy). Cardiac muscle has poor regenerative power.
Structural and functional unit of nervous system; formed from neuroblast; longest cell of the body; permanently lost power of division (no centriole); minimum power of regeneration.

Cell body of neuron; granular cytoplasm called neuroplasm; contains large spherical nucleus with single nucleolus, neurofibrils, Nissl's granules and mitochondria. Purkinje cells are flask-shaped cytons in cerebellum.
Small basophilic granules in cyton formed of rough endoplasmic reticulum with ribosomes; sites of protein synthesis; absent in axon and axon hillock.
Several short, tapering, much-branched neuron processes; contain neurofibrils, neurotubules, Nissl's granules and mitochondria; conduct nerve impulse towards the cell body.

Single, very long cylindrical process of uniform diameter arising from axon hillock; contains neurofibrils and neurotubules but lacks Nissl's granules; definitioninates in telodendria with synaptic knobs (end bulbs/buttons) containing mitochondria and secretory vesicles.
Found in sense organs; receive impulse from receptor cell via dendrons; axon forms synapse with dendron of next neuron. e.g. Olfactoreceptors, gustatorecptors.
Located in dorsal horn of spinal cord; association neuron when axon synapses with motor neuron of same side; commissural neuron when with motor neuron of opposite side.
Always present in ventral horn of spinal cord; axon ends in muscle fibres or gland cells; conduct impulses to effector organs.
Single nerve process that immediately divides into dendron and axon; found in dorsal root ganglia of spinal nerves and in roots of V, IX and X cranial nerves.
Two nerve processes — one axon and one dendron; found in olfactory epithelium, retina of eye, ganglia of VIII cranial nerve.
Several nerve processes — one axon and multiple dendrons; found in CNS and ganglia of autonomic nervous system; most common type in adults.
Supporting and packing non-nervous cells found in brain, spinal cord and ganglia; ten times more numerous than neurons; types include astrocytes, oligodendrocytes, microglial cells.
Large, star-shaped neuroglial cells with numerous processes forming blood-brain barrier; located only in brain.
Neuroglial cells with few branched processes resembling dendrons; form myelin sheath around CNS axons (Schwann cells for PNS).
Small, spindle-shaped neuroglial cells; act as defensive phagocytes in CNS; arise from monocytes.
Cuboidal and ciliated epithelial cells lining cavities of brain (ventricles) and spinal cord (central canal); form epithelium called ependyma; circulate cerebrospinal fluid (CSF).
Special neurons of hypothalamus; endocrine in function; secrete neurohormones carried by hypophyseal portal system to anterior pituitary to stimulate trophic hormones (TSH, STH, FSH, LH, ACTH etc.).

Has axis cylinder surrounded by inner thick medullary sheath (myelin) and outer thin neurilemma (Schwann's cells); myelin is insulating white fatty substance causing saltatory conduction; nodes of Ranvier are points of absence of medullary sheath in peripheral nerves.
Points where medullary sheath is absent in peripheral nerve fibres; part between successive nodes is definitioned internode; enables saltatory conduction.
Tubular sheath of Schwann's cells placed end-to-end; continuous over Nodes of Ranvier; enables peripheral nerve fibres to regenerate after injury; absent in brain and spinal cord nerve fibres.
Axis cylinder enclosed only by neurilemma and connective tissue; grey in colour; found in autonomic nerves; slower conduction, no nodes of Ranvier.
Bundle of nerve fibres within a nerve; each fibre covered by endoneurium, each fasciculus enclosed by perineurium, all fasciculi held together by epineurium.
Nerves composed of sensory fibres; bring impulse towards CNS. e.g. Olfactory (I), optic (II) and auditory (VIII) cranial nerves.
Nerves composed of efferent fibres; carry impulses away from CNS. e.g. Oculomotor (III), Pathetic (IV) and abducens (VI) cranial nerves.
Nerves with both afferent and efferent fibres. e.g. Trigeminal (V), facial (VII), glossopharyngeal (IX) and vagus (X) cranial nerves.
Most widely distributed species of frog in Northern India; Phylum Chordata, Sub phylum Vertebrata or Craniata, Class Amphibia, Order Salientia or Anura, Genus Rana, Species tigrina.
Sexual embrace in frog; male jumps on female and grips her tightly with fore-limbs aided by nuptial pads. Fertilization is external.
Periodic shedding of skin in frog occurring almost once a month during active life.
Winter sleep in frogs; during this period frog respires only through skin (cutaneous respiration).
Summer sleep; frog takes rest in shady, cool and moist place and recuperates its energy.
Ability of frog to change body colour with surroundings (camouflage) to avoid enemies and catch prey unnoticed.
Transparent protective membrane covering the eyes of frog.
Obliquely placed pigmented circular ear drum present on the head of frog.
Pad developed on first finger of each hand of male frog during breeding season; aids in amplexus.
Oudefinitionost horny layer of frog epidermis; protective in function and periodically cast off (ecdysis).
Pigment cells in frog dermis (stratum spongiosum); three types: melanophores (brownish black), lipophores (reddish and yellowish), and guanophores (whitish).
Loose connective tissue layer of frog dermis containing lymph spaces, nerve fibres, blood capillaries, mucous glands, poison glands, and chromatophores.
Teeth of frog are homodont, acrodont and polyphyodont; present on premaxillae, maxillae and vomers. Lower jaw is toothless. Teeth hold (not chew) prey.
Last part of alimentary canal in frog; receives rectum, ureters and oviducts (female) or urinogenital ducts (male); opens out through cloacal aperture.
Common duct from liver and pancreas that empties bile and pancreatic juice into the duodenum of frog.
Respiration through moist skin in frog; most vital mode; occurs in water, during hibernation and aestivation. Oxygen diffuses through moisture over skin.
Exchange of gases in buccopharyngeal cavity lining; occurs during rest on land and while floating in water when oxygen demand is low.
Lung-based respiration in frog; occurs when oxygen demand is high; rate not more than 20/min. Lungs are two ovoid pinkish elastic bags with alveoli.
Voice box of frog; possesses two vocal cords, opening of glottis into pharynx and bronchus into lungs; supported by three cartilages (two arytenoid, one cricoid).
Triangular chamber attached dorsally to frog's heart; formed by union of three main vena cava; receives deoxygenated blood from body.
Short sac-like additional chamber on ventral side of frog's heart; divided into conus arteriosus (pylangium) and bulbous aorta (synangium).
Large twisted valve in pylangium (conus arteriosus) of frog; divides its cavity incompletely into left dorsal cavum pulmocutaneum and right ventral cavum aorticum.
In frog both oxygenated and deoxygenated blood enter the heart and mix in the single ventricle; it is a closed type circulatory system.
Formed by femororenal branch combined with sciatic vein; proceeds towards kidney, breaks into capillaries for extraction of nitrogenous waste products.
Formed of hepatic portal vein (from digestive tract veins) and anterior abdominal vein; blood enters liver, breaks into capillaries for processing.
Frog has 10 pairs of cranial nerves originating from the brain.
Pair of large rounded dorsolateral lobes of frog midbrain; largest part of midbrain.
Bony ossicle in the middle ear of frog (homologous to stapes of mammals); passes vibrations from tympanum to internal ear.
Organism that excretes urea as the main nitrogenous waste product; frog is ureotelic.
Type of kidneys in frog; compact, dark red, bean-like structures; functional unit is nephron (uriniferous tubule); each nephron has Malpighian capsule, neck, coiled tubule.
Double fold of peritoneum attaching testes to the anterio-ventral surface of kidneys in frog.
10–12 tubules arising from testes of frog, running through mesorchium into kidneys where they open into Bidder's canal for sperm passage.
Canal running longitudinally in the medial region of frog kidney; connected to urinogenital duct through collecting tubules; pathway for sperm.
Peritoneal fold supporting the ovaries to dorsal abdominal wall in female frog.
Long, stiff, bristle-like hairs on both sides of nostrils in rat; help the animal measure the width of an area even in perfect darkness.
Typical walking pads present on tips of digits, palm and at the base of palm in rat; also present on feet.

Skin derivatives in rat including sudoriferous glands (sweat), sebaceous glands (oil), mammary glands (modified sweat), meibomian glands (modified oil), and ceruminous glands (wax in external auditory canal).
Natural toothless space between incisors and molars in rat; characteristic of rodents.
Heterodont (different types), thecodont (set in sockets), monophyodont (one set per lifetime). Dental formula: 1003/1003 × 2 = 16. Canines and premolars absent; incisors grow throughout life.
Gall bladder is absent in rat (also absent in whale and horse). Bile is carried directly to duodenum by bile duct.
Like other mammals, rat has closed and double circulation. Heart has four chambers: right and left atria, right and left ventricles.
Renal portal system is absent in rat (unlike frog). Only hepatic portal system is present.
Functional kidney of rat (and other higher vertebrates/amniotes); consists of outer cortex and inner medulla; functional unit is nephron with Bowman's capsule, PCT, Henle's loop, DCT.
Three protective membranes covering the brain of rat: piamater (innermost), arachnoid mater (middle), duramater (oudefinitionost).
Rat has 12 pairs of cranial nerves originating from brain. Spinal nerves: 33 pairs.
Pair of organs present in the wall of buccal cavity of rat; involved in perception of smell.
Structure attaching each testis posteriorly to scrotal sac in rat; testis descends into scrotal sac through inguinal canal between the 30th and 40th day of life.
Bony process present in the penis of rat (also present in bat, dog, walrus and whale).
Haemoendothelial placenta in rat; provides physiological connection between developing foetus and uterine wall (endometrium). All three uterine tissue barriers absent.
One pair of glands originating from urethra at base of penis in rat; produce secretion during sexual excitement to protect sperms from acid traces in urethra.
Modified sebaceous glands that develop from skin forming prepuce in rat; secrete peculiar odorous secretion.

chapters.name: Cell as a unit of life

subtopics.definition_content: Branch of biology comprising study of cell structure and function. (Gk Kytos = cell; logos = study). Study of metabolic aspects of cell components called cell biology.

All living organisms are composed of cells. Idea given by H.J. Dutrochet (1824); credit goes to Schleiden (botanist, 1838) and Schwann (zoologist, 1839). Robert Hooke (1665) discovered hollow cavities (cells) in cork slice and wrote Micrographia.

topics.name: Modifications and exceptions to cell theory

subtopics.definition_content: Proposed by Rudolf Virchow (1855); cells originate from pre-existing cells (omnis cellula-e-cellula). Also called cell principle or cell doctrine.
Viruses, viroids and prions are exceptions to cell theory as they are obligate parasites (sub-cellular in nature).

topics.name: Cellular totipotency and surface volume ratio

subtopics.definition_content: Ability of cells to divide and redivide. Suggested by Haberlandt (1902). Demonstrated by Steward et al. in phloem tissue of carrot.

Metabolically active cells are small; small cells have higher nucleocytoplasmic ratio and higher surface volume ratio for quicker exchange of materials. Surface volume ratio decreases as cell size increases.

Oudefinitionost, rigid, protective, non-living and supportive layer; found in all plant cells, bacteria, cyanobacteria and some protists. First discovered by Robert Hooke (1665) in cork. Not found in animal cells.
Oudefinitionost region of cell wall; common cementing layer between adjacent cells; absent on outer free surface; forms of calcium and magnesium pectate. Pectin used as commercial jellying agent.
Thin, elastic, capable of expansion in growing cell; grows by intussusception; present in meristematic and parenchymatous cells; has more hemicellulose and less cellulose.

Deposited internal to primary wall in mature cell by accretion; thick and rigid; consists of three layers S1, S2, S3; has more cellulose and less hemicellulose; found in collenchyma, sclerenchyma, xylem vessels.
Laid down on secondary wall; composed of cellulose and xylan; e.g., tracheids of gymnosperms.
Growth of cell wall by embedding new wall material secreted by protoplasm within the original wall as the wall stretches.
Growth of cell wall by deposition of new wall material secreted by protoplasm as definite thin plates one after the other.

Places in secondary walls with irregular thickenings. Types: Simple pit (uniform chamber), Bordered pit (flask shaped), Blind pit (no corresponding pit), Half bordered pit, Aspirated pit (non-functional; aperture blocked by torus).

Cytoplasmic strands in pits connecting cytoplasm of adjacent cells. First discovered by Tangle (1879); studied by Strasburger (1901). ER plays a role in origin. Symplast/symplasm = linked protoplasts via plasmodesmata.

Cell with incipient nucleus (nucleoid); no nuclear membrane; single membrane system; 70S ribosomes only; no membrane-bound organelles; cell wall of peptidoglycans (murein); circular DNA not associated with histones; cell division mostly amitotic.

Cell with well-organised nucleus enclosed by nuclear envelope; double membrane system; both 80S and 70S ribosomes; membrane-bound organelles present; linear DNA associated with histones; cell division typically mitotic.
definition given by Dodge for dinoflagellates; indefinitionediate type of cell organisation with true eukaryotic nucleus (nuclear membrane + chromosomes) in dinophyceae algae.

Thin, transparent, electron microscopic, elastic, regenerative, selectively permeable membrane covering every living cell; quasifluid in nature. definition plasmalemma given by Plowe (1931) replacing cell membrane (Nageli and Cramer, 1855). Proteins form 60%; lipids 28-79%; carbohydrates 2-10%.

Most widely accepted model proposed by Singer and Nicolson (1972); plasma membrane described as 'protein iceberg in a sea of lipids'; highly viscous phospholipid bilayer with peripheral (extrinsic) proteins at surfaces and integral (intrinsic/transmembrane) proteins penetrating through; carbohydrates only at outer surface forming glycolipids and glycoproteins.
Proposed by Davson and Danielli (1935); biomolecular lipid layer sandwiched between two dense protein layers (globular α type); also called unit membrane hypothesis.
All cytoplasmic membranes have similar trilaminar structure; electron-transparent phospholipid bilayer sandwiched between two electron-dense protein (extended β type) layers; thickness ~75 Å.
Finger-like evaginations of 0.1 μm diameter engaged in absorption; e.g., intestinal cells, hepatic cells, mesothelial cells. Surface having microvilli called striated border or brush border.
Plasmalemma foldings found in fungal cells. Reported by Moore and Maclean.
Site for cellular respiration in prokaryotes; infolding of plasma membrane; analogous to mitochondria of eukaryotes.
Plasma membranes of adjacent cells fused at sealing strands; e.g., capillaries, brain cells, collecting tubules.
Modification of plasma membrane concerned with cell adherence.

Movement of substances without energy expenditure; occurs through diffusion and osmosis. Diffusion: movement from higher to lower concentration/electrochemical potential. Osmosis: diffusion of water across semipermeable membrane.
Passage of substances along concentration gradient without energy expenditure; occurs with help of special permeases forming pathways; occurs for sugars, amino acids and nucleotides.

Transport with energy (ATP), usually against concentration gradient; uses carrier particles (integral proteins) or gated channels. Examples: K+-H+ exchange pump in guard cells; Na+-K+ exchange pump across animal membranes.
Bulk intake of fluid, ions and molecules through small endocytotic vesicles (100-200 nm); requires ATP, Ca2+, clathrin and actin. (Lewis, 1931). Also called cell drinking.
Cell eating; ingestion of large particles forming phagosome; phagosome fuses with lysosome forming digestive vacuole; e.g., WBCs, Kupffer's cells, macrophages, Amoeba. (Metchnikoff, 1883).

Complex, granular, elastic, viscous, colourless substance; selectively permeable; polyphasic colloidal system (physical basis of life - Huxley). Discovered by Dujardin (1835) as sarcode; renamed Protoplasm by Purkinje (1837). Water 75-85%; Proteins 10-25%; Lipids 2-3%.
Most advanced and accepted theory; proposed by Kolliker; protoplasm is crystal, polyphasic, colloidal solution of inorganic substances, proteins, carbohydrates and fats.
Protoplasm is colloidal solution existing in two states: sol state (more water) and gel state (less water). Proposed by Hyman.
Rotation (clockwise/anticlockwise; e.g., Hydrilla, Vallisneria) and circulation (multidirectional around vacuole; e.g., Tradescantia). Found only in eukaryotes.
definition coined by Hanstein (1880); includes all living constituents (nucleus, cytoplasm and plasmalemma); simply organised mass of protoplasm bounded by cell membrane with no cell wall.

Substance around nucleus and inside plasma membrane containing various organelles and inclusions; semisolid, jelly-like; aqueous ground substance called cytoplasmic matrix (hyaloplasm or cytosol). About 90% is water. Differentiated into ectoplasm (plasmagel) and endoplasm (plasmasol).

Semi-autonomous hollow sac-like structures in all eukaryotes except mature RBCs and sieve tubes of phloem; also called chondriosome, plastosomes. definition given by C. Benda (1897). First observed by Kolliker (1850) in insect muscle as granules (sarcosomes). Called power house of the cell by Seekevitz.

Folds/convolutions of inner mitochondrial membrane in animals (called tubuli or microvilli in plants). Inner membrane has M face (matrix-facing) with oxysomes and C face (towards perimitochondrial space).

Small stalked particles on M face of inner mitochondrial membrane; 10^4-10^5 per mitochondria; each made of base, stalk and head (~10 nm length); contain ATPase enzyme (Packer, 1967); responsible for ATP synthesis. Also called Fernandez-Moran particles or Racker's particles.

Colourless plastids near nucleus in non-green cells; lack grana and photosynthetic pigments; store food; occur in cells not exposed to sunlight. Three types: (1) Amyloplast (stores starch, e.g., potato), (2) Elaioplast/Lipidoplast (stores lipids), (3) Aleuroplast/Proteinoplast (stores proteins, e.g., aleurone cells).

Coloured plastids other than green; present in petals and fruits; may arise from chloroplasts by replacement of chlorophyll by other pigments. Green tomatoes turn red on ripening due to replacement of chlorophyll by lycopene.

Greenish plastid with photosynthetic pigments; factory for synthesis of sugars. Discovered by Sachs; named by Schimper. Double membrane structure; inner membrane invaginated to form thylakoids arranged in grana stacks in stroma. Size 3-10 μm diameter.
Oval-shaped closed sacs formed from inner plastidial membrane invaginations; structural and functional elements of chloroplasts. definition given by Menke (1962). Stacks of thylakoids form grana.
C55H72O5N4Mg (with methyl group); primary photosynthetic pigment.
C55H70O6N4Mg (with aldehyde group); accessory pigment.

Hydrocarbons soluble in organic solvents. Two types: (1) Carotenes: C40H56 derivatives of vitamin A (α, β, γ carotene, lycopene); β-carotene most common. (2) Xanthophylls: C40H56O2, yellowish (fucoxanthin, violaxanthin). Molar ratio carotene:xanthophyll in young leaves = 2:1.

Well-developed electron microscopic network of interconnected cisternae, tubules and vesicles throughout cytoplasm. First noted by Porter, Claude and Fullman (1945); named by Porter (1953). Absent in prokaryotes, ova, embryonic cells and mature RBCs. Three components: cisternae (flat sacs), vesicles (oval/rounded), tubules (branched, near cell membrane).

Agranular; no ribosomes; mainly tubules and vesicles; well developed in muscle cells, adipose tissue, interstitial cells, steroid-secreting cells; involved in lipid/steroid/vitamin synthesis and detoxification; gives rise to sphaerosomes.

Granular; ribosomes attached on cytoplasmic surface (via ribophorins I and II); mainly cisternae; basophilic; abundant in cells producing proteins for export (plasma cells, goblet cells, pancreatic acinus cells); formed from nuclear membrane.

Modified SER in striated muscle fibres (Veratti, 1902); forms network of interconnected tubules in sarcoplasm; helps in conduction of motor nerve impulses and removal of lactic acid (prevents muscle fatigue).

Membranous system of cisternae, vesicles and golgian vacuoles; also called golgi bodies, golgisomes, dictyosomes, Dalton complex, idiosomes, Baker's body; traffic police of the cell. First observed by George (1867); morphological details by Camillo Golgi (1898) in nerve cells. Absent in bacteria, blue-green algae, RBCs, spermatozoa of bryophytes and pteridophytes, sieve tube cells.
Convex end of golgi stack facing ER or nucleus; receives transport vesicles from RER.
Concave end of golgi stack facing plasma membrane; gives rise to secretory vesicles and golgian vacuoles.

Electron microscopic vesicular structures (0.2-0.8 μm) bounded by single lipoproteinous membrane; contain hydrolytic enzymes (acid hydrolases, pH 5); discovered by Christian de Duve (1955); definition given by Novikoff. Called suicidal bags or disposal units. Absent in prokaryotes.
Newly formed lysosomes containing enzymes only in inactive state.
Formed when material to be digested enters a primary lysosome.
Formed from primary lysosomes; digest cell's own organelles (autophagy); acid hydrolases digest mitochondria, ER etc.
Secondary lysosome containing indigestible matter; released by exocytosis (ephagy).

Smallest known electron microscopic non-membranous ribonucleoprotein particles; site of protein synthesis. definition coined by Palade (1955). First isolated by Tissieres and Watson (1958) from E. coli. Two types: 70S (prokaryotes, mitochondria, plastids) and 80S (eukaryotic cytoplasm).
Subunits: 50S (large, dome-shaped; contains 23S and 5S rRNA) + 30S (small, oval; contains 16S rRNA); 60-65% rRNA and 35-40% proteins (ratio 2:1).
Subunits: 60S (large; contains 28S, 5S and 5.8S rRNA) + 40S (small; contains 18S rRNA); 45% rRNA and 55% proteins (ratio ~1:1).

Multiple ribosomes (generally 6-8) attached to a single mRNA strand; functional units of protein synthesis. Distance between adjacent ribosomes is 90 nucleotides. Observed first by Rich and Warner (1963).

Spherical/oval organelles (0.5-2.5 μm) arising from ER; found in plant cells for lipid synthesis and storage (endosperm, cotyledon of oil seeds); bounded by single unit membrane; contain hydrolytic enzymes (protease, RNAase, phosphatase, esterase). Also known as plant lysosomes. First observed by Hanstein (1880); discovered by Perner (1953); definition by Dangeard.

Spherical microbodies (~1.5 μm); contain peroxide-producing enzymes (oxidases: urate oxidase, d-amino oxidase, α-hydroxy acid oxidase) and peroxide-destroying catalases; involved in H2O2 metabolism and photorespiration in plants. Discovered by Tolbert (1969); isolated by De Duve (1965).

Spherical microbodies (0.5-1 μm) found in fungi, protists and germinating fatty seeds; bounded by single unit membrane; contain glyoxylate cycle enzymes and β-oxidation enzymes; convert fats into carbohydrates. Absent in animal cells. Discovered by Beevers (1961) and Briedenbach (1967).
Sac-like structures between cell wall and plasmalemma in haustoria of fungal hyphae. Discovered by Moore and McAlear (1961). Called border bodies by Webster.

Non-membranous structure; two centrioles (diplosome) surrounded by centrosphere/kinetoplasm. Absent in prokaryotes, fungi, gymnosperms and angiosperms. Found in animal cells, most protists, motile plant cells. Discovered by Van Benden (1887); definition and structure by T. Boweri.

Microtubular structure with 9+0 arrangement (all 9 microtubules peripheral); contains intra-centriolar cart-wheel structure with central hub (~25 Å diameter) and 9 radial spokes; composed of tubulin protein and lipids; rich in ATPase. Daughter centriole forms from pre-existing centriole in G2 of interphase.

Microscopic, hair-like motile structures; extra-cellular but originate from basal body (blepharoplast/kinetosome); small (5-10 μm); numerous (up to 14,000/cell); in ciliate protozoans (Paramecium, Vorticella); 9+2 axoneme arrangement; beat in metachronous or synchronous coordination; sweeping/rowing motion.

Longer motile structures (100-200 μm); fewer (1-8); at anterior end; in flagellate protozoans (Euglena, Trichonympha); 9+2 axoneme arrangement; beat independently; undulatory motion. Two types: Tinsel (with flimmers/mastigonemes) and Whiplash (without flimmers).
9+2 arrangement in cilia/flagella shaft; 9 doublets of peripheral microtubules (tubulin) + 2 central tubules (dynein protein); 11 fibrils total.

Hollow cylindrical structures (~250 Å diameter, 150 Å lumen, 50 Å wall); wall formed of 13 parallel proto-tubules; composed of α and β tubulin subunits in helical arrangement. Found in cilia, flagella, centrioles, basal bodies, spindle fibres, astral fibres. First discovered by De Robertis and Franchi (1953) as neurotubules; definition by Slautterback (1963). Only in eukaryotes.

Solid filaments (50-60 Å diameter) of helical globular actin protein (contractile); in eukaryotic cytoplasm, microvilli, muscle fibres (myofilaments); absent in prokaryotes. Responsible for cytoplasmic streaming, cell shape changes, membrane movements during endocytosis/exocytosis. Discovered by Paleviz et al. (1974).

Supportive elements in eukaryotic cytoplasm (~10 nm thick); solid, unbranched; composed of non-motile structural proteins (keratin, desmine, vimentin); absent in mammalian RBCs. Support fluid cytosol, maintain cell shape and keep nucleus/organelles in place.

Director of the cell; controls all cellular functions. First observed by Robert Brown (1831) in orchid root cells. Absent in mature mammalian RBCs, sieve tube cells, tracheids, vessels. Prokaryotes have nucleoid (incipient nucleus/prokaryon/genophore). Chemical composition: Proteins 80%, DNA 12%, RNA 5%, Lipids 3%.

Bilayered lipoproteinous envelope first discovered by Erclab (1845); outer membrane (ectokaryotheca) bears ribosomes; inner membrane (endokaryotheca) lacks ribosomes; separated by perinuclear space (100-300 Å); has 1,000-10,000 octagonal nuclear pores (~800 Å diameter) surrounded by annuli (pore complex/pore basket). Formed from ER at telophase.

First observed by Fontana (1781); definition coined by Bowman (1840); associated with NOR (nucleolar organizer region) of nucleolar chromosomes; store house of rRNA; ribosome-producing machine. Structure: perinucleolar chromatin, pars fibrosa (80-100 Å fibrils), pars granulosa (150-200 Å granules; assembly of ribosomes), pars amorpha. Not bounded by limiting membrane.
Thread-like, coiled, elongated structures in nucleoplasm stained by basic fuchsine; observed only in interphase. Chromosomes are condensed chromatin at cell division. Haploid chromosome set = genome.

Discovered by Hofmeister (1848); named by Waldeyer (1888). Structure: two coiled chromatids (chromonemata) bearing chromomeres with genes; connected at centromere (primary constriction); secondary constriction bears NOR (SAT chromosomes); ends called telomeres. Chemical: DNA 40%, Histone 50%, Other proteins 8.5%, RNA 1.5%.

Condensed, darkly stained chromatin; transcriptionally inactive and late replicating; near nuclear lamina. Types: constitutive (all cells, all stages; e.g., centromere) and facultative (e.g., Barr body).
True chromatin; thin, less darkly stained; loose DNA; transcriptionally active and early replicating.

Non-living reservoir bounded by tonoplast (differentially permeable); filled with cell sap (tonoplasm) containing water, minerals and anthocyanin pigments. Discovered by Spallanzani. Types: sap vacuoles, contractile vacuoles, gas vacuoles (pseudo vacuoles).

Non-living inclusions within the cytoplasm; include reserve food materials (carbohydrates, fats, proteins), excretory products (resins, tannins, alkaloids, glucosides, essential oils, mineral matter, latex, organic acids, gums). Starch occurs as assimilation starch (from photosynthesis) or reserve starch (deposited around hilum).
All molecules or chemicals functional in life activity are called biomolecules. Elements grouped into major (Ca, P, Na, Mg, S, K, N) and minor (Fe, Cu, Co, Mn, Mo, Zn, I) bioelements.
Carbon, oxygen and hydrogen; form the structural framework of biomolecules.
Elements that form protein, nucleic acid, lipids, chlorophyll, enzymes, etc.

Aggregated and interlinked various kinds of biomolecules in a living system; includes over 5000 chemicals. Inorganic chemicals present mostly in aqueous phase; organic in both aqueous and nonaqueous. Comprises both crystalloid and colloidal particles (crystal colloids).

Liquid of life; major constituent of cell (about 60-90%); exists in intracellular, intercellular and in vacuoles. Universal solvent, can dissolve both polar and non-polar solutes. High boiling point due to hydrogen bonding.
Single sugar units which cannot be hydrolysed further into smaller carbohydrates. General formula CₙH₂ₙOₙ. e.g., Trioses-3C, Pentoses-5C, Hexoses-6C.
Fruit sugar (levulose); sweetest among natural sugars (sweetening index 170); laevorotatory. Occurs naturally in fruit juices and honey.
Sugars having free aldehyde or ketone group which can reduce Cu²⁺ to Cu⁺. Benedict's or Fehling's test confirm their presence.
Formed due to condensation of 2-10 monosaccharide units; the oxygen bridge is known as glycoside linkage and water molecule is eliminated.

Composed of two molecules of same or different monosaccharide units. Molecular formula C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁. Also called double sugars. e.g., Maltose (malt sugar), Sucrose (cane sugar, non-reducing), Lactose (milk sugar).
Esters of fatty acids and alcohol. Hydrophobic, insoluble in water but soluble in benzene, ether and chloroform. definition coined by Bloor (1943).
Fatty acids which do not have double bond in between carbon atoms. e.g., butyric acid, palmitic acid. High melting points; solid at room temperature.

Fatty acids which have double bonds in carbon atoms. e.g., oleic acid (1 DB), linoleic acid (2 DB). MUFA = one double bond; PUFA = more than one double bond. Lower melting points; mostly liquid at room temperature.
Amphipathic molecule containing phosphoric acid. Helps in transport, metabolism, blood clotting and permeability of cell membrane. e.g., Lecithin, cephalin.
Lipids without straight chains, composed of fused hydrocarbon rings and a long hydrocarbon side chain. Best known is cholesterol.

Organic acids with a carboxyl group (-COOH) and one amino group (-NH₂) on the α-carbon atom. They are 20 in number specified in genetic code. General formula R-CHNH₂.COOH. Total known amino acids are more than 200.
Amino acids are amphoteric (bipolar ions or Zwitter ions) as they possess both acidic (-COOH) and basic (-NH₂) groups.
Not synthesised in body; must be provided in diet. e.g., valine, leucine, isoleucine, threonine, lysine, tryptophan, phenylalanine, methionine.
Synthesised partially in the body but not at the rate to meet individual requirements. e.g., arginine and histidine.
Bond formed when carboxyl group (-COOH) of one amino acid binds with amino group (-NH₂) of another amino acid. Long chains are called polypeptide chains.
Combination of nitrogenous base and a pentose sugar.
Combination of a nitrogenous base, a pentose sugar and a phosphate group (Nucleoside + Phosphoric acid → Nucleotide + H₂O).
9-membered double-ringed nitrogenous bases with nitrogen at 1', 3', 7' and 9' positions. They are adenine (A) and guanine (G).
6-membered single-ringed nitrogenous bases with nitrogen at 1' and 3' positions. They are cytosine (C), thymine (T) and uracil (U). In DNA: A pairs with T (2 H-bonds); C pairs with G (3 H-bonds).
Adenosine triphosphate. Discovered by Karl Lohmann (1929). The II and III phosphate bonds are called high energy bonds releasing about 8 K cal each.
Branched or unbranched polymers of monosaccharides joined by glycosidic bond. General formula (C₆H₁₀O₅)ₙ. Amorphous, tasteless and insoluble or only slightly soluble in water.

Branched polymer of glucose containing 30,000 glucose units; also called animal starch. Found in blue green algae, slime moulds, fungi and bacteria. Non-reducing sugar; gives red colour with iodine. Branch points every 8-10 glucose units.
Formed in photosynthesis; energy-storing substance. Two types: amylose (straight chain, α-1-4 linkage) and amylopectin (branched chain, α-1-4 and α-1-6 linkages). Gives blue colour with iodine.
Important constituent of cell wall (20-40%), made up of unbranched chain of 6000 β-D glucose units linked by 1-4 glycosidic linkage. Fibrous, rigid, insoluble in water. Most abundant polysaccharide.

Polyglycol consisting of N-acetyl-D-glucosamine units connected with β-1,4 glycosidic linkage. Found in exoskeleton of insects and crustaceans and fungal cell wall. Most abundant heteropolysaccharide.
Gelatinous substances containing amino sugars, uronic acid, etc. All slimy substances of plant are mucopolysaccharide. e.g., hyaluronic acid, vitreous humour, chondridine sulphate, heparin.
Peptidoglycan linked to short chains of peptides. Constituent of cell wall of bacteria and blue green algae.
Include some plasma protein and blood group substances. Do not contain uronic acid.
Word coined by Berzelius in 1838; first used by G. J. Mulder in 1840. 15% of protoplasm is made up of protein. Contains ~16% N, 50-55% C, 20-24% O, 7% H and 0.3-0.5% S.

Insoluble in water; animal protein resistant to proteolytic enzyme; spirally coiled thread-like structure forming fibres. e.g., collagen (connective tissue), actin and myosin, keratin (hair, claws, feathers).
Soluble in water; polypeptides coiled to form oval or spherical molecules. e.g., albumin, insulin, hormones like ACTH, oxytocin.

Complex proteins combined with characteristic non-amino acid substance called prosthetic group. Types: nucleoproteins, mucoproteins, glycoproteins, chromoproteins, lipoproteins, metalloprotein, phosphoprotein.
Covalent connections of a protein; refers to linear sequence, number and nature of amino acids bonded together with peptide bonds only. e.g., ribonuclease, insulin, myoglobin, lysozyme.

Folding of a linear polypeptide chain into specific coiled structure (α-helix). Discovered by Linus Pauling and Robert Corey (1952) using X-ray diffraction technique. e.g., keratin of hair, claws and feathers.
Arrangement and interconnection of proteins into specific loops and bends. Found in globular proteins.
Shown by protein containing more than one peptide chain. Homologous quaternary = identical units (e.g., lactic dehydrogenase); heterogeneous quaternary = dissimilar units (e.g., haemoglobin).

Polymers of nucleotides made up of C, H, O, N and P which control basic functions of the cell. First reported by Friedrich Miescher (1871) from nucleus of pus cells. Renamed nucleic acid by Altman (1889).
DNA helix bearing nucleotides in a serial arrangement but opposite in two strands.
Found near centromere of chromosome; inert in RNA synthesis; the sequence of nitrogenous bases is repeated several times.
Single helixed circular DNA isolated from bacteriophage φ×174 by Sinsheimer (1959). Does not follow Chargaff's rule. e.g., parvovirus.

subtopics.definition: Watson and Crick model (Double helical model)
subtopics.definition_content: Double helical model of DNA. X-ray crystallography by Wilkins showed two polynucleotide chains in helical configuration.

Second type of nucleic acid found in nucleus as well as cytoplasm (mitochondria, plastids, ribosomes). Carries genetic information in some viruses. Genomic RNA discovered by Franklin and Conrat (1957).

Proteinaceous substances capable of catalysing chemical reactions of biological origins without themselves undergoing any change. Biocatalysts produced by living cells only. definition used by Willy Kuhne (1878). Maximum enzymes (70%) in the cell are found in mitochondrion.
Complex of enzymes isolated from yeast cells by Edward Buchner (1897); has the power of fermenting sugar (alcoholic fermentation). First enzyme isolated.
First pure crystalline enzyme prepared by J. B. Sumner (1926) from Jack Bean (Canavalia ensiformis); confirmed that enzymes are proteins.
Enzymes that break both strands of DNA at specific sites producing sticky ends. Used as microscissors in genetic engineering. Discovered by Arber and Nathans (Nobel Prize 1978).
Protein component of an enzyme when combined with a non-protein cofactor.
Non-protein component of enzyme; small, heat stable, may be organic or inorganic. Three types: prosthetic group, coenzyme and metal ions.
Complete catalytically active enzyme formed by apoenzyme and prosthetic group together.

Organic compounds whose association with the apoenzyme is transient, occurring only during catalysis. Discovered by Fritz Lipmann. Essential chemical component of many coenzymes are vitamins. e.g., NAD, NADP (contain niacin), CoA (contains pantothenic acid), FAD (contains riboflavin B₂).
Organic cofactors permanently bound to the apoenzyme. e.g., in peroxidase and catalase which catalyse breakdown of hydrogen peroxide to water and oxygen.
Enzymes activated by inorganic metal ions as prosthetic group. e.g., Catalase (Fe), Carbonic anhydrase (Zn), Nitrogenase (Mo), Urease (Ni).

Framed in 1961 by Commission on Enzymes of the International Union of Biochemistry (IUB). Reactions divided into 6 major classes. Enzyme has a systematic code number (EC) with 4 digits denoting class, subclass, sub-subclass and specific enzyme.
Class 1. Catalyse oxidation-reduction reactions involving transfer of hydrogen atoms or ions. Three types: Oxidases (H→O₂), Dehydrogenases (H→coenzyme NAD⁺), Reductases (add H or e⁻, remove O).
Class 2. Catalyse transfer of specific group (amino, methyl, acyl, phosphate) from one molecule to another. e.g., transphosphorylases, transaminases, kinases.
Class 3. Catalyse hydrolysis of organic foods by addition of water. Most digestive enzymes are hydrolases located in lysosomes. e.g., lipases, amylases, proteases.
Class 4. Catalyse breakage of specific covalent bonds and removal of groups without hydrolysis. e.g., fumerases, carboxylases, histidine decarboxylase.
Class 5. Catalyse rearrangement of molecular structure to form isomers. e.g., phosphohexose isomerase (converts glucose 6-phosphate to fructose 6-phosphate), epimerase, racemase.
Class 6. Form bonds and join two molecules together using energy from ATP. e.g., DNA ligase (repair breaks in DNA), Amino-Acyl synthetase (activates t-RNA), Tryptophan synthetase.
Enzymes that remain and function inside cells. Found in cytoplasmic matrix, ribosomes, mitochondria and chloroplast. About 3,000 enzymes recorded.
Enzymes that leave the cells and function outside them; mainly digestive enzymes. e.g., salivary amylase, gastric pepsin, lysozyme in tears.

Amount of energy required to raise the energy of molecules at which chemical reaction can occur. Enzymes act by decreasing activation energy so that more molecules can participate at lower energy levels.
Specific sites on enzyme surface for binding substrate molecules. Usually 3-12 amino acids form an active site; R-groups of amino acids form active sites during polypeptide folding.
Proposed by Michaelis and Menten (1913). For catalytic reaction to occur, enzyme and substrate must bind to form E-S complex, which then breaks into enzyme and products (E + P).
Proposed by Emil Fisher (1894). Enzyme and substrate have a complementary shape; specific substrate can only attach to specific site on enzyme molecule. Accounts for enzyme specificity.

Proposed by Daniel E. Koshland (1959). Active site is not rigid but static; has buttressing group and catalytic group. Substrate binding to buttressing group induces catalytic group to fit the substrate by electrophilic and nucleophilic forces.
All enzymes are colloidal in nature, providing large surface area for reaction. Possess extremely low rates of diffusion and form colloidal system in water.
A single enzyme will generally catalyse only a single substrate or group of closely related substrates. Specificity dedefinitionined by sequence of amino acids in the active sites.
Enzymes are thermolabile; function best at optimum temperature (20°C-40°C). Activity stops at 0°C and above 80°C.
Number of substrate molecules on which one enzyme molecule acts in one minute. Highest: carbonic anhydrase (36 million/min). Lowest: lysozyme (30/min). Carbonic anhydrase is the fastest enzyme.
Enzyme-controlled reactions are reversible; enzymes affect only the rate, not the direction of biochemical reactions.

Structurally similar inhibitors compete for the active site of the enzyme. Effect reversed by increasing substrate concentration. Km increases, Vmax remains constant. Classic example: malonic acid inhibits succinic dehydrogenase.

Inhibitors attach to site other than active site and destroy enzyme activity by altering three-dimensional configuration. Cannot be reversed by increasing substrate concentration. Vmax is lowered. e.g., cyanide inhibits cytochrome oxidase.

Accumulation of final product of a metabolic pathway inhibits the first step of the reaction. Demonstrated in E. coli: isoleucine inhibits threonine deaminase (first step in isoleucine synthesis). Also called end product inhibition.

Inhibitors join enzyme at allosteric site and change the form of the active site. Modulators: positive (activators) or negative (inhibitors). definitioned allosteric transition by Jacob and Monod. Allosteric enzymes = enzymes with allosteric sites.
Enzymes produced by living cells in an inactive (non-functional) form; converted to active form usually by proteolysis. e.g., Pepsinogen (gastric glands) and trypsinogen (pancreas).

Enzymes with slightly different molecular structure but performing the same catalytic function. Differ in amino acid sequence, molecular weight, immunological and electrophoretic behaviour. e.g., lactic dehydrogenase (LDH).
Enzyme synthesised only in the presence of its substrate (inducer). e.g., β-galactosidase.
Enzymes found in constant amounts under different growth conditions regardless of metabolic state. e.g., enzymes of sugar breakdown (glycolysis).
Enzymes whose production is inhibited by the presence of a specific substance (enzyme repressor). e.g., glucokinase.

Catalytic RNA molecules (RNA enzymes). Reported from Tetrahymena by Cech (1981). Confirmed by Altman (1983). Substrate for ribozyme is usually an RNA molecule. RNAase-P discovered by Altman. Peptidyl transferase is a non-proteinaceous enzyme (Noller).

Substrate concentration at which a chemical reaction catalysed by an enzyme attains half its maximum velocity (Vmax). Introduced by Michaelis and Menten (1913). High Km = low affinity; low Km = strong affinity. Equation: V₀ = Vmax[S] / (Km + [S]).
Dissociation constant of enzyme-inhibitor complex. Ki = [E][I] / [EI]. High Ki decreases enzyme activity; applicable to competitive inhibitors.
Progressive increase in substrate molecules increases velocity of reaction until all active sites are occupied (Vmax). Further increase in substrate cannot increase reaction rate.
Rate of reaction is directly proportional to enzyme concentration; an increase in enzyme concentration raises the reaction rate by increasing number of available active sites.
Enzymes show maximum activity in pH range 6.0 to 7.5 (endoenzymes). Each enzyme has its own optimum: pepsin = 2; salivary amylase = 6.8; trypsin = 8.5. Denaturation occurs at extreme pH values.
Rate increases within 5-40°C. Q₁₀ of most enzymatic reactions is 2 (10°C rise doubles rate). Above 50°C enzymes get completely denatured. Below freezing, enzymes become inactive but do not denature.

chapters.name: Cell division / Cell reproduction / Cell cycle

topics.name: Interphase

subtopics.definition_content: The sequence of events which occur during cell growth and cell division are collectively called cell cycle. First described by Howard and Pelc (1953). Completes in two steps: Interphase and M-phase/Dividing phase.
A cell divides when it has grown to a certain maximum size which disturbs the karyoplasmic index/Nucleoplasmic ratio/Kemplasm connection. This ratio triggers cell division.

The period between the end of one cell division to the beginning of next cell division. Also called resting phase or not dividing phase. Actually highly metabolically active phase, in which cell prepares itself for next cell division. In case of human beings it takes approx 25 hours. Completed into three successive stages: G₁ phase, S-phase, and G₂-phase.

key_concepts: omnis cellula e cellula and cell lineage theory proposed by Rudolf Virchow (1859)
Discovery of cell division: Prevost and Dumans (1824) first studied division during cleavage of frog zygote
Nageli (1846) first proposed that new cells are formed by division of pre-existing cells
Cell cycle duration is species specific: 20 minutes for bacterial cell, 8-10 hours for intestinal epithelial cell, 20 hours for onion root tip cells

topics.name: G₁ phase/Post mitotic/Pre-DNA synthetic phase/gap Iᵃ⁴

subtopics.definition_content: Post mitotic/Pre-DNA synthetic phase. Intensive cellular synthesis; synthesis of rRNA, mRNA, ribosomes and proteins; metabolic rate is high; cells become differentiated; synthesis of enzymes and ATP storage; cell size increases; decision for a division occurs; substances of G₁ stimulate onset of next S-phase; synthesis of NHC protein, carbohydrates, proteins, lipids; no change in DNA amount.
DNA replicates and its amount becomes double (2C → 4C). Synthesis of histone proteins and NHC (non-histone chromosomal proteins). Euchromatin replicates earlier than heterochromatin.

Pre mitotic/Post synthetic phase. Mitotic spindle protein (tubulin) synthesis begins; chromosome condensation factor appears; synthesis of 3 types of RNA, NHC proteins, and ATP molecule; duplication of mitochondria, plastids and other cellular macromolecular complements; damaged DNA repair occurs.
Proposed by Lajtha (1963). Cells which are not to divide further do not proceed beyond the G₁ phase and start undergoing differentiation into specific type. Such cells are said to be in G₀ phase.

topics.name: M-phase/Dividing phase/Mitotic phase

subtopics.definition_content: Dividing phase/Mitotic phase. Divided into two phases: karyokinesis (division of nucleus) and cytokinesis (division of cytoplasm).

key_concepts: Types of cell division: Amitosis, Mitosis and Meiosis

subtopics.definition_content: Also called direct cell division. Discovered by Remak (1855) in RBC of chick embryo. No differentiation of chromosomes and spindle; nuclear envelope does not degenerate; nucleus elongates and constricts in the middle to form two daughter nuclei; followed by centripetal constriction of cytoplasm. Primitive type of division occurring in prokaryotes, protozoans, yeasts, foetal membrane of mammals, cartilage of mammals.

topics.name: Karyokinesis

subtopics.definition_content: Also called indirect cell division or somatic cell division or equational division. Mature somatic cell divides such that chromosome number is kept constant in daughter cells equal to those in parent cell. First observed by Strasburger (1875) in plant cell and in animal cell by W. Flemming (1879). definition mitosis given by Flemming (1882). Occurs in meristematic cells in plants (root apex and shoot apex).
Division of nucleus. definition given by Schneider (1887). Takes 5-10% (shortest phase) time of whole division. Comprises four phases: Prophase, Metaphase, Anaphase, Telophase.

Longest phase of karyokinesis. Chromatin fibres thicken and shorten to form chromosomes (Spireme stage); each chromosome divides longitudinally into 2 chromatids attached at centromere; nuclear membrane starts disintegrating (except in dinoflagellates); nucleolus starts disintegrating; spindle formation begins; cell becomes viscous, refractive and oval; in animal cells centrioles move towards opposite sides; spindle formed from centriole in animal cells (astral) or MTOC in plant cells (anastral); lampbrush chromosomes can be studied; chromomeres are small globular (beaded) structures on chromosomes.

Chromosomes become maximally distinct; bipolar spindle appears; spindle fibres made up of 97% tubulin protein and 3% RNA; chromosomes move to equatorial plane (congression); spindle fibres attach to kinetochores; best stage for studying chromosome morphology (structure, size, number). Two types of spindle fibres: continuous (pole to pole) and discontinuous (pole to centromere).

Centromere splits from the middle and two chromatids get separated; chromatids move to opposite poles due to repulsive force (anaphasic movement); brought about by repolymerisation of continuous fibres and depolymerisation of chromosomal fibres; chromosomes show V, J, I or L shapes depending on centromere position; centromere faces towards equator; chromatids move at speed of 1 μm/minute; about 30 ATP molecules used to move one chromosome from equator to pole; shape of chromosome best studied at anaphase.

Chromosomes reach poles by spindle fibres and form two groups; chromosomes begin to uncoil and form chromatin net; nuclear membrane and nucleolus reappear; two daughter nuclei are formed; Golgi complex and ER reform. Also known as reverse prophase.

key_concepts: Congression: movement of chromosomes towards equatorial plane in metaphase
Chromomeres: small globular (beaded) structures on chromosomes in prophase
Anaphasic movement caused by repolymerisation of continuous fibres and depolymerisation of chromosomal fibres

subtopics.definition_content: Division of cytoplasm into 2 equal parts. definition given by Whiteman (1887). Two methods: (1) Cell furrow method – characteristic of animal cells; circular constriction/invagination appears at equator, deepens centripetally to form two daughter cells; microfilament ring brings about cleavage. (2) Cell plate method – characteristic of plant cells; vesicles from Golgi apparatus form phragmoplasts which join to form cell plate; grows centrifugally; cell wall materials deposited on both sides; daughter cells remain adhered by middle lamella.

topics.name: Significance of mitosis
key_concepts: Keeps chromosome number constant and maintains genetic stability in daughter cells; maintains linear heredity
Provides new cells for repair and regeneration of lost parts and healing of wounds
Helps in asexual reproduction by fragmentation, budding, stem cutting
Somatic variations maintained by vegetative propagation can play role in speciation

subtopics.definition_content: Nuclear membrane is not lost and spindle is formed inside the nuclear membrane. e.g., Protozoans (Amoeba) and yeast. Centriole is present within the nucleus.
Nuclear membrane is lost and spindle is formed outside nuclear membrane. e.g., in plants and animals.

Chromosomes and their DNA duplicate but fail to separate, leading to polyploidy. e.g., in liver of man both diploid (2N) and polyploid cells (4N) have been reported. Also called endoduplication and endopolyploidy.
Nuclear envelope persists and microtubular spindle is not formed. During movement the chromosomes are attached with nuclear membrane.
A type of mitosis in fungi in which the intranuclear nucleus divides by furrow formation.

Agents which inhibit cell division. Types: (1) Azides and Cyanides – inhibit prophase; (2) Colchicine – prevents assembly of microtubules, inhibits spindle formation; (3) Mustard gas – agglutinates chromosomes; (4) Chalones – peptides and glycoproteins secreted by extracellular fluid of healthy cells, first reported by Laurence and Bullough (1960).

Extracted from the corms of Autumn Crocus (Colchicum autumnale). Does not allow formation of spindle as it prevents assembly of microtubules. Called 'mitotic poison'. Does not affect chromosome replication. Colchicine treated cells show doubling of chromosome number (polyploidy), providing new varieties and species and vigorous offspring. Widely used in plant breeding for doubling chromosome number.
Peptides and glycoproteins secreted by extracellular fluid of healthy cells; inhibit cellular division. First reported by Laurence and Bullough (1960).

topics.name: Meiosis-I

subtopics.definition_content: A double division occurring in a mature diploid reproductive cell (2x) in which nucleus divides twice but chromosome (DNA) replicates only once to form four haploid cells, each having half the number of chromosomes of parent cell. Also called reduction division. Meiosis in a cell occurs only once. First demonstrated by Van Beneden (1887), described by Winiwarter (1900), definition given by Farmer and Moore (1905). Gregoire used the definition meiosis I and II.

First meiotic division in which the actual chromosome number is reduced to half. Also known as reductional division or heterotypic division. Results in formation of two haploid cells from one diploid cell. Divided into Karyokinesis-I and Cytokinesis-I.
Division of nucleus in meiosis-I. Divided into four phases: Prophase-I, Metaphase-I, Anaphase-I, Telophase-I.
Longest phase of karyokinesis of meiosis. Divisible into five subphases: Leptotene, Zygotene, Pachytene, Diplotene, Diakinesis.

Chromosomes are long thread-like with chromomeres on it; volume of nucleus increases; chromatin network has half chromosomes from male and half from female parent; homologous chromosomes have similar structure; bouquet stage in animals (ends attached to nuclear envelope near centrioles) and syndet knot in plants; lampbrush chromosome found in oocyte of amphibians seen in leptotene.

Pairing or 'synapsis' of homologous chromosomes takes place; paired chromosomes called bivalents; pairing occurs in zipper-fashion; synaptonemal complex (filamentous ladder-like nucleoproteinous complex) forms between homologous chromosomes; discovered by Moses (1956). Number of bivalents is half the total chromosome number.

Crossing over – exchange of segments between non-sister chromatids of homologous chromosomes occurs; takes place by breakage (nicking by endonuclease) and reunion (annealing by ligase); Breakage and reunion hypothesis proposed by Darlington (1937); recombination nodules (~100 nm diameter) seen at irregular intervals within synaptonemal complex; DNA polymerase responsible for repair synthesis; tetrad consists of two sets of homologous chromosomes each with two chromatids (four kinetochores).

Paired chromosomes begin to separate (desynapsis); definitioninalization starts; chiasmata formed at place of crossing over between non-sister chromatids; homologous chromosomes move apart but remain attached at chiasmata; at least one chiasma formed in each bivalent; chromosomes attached only at chiasmata; chromatin bridges formed in place of synaptonemal complex.

definitioninalization completes; nuclear membrane and nucleolus degenerate; chromosomes recondense and tetrad moves to the metaphase plate; spindle formation begins; bivalents are irregularly and freely scattered in the nucleocytoplasmic matrix.

Chromosomes come on the equator; bivalents arrange in two parallel equatorial or metaphase plates (each plate has one genome); centromeres of homologous chromosomes equidistant from equator and directed towards poles while arms generally lie horizontally; each homologous chromosome has two kinetochores both joined to chromosomal/tractile fibre of same side.

Separation of homologous chromosomes moving to opposite poles; each tetrad divided into two daughter dyads (disjunction); chromosome number reduced; shape of separating chromosomes may be rod, J or V depending on centromere position; segregation of Mendelian factors/independent assortment occurs; paternal and maternal chromosomes of each homologous pair segregate, introducing genetic variability.

Two daughter nuclei formed with chromosome number half that of mother cell; nuclear membrane reappears; cytokinesis may or may not occur; cells reformed as secondary spermatocytes or secondary oocytes with haploid number of chromosomes but diploid amount of DNA; chromosomes undergo decondensation by hydration and despiralization into long thread-like chromatin fibres.

Brief interphase between meiosis-I and meiosis-II (also called intrameiotic interphase). Generally no interphase between meiosis-I and meiosis-II. There is no replication of chromosomes during this interphase.
May or may not be present. When present, occurs by cell-furrow formation in animal cells and cell plate formation in plant cells.

key_concepts: Synaptonemal complex: filamentous ladder-like nucleoproteinous complex between homologous chromosomes; discovered by Moses (1956)
Bivalent: paired homologous chromosomes; consists of one paternal and one maternal chromosome
Tetrad: bivalent with two chromatids per chromosome = four chromatids; four kinetochores
Chiasmata first observed by Janssens (1909)
Sister chromatids: two similar chromatids of same chromosome; non-sister chromatids: of two homologous chromosomes
Meiosis occurs in pollen mother cells (microsporocytes) of anther and megasporocytes of ovule in plants; primary spermatocytes and primary oocytes in animals

topics.name: Significance of meiosis-I
key_concepts: Separates homologous chromosomes to reduce chromosome number to haploid state – a necessity for sexual reproduction
Introduces variation by forming new gene combinations through crossing over and random assortment of paternal and maternal chromosomes
Induces cells to produce gametes for sexual reproduction or spores for asexual reproduction

topics.name: Meiosis-II

subtopics.definition_content: Also called equational or homotypical division because chromosome number remains same as after meiosis-I. Shorter duration than even typical mitotic division. Divided into Karyokinesis-II (Prophase-II, Metaphase-II, Anaphase-II, Telophase-II) and Cytokinesis-II. Exactly similar to mitosis. Centromere splits at anaphase-II. At end, four daughter cells formed each with one chromatid of the tetravalent.

Involves separation of two chromatids of each chromosome and their movement to separate cells. Similar to mitosis. Sister kinetochores of one chromosome are separated. Centromere splits at anaphase-II.
Always present; occurs by cell furrow formation in animal cells and cell plate formation in plant cells. Results in formation of four haploid daughter cells from one diploid parental cell.

key_concepts: Brachymeiosis: failure of meiosis-II; characteristic feature of fungi
Four daughter cells have different types of chromatids due to crossing over in meiosis-I

topics.name: Significance of meiosis-II
key_concepts: Constancy of chromosome number in successive generations brought by this process
Chromosome number becomes half during meiosis
Helps in introducing variations and mutation
Brings about gamete formation
Maintains the amount of genetic informative material
Sexual reproduction includes one meiosis and fusion
Four daughter cells will have different types of chromatids

subtopics.definition_content: In many protozoans, all animals and some lower plants, meiosis takes place before fertilization during the formation of gametes.

In fungi, certain protozoan groups, and some algae, fertilization is immediately followed by meiosis in the zygote, and the resulting adult organisms are haploid. Life cycle with haploid adult and zygotic meiosis is definitioned the haplontic cycle.

Diploid sporocytes or spore mother cells of sporophytic plant undergo meiosis to form haploid spores in sporangia; haploid spore germinates to form haploid gametophyte which produces haploid gametes by mitosis; haploid gametes fuse to form diploid zygote developing into diploid sporophyte. e.g., pteridophytes, gymnosperms and angiosperms.

topics.name: Table : 3.3-3 Differences between Mitosis and Meiosis

subtopics.definition_content: 3.3-3 Differences between Mitosis and Meiosis

key_concepts: Mitosis: single division; 2 daughter cells with same chromosome number as parent; shorter duration; may occur in diploid or haploid cells; occurs in all somatic cells
Meiosis: double division; 4 daughter cells with half chromosome number; longer duration; always occurs in diploid cells (meiocytes); occurs in reproductive cells
Prophase mitosis: short, no sub-stages; no synapsis; no crossing over; no synaptonemal complex; chromosomes appear double from beginning; no transcription

Prophase-I meiosis: prolonged with 5 sub-stages (leptotene, zygotene, pachytene, diplotene, diakinesis); synapsis in zygotene; crossing over; synaptonemal complex formed; chromosomes single-threaded initially; transcription may occur in diplotene
Metaphase mitosis: single equatorial plate; chromosomes two-threaded
Metaphase-I meiosis: two parallel equatorial plates; chromosomes four-threaded (bivalents)
Anaphase mitosis: centromere splits, chromatids separate
Anaphase-I meiosis: no centromere splitting; homologous chromosomes separate; anaphase-II similar to mitotic anaphase
Telophase mitosis: always occurs; daughter cells have same chromosome number as parent
Telophase-I meiosis: may be omitted; chromosome number reduced to half at end of telophase-I
Cytokinesis mitosis: karyokinesis usually followed by cytokinesis
Cytokinesis meiosis: sometimes skipped after meiosis-I but always occurs after meiosis-II forming 4 cells simultaneously
Mitosis index: ratio of dividing and non-dividing cells
Plectonemic coiling in mitosis (tightly coiled, not easily separable); Paranemic coiling in meiosis

subtopics.definition_content: The process of adsorption of water by hydrophilic surfaces of a substance without forming a solution is called 'imbibition'. It is a type of diffusion by which movement of water takes place along a diffusion gradient.
The solid particles which adsorb water or any other liquid are called imbibants. The liquid which is imbibed is known as imbibate.

Imbibition pressure can be defined as the maximum pressure that an imbibant will develop when it is completely soaked in pure water. Also called as the matric potential because it exists due to the presence of hydrophilic substances in the cell which include organic colloids and cell wall.

As water molecules are adsorbed on the surface of the imbibant, their kinetic energy is released in the form of heat which increases the temperature of the medium. e.g., during kneading, the flour of wheat gives a warm feeling due to imbibition.
The movement of the molecules of gases, liquids and solids from the region of higher concentration to the region of lower concentration is known as diffusion.

A hypothetical definition coined by Meyer (1938) to denote the potential ability of the molecules or ions of any substance to diffuse from an area of their higher concentration to that of their lower concentration.

subtopics.definition: Diffusion pressure deficit (DPD) or Suction pressure (SP)
