subtopics.definition_content: The amount by which the diffusion pressure of water or solvent in a solution is lower than that of pure water or solvent is known as diffusion pressure deficit (DPD). DPD is also called suction pressure (SP). definitions put forth by B.S. Meyer in 1938; suction pressure by Renner (1915).
The degree of diffusion of gases, liquids and dissolved substances through a membrane. Different types of membranes may be differentially permeable to different substances.

These membranes permit the movement of solvent molecules only through them, but prevent the movement of solute particles. e.g., egg membrane, animal bladder, parchment paper, copper ferrocyanide membrane.

This type of membrane allows selective passage of solutes along with solvent. Many biological membranes such as cell membrane (plasmalemma), tonoplast and membranes surrounding sub-cellular organelles are selectively permeable. A non-living selectively permeable membrane is cellophane.
The rate of diffusion is inversely proportional to the square root of density of the diffusing substance. Larger the molecules, slower will be the rate of diffusion. D ∝ 1/√d.

Osmosis (Gr. Osmos = a pushing or impulse) was discovered by Abbe Nollet in 1748. It is special type of diffusion when solvent moves through a semipermeable membrane from a place of higher diffusion pressure to a place of lower diffusion pressure. It is the migration of solvent from a hypotonic solution to hypertonic solution through a semi-permeable membrane.

Osmotic pressure is that equivalent of maximum hydrostatic pressure which is produced in the solution, when this solution is separated from its pure solvent by a semipermeable membrane. Osmotic pressure of a solution is greater than pure solvent. definition coined by Pfeffer. Formula: OP = CST.

The reverse movement of water through a semipermeable membrane from a more concentrated solution to a more dilute solution by applying external pressure on the more concentrated solution. Used in removing salts from saline water and extra-purification of water.
The osmotic flow of water into a cell, when it is placed in a solution whose solute concentration is less than that of the cell sap. e.g., swelling of raisins when placed in water.
The osmotic outflow of water from a cell, when it is placed in a solution whose solute concentration is more than that of the cell sap. e.g., shrinkage of grapes when placed in strong sugar solution.

A solution whose osmotic concentration (solute potential) is less than that of another solution or cell sap. If a cell is placed in such a solution, water starts moving into the cell by endosmosis, and cell becomes turgid.

A solution whose osmotic concentration (solute potential) is more than that of another solution or cell sap. If a cell is placed in such a solution, water comes out by exosmosis and cell becomes flaccid.

A solution whose osmotic concentration (solute potential) is equal to that of another solution or cell sap. If a cell is placed in isotonic solution, there is no net change of water between the cell and the solution.
The actual pressure that develops due to osmotic entry of water into a cell, responsible for pushing the membrane against the cell wall, is definitioned turgor pressure.

The pressure exerted by the cell wall over the protoplast to counter the turgor pressure. Normally wall pressure is equal and opposite to turgor pressure (WP = TP) except when the cell becomes flaccid.
The shrinkage of the protoplast of a living cell from its cell wall due to exosmosis under the influence of a hypertonic solution is called plasmolysis.
The stage of plasmolysis when the protoplast just begins to contract away from the cell wall. TP becomes zero at the time of limiting plasmolysis.
The stage when the cell wall has reached its limit of contraction and the protoplast has detached from cell wall attaining spherical shape.

The swelling up of a plasmolysed protoplast due to endosmosis under the influence of a hypotonic solution or water. Possible only immediately after plasmolysis otherwise the cell protoplast becomes permanently damaged. Leaf of Tradescantia is used for demonstration.

The potential energy of water is called water potential. It is the difference between chemical potential of water at any point in a system and that of pure water under standard conditions. definition coined by Slatyer and Taylor (1960). Measured in pascal (Pa) or bars. Water potential of pure water at normal temperature and pressure is zero.

The component of water potential influenced by the presence of a matrix (surface to which water molecules are adsorbed, such as soil particles, cell walls, protoplasms). Has a negative value. In mesophytes and hydrophytes it is almost negligible.
Also known as Osmotic potential. Defined as the amount by which the water potential is reduced as a result of the presence of solute. Solute potentials are always negative. ψs = -π.

Plant cell wall exerts a pressure on the cellular contents. The pressure potential is usually positive and operates in plant cells as wall pressure and turgor pressure. Its magnitude varies between +5 bars (during day) and +15 bars (during night).
Loss of turgidity causing folding and drooping of leaves and other soft aerial parts of the plant. Symptoms appear in the plant due to scarcity of water.
No external symptoms but the mesophyll cells lose a part of their water content during midday due to transpiration.

Occurs during midday and is visible externally due to drooping of leaves and young shoots. Rate of transpiration is high compared to water absorption. Corrected in the afternoon when transpiration decreases.

The last stage in wilting when the aerial parts do not regain turgidity even if placed in water saturated atmosphere. Caused by decrease in water content of the soil which increases TSMS (Total soil moisture stress).

The percentage of water on the dry weight basis of the soil that is present in the soil when plants growing in it first touch the condition of permanent wilting. Varies between 1-15% depending upon texture of soil.

formulas.expression: DPD = OP - TP (also DPD = OP - WP since WP = TP)
formulas.description: Diffusion pressure deficit equals osmotic pressure minus turgor pressure. DPD is maximum in a flaccid cell and zero in a fully turgid cell.

formulas.expression: ψ = ψs + ψp (simplified); ψ = ψm + ψs + ψp (full)
formulas.description: Water potential equation. In mesophytes/hydrophytes, matric potential is negligible so simplified form is used.
Solute potential equals negative of osmotic pressure (π).
Osmotic pressure: C = molar concentration, S = solution constant (0.082), T = absolute temperature in K.
Graham's law of diffusion: Rate of diffusion is inversely proportional to the square root of density of the diffusing substance.

subtopics.definition_content: The total amount of water present in the soil.
The water available to the plant from the soil.
The water which cannot be absorbed by the plants.
Water that enters the soil and passes through spaces between soil particles to reach the water table.
Water held around soil particles in the capillary space due to cohesion and surface tension. This is the water which can be utilised by the plants. Also called growth water.
Water held tightly around soil particle surfaces due to cohesive and adhesive forces. This type of water is not available to plants.
The amount of water actually retained by the soil. About 25-35% in common loam soil. Excess water beyond field capacity produces water logging.

Unicellular tubular prolongations of the outer wall of the epiblema. Root hairs develop mainly at the tip just above the zone of elongation. Increase the absorption surface of a root system about 5 to 20 times. Water potential of root hair cells is generally -1 to -4 atm.
A special type of hygroscopic tissue developed in roots of epiphytes (orchid) that can absorb atmospheric moisture.

topics.name: Pathway of water movement in root

subtopics.definition_content: Apoplastic movement of water occurs exclusively through the intercellular spaces and cell wall without crossing any membrane. definition coined by Munch.
Symplastic movement of water occurs from cell to cell through the plasmodesmata. definition coined by Munch.
Water crosses at least two membranes from each cell in its path. Water may also enter through tonoplast surrounding the vacuole. Casparian strips on endodermis force water through this pathway.
Strips formed on the endodermis due to deposition of wax-like substance, suberin. They block apoplastic water movement at the endodermis.

Absorption by the activity of root itself, particularly root hairs. Utilizes metabolic energy. Includes Osmotic theory (Atkins, 1916; Priestley, 1922) and Non-osmotic theory (Thimann, 1951; Kramer, 1959). definition coined by Renner (1912, 1915).

Most common and rapid method of water absorption, accounting for about 98% of total water uptake. Forces responsible originate in transpiring shoots; root cells remain passive. Mainly through apoplast in cortex but through symplast in endodermis and pericycle. definition coined by Renner (1912, 1915).

When the concentration of solutes in soil water increases (due to addition of excess fertilizers increasing salinity), the osmotic pressure increases which slows down or inhibits absorption of water. This condition is called physiological dryness.

topics.name: Factors affecting rate of water absorption

subtopics.definition_content: The upward transport of water along with dissolved minerals from roots to the aerial parts of the plant is called Ascent of sap. Also called translocation of water. The water with dissolved minerals is called sap.

Theories suggesting that forces for ascent of sap are generated in living cells (Wesdefinitionaier, 1883). Not supported by experimental evidence. Disproved by Strasburger (1891) and Overton (1911) who used poisons to kill living cells and showed ascent still occurred.
Proposed by Godlewski (1884). Ascent of sap takes place due to rhythmic change in osmotic pressure of living cells of xylem parenchyma and medullary rays, which pump water upward.
Proposed by Sir J.C. Bose (1923). Living cells of innermost layer of cortex just outside the endodermis are in rhythmic pulsations responsible for pumping water upward.

Proposed by Priestley (1916). Cortical cells become fully turgid, their elastic walls exert pressure on fluid-contents forcing them towards xylem vessels. Root pressure = osmotic pressure set up in cortical cells. definition used by Stephen Hales. Range: 2-5 atmospheres.

subtopics.definition: Cohesion of water and transpiration pull theory
subtopics.definition_content: Most widely accepted theory proposed by Dixon and Jolly (1894). Also known as cohesion-tension theory. Depends on: (1) continuous water column in xylem, (2) transpiration pull created by water deficit in leaf cells, (3) cohesive power of water molecules (~350 atm). Supported by Renner (1911, 1915), Kramer and Kozlowski (1960).

The phenomenon of continuity of water column breaking due to presence of air bubbles in xylem vessels. Demonstrated by Milburn and Johnson (1966). Continuity maintained due to pits in lateral walls of xylem vessels (Scholander).
Proposed by Boehm (1809). In fine tubes, water rises due to surface tension depending on capillarity of the tube. Not acceptable as xylem vessels can be broader than capillarity range.

Calculated by Huber and Schmidt (1936) using radioactive 32P, specific dyes and heat-pulse transport. Varies between 1 and 6 meters per hour; under high transpirational conditions may reach 45 meters per hour. More in ring porous woods having large vessels; slowest in gymnosperms.

The loss of water in the form of vapours from the aerial parts of a plant is called transpiration. Maximum transpiration occurs in mesophytic plants. About 98% of the water absorbed by land plants evaporates from aerial parts.
Transpiration through the cuticle (wax-like covering on the epidermis of leaves). If cuticle is thin, up to 20% of total transpiration may occur through it; reduced in xerophytes with thick cuticle.
Loss of water vapours through lenticels. Amounts to about 0.1% of the total water loss through transpiration.
Loss of water vapour through stomata on leaf surface. Amounts to 80-90% of total water vapour loss. The most common type of transpiration.
Transpiration through corky covering of the stems. Amount is 0.5% of total transpiration.

Two specialized epidermal cells surrounding each stomatal opening. Guard cells of dicot leaves are kidney-shaped or raniform; those of monocots (family Gramineae) are dumb-bell shaped or elliptical. Guard cells contain chloroplasts. Average stomata length 20-28 μm, breadth 3-10 μm.
Epidermal cells surrounding the guard cells, different from normal epidermal cells; they develop from the same cell from which guard cells develop.
The stoma along with the subsidiary cells. Each stoma leads into an air space called sub-stomatal cavity.
Guard cells surrounded by a limited number of unspecialized subsidiary cells similar to other epidermal cells. Classification by Metcalfe and Chalk. e.g., Ranunculaceae family.
Guard cells surrounded by three subsidiary cells, two of which are large and one is very small. e.g., Solanaceae and Cruciferae families.
Guard cells surrounded by only two subsidiary cells lying parallel to the guard cells. e.g., Magnoliaceae family.
Guard cells surrounded by only two subsidiary cells lying at right angles to the longitudinal axis of the guard cells. e.g., Acanthaceae and Labiatae families.
Guard cells surrounded by four or more subsidiary cells elongated radially to stomata.
Stomata present only on the upper epidermis of leaves. Found in water Lily, Nymphaea and many other floating hydrophytes.
Stomata present only on the lower surface of leaves. e.g., Apple, mulberry, peach and walnut.
More stomata on the lower surface and less on the upper surface (dorsiventral leaves). e.g., Potato, tomato, bean, pea, cabbage.
Stomata equally distributed on both surfaces (isobilateral leaves). Found in monocots: oat, maize, grasses.
Stomata either absent altogether or vestigial. e.g., Potamogeton and submerged hydrophytes.
Stomata remain open throughout the day but close during night. e.g., Pea, bean, mustard, cucumber, sunflower, radish, turnip, apple, grape. Classified by Loftfield (1921).
Stomata close only for a few hours in the evening, otherwise remain open throughout day and night. e.g., Cucurbita, Allium, Cabbage, Tulip, Banana.
Stomata open only for a few hours in the day time, otherwise remain closed throughout day and night. e.g., Cereals.
Stomata remain always open throughout day and night. e.g., Amphibious plants or emergent hydrophytes.

topics.name: Mechanism of opening and closing of stomata

subtopics.definition_content: Proposed by Von Mohl (1856). Chloroplasts in guard cells prepare osmotically active substances by photosynthesis; osmotic pressure and turgor pressure increase causing stomata to open.

Proposed by Lloyd (1908). Turgidity of guard cell depends on interconversion of starch and sugar. Guard cells contain sugar during day (stomata open) and starch during night (stomata closed). Regulated by pH. Enzyme phosphorylase responsible (detected by Yin and Tung, 1948).

Initially given by Fujino (1959), modified by Levitt (1974). Stomatal opening and closing occurs due to active transport of K+ into or out of guard cells. K+ accumulation in guard cells increases osmotic pressure, causing endosmosis and stomatal opening.

Proposed by Levitt (1974). Stomatal opening: decreased CO2 → increased pH → starch converted to malic acid by PEPC enzyme (Willmer et al., 1973) → H+ transported out and K+ taken in via H+-K+ pump → osmotic pressure rises → endosmosis → stomata open. ABA mediates closure.

Stomata in succulent/CAM plants (Opuntia, Bryophyllum) that open during night (darkness) and remain closed during day. Explained by Nishida (1963). During night, incomplete oxidation of carbohydrates accumulates organic acids (malic acid) without releasing CO2.

Chemical substances which reduce transpiration (by increasing leaf resistance to water vapour diffusion) without affecting gaseous exchange. Types: (1) Metabolic inhibitors (e.g., phenyl mercuric acetate, ABA) and (2) Film forming anti-transpirants (e.g., silicon emulsion, colourless plastic resins, low viscosity waxes).

The process of exudation of liquid drops from the edges of leaves during night or early morning. First studied by Bergerstein in 1887. Occurs through stomata-like pores called hydathodes (Water Stomata). Caused by root pressure. Found in 115 families and 333 genera. e.g., Garden nasturtium, Oat, Colocasia.

Stomata-like pores present at the tip or margin of leaves, above a mass of loosely arranged cells with large intercellular spaces called epithem. Xylem of a small vein definitioninates among thin-walled parenchymatous cells of epithem.

formulas.description: Stomatal Index: S = number of stomata per unit area, E = number of epidermal cells per unit area.

subtopics.definition_content: The movement of organic food or solute in soluble form, from one organ to another organ. Requires expenditure of metabolic energy; solute moves at the rate of 100 cm/hr.
Most important type; from leaves to stem and roots via phloem.
From leaves to developing flowers, buds, fruits and also during germination of seeds and tubers; via phloem (according to Curtis).
From pith to cortex and epidermis.

First performed by Hartig (1837). Removing the ring of bark (phloem + cambium) above the root causes accumulation of food as swelling just above the ring, proving phloem is the path for downward translocation of food.
A substance that blocks sieve pores during winter, thus blocking translocation of solutes.

Demonstrated by Mason and Maskel (1928). Translocation occurs from high concentration (place of manufacture/storage) to lower concentration (place of consumption). Modified to activated diffusion hypothesis by Mason and Phillis (1936): food particles are first energy activated then translocated.
Proposed by de Vries (1885). Food transported by streaming current of protoplasm (cyclosis: rotation and circulation). Supported by Curtis (1950).

Proposed by Thaine (1964). Movement of particulate and fluid constituents of cytoplasm through linear files of longitudinally oriented plant cells via proteinaceous transcellular strands. Explains bidirectional translocation.
Proposed independently by Fensom (1957) and Spanner (1958). Solute moves in the positive direction of the electrical gradient along with K+ ions.

subtopics.definition: Munch's mass flow or pressure flow hypothesis
subtopics.definition_content: First proposed by Hartig (1860), modified by Munch (1930), elaborated by Crafts (1938). Translocation of solutes occurs in a mass along with cell sap through sieve tubes from a region of higher turgor pressure to lower turgor pressure. Munch assumed protoplasm of sieve tubes is connected through plasmodesmata forming a continuous symplast.

topics.name: Macronutrients (Macroelements or major elements)

subtopics.definition_content: Which are required by plants in larger amounts (Generally present in the plant tissues in concentrations of 1 to 10 mg per gram of dry matter).
Mineral salts dissolved in soil solution are constantly passing downwards along with percolating (gravitational) water. The phenomenon is called leaching. Leaching is more in case of anions.

key_concepts: Macroelements are usually involved in the synthesis of organic molecules and development of osmotic potential
Silicon and sodium often occur in the range of macroelements among non-essential functional elements

topics.name: Micronutrients (Microelements or minor elements or trace elements)

subtopics.definition_content: Which are required by plants in very small amounts, i.e., in traces (equal to or less than 0.1 mg per gram dry matter). Cobalt, vanadium, aluminium and nickel, may be essential for certain plants.

key_concepts: Microelements are mostly involved in the functioning of enzymes, as cofactors or metal activators
Criteria of essentiality (Arnon and Stout, 1939): element must be essential for normal growth and reproduction; requirement must be specific and irreplaceable; requirement must be direct

topics.name: Ash analysis
key_concepts: Plant tissue subjected to 550–600°C in electric muffle furnace; ash shows ~92 mineral elements; 30 elements in every plant; 16 are essential elements

topics.name: Sand culture
key_concepts: Plants grown in inert sand + nutrient solution; roots get natural environment and proper aeration

topics.name: Aeroponics
key_concepts: Plants grown with their roots bathed in nutrients mist; successfully used in growing Citrus and olive

topics.name: Carbon, hydrogen and oxygen
key_concepts: Together constitute ~94% of total dry weight of plant; Carbon from atmospheric CO₂; H and O from water absorbed from soil

topics.name: Nitrogen

subtopics.definition_content: Yellowing of leaves due to loss of chlorophyll.

key_concepts: Absorbed mainly as NO₃⁻; constituent of proteins, nucleic acids, vitamins, chlorophyll, hormones, coenzymes, ATP
Deficiency: chlorosis appearing first in older leaves, development of anthocyanin pigmentation, delayed flowering

topics.name: Phosphorus

subtopics.definition_content: Localised death of cells.

key_concepts: Absorbed as H₂PO₄⁻ (low pH) and HPO₄³⁻ (high pH); present in plasma membrane, nucleic acid, nucleotides, ATP; indispensable in energy metabolism (phosphorylation)
Deficiency: dark green/purplish leaves, anthocyanin in veins, sickle leaf disease, decreased cambial activity

topics.name: Sulphur
key_concepts: Absorbed as SO₄²⁻; constituent of amino acids cystine, cysteine, methionine; vitamins biotin and thiamine; coenzyme A
Deficiency: chlorosis in young leaves (immobile element), tea yellow disease in tea plants, decrease in stroma lamellae

topics.name: Potassium
key_concepts: Only monovalent cation essential for plants; activates DNA polymerase; essential for translocation of photosynthates, stomatal opening/closing, synthesis of nucleic acid and chlorophyll
Deficiency: mottled chlorosis with necrotic areas at tips and margins, inhibits protein synthesis and photosynthesis, increases respiration rate

topics.name: Calcium
key_concepts: Absorbed as Ca²⁺; forms calcium pectate in middle lamella; activates ATPase, succinic dehydrogenase, adenylate kinase; essential for spindle fibre organisation in mitosis
Deficiency: death of meristems, chlorosis and necrosis of young leaf margins, hooked leaf tips, malformed potato tubers

topics.name: Magnesium
key_concepts: Important constituent of chlorophyll; present in middle lamella as magnesium pectate; required for binding larger and smaller subunits of ribosomes during protein synthesis; activates several enzymes
Deficiency: interveinal chlorosis then anthocyanin pigmentation then necrotic spots; deficiency appears first in mature leaves

topics.name: Iron
key_concepts: Structural component of ferredoxin, flavoproteins, cytochromes, peroxidases, catalases; activates nitrate reductase and aconitase; essential for chlorophyll synthesis
Absorbed as Fe³⁺ and Fe²⁺ but active in ferrous state; deficiency: interveinal chlorosis in younger leaves, grey/white chlorosis

topics.name: Manganese
key_concepts: Activates malic dehydrogenase, oxalosuccinic decarboxylase, nitrite reductase; required for photolysis of water in photosynthesis
Deficiency: grey speck disease in oat, marsh spots in seeds of pea; excess causes iron, magnesium and calcium deficiency symptoms

topics.name: Copper
key_concepts: Component of plastocyanin; activates phenolases, ascorbic acid oxidase, tyrosinase, cytochrome oxidase; maintains carbohydrate nitrogen balance
Deficiency: exanthema in fruit trees, reclamation of cereals/legumes, die back (necrosis of tip of young leaves in Citrus)

topics.name: Molybdenum
key_concepts: Activator of nitrate reductase; required for nitrogen fixation; required for synthesis of ascorbic acid
Deficiency: whiptail disease in cauliflower and cabbage; mottled chlorosis in older leaves

topics.name: Zinc
key_concepts: Required for tryptophan synthesis (precursor of indole acetic acid—an auxin); constituent of carbonic anhydrase, hexokinase, alcohol dehydrogenase, carboxypeptidase
Deficiency: little leaf disease (sickle-shaped leaves in rosettes), white bud disease in maize, khaira disease of rice, mottled leaf of apple

topics.name: Boron
key_concepts: Absorbed as boric acid (H₃BO₃) and tetraborate anions; required for DNA synthesis, pollen germination, Ca²⁺ uptake, cell differentiation
Deficiency: death of shoot tip (boron needed for DNA synthesis), internal cork of apple, top rot of tobacco, browning of cauliflower, heart rot of sugar beets

topics.name: Chlorine
key_concepts: Absorbed as chloride ions; required for photolysis of water in photosystem-II; essential for oxygen evolution in photosynthesis
Deficiency: wilted leaves turning chlorotic then bronze colour; stunted/club-shaped roots

subtopics.definition_content: Macroelements which become commonly deficient in the soils are called critical elements. They are N, P and K. Most fertilisers contain critical elements. They are called complete fertilisers.

chapters.name: Mechanism of absorption of mineral elements

topics.name: Passive absorption

subtopics.definition_content: Absorption of ions without the use of metabolic energy is known as passive absorption. This type of absorption is carried out by purely physical forces.
The part of plant cell or tissue that permits free diffusion; the apparent volume that accommodates ions entering by diffusion.
A mechanism (F.G. Donnan, 1927) accounting for the effect of non-diffusible (fixed) ions on one side of the membrane, causing unequal distribution of diffusible ions: [Ci⁺][Ai⁻] = [Co⁺][Ao⁻].

key_concepts: Mass flow hypothesis (Hylmo, 1953): ion absorption increases with transpiration; ions move in mass flow with water
Simple diffusion hypothesis: ions migrate into root by simple diffusion when concentration outside > inside
Facilitated diffusion hypothesis: ions transported across membrane by carrier protein through protein channels
Ion exchange hypothesis: Contact exchange theory (oscillating ion volumes overlap) and Carbonic acid exchange theory (CO₂ + H₂O → H₂CO₃ → H⁺ + HCO₃⁻ exchange ions)

topics.name: Active absorption

subtopics.definition_content: The absorption of ions involving use of metabolic energy is called active absorption. Energy comes from metabolic activities, especially respiration.
The space in a cell or tissue where mineral ions enter by the usage of metabolic energy.
The increased respiration when a plant is transferred from water to salt solution, as proposed in support of Lundegardh's cytochrome-pump hypothesis. Also called anion respiration.

key_concepts: Carrier concept (Van den Honert, 1937): ion-carrier complex forms on outer membrane surface; ATP used to reactivate carrier by kinase; phosphatase releases ion into inner space
Cytochrome-pump hypothesis (Lundegardh, 1950): only anions absorbed actively via cytochrome chain; cations move passively along electrical gradient

Protein-lecithin carrier concept (Bennet-Clark, 1956): lecithin (phosphatide) carries both anions and cations; phosphate group = cation binding site; choline = anion binding site; lecithinase releases ions

topics.name: Apoplast pathway
key_concepts: Ion movement through cell wall spaces (polysaccharides) from epidermis to xylem; proved by P.R. Stout and D.R. Hoagland (1939)

topics.name: Symplast pathway
key_concepts: Ion movement through cytoplasm via plasmodesmata from epidermis through cortex, endodermis, pericycle to xylem

chapters.name: Nitrogen nutrition in plants

topics.name: Process of Nitrogen fixation

subtopics.definition_content: The conversion of atmospheric nitrogen into inorganic or organic usable forms through the agency of living organisms.
A pigment present in root nodules, closely related to haemoglobin; acts as an oxygen scavenger to protect nitrogenase and create optimal anaerobic conditions for nitrogen fixation.

An enzyme complex that catalyzes biological nitrogen fixation under anaerobic conditions; consists of Fe protein (dinitrogen reductase, component I) and MoFe protein (dinitrogenase, component II); requires 16 ATP, 8H⁺ and 8e⁻ to reduce one N₂ to 2NH₃.

key_concepts: Abiological: (i) Atmospheric/natural (lightening, thunder—10% of total); (ii) Industrial (Haber-Bosch method—N₂ + H₂ → NH₃)

Asymbiotic biological: Azotobacter (aerobic), Clostridium (anaerobic), Chlorobium (photosynthetic), Thiobacillus (chemosynthetic); Cyanobacteria: Anabaena, Nostoc; free-living fungi: Yeast, Pullularia
Symbiotic biological: Rhizobium leguminosarum in legume root nodules; Frankia in non-legumes (Casuarina, Alnus, Cycas); Cyanobacteria in Azolla, Anthoceros

topics.name: Ammonification and nitrification

subtopics.definition_content: The conversion of nitrogenous organic compounds in dead bodies of plants and animals into ammonia or ammonium ions in the soil, carried out by ammonifying bacteria.
The conversion of ammonia into nitrates by nitrifying bacteria: Nitrosomonas and Nitrosococcus convert NH₃ to NO₂⁻; Nitrobacter converts NO₂⁻ to NO₃⁻.
The conversion of nitrates and ammonia into atmospheric free elemental nitrogen by denitrifying bacteria such as Thiobacillus denitrificans and Micrococcus denitrificans.

key_concepts: Nitrifying bacteria are chemoautotrophs; maximum activity at 30–35°C in alkaline soils with sufficient moisture and aeration

topics.name: Nitrate assimilation in plants

subtopics.definition_content: A flavoprotein enzyme containing FAD as prosthetic group that reduces nitrate to nitrite; molybdenum serves as electron carrier; receives hydrogen from reduced NADP or NAD.
An enzyme that reduces nitrite ions to ammonia; requires iron and copper; NADH and NADPH act as hydrogen donors.

key_concepts: Pathway: Nitrate → Nitrite → Hyponitrite → Hydroxylamine → Ammonia
Ammonia is immediately converted to glutamate: Ammonia + α-ketoglutarate + NADH → Glutamate + NAD⁺ + H₂O

topics.name: Application of fertilizers
key_concepts: Critical elements N, P, K typically deficient; NPK fertilizers: ammonium chloride, ammonium sulphate, ammonium nitrate, bone meal, calcium magnesium phosphate, nitrate of soda

formulas: NO₃⁻ + NADPH + H⁺ → NO₂⁻ + H₂O + NADP⁺

topics.name: Parasites

subtopics.definition_content: Any part of the body of a parasite modified into a special organ which enters into the cells of the host and absorbs food, water and minerals from the host.

key_concepts: Total stem parasite: Cuscuta (rootless, yellow, twines around host; haustoria connect with xylem and phloem)
Total root parasites: Orobanche (on brinjal/tobacco), Rafflesia (stinking corpse lily; largest flower), Balanophora
Partial stem parasites: Viscum album (mistletoe), Loranthus
Partial root parasites: Santalum album (sandal wood), Striga (on sugarcane), Thesium (on grasses)

topics.name: Saprophyte

subtopics.definition_content: A mutually beneficial association between a fungus and the root of a higher plant in which fungal mycelium forms a mantle over the root surface and metabolites are transferred in both directions.

key_concepts: Live on dead organic matter; examples: Monotropa (Indian pipe; no chlorophyll, nourished through mycorrhiza), Neottia (Bird's nest orchid; nutrients absorbed by mycorrhiza)

topics.name: Symbiotic plant

subtopics.definition_content: When two different species of organisms spend much or all of their lives in close physical association, deriving mutual benefit.

key_concepts: Lichens: phycobiont (Trebouxia/Nostoc) + mycobiont (Ascomycetes/Basidiomycetes); alga provides food, fungus provides water, minerals and protection
Root nodules of Leguminosae: Rhizobium fixes N₂, plant provides food and shelter
Myrmecophily: ants + higher plants (e.g., Acacia sphaerocephala); ants protect plant, obtain food and shelter

topics.name: Carnivorous or Insectivorous plants
key_concepts: Grow in nitrogen-deficient marshy soils; catch small insects to fulfil nitrogen requirements; secrete proteolytic enzymes
Drosera (sundew): glandular tentacles; thigmonasty; digestion by pepsin hydrochloride
Utricularia (bladderwort): rootless aquatic; bladder trap mechanism with valve
Nepenthes (pitcher plant): N. khasiana found in Assam/Meghalaya (endangered); leaf lamina modified into pitcher; lid with alluring glands
Dionaea (Venus fly trap): bilobed lamina with trigger hairs; lobes fold on touch; pepsin hydrochloride digestion
Sarracenia: trumpet-shaped sessile pitchers; digestion by bacteria (no digestive enzymes)
Pinguicula (butterwort): stalked glands secrete mucilage; sessile glands secrete digestive enzymes
Aldrovanda: rootless submerged aquatic; bilobed leaves; digestion enzymatically

topics.name: Key contributors to photosynthesis research

subtopics.definition_content: Transformation of photonic energy (i.e., light or radiant energy) into chemical energy; energy rich compounds like carbohydrates are synthesized from simple inorganic compounds like carbon dioxide and water in the presence of chlorophyll and sunlight with liberation of oxygen.

key_concepts: Van Helmont (1648): all food of plant derived from water, not soil
Stephen Hales (Father of Plant Physiology, 1727): plants obtain part of nutrition from air; light may play a role
Joseph Priestley (1772): green plants (mint) purify foul air (Phlogiston) and convert it to pure air (Dephlogiston)
Jan Ingen-Housz (1779): purification by green parts only and in sunlight; green leaves liberate dephlogisticated air (O2) in sunlight and phlogisticated air (CO2) in dark
Jean Senebier (1782): plants absorb CO2 and release O2 in light; rate of O2 evolution depends on CO2 consumption
Nicolus de Saussure (1804): importance of water; amount of CO2 absorbed equals O2 released
Julius Robert Mayer (1845): light energy converted to chemical energy by plants
Liebig (1845): main source of carbon in plants is CO2
Bousingault (1860): volume of CO2 absorbed equals volume of O2 evolved
Julius Von Sachs (1862): first visible product of photosynthesis is starch; chlorophyll confined to chloroplasts
Melvin Calvin (1954): traced path of carbon in photosynthesis (dark reactions); C3 cycle (Calvin cycle); Nobel prize 1961
Huber, Michel and Deisenhofer (1985): crystallised photosynthetic reaction center from Rhodopseudomonas viridis; Nobel prize 1988

subtopics.definition_content: Green plastids which function as the site of photosynthesis in eukaryotic photoautotrophs; fixes CO2 into carbohydrate.

Number of pigment molecules required to affect a photochemical act, that is the release of a molecule of oxygen. Park and Biggins (1964) gave the definition quantasome for photosynthetic units which is equivalent to 230 chlorophyll molecules.

topics.name: Chloroplast pigments

subtopics.definition_content: Organic molecules that absorb light of specific wavelengths in the visible region due to presence of conjugated double bonds in their structures; fat soluble; located in the lipid part of the thylakoid membranes (fret membrane).

Primary photosynthetic pigment (bluish-green); found in all oxygen-evolving photosynthetic plants except photosynthetic bacteria; reaction centre of photosynthesis; molecular formula C55H72O9N4Mg; has —CH3 (methyl group); directly takes part in photochemical reaction.
Accessory pigment (olive-green); molecular formula C55H70O8N4Mg; has —CHO (aldehyde) group in place of —CH3 in chlorophyll a.

Pigments including chlorophyll b, c, d and e; carotenoids and phycobilins that do not directly take part in photochemical act; absorb specific wavelengths and transfer energy finally to chlorophyll a through electron spin resonance.

Lipid-soluble pigments (lipochromes) found in non-green parts of plants; mainly absorb violet, indigo and blue wavelengths; protect chlorophyll molecules from photo-oxidation; classified into carotenes and xanthophylls.
Orange-red coloured carotenoids with general formula C40H56; isolated from carrot; found in all groups of plants; beta-carotene on hydrolysis gives vitamin A (provitamin A).
Yellow coloured carotenoids (also called xanthols); contain oxygen along with carbon and hydrogen; general formula C40H56O2; e.g., Lutein (widely distributed), Fucoxanthin (in Phaeophyceae).

Water-soluble accessory pigments mainly found in blue-green algae (Cyanobacteria) and red algae; open tetrapyrrolic structure without magnesium and phytol chain; include phycocyanin and phycoerythrin.

key_concepts: Chlorophyll molecule: squarish tetrapyrrolic ring (porphyrin head) + phytol alcohol tail; Mg at central position
When central Mg replaced by Fe: chlorophyll becomes cytochrome (used in photophosphorylation and respiration)
Blue-green algae: more phycocyanin; Red algae: more phycoerythrin

topics.name: Nature of light

subtopics.definition_content: Distance between the crest of two adjacent waves; shorter the wavelength, greater the energy.
Range of light wavelengths (400–700 nm) used for photosynthesis.

key_concepts: Visible light: 390 nm (3900 Å) to 760 nm (7600 Å)
Violet (390–430 nm), Blue (430–470 nm), Green (500–580 nm), Red (660–760 nm)
Of total solar energy reaching earth: ~2% used in photosynthesis, ~10% in other metabolic activities
Blue light carries more energy than red light
Leaves appear green because chlorophylls do not absorb green light (reflected and transmitted)

topics.name: Absorption and action spectra

subtopics.definition_content: The curve representing the light absorbed at each wavelength by a pigment; studied with spectrophotometer.
Curve showing rate of photosynthesis at different wavelengths of light.

key_concepts: Chlorophyll a absorption peaks: 430 nm (blue) and 662 nm (red)
Chlorophyll b absorption peaks: 455 nm (blue) and 644 nm (red)
Maximum photosynthesis in blue and red spectral regions
First action spectrum by T.W. Engelmann (1882) using Spirogyra and oxygen-seeking bacteria

topics.name: Van Niel hypothesis and isotope experiments

subtopics.definition_content: Splitting of water during photosynthesis; the O2 released in photosynthesis comes from water (H2O), not from CO2; confirmed by Ruben and Kamen (1941) using H2(18)O isotope tracer in Chlorella.

key_concepts: C.B. Van Niel (1930): sulphur bacteria use H2S and CO2; by analogy, green plants use H2O and O2 is evolved from water not CO2
Ruben and Kamen (1941): confirmed using H2(18)O; liberated O2 contains 18O of water not of CO2

formulas.description: Overall reaction of photosynthesis with isotopically labelled water showing O2 comes from water

topics.name: Light phase (Photochemical reactions)

subtopics.definition_content: Phase of photosynthesis occurring in grana fraction of chloroplast; energy from sunlight absorbed and converted to chemical energy stored in ATP and NADPH + H+. Also called photochemical reactions, light-dependent reactions, or Hill's reactions.

Phase of photosynthesis in which carbohydrates are synthesized from CO2 using ATP and NADPH formed in light-dependent reactions; also called Blackman reaction, biosynthetic phase, light-independent reactions; occurs in stroma; sensitive to temperature but independent of light.

Robin Hill (1939): extracted chloroplasts of Stellaria media and Lamium album suspended with electron acceptors (potassium ferroxalate, potassium ferricyanide) release O2 due to photochemical splitting of water without CO2 consumption or carbohydrate production.
ATP and NADPH2 produced during light reactions of photosynthesis; used in dark reactions for CO2 reduction.

key_concepts: Physical separation: grana (light + H-acceptor, no CO2) → ATP and NADPH2; stroma (ATP/NADPH2 + CO2, no light) → carbohydrates
Indefinitionittent light: faster photosynthesis than continuous light because dark reaction fully consumes accumulated assimilatory powers
Q10 > 2 for dark reaction; Q10 = 1 for light (photochemical) reaction
Dye used by Hill for Hill reaction: Dichlorophenol indophenol

subtopics.definition_content: Emission of radiation when electron at excited singlet state returns to ground state; temperature independent.
Loss of energy as radiation when electron at triplet state returns to ground state; electron first had its spin reversed at excited singlet state and became trapped at triplet state.
Number of O2 molecules evolved per quantum of light absorbed in photosynthesis; quantum requirement = 8 quanta per O2 molecule; quantum yield = 1/8 = 0.125 (approximately 12%).

topics.name: Emerson effect and Red drop

subtopics.definition_content: Decrease in quantum yield of photosynthesis at far red end of spectrum (680 nm or longer); observed by R. Emerson and C.M. Lewis (1943).

Increase in photosynthetic efficiency (quantum yield) when light of short wavelengths (<680 nm) and long wavelengths (>680 nm) are given simultaneously; quantum yield in combined beam is greater than sum of individual beams.

key_concepts: Shows existence of two distinct photochemical processes associated with two different specific groups of pigments

topics.name: Two pigment systems

subtopics.definition_content: Pigment system with reaction centre P700; important pigments: chlorophyll a 670, 683, 695, P700; lies on outer surface of thylakoids; absorbs both wavelengths shorter and longer than 680 nm; involved in both cyclic and non-cyclic photophosphorylation; generates strong reductant NADPH.

Pigment system with reaction centre P680; main pigments: chlorophyll a 673, P680, chlorophyll b and phycobilins; lies on inner surface of thylakoid; absorbs wavelengths shorter than 680 nm only; involved only in non-cyclic photophosphorylation; produces strong oxidant forming O2 from water.

key_concepts: PS I: outer surface; reaction centre P700; no O2 evolved; participates in cyclic and non-cyclic photophosphorylation; receives electrons from PS II
PS II: inner surface; reaction centre P680; O2 evolved from photolysis of water; only non-cyclic photophosphorylation; receives electrons from photolytic dissociation of water

topics.name: Photophosphorylation

subtopics.definition_content: Formation of high energy phosphates (ATP) during light reaction of photosynthesis; demonstrated by Arnon et al. (1954).
Photophosphorylation involving only PS I; electron flow is cyclic (returns to P700); produces 2 ATP at two steps; no NADPH or O2 produced; dominant in bacteria; occurs when NADP is unavailable.

Photophosphorylation involving both PS I and PS II; electron flow is unidirectional; electrons from P680 do not return to P680; produces ATP (1 molecule at PQ to cytochrome b6-f step) and NADPH; H2O utilized and O2 evolved; dominant in green plants; also called Z-scheme.
Shape of path of electron flow in non-cyclic photophosphorylation; given by Hill and Bendall (1960); inhibited by CMU and DCMU.

Oxygen-dependent FMN-catalysed photophosphorylation demonstrated by Arnon et al. (1954); produces ATP from ADP + iP in absence of CO2 and NADP in presence of FMN or vitamin K and oxygen; named by Arnon.

key_concepts: Cyclic: P700 activated → primary acceptor → ferredoxin → plastoquinone → cytochrome complex → plastocyanin → P700 (2 ATP synthesised)
Non-cyclic: P680 → pheophytin → plastoquinone → cytochrome b6-f complex → plastocyanin → P700; plastocyanin is Cu-containing pigment
DCMU (herbicide): inhibits CO2 fixation by strongly inhibiting PS II

topics.name: ATP synthesis in chloroplast

subtopics.definition_content: Mechanism explaining ATP synthesis in chloroplast; ATP synthesis linked to development of proton gradient across thylakoid membrane; protons accumulate in thylakoid lumen; CF0-CF1 ATP synthase uses proton gradient (broken down through CF0 channel) to synthesize ATP.

ATP synthase consisting of CF0 (transmembrane channel embedded in thylakoid membrane; carries out facilitated diffusion of protons) and CF1 (protrudes on outer/stroma surface; synthesises ATP via conformational change when proton gradient breaks down).

key_concepts: Proton gradient created by: (a) splitting of water on lumen side releases H+ into lumen; (b) plastoquinone transports protons from stroma to lumen while transferring electrons; (c) NADP reductase on stroma side removes protons from stroma for NADPH formation
Proton gradient: increase in lumen, decrease in stroma → measurable decrease in pH in lumen
Chemiosmosis requires: membrane, proton pump, proton gradient, ATP synthase

topics.name: Calvin cycle

subtopics.definition_content: Photosynthetic carbon reduction (PCR) cycle discovered by Calvin and Benson; called C3 cycle because first stable product is 3-phosphoglyceric acid (3-PGA), a 3-carbon compound; CO2 acceptor is RuBP (Ribulose 1,5-biphosphate); catalysed by Rubisco; occurs in stroma. Techniques used: radioactive tracer (14C), chromatography, autoradiography; material: Chlorella and Scenedesmus.
CO2 acceptor molecule in Calvin cycle; reacts with CO2 catalysed by RuBP-carboxylase/oxygenase (Rubisco) to form 3-PGA; regenerated at end of cycle.
Key enzyme catalysing covalent bonding of CO2 to RuBP in Calvin cycle; constitutes 16% of chloroplast protein; most abundant protein on the planet.

Plants in which CO2 reduction occurs via Calvin (C3) cycle; first stable product is 3-PGA (3-carbon); ~85% of plant species; include cereals (barley, rice, oat, wheat), groundnut, sugarbeet, cotton, tobacco, spinach, soybean.

key_concepts: Three distinct phases: Carboxylation, Glycolytic reversal, Regeneration of RuBP
6 turns of cycle to produce net gain of 6 carbons (hexose/glucose)
ATP and NADPH requirement: 18 ATP and 12 NADPH2 per mole of hexose sugar
Enzymes: Phosphopentokinase, Carboxydismutase (Rubisco), Phosphoglyceric kinase, Triose phosphate dehydrogenase, Phosphotriose isomerase, Aldolase, Phosphatase

topics.name: Hatch and Slack cycle (C4 cycle)

subtopics.definition_content: Pathway of CO2 fixation where first stable product is oxaloacetic acid (OAA), a 4-carbon compound; detailed by M.D. Hatch and C.R. Slack (1966); first observed by Kortschak and Hart in sugarcane; also called dicarboxylic acid cycle (DCA-cycle).

Plants whose first stable product of CO2 fixation is a 4-carbon dicarboxylic acid (OAA); have Kranz type leaf anatomy; examples: sugarcane, maize, Sorghum, Cyperus rotundus, Digitaria brownii, Amaranthus; better photosynthesizers with no photorespiration; true xerophytic plants.

German definition meaning halo or wreath; leaf anatomy in C4 plants where vascular bundles are surrounded by a layer of bundle sheath cells containing large numbers of dimorphic chloroplasts; mesophyll and bundle sheath cells connected by plasmodesmata.
CO2 acceptor molecule in C4 plants (mesophyll chloroplasts); reacts with CO2 via PEP carboxylase (PEPCO) to form oxaloacetic acid (OAA).

Two morphologically distinct types of chloroplasts in C4 leaves: bundle sheath chloroplasts (larger, centripetal arrangement, contain starch grains, lack grana/agranal) and mesophyll chloroplasts (normal type).

key_concepts: Two carboxylation reactions: first in mesophyll chloroplast (CO2 + PEP → OAA); second in bundle sheath (CO2 + RuBP via Calvin cycle)
OAA → malic acid (4-C) → transported to bundle sheath → decarboxylation releases CO2 → Calvin cycle; pyruvic acid returns to mesophyll to regenerate PEP
Mesophyll cells: C4 cycle; Bundle sheath cells: C3 cycle
30 ATP and 12 NADPH2 per hexose molecule (vs 18 ATP + 12 NADPH2 in C3)
Key enzyme PEPCO in mesophyll; RuBP carboxylase in bundle sheath
No photorespiration in C4 plants

topics.name: Characteristics of C4 cycle
key_concepts: Greater rate of CO2 assimilation than C3; PEP carboxylase has high affinity for CO2
Little photorespiration as compared to C3 plants; higher dry matter production
More adapted to environmental stresses (hot and dry climate)
CO2 fixation by C4 requires more ATP than C3; additional ATP for pyruvic acid to PEP conversion

topics.name: Crassulacean acid metabolism (CAM)

subtopics.definition_content: Dark CO2 fixation pathway proposed by Ting (1971); operates in succulent plants (Cactus, Sedum, Kalanchoe, Opuntia, Agave, Orchid, Pineapple, Bryophyllum); stomata open at night (scotoactive) to fix CO2 as malic acid; malic acid used in Calvin cycle during day; first discovered in crassulacean plants (e.g., Bryophyllum).
Stomata that open at night and close during day in succulent plants to avoid water loss while allowing CO2 uptake.
Formation of malic acid during dark in CAM plants; CO2 stored as malic acid in vacuoles in presence of PEP carboxylase.
Release of CO2 from malic acid during day in CAM plants for actual photosynthesis (Calvin cycle); malic acid decarboxylated, releasing CO2.

key_concepts: CAM plants have enzymes of both C3 and C4 cycle in mesophyll cells (no structural compartmentation)
pH decreases during night (acidification) and increases during day (deacidification)
Can fix CO2 lost in respiration

subtopics.definition_content: Under saturating light, the CO2 concentration at which rate of photosynthesis equals rate of respiration (apparent photosynthesis is nil); low in C4 plants (0–5 ppm) and high in C3 plants (25–100 ppm).

topics.name: Biochemical mechanism

subtopics.definition_content: Uptake of O2 and release of CO2 in light resulting from biosynthesis of glycolate in chloroplasts and subsequent metabolism of glycolate (glycolate metabolism); defined by Krotkov (1963) as extra O2 input and extra CO2 release by green plants in light; reported by Decker and Tio (1959) in tobacco; also called C2 cycle.

key_concepts: Biochemical steps: RuBP + O2 → 2-phosphoglycolic acid + 3-PGA; phosphoglycolic acid → glycolic acid; glycolic acid + O2 → glyoxylic acid + H2O2; H2O2 → H2O + O2 (catalase); glyoxylic acid + glutamic acid → glycine; 2 glycine → serine + CO2 + NH3; serine → hydroxypyruvic acid → glyceric acid → 3-PGA
No ATP or NADH produced; up to half of photosynthetically fixed CO2 may be lost
Involves chloroplasts, peroxisomes and mitochondria
Enhanced by: bright light, high temperature, high O2, low CO2 concentration
Only in C3 plants (high CO2 compensation point); absent in C4 plants (Kranz anatomy keeps high CO2 in bundle sheath)
Inhibitors: hydroxy sulphonates inhibit glycolic acid oxidase

topics.name: The photosynthetic bacteria fall under three categories

subtopics.definition_content: Photosynthesis in purple and green sulphur bacteria synthesizing organic food in presence of light but absence of O2; non-oxygenic; anaerobic; uses H2S (not H2O) as hydrogen donor; only one pigment system (PS I) except cyanobacteria; first described by Van Niel.
Simple structures in photosynthetic bacteria containing pigments (definition coined by Schmitz); equivalent to chloroplasts; contain bacteriochlorophyll or bacterioviridin.
Photosynthetic pigment in purple photosynthetic bacteria; differs from chlorophyll a in having one pyrrole ring with two hydrogen atoms.

key_concepts: Green sulphur bacteria: contain chlorobium chlorophyll; absorb 720–750 nm (far red); e.g., Chlorobium
Purple sulphur bacteria: e.g., Chromatium
Purple non-sulphur bacteria: e.g., Rhodospirillum, Rhodopseudomonas
Photoreductant: NADH2 (not NADPH2)
Reaction centre: P890
Use longer wavelengths (720–950 nm); cyclic photophosphorylation dominant
No definite chloroplasts; no O2 evolution; one photoact, one pigment system

topics.name: Types of chemosynthetic bacteria

subtopics.definition_content: Process of carbohydrate formation in which chemoautotrophic bacteria use chemical reactions to obtain energy from inorganic compounds (no light required); synthesize organic cell requirements from CO2, H2O and salts at the expense of oxidation of inorganic substances like H2, NO3-, SO4 or carbonate.

key_concepts: Nitrifying bacteria: e.g., Nitrosomonas, Nitrosococcus, Nitrobacter
Sulphur bacteria: e.g., Beggiatoa, Thiothrix, Thiobacillus
Iron bacteria: e.g., Ferrobacillus, Leptothrix, Cladothrix
Hydrogen bacteria: e.g., Bacillus pentotrophus
Carbon bacteria: e.g., Carboxydomonas, Bacillus oligocarbophilus

topics.name: Blackman's law of limiting factors

subtopics.definition_content: F.F. Blackman (1905): when a process is conditioned as to its rapidity by a number of factors, the rate of the process is limited by the pace of the slowest factor; CO2 is usually a limiting factor in photosynthesis under field conditions on clear summer days.
The value of light intensity at which further increase is not accompanied by an increase in CO2 uptake.

key_concepts: Modification of Liebig's law of minimum
Theory of three cardinal points by Sachs (1860): minimum, optimum, maximum for each factor

topics.name: External factors

subtopics.definition_content: Inhibition of photosynthesis by increased O2 concentration due to competitive inhibition of RuBP-carboxylase by O2 at active sites (O2 competes with CO2 for active sites of Rubisco); reported by Warburg (1920) in Chlorella algae.
Photo-oxidation of different cellular components including chlorophyll occurring at very high light intensities; causes decline in rate of photosynthesis at very high light intensity.
Photochemical smog product formed from reaction of oxides of nitrogen and hydrocarbons in smoke; inhibits Hill reaction.

key_concepts: Light: 80% of total light falling on leaf absorbed; 10% reflected; 10% transmitted; max photosynthesis at blue and red wavelengths
Temperature: optimum 20–35°C; denatures enzymes at high temperature; some conifers photosynthesize at -35°C; hot spring algae at 75°C
CO2: ~0.032% in atmosphere; usually limiting factor; increases photosynthesis up to 1%; very high concentrations toxic
Water: rarely limiting; <1% absorbed water used in photosynthesis
O2: ~21% in atmosphere; not limiting factor; excess inhibits via Warburg effect
Minerals: Mn++ and Cl- essential for light reactions (photolysis of water/O2 evolution); Mg++, Cu++, Fe++ important for chlorophyll synthesis

topics.name: Internal factors

subtopics.definition_content: Amount of CO2 fixed by a gram of chlorophyll in an hour; usually constant for a plant species.

key_concepts: Protoplasmic factors: unknown factor affecting dark reactions; decline at >30°C or strong light suggests enzymatic nature
Chlorophyll content: essential internal factor; variegated variety has higher assimilation number
Accumulation of end products in chloroplasts reduces rate of photosynthesis
Structure of leaves: stomata size/position/behaviour, intercellular spaces, cuticle thickness, epidermis, mesophyll tissue influence photosynthesis

subtopics.definition_content: an enzyme controlled process of biological oxidation of food materials in a living cell, using molecular O₂, producing CO₂ and H₂O, and releasing energy in small steps and storing it in biologically useful forms, generally ATP
CO₂ intake in photosynthesis balanced with CO₂ release in respiration

a stepped process by which one molecule of glucose (6C) breaks into two molecules of pyruvic acid (3C); discovered by Embden, Meyerhof and Parnas in 1930; takes place in the cytoplasm and does not use oxygen; also called EMP pathway

topics.name: Aerobic Oxidation of Pyruvic Acid (Oxidative Decarboxylation)

subtopics.definition_content: gateway step or link reaction between glycolysis and Kreb's cycle; each 3-carbon pyruvate molecule enters the mitochondrial matrix where its oxidation is completed by aerobic means; cofactors include Mg²⁺, thiamine pyrophosphate (Vit B₁), NAD⁺, CoA and lipoic acid

topics.name: Kreb's Cycle / TCA Cycle / Citric Acid Cycle

subtopics.definition_content: also called TCA cycle (tricarboxylic acid cycle) or citric acid cycle; takes place in the mitochondrial matrix; discovered by Sir Hans Krebs in 1937 who won Noble Prize in 1953; one mole of acetyl CoA gives 12 ATP during oxidation; succinate dehydrogenase is the mitochondrial marker enzyme located in the inner mitochondrial membrane

a nucleotide consisting of adenine (purine base), ribose (5C sugar), and three inorganic phosphate groups; serves as the energy currency of the cell; the bonds attaching the last two phosphate groups are high energy bonds containing more than twice the energy of an average chemical bond; discovered by Karl Lohmann (1929)

the addition of phosphate group to ADP and AMP; energy required for bond formation is equal to the energy released in hydrolysis; oxysomes act as the unit of phosphorylation in respiration and are present in cristae of mitochondria

called universal hydrogen acceptor; produced during aerobic respiration (glycolysis and Kreb's cycle) and also in anaerobic respiration; work as coenzyme in ATP generation via electron transport system; NADP has one additional phosphate compared to NAD; formerly called Coenzyme II or Triphosphopyridine nucleotide (TPN); nicotinamide is a vitamin of B group

also called electron transport chain (ETC) or cytochrome system; major source of cell energy; located in the inner membrane of mitochondria; consists of nine electron-carriers in specific sequence: NAD, FMN, FAD, Coenzyme-Q (ubiquinone), Cytochrome-b, Cytochrome-c₁, Cytochrome-c, Cytochrome-a, and Cytochrome-a₃

the process of ATP synthesis during oxidation of reduced coenzymes in ETC; ATP is synthesized by phosphorylation of ADP in the presence of enzyme ATP synthetase present in the head of F₁-particles on mitochondrial cristae

proposed by Peter Mitchell (1961), Nobel prize 1978; ATP synthesis occurs due to H⁺-flow through a membrane; involves development of proton gradient across inner mitochondrial membrane (proton motive force = proton gradient ΔpH + electrical potential Δψ) and proton flow back through F₀-F₁ ATPase

topics.name: Role of Shuttle System in Energy Production

subtopics.definition_content: the exchange of respiratory gases (O₂ and CO₂) between an organism and its environment
Exchange of respiratory gases between tissue and extra cellular environment
an enzymatically-controlled stepped chemical process in which glucose is oxidised inside the mitochondria to produce energy-rich ATP molecules with high-energy bonds
high energy compounds oxidised in respiration, which may include carbohydrates, fats and protein; also called respiratory fuel
respiration using carbohydrate and fat as respiratory substrate (Blackmann)
respiration in which protein is used as respiratory substrate when carbohydrates and fats are absent
organisms that can respire only in the presence of oxygen; oxygen is essential for their survival
organisms that usually respire aerobically but under certain conditions may also respire anaerobically (e.g., Yeast, parasites of the alimentary canal)
organisms that normally respire anaerobically as their major ATP-yielding process; killed in the presence of substantial amount of oxygen (e.g., Clostridium botulinum and C. tetani)
primarily anaerobic organisms that under certain conditions may also respire aerobically

alternative glycolytic pathway also called glycolysis of bacteria; discovered by Entner and Doudoroff; used by certain bacteria lacking phosphofructokinase enzyme such as Pseudomonas saccharophila, P. fluorescens, P. lindeneri, and P. averaginosa

also called Hexose monophosphate shunt, Warburg Dickens pathway, or direct oxidation pathway; provides an alternative pathway for breakdown of glucose independent of EMP pathway and Krebs cycle; existence suggested by Warburg et al. (1935) and Dickens (1938); reactions described by Horecker et al. (1951) and Racker (1954); takes place in the cytoplasm; the only pathway of carbohydrate oxidation that gives NADPH₂

alternate ETS pathway widespread in higher plant tissues; a branch point in ETS precedes the highly cyanide-sensitive cytochromes; allows continued oxidation of NADH and operation of tricarboxylic acid cycle even when cyanide blocks electron flow from Cyt a₃ to oxygen; significant in respiratory climacteric of ripening fruits

an enzyme-controlled, partial breakdown of organic compounds without using oxygen and releasing only a fraction of the energy; first studied by Kostychev (1902); also called intramolecular respiration (Pfluger, 1875); completed in cytoplasm; occurs in roots of waterlogged plants, parasitic worms, animal muscle, and some microorganisms

the inhibition of sugar breakdown due to the presence of oxygen under aerobic condition; Dixon (1937) stated that it is the action of oxygen in checking the high rate of loss of carbohydrate and in suppressing or diminishing the accumulation of products of fermentation

a kind of anaerobic respiration carried out by microorganisms fungi and bacteria; named after its major product (e.g., alcohol fermentation, lactic acid fermentation); types include butyric acid fermentation (Clostridium butyricum), lactic acid fermentation (lactic acid bacteria and muscles), and acetic acid fermentation (acetic acid bacteria)

the ratio of the volume of CO₂ released to the volume of oxygen taken in respiration; R.Q. = Volume of CO₂ evolved / Volume of O₂ absorbed; usually measured by Ganong's respirometer; value at compensation point is zero
A vital process which brings about irreversible permanent change in any plant or its part with respect to its size, form, weight and volume.

Meristems found at shoot and root apex. As a result of activity of these meristems plant increases in length. In angiosperms and gymnosperms there is a group of meristematic cells but in bryophytes and pteridophytes there is a single tetrahedral cell at the shoot apex.
Meristems found above the nodes. As a result of the activity of these meristems increase in length takes place. e.g., Bambusa.

Meristems made up of cells which divide in radial direction only, forming laterally placed new cells towards centre and periphery. Cork cambium (phellogen) and vascular cambium are examples. Increase in girth of shoots and roots take place because of the activity of this cambium.

topics.name: Phases of Growth and Growth Curve

subtopics.definition_content: Time in which growth takes place. definition coined by Sachs. The log phase is referred to as grand period of growth.
The mathematical curve which represents variation in growth rate is somewhat flattened S-shaped curve or sigmoid curve.
First phase of growth curve where the rate of growth is very slow. More time is needed for little growth in this phase.
Second phase of growth curve where growth rate becomes maximum and more rapid. Physiological activities of cells are at their maximum. Also referred to as grand period of growth.
Final phase of growth where nutrients become limiting, growth slows down, and physiological activities also slow down. Indicates maturity of growth system.
The rate of growth measured by an increase in size or area of an organ of plant like leaf, flower, fruit etc.
Instrument devised to measure the growth in length of a plant. Types include Arch auxanometer and Pfeffer's auxanometer (Automatic auxanometer).
Instrument invented by Sir Jagdish Chandra Bose that gives magnification up to 10,000 times for measuring plant growth.
In the absence of light, etiolin compounds are formed which gives yellow colour to the plant. The phenomenon is called etiolation.
The remarkable effect of duration of light on the growth of vegetative as well as reproductive structures. The induction and suppression of flowering are dependent on duration.
Carbohydrate/Nitrogen ratio that dedefinitionines the type of growth. High C/N ratio stimulates wall thickening. Low C/N ratio favours more protoplasm producing thin walled soft cells.

Cells derived from root apical and shoot-apical meristems and cambium differentiate and mature to perform specific functions. This act leading to maturation is definitioned as differentiation. e.g., xylem tracheary elements develop strong lignocellulosic secondary cell walls.

The living differentiated cells that have lost the capacity to divide can regain the capacity of division under certain conditions. This phenomenon is definitioned as dedifferentiation. e.g., formation of interfascicular cambium and cork cambium from fully differentiated parenchyma cells.
Meristems/tissues produced during dedifferentiation divide and produce cells that once again lose the capacity to divide but mature to perform specific functions.

Organic substances which are synthesized in minute quantities in one part of the plant body and transported to another part where they influence specific physiological processes. definition given by Thimann (1948).

Weakly acidic growth hormones having an unsaturated ring structure and capable of promoting cell elongation, especially of shoots at a concentration of less than 100 ppm which is inhibitory to the roots. First discovered among plant growth regulators.
Best known and universal auxin (heteroauxin). Found in all plants and fungi. First naturally occurring auxin; first collected by Went (1928) from coleoptile tip of Avena.
First naturally occurring auxin isolated by Kogl and Haagen-Smith (1931) from human urine. Molecular formula C18H32O5.
Auxin obtained by Kogl, Haagen-Smith and Erxleben in 1934 from corn germ oil. Molecular formula C18H30O4.

In many plants, the apical bud grows and the lower axillary buds are suppressed. The auxin (IAA) of the definitioninal bud inhibits the growth of lateral buds. This phenomenon is known as apical dominance.
Development of fruit without fertilization. Can be induced by application of IAA in paste form to the stigma of a flower or by spraying flowers with dilute solution of IAA.
2,4-dichlorophenoxy acetic acid - a synthetic auxin used as weedicide. Can destroy broad-leaved weeds but does not affect mature monocotyledonous plants.
Both natural and synthetic auxin. Gives very good results for development of roots on cut ends of stem cuttings.
Compounds that inhibit action of auxin and compete with auxins for active sites. e.g., PCIB (p-chlorophenoxy isobutyric acid), TIBA (2,3,5-tri iodobenzoic acid). TIBA is used in picking cotton balls.
Bioassay for auxins developed by F.W. Went (1928) demonstrating the effect of auxins on plant growth by experiments with oat (Avena sativa) coleoptile.
The chemical involved in healing of injury. Auxin induced division in cells around the injured area.
Weakly acidic hormones having gibbane ring structure which cause cell elongation of intact plants in general and increased internodal length of genetically dwarfed plants (corn, pea) in particular.
Disease of rice plants caused by fungus Gibberella fujikuroi (Fusarium moniliforme). Gibberellins were first isolated from this fungus by Kurosawa in 1926.
Active substance isolated from Gibberella fujikuroi by Yabuta and Sumiki and coworkers (1939) in Tokyo. Probably a mixture of several gibberellins. First gibberellin obtained was Gibberellin A-3.

Gibberellins induce stem elongation in rosette plants (e.g., cabbage, henbane) just prior to the reproductive phase, causing internodes to elongate enormously resulting in marked increase in stem height. Needs long days or cold nights.
Compounds including malic hydrazide, phosphon D, Alar and chlorocholine chloride (CCC) or cycocel. Also called antiretardants (stimulate flowering and inhibit growth of nodes).
Plant growth hormones which are basic in nature, either aminopurine or phenyl urea derivatives that promote cell division (cytokinesis) either alone or in conjugation with auxin.
First cytokinin discovered by Miller, Skoog and Strong (1955). Synthetic product formed by autoclaving Herring sperm (fish sperm) DNA. Identified as 6-furfuryl amino-purine.
First natural cytokinin discovered by Letham et al. (1964) in unripe maize grain (Zea mays). Named zeatin (6 hydroxy 3 methyl trans 2-butenyl amino purine).

Cytokinin delays the senescence (ageing) of leaves and other organs by controlling protein synthesis and mobilization of resources (disappearance of chlorophyll). Reported by Richmond and Lang (1957) working on detached leaves of Xanthium.
Cytokinins in coconut milk. Coconut milk and apple fruit extract are rich in cytokinins.
A combination of cytokinin (6-benzladene) and gibberellin (GA4, GA7) particularly effective in increasing apple size.

A gaseous hormone which stimulates transverse growth but retards the longitudinal one. Produced in plants from amino acid methionine. Synthesized in roots, leaves, flowers, fruits, seeds; more in nodal regions.
Commercial product (2-chloroethyl phosphoric acid) from which ethylene gas is released. Used for artificial ripening of fruits.

Bioassay principle for ethylene. Three characteristic effects on etiolated seedlings of pea: swelling of nodes, inhibition of elongation of internodes of stem, and induction of horizontal growth of stem against gravity.
When a fruit ripens, its respiration rate gradually decreases, but when it is reversed by a sharp increase, it is called climacteric. Maximum synthesis of ethylene occurs during climacteric ripening.
More growth on upper surface of leaf than on lower surface. Said to be controlled by ethylene in many plants.

A mildly acidic growth hormone functioning as a general growth inhibitor by counteracting other hormones (auxin, gibberellins, cytokinins). First isolated by Addicott et al. (1963) from cotton balls as abscisin II. Chemically it is dextro-rotatory cis sesquiterpene.
ABA is also called stress hormone because its synthesis is stimulated by drought, water logging and other adverse environmental conditions.
ABA is also called dormin because it acts as growth inhibitor and induces dormancy of buds towards the approach of winter. First named dormin by Wareing and Cornforth.
ABA can be used as antitranspirant. Application of minute quantity of ABA to leaves reduces transpiration through partial closure of stomata.
Wound hormone reported by Haberlandt (1913). Isolated and crystallized by English et al. (1939). Chemically traumatic acid stimulates adjacent cells to divide rapidly to heal the wound.

Synthetic growth regulators that act in variety of ways on the natural regulation mechanisms of plants. Generally growth inhibitors. Contain fluorene ring in their structure. Transported non-polarly (both basi- and acropetally).
Ubiquitous in plants. Help regulating plant growth, development, participate in leaf senescence and defense mechanism against fungi.

Natural formative growth hormones essential for the effect of auxin on root, stem and leaf growth. Include: rhizocaline (root forming hormone, produced by leaves), caulocaline (stem forming hormone, produced by roots), phyllocaline (leaf forming hormone, produced by cotyledons).

The effects of photoperiods or daily duration of light periods (and dark periods) on the growth and development of plants, especially flowering. First demonstrated by W.W. Garner and H.A. Allard (1920).

Plants that initiate flowering when the day length (photoperiod) becomes shorter than a certain critical period. Most winter flowering plants. e.g., cocklebur (Xanthium), Chrysanthemum, sugarcane, tobacco (Mutant Maryland mammoth), soybean, strawberry, Dahlia.
Plants that begin flowering when the day length exceeds a critical length. e.g., spinach (Spinacea oleracea), henbane (Hyoscymus niger), radish, sugarbeet, wheat, lettuce, poppy, larkspur, maize.
Plants that can flower in all possible photoperiods throughout the year. e.g., cucumber, cotton, sunflower, tomato, some varieties of pea.
Plants that flower only under day lengths within a certain range (usually 12-16 hours of light) but fail to flower under either longer or shorter photoperiods. e.g., Mikania scandens.

That continuous duration of light which must not be exceeded in short day plants and should always be exceeded in long day plants in order to bring them to flower. The critical day length for Xanthium (SDP) is 5-6 hours; for Hyoscymus niger (LDP) is about 11 hours.

Observation that the critical factor for flowering is the duration of dark period, not just light period. Short day plants require a long night or critical dark period. SDP is also called long night plant; LDP is called short night plant.
In night interruption experiments, when short day plants were exposed to a flash of light before achieving a critical dark period, flowering was prevented.

Chemical that perceives the photoperiodic stimulus in leaves. Exists in two interconvertible forms: Pr (red absorbing, 660nm) and Pfr (far red absorbing, 730nm). Pfr is the biologically active form. Isolated by Butter et al. (1959). Located in plasma membrane.

Flowering hormone proposed by Chailakhyan (1936). Produced in leaves when proper amount of light is perceived and passes to shoot apex causing its differentiation into flowering shoot. Consists of gibberellins (necessary for stem growth) and anthesins (necessary for flower formation).

The effect of light in reversing etiolation. Light acts to promote expansion of leaves and inhibit elongation of internodes. Independent of the direction of light. Plants are most sensitive to red light.

The method of inducing early flowering in plants by pretreatment of their seeds at low temperatures. definition coined by Russian agronomist Lysenko (1929-30). Defined by Chourad (1960) as acquisition or acceleration of ability to flower by chilling treatment.
If the chilling treatment is immediately followed by high temperature (about 40°C), the effect of vernalization is lost. This phenomenon is called de-vernalization.
The name given to the vernalization stimulus (reported by Mechlers). The stimulus is believed to be a particular gibberellin or mixture of gibberellins.
First stage of vernalization process. Germinating seeds are treated with 0-5°C in presence of oxygen and slight moisture. The seed dormancy is broken.
Second stage of vernalization, essential to initiate the reproductive phase. After vernalization, plants must be subjected to a correct photoperiod in order to produce flowers.
The latter part of the developmental process, which leads from maturity to the ultimate complete loss of organization and function. The study of plant senescence is called phytogerontology.

Found in monocarpic plants which flower and fruit only once in their life cycle. The plant dies soon after ripening of seeds. e.g., rice, wheat, gram, mustard (annuals); cabbage, henbane (biennials); certain bamboos (perennials).

Found in certain perennial plants which possess underground perennating structures like rhizomes, bulbs, corm. The above ground shoot dies each year after flowering and fruiting, but underground part survives. e.g., banana, gladiolus, ginger.
Found in many evergreen perennial plants where tips remain meristematic and produce new buds and leaves while older leaves and lateral branches show senescence. e.g., Eucalyptus, Pinus, Mango.
Found in temperate deciduous trees that shed all leaves in autumn and develop new leaves in spring. e.g., Dalbergia, Elm, Mulberry, Poplar.

The process of shedding of leaves, fruits or flowers by a plant. Shedding takes place by formation of a special layer of cells called abscission layer within the region of attachment. Middle lamella is digested by polysaccharide hydrolyzing enzymes such as cellulase and pectinases.
A special layer of cells formed within the region of attachment of leaves, fruits or flowers. Its formation is promoted by ABA and ethylene, and inhibited by auxin.
The sum total of all the processes related with the conversion of the raw foodstuff into the stuff of the body to supply energy for different metabolic activities and also for the repair and growth.
The process by which complex food is converted into simplest food with the help of digestive enzymes (Hydrolytic enzymes) is called digestion. Hence process of digestion is a hydrolytic process.
When the process of digestion occurs within the cell in the food vacuole. Examples: Protozoa, Porifera, Coelenterata and free living platyhelminthes.
When the process of digestion occurs outside the cell. Examples: Coelenterates and phylum platyhelminthes to phylum chordata.
Ectodermal part of alimentary canal. It includes buccal cavity / oral cavity, pharynx, oesophagus, stomach and small part of duodenum.
Endodermal part of alimentary canal. It includes small intestine and large intestine.
Ectodermal part of alimentary canal. It includes anal canal and anus.
A narrow space between lips and gums in front and gums and cheeks on the sides. Its lining contains mucous glands.
When all the teeth are structurally and functionally similar. Examples: Vertebrates except metatherian and eutherian mammals.
When the teeth are different in structure and functions. They are distinguished into four types: incisors, canines, premolars and molars. Examples: metatherian and eutherian mammals.

Teeth have well developed roots implanted in deep individual pits or sockets called alveoli or theca, in the jaw bone. Characteristic of mammals. Also present in crocodilians and fossil toothed bird (Archaeopteryx).
Teeth are attached to the free surface or summit of the jaw bone, as in a shark or frog. Such teeth are apt to break off easily but are replaced.
Teeth are attached to the inner side of jaw bone by their base as well as one side. Common in urodeles and lizards.
In lower vertebrates, teeth can be replaced an indefinite number of times during life. e.g., Fishes, amphibia, reptilia.
In most mammals teeth develop during life in two successive sets. Teeth of the first set are known as deciduous teeth or milk teeth or lacteal teeth whereas the second set is called permanent teeth.
In some mammals such as platypus, marsupials, moles, sirenians, toothed whale etc. only one set of teeth develops, known as monophyodont condition.
Secreted by Ameloblast/Enameloblast cells, forms the oudefinitionost covering of teeth. It is ectodermal and made up of 92% of inorganic substances, hence considered as hardest part of the body.
A layer of inorganic substances (62-69%) which surrounds the odontoblast cells. It is mesodermal in origin and forms bulk of tooth in a mammal.
Oval, rounded masses of lymphatic tissue present in between lamina propria and epithelial lining of small intestine. They produce lymphocytes and phagocytose harmful bacteria.
Multicellular mucous glands present in the submucosa of duodenum only. They secrete mucous.
Known as intestinal glands. Found in duodenum and ileum only. Secrete succus entericus i.e. intestinal juice. Formed by folding of lamina propria.
Posterior end of caecum present as a blind sac in abdominal cavity. Vermiform appendix is vestigial but contains lymphatic tissue.
Dilated sac-like or pocket-like structures surrounding taeniae coli present in colon.
The secretion of salivary glands. Amount: 1.0-1.5 litre/day. Slightly acidic. pH: 6.3-6.8. Chemical composition: Water (99.5%), mucous (lubricant), salts (NaCl, NaHCO3), enzymes (ptyalin, lysozyme).

Secretion of gastric glands. Amount: 2-3 litres/day. Highly acidic. pH: 1.0-3.5 (due to HCl). Contains water (99%), mucous, inorganic salts, castle's intrinsic factor, HCl (0.5%) and enzymes prorennin, pepsinogen and gastric lipase.

In some humans, secretion of lactase decreases or ceases with age. Lactose intolerant persons fail to digest lactose of milk. In their large intestine, lactose is fermented by bacteria, producing gases and acids.

Secretion of intestinal glands (crypts of Lieberkuhn and Brunner's glands). Amount: 1.5-2.0 l/day. pH: 7.6-8.3 (alkaline). Control: nervous and hormonal (Enterocrinin, Duocrinin). Contains water (99%), mucous, inorganic salts, enzymes.
