The major part (about 99%) of pancreas. Consists of rounded lobules (acini) that secrete alkaline pancreatic juice carried by the main pancreatic duct (duct of Wirsung / duct of Santorini) into the duodenum through hepatopancreatic ampulla.

The endocrine part (1%) of pancreas scattered in the exocrine part. Consists of alpha(A) cells (glucagon), beta(B) cells (insulin), delta(D) cells (somatostatin) and F or PP cells (pancreatic polypeptide).

Secreted by exocrine pancreas. Amount: 1-1.5 l/day. pH: 7.1-8.2 (alkaline). Contains water (99%), enzymes and salts. Called 'complete digestive juice' as its protease enzymes can break all types of peptide bonds.

The basic structural and functional unit of the liver. Composed of plates of polyhedral, glycogen-rich cells (hepatocytes) arranged radially around a central vein with radial blood sinusoids between the plates.
Phagocytic cells lining the sinusoids of liver that eat bacteria, worn-out blood elements and foreign particles.

Secreted by hepatic cells; stored in gall bladder. Amount: 800-1000 ml daily. Colour: Greenish-blue. pH: 7.6-8.6 (alkaline). Contains bile salts (Na taurocholate and Na glycocholate), bile pigments (biliverdin and bilirubin), cholesterol, mucin, lecithin and fats.
Excess quantities of carbohydrates (glucose) are converted to glycogen in the presence of insulin in the liver cells, and stored therein.
Glycogen is a reserve food material which is changed into glucose and released into the blood at concentrations maintained constant by the liver.
Under abnormal conditions, liver can convert proteins and fats into glucose by complex chemical reactions. Formation of this 'new sugar' from non-carbohydrate sources is called gluconeogenesis.
If the level of blood-glucose rises beyond normal even after glycogenesis and catabolism, the excess glucose is converted into fat and stored in the liver. The process is definitioned lipogenesis.
It is the intake of food. Most animals capture the prey/food with the help of mouth or tongue.
The process occurs in the buccopharyngeal cavity of mammals with the help of teeth. During this process food is broken down into small pieces, which increases its surface area.

The passage of food from buccopharyngeal cavity to oesophagus/stomach. In mammals bolus of the masticated food is formed in buccopharyngeal cavity which easily slides into oesophagus. It is a voluntary reflex mechanism.

Alternative contraction and relaxation of circular and longitudinal muscles produces the wave of contraction due to which the food passes from front to backward direction in the lumen of alimentary canal.
The peristaltic wave occurring in the reverse direction. It occurs in alimentary canal and results in vomiting. The phenomenon is called Regurgitation.
The lymph reaching the thoracic duct from the intestines contains an excess of fat giving it a milky appearance. It is called chyle.
Conversion of absorbed food into active cytoplasm within cell is called as assimilation.
The elimination of faeces from the alimentary canal is called egestion or defaecation. The faeces is waste matter discharged from the alimentary canal.
Animals swallows night faeces and recycle it through the gut to complete the digestion of cellulose, making full use of their food. This habit is called coprophagy. Example: Rabbit.
Hormone secreted by mucosa of pyloric stomach in response to distension of stomach on food entry. Stimulates secretion of gastric juice and constricts cardiac sphincter.
Hormone secreted by duodenal epithelium when acidic chyme enters duodenum. Causes release of sodium bicarbonate in pancreatic juice, steps up bile secretion from liver, and inhibits gastrin.
Hormone secreted by duodenal epithelium in presence of fats in duodenum. Causes release of enzymes in pancreatic juice and release of bile from gall bladder.
Hormone secreted by duodenal epithelium when chyme enters duodenum. Slows gastric contractions to delay emptying and stops secretion of gastric juice.
Hormone secreted by intestinal epithelium (duodenal mucosa) in response to acidic chyme. Acts on crypts of Lieberkuhn to release enzymes from intestinal glands.

Nutrition in which individuals synthesize their own food. Includes photoautotrophs (synthesize food from CO2 and H2O in presence of sunlight) and chemoautotrophs (synthesize food with the help of chemical energy).

Nutrition in which animals derive organic food materials by consuming bodies or products of other living or dead plants or animals. Types: Holotrophic (solid food), Saprotrophic (dead organic matter), Parasitic (food from host body).
Nutrition in which organisms carry out both autotrophic and heterotrophic nutrition. Example: Euglena.

Organic compounds composed of C:H:O, having H and O in the ratio of 2:1. General formula: (CH2O)n. Chief source of energy. Caloric value: 4.1 k cal/gm. Physiological value: 4.0 k cal/gm. Daily requirement approx. 500 gm.

Fats and fat like substances composed of C, H and O. Insoluble in water but soluble in ether, alcohol, chloroform. Caloric value: 9.45 k cal/gm. Physiological fuel value: 9.0 k cal/gm. Daily requirement: 50 gm.

Complex, versatile macromolecules composed of C, H, O, N (some also contain S and P). Very high molecular weight. Unit: amino acids. Caloric value: 5.6 k cal/gm. Physiological caloric value: 4 k cal/gm. Daily requirement: 70-100 gm.
Amino acids not synthesized by human body and must be obtained from food. Include: Arginine, Histidine, Isoleucine, Leucine, Methionine, Phenylalanine, Threonine, Tryptophan, Lysine, Valine.
Amino acids which can be synthesized by human body. Include: Glycine, Alanine, Serine, Aspartic acid, Asparagine, Cystine, Glutamic acid, Glutamine, Proline, Tyrosine.

Complex organic compounds needed daily in minute quantities and act as growth and metabolic regulatory substances. definition first used by Funk in 1912. Mostly act as constituents of coenzymes in the cells.
Vitamins A, D, E and K. Dissolved in micelles and enter mucosal cells by simple diffusion. Their absorption is markedly decreased in the absence of bile. Can be stored in body.
Members of B complex (except B12) and vitamin C. Readily diffuse across the walls of the intestine into the blood. B-complex vitamins mostly act as coenzymes.
The diet which contains the various nutrients in such proportions as can satisfy all the various needs of our body.
Amount of energy required daily by a person to maintain its basal metabolism. About 1600 k cal/day.
Amount of energy required daily by a person to do his routine work. About 2800 k cal/day for males and 2300 k cal for females.
Over-nutritional disorder caused when energy inputs exceeds energy output. Results in deposition of excess fat in the body.

Nutritional deficiency disease due to protein/malnutrition. Growth and replacement of tissue proteins impaired; emaciated body with thin limbs and prominent ribs, dry thin and wrinkled skin, diarrhoea. Affects infants under one year of age.
Protein deficiency disease. Symptoms: wasting muscles, thin limbs, retarded growth of body and brain, oedema, diarrhoea. Commonly affects babies between 1-3 years of age.

Hunger is defined as the intrinsic (involuntary) desire or craving for food. Hunger centre is located in the lateral regions of hypothalamus. When glucose levels fall in blood, hunger centre is stimulated and hunger pangs are felt.
Subconscious desire for water. Induced by a hypothalamic thirst centre. When amount of water decreases in body fluids due to fever, exercise, sweating, copious urination or diarrhoea.

A process which involves intake of oxygen from environment and deliver it to the cells; includes stepwise oxidation of food in cells with incoming oxygen, elimination of CO2 produced in oxidation, release of energy during oxidation and storing it in the form of ATP.
The first process of respiration; inspiration (inflow) and expiration (outflow) of air between the atmosphere and the lungs.
Exchange of gases between the air spaces of the lungs and blood in pulmonary capillaries; blood gains O2 and loses CO2.

Exchange of gases between blood in systemic capillaries and tissue cells; blood loses O2 and gains CO2; metabolic reactions that consume O2 and give off CO2 during production of ATP are definitioned cellular respiration.

The surface at which exchange of gases (CO2 and O2) takes place; must be vascular and have enough area for gas exchange; e.g. plasma membrane in protozoa, body wall (skin) in annelids, alveocapillary membrane in men.
Oxygen dissolved in air and water; water and air are external respiratory medium; tissue fluid is internal respiratory medium.

Occurs in the presence of molecular oxygen; oxygen completely oxidizes food into carbon dioxide and water, releasing large amount of energy (e.g. C6H12O6 + 6O2 → 6CO2 + 6H2O + 2830 kJ); found in most animals and plants.

Exchange of environmental oxygen with carbon dioxide of body cells without special respiratory organs and without the aid of blood; found in aerobic bacteria, protists, plants, sponges, coelenterates, flatworms, roundworms and most arthropods.
Involves special respiratory organs such as skin, buccopharyngeal lining, gills and lungs, and needs the help of blood; includes cutaneous, buccopharyngeal, bronchial and pulmonary respiration.

Occurs in the absence of molecular oxygen; also called fermentation; food is only partially oxidised so only part of energy (5%) is released and rest remains trapped in indefinitionediate compounds; found in bacteria, yeast and parasitic worms (Ascaris, Taenia).

Respiration by skin; skin is the only respiratory organ in most annelids (earthworm and leeches) and an additional respiratory organ in amphibians; skin must be thin, moist, naked, permeable and well vascular.

Complex system of whitish, shining, intercommunicating air tubules; ectodermal air tubes found in insects, peripatus, centipedes and millipedes; internally lined by chitinous cuticle called intima which is spirally thickened to form taenidae; trachioles are lined by trachein protein.
Respiratory organ in spiders, ticks, mites and scorpion (class arachnida); a chamber containing a series of thin vascular, parallel lamellae arranged like the pages of a book; scorpion has 4 pairs.
Found in marine king crab or horse shoe crab.
Respiration by gills; found in aquatic animals such as prawn, unio, fishes, sea stars and tadpoles; 80% of O2 of incoming water is absorbed.

Found in arenicola (lug worm), larvae of certain insects (e.g. damsel fly) and some amphibians (necturus, siren, proteus, frog tadpole - first develop external gills which are replaced by internal gills later).
Found in bronchioles epithelium; secrete serotonin and histamine; histamine dilates while serotonin constricts the bronchioles.

Found in bronchioles epithelium; secrete a phospholipid named diapalmityl lecithin which acts as a surfactant; prevents the collapse of bronchioles lacking cartilaginous rings; collapsing of lungs is called atelectesis.
Condition when infants at birth are devoid of surfactant causing great respiratory difficulty because lungs refuse to expand; also called hyaline membrane disease (HMD) or glassy lung disease.
Phagocytes in bronchioles epithelium that eat foreign particles (dust).
The passage for air in which gaseous exchange does not take place; also called dead air space or conductive zone; derived from endoderm.
Divides nasal cavity into two nasal chambers; two parts: small cartilaginous (hyaline) part and major bony part (vomer is the main bone).
Region of nasal cavity lined by non-keratinized squamous epithelium; ectodermal in origin; has sebaceous gland, sweat gland and hair; acts like a sieve to check entry of large dust particles.

Middle region of nasal cavity lined by respiratory epithelium (ciliated pseudostratified columnar epithelium with mucus and serous cells); highly vascular; acts as air conditioner, humidifier and filter.
Upper region of nasal cavity lined by olfactory epithelium; organ of smell; detects odour of inspired air; inspiration stopped if odour is foul.

Three pairs of shelf-like structures on lateral wall of nasal cavity covered with mucus membrane; increase the surface of nasal chamber; superior (nasoturbinate), middle (ethmoturbinate), inferior (maxilloturbinate).

Short vertical tube about 12 cm long where food and air passages cross; three parts: nasopharynx (only respiratory - upper), oropharynx (middle, has fauces, palatine and lingual tonsils), laryngopharynx (lowest, leads to trachea and oesophagus).
Foramen (aperture) by which pharynx opens into larynx; remains generally open and closed during swallowing by epiglottis.

Short tubular voice-producing chamber; found in frogs and rabbits; made of 9 cartilages - thyroid (single, with adam's apple), cricoid (single), arytenoid (paired) of hyaline cartilage; epiglottis (single), corniculate (paired), cuneiform (paired) of elastic cartilage; cricoid cartilage is landmark for emergency airway.
Folds of mucus membrane in larynx; gap between them called rema vestibuli; not responsible for sound production.
Made of yellow elastic fibres; gap between them called rema glottides or peep hole; length in males 2.25 cm and in females 1.75 cm; pitch controlled by tension of vocal folds.

Paired respiratory organs in men lying in thoracic cavity on both sides of heart in mediastinum; right lung = 3 lobes (Superior, Middle, Inferior); left lung = 2 lobes (Superior, Inferior); apex (cupula) is the narrow superior portion; respiration by lungs is called pulmonary respiration.
Two membranes enclosing each lung; layers of peritoneum of thorax; inner = visceral pleuron (firmly bound to lung surface); outer = parietal pleuron (attached to chest wall).
Narrow space between the two pleura containing pleural fluid; pressure inside is negative (-5 mm Hg); pleurisy is inflammation of pleura causing painful breathing (dyspnea).
Glycoprotein fluid found in pleural cavity; secreted by pleura; lubricates the pleura so they may slide over each other without friction.
Region on mediastinal (medial) surface of each lung through which bronchi, pulmonary blood vessels, lymphatic vessels and nerves enter and exit.
Apparatus commonly used to measure the volume of air exchanged during breathing and the rate of ventilation; the record is called a spirogram.
Volume of air inspired or expired in relaxed or resting position; approximately 500 ml; consists of 150 ml dead space volume and 350 ml alveolar volume.
Additional air that can be inspired beyond tidal volume by taking a very deep breath; approximately 3000 ml.
Extra air that can be pushed out in addition to tidal volume by forceful expiration; approximately 1100 ml.
Air remaining in the lungs even after expiratory reserve volume is expelled; cannot be measured by spirometry; approximately 1200 ml.
Portion of tracheobronchial tree where gaseous exchange does not occur; also called conductive zone; approximately 150 ml.
Amount of air remaining in the lungs after a normal expiration; FRC = ERV + RV = 1100 + 1200 = 2300 ml.
Maximum amount of air that can be expired forcefully after maximum deep inspiration; VC = IRV + TV + ERV = 3000 + 500 + 1100 = 4600 ml.
Sum of vital capacity (VC) and residual volume (RV); TLC = VC + RV = 4600 + 1200 = 5800 ml.
Total amount of air a person can inspire by maximum distension of his lungs; IC = TV + IRV = 500 + 3000 = 3500 ml.
Movement of thorax, expansion (inflation) and deflation of lungs and flow of air into the lungs and from the lungs; extracellular, energy-consuming and physical process.

Intake of fresh air in lungs from outside; active process; involves contraction of diaphragm (principal muscle, supplied by phrenic nerve) and external intercostal muscles; increases vertical, anteroposterior and transverse diameter of thoracic cavity.

Outflow of air from the lungs; passive during normal breathing; inspiratory muscles relax, ribs move inferiorly and diaphragm dome moves superiorly; internal intercostal muscles and abdominal muscles involved in forced expiration.

subtopics.definition: External respiration (exchange of gases in lungs)
subtopics.definition_content: Gaseous exchange in lungs; O2 passes from alveoli to pulmonary capillary blood and CO2 passes from pulmonary capillary to alveoli through the alveolocapillary (respiratory) membrane; a purely physical diffusion phenomenon.

Membrane through which gas exchange occurs; composed of epithelium lining of alveolar wall, epithelial basement membrane, thin interstitial space, capillary basement membrane and capillary endothelial membrane; thickness 0.5 μm.
Volume of gas that diffuses through the membrane per minute for a pressure difference of 1 mm Hg.
Pressure a gas exerts in a mixture of gases; equal to the total pressure of the mixture multiplied by the percentage of that gas; proportional to concentration of the gas.

subtopics.definition: Internal respiration (exchange of gases in tissues)
subtopics.definition_content: Exchange of gases between blood in tissue capillaries and tissue cells; O2 separates from oxyhaemoglobin and diffuses from blood into tissue cells; CO2 diffuses from tissue cells into blood; blood becomes deoxygenated.

Main form of oxygen transport in blood; 98.5% of O2 transported as oxyhaemoglobin; 1.5% carried in dissolved state in plasma; 100 ml oxygenated blood contains 20 ml oxygen (0.3 ml dissolved in plasma and 19.7 ml bound to Hb).
Sigmoid (S-shaped) graph plotted between percent saturation of haemoglobin and oxygen tension; characteristic for haemoglobin.

Hb-O2 dissociation curve shifts to right when CO2 tension in blood is high; discovered by Bohr in 1904; deoxygenation of oxyhaemoglobin is directly proportional to blood pCO2; effect depends only on CO2 tension in blood.
The oxygen concentration at which 50% haemoglobin of blood is saturated with O2; P50 for myoglobin is 5 mm Hg.

Respiratory pigment/oxygen carrier in vertebrate blood; made of haem (iron-containing protoporphyrin) and globin (four polypeptide chains: 2α chains of 141 amino acids and 2β chains of 146 amino acids; total 574 amino acids); iron remains in ferrous state (Fe++); each molecule combines with 4 molecules of O2.

Chemically and functionally similar to Hb; one polypeptide chain (153 amino acids) with one haem group; P50 = 5 mm Hg (releases O2 less readily than Hb); found in muscles; acts as O2 storehouse; Hb-O2 dissociation curve for myoglobin is hyperbolic.
Enzyme present in RBCs that accelerates the reaction CO2 + H2O → H2CO3 by about 5000 times; essential for CO2 transport as bicarbonate (~70% of CO2 transported this way).

When bicarbonate ions (HCO3-) diffuse from RBCs into plasma, chloride ions diffuse from plasma into RBCs to maintain electrostatic neutrality; chloride content of RBCs increases when oxygenated blood becomes deoxygenated.
Unstable compound formed when CO2 directly and reversibly reacts with haemoglobin; approximately 23% of CO2 transported in this form (about 1 ml per 100 ml of blood).

Oxyhaemoglobin promotes CO2 release from blood; oxyhaemoglobin behaves as a strong acid causing: (1) haemoglobin loses capacity to bind CO2, thus carbaminohaemoglobin dissociates; (2) released H+ binds bicarbonate ions forming carbonic acid which dissociates into H2O and CO2 which diffuses into alveoli.
Located in medulla oblongata; controls inspiration (active for 2 seconds); dominant over expiratory centre; rhythmicity centre.
Located in medulla oblongata; controls expiration (inactive for 3 seconds); rhythmicity centre.
Located in pons; causes slow and deep inspiration.
Located in pons; controls other respiratory centres and produces normal quiet breathing; stimulation increases respiration rate and shortens inspiration and expiration.
Located in pons; causes sudden and shallow respiration.

Include carotid body (in wall of left and right common carotid arteries) and aortic bodies (in arch of aorta); placed in vascular endothelium; detect PO2 in arterial blood and send impulses to respiratory centre.

Present on ventral surface of medulla; bathed in brain tissue fluid in close vicinity of CSF; detect CO2/H+ concentration; increased CO2 → H2CO3 → H+ ions → stimulate central chemoreceptors → respiration stimulated.

Condition of oxygen shortage in the tissues; two types: (a) Artificial Hypoxia - shortage at high altitudes (above 2400 m), causes mountain sickness with breathlessness, headache, dizziness, nausea, bluish tinge; (b) Anaemic Hypoxia - reduced oxygen-carrying capacity due to anaemia or CO poisoning.
O2 content of blood falls and CO2 content rises and paralyses the respiratory centre; breathing stops and death occurs.
Air-pollutants and chronic bronchitis may cause breakdown of alveoli of the lungs, reducing the surface area for gas exchange; patient becomes permanently short of breath.

Allergic attack of breathlessness associated with bronchial obstruction or spasm of smooth muscle (contraction); characterised by coughing, difficult breathing and wheezing; patient has trouble exhaling.
Caused by permanent swelling in bronchi; cough with thick mucus containing pus cells; dyspnea (hunger of air / hypercapnia) and fever; hypertrophy and hyperplasia of bronchi occurs.

Oxygen has difficulty diffusing through inflamed alveoli reducing blood PO2; caused by Streptococcus pneumoniae, other bacteria, fungi, protozoans and viruses; symptoms include trembling, pain in chest, fever, cough and delirium.

Caused by bacteria Mycobacterium tuberculosis; bacteria settle in lungs and convert normal tissue into fibrous tissue reducing respiratory surface; treated with rifampin, isoniazid and DOT (Direct Observed Treatment) therapy.

Caused by exposure to harmful substances (gas, fumes, dusts) in the workplace; silicosis (silica dust) and asbestosis (asbestos dust) are common examples from mining industry; characterised by fibrosis (proliferation of fibrous connective tissue) of upper part of lung.
Reflex action stimulated from trachea and lungs; centre is medulla oblongata; forcible expiration after prolonged inspiration; air expelled through the mouth.
Reflex action stimulated by olfactory epithelium of nasal chamber; forcible expiration with air exploding out through nose and mouth.

Noisy inspiration caused by muscular spasm of diaphragm at irregular intervals; noise is due to sudden sucking of air through vocal cords; stimulation usually from irritation of sensory nerve endings of digestive tract.
Prolonged inspiration; caused by low oxygen tension in the blood.

topics.name: Respiratory quotient and effect of gases

subtopics.definition_content: Ratio of volume of CO2 formed to volume of O2 utilized during respiration; measured by Ganong's respirometer.

Stable compound formed when CO binds with haemoglobin; CO binds about 250 times more readily than O2; even 0.4 mm Hg partial pressure of CO occupies about half of haemoglobin; concentration of ~1% CO in alveolar air can be lethal.
Occurs inside the individual cells where the distribution of substances is through cyclosis of cell cytoplasm. Example — Protozoans.
When the distribution of the substances occurs inside the body through extracellular or intracellular fluids. Types: Extra organismic and Intra-organismic circulation.
Blood flows through large open spaces and channels called lacunae and sinuses among the tissues. Tissues are in direct contact with blood. Found in most arthropods and some molluscs.
Blood flows through a closed system of chambers called heart and blood vessels. Blood does not come in direct contact with tissue. Found in echinoderms, some molluscs, annelids and all vertebrates.
Blood flows only once through the heart in a complete cycle. Heart pumps only deoxygenated blood, hence called Venous Heart. Found only in fishes, petromyzon.

Blood flows in two circuits — pulmonary and systemic. Heart pumps both deoxygenated and oxygenated blood to lungs and body respectively, hence called arteriovenous heart. Found in dipnoi, amphibians, reptiles, birds and mammals.
The amount of blood present in the heart. Its value is 8%.
The amount of blood present in the surrounding of lungs and pulmonary blood vessels. Its value is 12%.
The amount of blood which circulates in the rest part of the body. Its value is 80%. Divided into arterial (15%), capillary (5%) and venous (60%) circulations.

chapters.name: Heart

subtopics.definition_content: Tough, 2-layered fibroserous sac enclosing the heart. Outer layer is non-distensible fibrous pericardium and inner layer is thin serous pericardium (parietal and visceral layers). Pericardial cavity is filled with serous pericardial fluid for frictionless movement.
Oudefinitionost layer of heart wall, also called visceral layer of serous pericardium. Thin, transparent, composed of mesothelium and connective tissue.

Middle, highly vascular layer of heart wall composed of cardiac muscle fibres joined together by intercalated disc. The connective tissue in myocardium acts as cardiac skeleton. Thickest where the endocardium is thinnest.
Innermost layer lining the cavity of heart, consisting of endothelium of squamous cells resting on thin basement membrane of loose connective tissue.

Aperture between septum primum and septum secondum in embryonic heart interatrial septum. Allows blood to bypass non-functional foetal lungs. Closes at birth; its remnant on right side is called fossa ovalis.
Numerous, strong, inelastic thread-like tendons present in mammalian heart connecting papillary muscles to AV valves. Prevent AV valve flaps from reverting into auricles during ventricular systole.
Large muscular elevations in ventricles that act as anchors for chordae tendinae. 3 in right ventricle and 2 in left ventricle. Found only in mammals.
Right AV valve present between right auricle and right ventricle. Consists of 3 membranous flaps or cusps.
Left AV valve present between left auricle and left ventricle. Consists of 2 flaps or cusps. Resembles mitre or topi of bishop, hence called Mitral valve.
Three membranous, pocket-shaped flaps at the base of pulmonary arch and systemic aorta. Allow passage of blood from ventricles to respective blood vessels, but prevent return of blood.
Structure formed after birth by deposition of elastin fibre blocking ductus botalli (ductus arteriosus), which connected pulmonary aorta to systemic aorta in foetal life.

Also called pacemaker, node of Keith and Flack, 'heart of heart', 'brain of heart'. Located in right wall of right atrium below opening of superior vena cava. Has highest degree of autorhythmicity — generates impulse at 70-80 times/minute but has least conductivity.

Also called reserve pacemaker, node of Twara and Aschoff. Lies in right atrium near junction of interauricular and interventricular septum close to coronary sinus opening. Conducts impulse from SA node; generates impulse at 40-60/minute. Absent in frog.

Arises from AV node, descends in interventricular septum and bifurcates to innervate walls of right and left ventricle. Only muscular connection between atria and ventricles. Generates non-rhythmic impulse at 35-40/minute.
Numerous modified muscle fibres arising from branches of Bundle of His that provide impulse to myocardium of ventricles. Generate non-rhythmic impulse at 30-35/minute.
The spontaneous and rhythmic contraction and relaxation of the heart to pump out and receive blood to and from the body.

Heart in which the beat is initiated by a ganglion situated near the heart; impulse originates from nervous system. Heart stops immediately after removal from body. Examples: hearts of some annelids and most arthropods.

Heart in which the beat is initiated by a patch of modified heart muscle; impulse originates in the heart itself. Heart removed from body continues to beat for some time. Examples: hearts of molluscs and vertebrates.
Decrease in heart rate below normal. Caused by parasympathetic stimulation releasing acetylcholine.
Increase in heart rate above normal. Caused by sympathetic stimulation releasing sympathin (adrenaline + noradrenaline).
Stimulation of vagus nerve decreases the heart rate but its continuous stimulation shows no further decrease — this phenomenon is called Vagus escape.
The sequence of events which takes place from the beginning of one heart beat to the beginning of the next. Completed in 0.8 seconds.
Contraction of atria stimulated by SA node, driving blood into respective ventricles (AV valves open). Takes 0.1 second; atrial diastole is about 0.7 seconds.
Contraction of ventricles stimulated by AV node. AV valves close (producing 'Lubb' sound). Semilunar valves open when ventricular pressure exceeds arterial pressure. Takes about 0.3 seconds.

Both ventricles and auricles are in diastolic phase simultaneously. Semilunar valves close (producing 'Dup' sound) to prevent backflow from great arteries. Lasts 0.4 seconds; also called blood receiving period of heart.
First heart sound (S1); long and louder sound produced by closure of AV valves (tricuspid and bicuspid) at beginning of ventricular systole.
Second heart sound (S2); short and sharper sound created by closure of semilunar valves towards end of ventricular systole / beginning of ventricular diastole.
Instrument used to listen to heart sounds. Invented by Laennae.
Abnormal hissing sound heard due to defective or damaged heart valves. Caused by backflow of blood from ventricles to auricles or from aorta to ventricles.
Instrument used to magnify and record heart sounds.

Volume of blood pumped from heart (left ventricle) into the systemic aorta in one minute. Also called minute volume. Cardiac output = Stroke volume × Rate of heart beat = 70 ml × 75/min = 5250 ml/min (5.25 litres/min).
Amount of blood pumped by left ventricle each time it contracts. Equal to difference between End Diastolic Volume (EDV) and End Systolic Volume (ESV). Normal value = 70 ml. SV = EDV − ESV.
Amount of blood present in left ventricle at the end of diastole. Maximum volume of the cavity of left ventricle — equal to 120–130 ml.
Amount of blood present in left ventricle at the end of systole. Least volume of the cavity of left ventricle — equal to 50–60 ml.

Maximum amount of blood that can be pumped by left ventricle under conditions of maximum needs. Heart beat can go up to 250 and stroke volume up to 100 ml per systole. Cardiac reserve is 25–30 litres (about 5–6 times cardiac output).
Amount of impure blood returning to right atrium per minute. Equal to 5.25 litres.

formulas: Cardiac output = Stroke volume × Rate of heart beat = 70 ml × 75/min = 5250 ml/min

subtopics.definition_content: A graphic record of electrical events occurring during a cardiac cycle. Invented by Einthoven. Recorded using Electrocardiograph. Normal ECG has 5 deflection waves — P, Q, R, S and T. P, R and T are positive (above baseline); Q and S are negative (below baseline).
Indicates impulse of contraction generated by SA node and its spread in atria causing atrial depolarisation. Interval PQ represents atrial contraction — takes 0.1 second.

Indicates spread of impulse from AV node to wall of ventricles through Bundle of His and Purkinje fibres causing ventricular depolarisation. Also represents repolarization of SA node. Related to ventricular systole.
Indicates repolarisation during ventricular relaxation.
ECG waves representing generation of the potential difference. Appear only when both electrodes of galvanometer are in different fields.
ECG waves appearing when depolarisation is over and the muscle fibre is returning to its original polarity.
Thick-walled blood vessels carrying oxygenated blood (deoxygenated in pulmonary artery) from heart to various parts of body. Aorta → arteries → arterioles → meta-arterioles → capillaries.
Circular muscles at the beginning of capillary possessed by arterioles. Regulates flow of blood into the capillaries — this process is called vasomotion.
Network formed by union of branches of more than one artery supplying one organ. Provides collateral/alternate pathways of blood supply. Prevents necrosis if any artery is blocked.

Smallest blood vessels discovered by Marcello Malpighi. Wall formed of single layer of endothelium on basement membrane. Diameter ~8μ. Called exchange vessels — site of exchange of material between blood and tissue. Possess about 7% of total body blood.
Pericapillary cells surrounding capillaries. Some are contractile and phagocytic in nature.
Capillaries without fenestra/aperture — less permeable. Present in lungs, muscles, connective tissues and brain tissues.
Capillaries with apertures/fenestra — found in organs requiring maximum permeability such as endocrine glands, intestinal villi, cavities of brain, kidney, ciliary body of eye.
Irregularly dilated capillaries found in organs where there is decrease in flow rate such as liver, spleen, bone marrow, parathyroid, pituitary gland.
Thin-walled blood vessels carrying deoxygenated blood (oxygenated in pulmonary vein) from tissues to the heart. Venules → veins → vena cava. Largest vein is inferior vena cava (post caval).
Stout, blood-filled, painful veins especially of the limbs due to defective watch-pocket valves.
Oudefinitionost layer of blood vessel wall; fibrous, made up of collagen-rich connective tissue and less elastin fibres. Collagen fibres give strength and prevent overdilation.
Middle, thickest layer of blood vessel wall; made of smooth involuntary muscle fibres and elastin fibres. Variable as number of fibres depends upon position from heart.

Innermost, thinnest layer of blood vessel wall; single layer of simple squamous epithelial cells called endothelium resting on basement membrane and outer elastic connective tissue. The hollow space is called lumen.

Involves blood supply to heart wall and drainage of heart wall. Coronary arteries (one pair, arising from aortic arch above semilunar valves) supply oxygenated blood to heart wall. Most coronary veins drain into right auricle through coronary sinus.
Structure formed by joining of most coronary veins. Returns deoxygenated blood from heart wall into right auricle.
Circulation between heart and lungs. Right ventricle pumps deoxygenated blood into pulmonary aorta → right and left pulmonary arteries → lungs (oxygenation) → four pulmonary veins → left auricle.
Circulation of blood between heart and body organs. Left ventricle → systemic arch → body organs → precaval and post caval → right auricle.

Ring-shaped artery formed at base of brain by union of internal carotids of both sides along with vertebral arteries of both sides. From this circle, many branches arise to supply different parts of brain.
Single vein formed by union of right and left brachiocephalic (innominate) veins. Collects blood from head, neck, arms and chest region and drains into right atrium.
Largest vein, originating in inferior lumbar region by union of right and left common iliac veins. Collects blood from all body structures below the diaphragm. Opens into right atrium.
Longest vein of the body.
Part of venous circulation present between two groups of capillaries — starts in capillaries and ends in capillaries. The vein which drains blood into organs other than heart is called portal vein.

Portal system in higher vertebrates (amphibia, reptiles, birds and mammals). Blood from hypothalamus is collected by hypophyseal portal vein which ends in anterior lobe of pituitary gland. Enables releasing and inhibiting factors from hypothalamus to reach anterior pituitary.

Found in all chordates. In mammals, hepatic portal vein is formed by union of six main veins (inferior mesenteric, superior mesenteric, splenic, right gastric, left gastric, cystic veins) draining blood from digestive system into liver.

Well developed in fishes and amphibians, reduced in reptiles and birds, absent in mammals. Carries blood from posterior region of body to kidneys by renal portal veins. In frog: one pair of renal portal veins formed by union of femoral and sciatic veins.

topics.name: Types of Blood Pressure and Pulse

subtopics.definition_content: Lateral pressure exerted by the column of blood on the wall of the blood vessel in which it is present. Usually measured in brachial artery by sphygmomanometer (invented by Riva-Rocci 1896). Normal value: 120/80 mm Hg.
Pressure exerted by blood on walls of blood vessels due to systole of ventricles. Equal to 120 mm Hg. Maximum in arteries.
Pressure exerted on walls of blood vessels when ventricles are relaxed. Drops to 80 mm Hg during ventricular diastole.
Difference between systolic and diastolic pressures. Normal value = 120 − 80 = 40 mm Hg.
Pressure wave of distension and recoiling felt in the radial artery due to contraction of left ventricle forcing 70–90 ml of blood into aorta each cardiac cycle. Normal pulse rate = 72/minute.

Low blood pressure with systolic below 110 mm Hg and diastolic below 70 mm Hg. Caused by low metabolic rate, starvation, anaemia, loss of blood in haemorrhage, valvular defects etc. May cause fainting.
Persistent high blood pressure with systolic more than 140 mm Hg and diastolic more than 90 mm Hg. Caused by decrease in extensibility of arteries due to atherosclerosis and arteriosclerosis.
Deposition mainly in tunica interna of blood vessels preventing their dilation; beginning of thickening and hardening.
Hardening and narrowing of blood vessels due to deposition of cholesterol, calcium or lipid in both tunica media and tunica interna.

Lymph = Blood − RBC's. Colourless or yellowish fluid present in lymph vessels. A mobile connective tissue formed by filtration of blood. Composition: Water 94%, solids 6% (proteins, fats, carbohydrates, urea, enzymes, antibodies). Contains large number of leucocytes (mostly lymphocytes) 500–75,000/cu mm. No blood platelets.
Process of lymph formation — diffusion of substances from blood capillaries into the interstitial space.
Accumulation of fluid around cells caused when rate of lymph formation exceeds its return to blood stream. Also caused by blocking of lymph flow.

Largest solid mass of reticulo-endothelial tissue in body. Mesodermal in origin. In human, ~12 cm long, in left hypochondriac region. Primary functions: phagocytosis of worn-out RBCs and pathogens, haemopoiesis in foetal life, blood reservoir in adults, site of B cell proliferation. Called 'graveyard' or 'slaughter house' of RBCs and 'first reservoir' of blood.
Component of splenic pulp; functions in immunity as a site of B cell proliferation into antibody-producing plasma cells.
Component of splenic pulp; contains macrophages, plasma cells and lymphocytes in adults. In foetus contains myeloblast, erythroblast and megakaryocytes for blood production.

Primary lymphatic organ located in mediastinum between lungs. Two lobes divided into lobules by trabeculae. Each lobule has cortex (tightly packed lymphocytes, epithelial cells, macrophages) and medulla (epithelial cells, scattered lymphocytes). Pre-T cells migrate from red bone marrow to thymus for proliferation into mature T cells. Produces thymin hormone.
Characteristic structures found in medulla of thymus; possibly remnants of dying cells.
Lymph capillaries in the region of small intestine villi that collect chyle (milky white fluid due to absorbed fat). Help in absorption of digested fat.

Smallest lymphatic duct (~1.25 cm). Opens into right subclavian vein at junction with right internal jugular vein. Collects lymph from one-fourth of body (right part of head, neck, thoracic cavity and right arm).
Longest lymphatic duct (~38–45 cm). Originates from cisterna chyli and empties into left subclavian vein. Collects lymph from three-fourths of body.

Dilated sac-like structure present below diaphragm in lumbar region at level of second lumbar vertebra. Collects lymph from posterior part of body and drains into left lymphatic duct. Also called 'passive lymphatic artery' or 'second heart'.

Masses of lymphatic tissue and connective tissue (reticular tissue) located on lymph capillaries. Functions: produce lymphocytes, screen lymph by phagocytic activity, defend against bacterial infections, produce γ-globulin, carry out immunological responses.

Clusters of lymph nodes, often called policemen. Types: Normal tonsils (pharynx), adenoid tonsils (nasopharynx), abdominal tonsils (vermiform appendix), policeman of intestine (lamina propria of ileum).
Inflammation of lymph nodes during infection, when central part of follicle shows rapid division and formation of plasma cells (reaction centre).
The process by which cells throw out metabolic waste products by diffusion into their surrounding medium, and these wastes are then eliminated by the body into its external environment.
Organelle in protozoans like Amoeba and Paramecium that plays some role in removal of excretory products.
Excretory cells found in Platyhelminthes (Planaria, liverfluke and tapeworm); open into ductules which open into the excretory duct.

Single excretory cell forming the H-shaped excretory system in Ascaris, consisting of two longitudinal excretory canals connected anteriorly by transverse canals with a short definitioninal duct opening via excretory pore.
Excretory system of earthworm consisting of 3 types of nephridia: pharyngeal, septal, and integumentary nephridia, all commonly called micronephridia.
Cells found in coelomic fluid of earthworm that are excretory in nature.

Principal excretory organs of insects, centipedes, millipedes and arachnids; bicarbonates of potassium and sodium, water and uric acid are formed in them; water and bicarbonates are reabsorbed and uric acid is passed out through anus.
Excretory organs of adult Prawn (crustacean) consisting of a pair of antennary or green glands, a pair of lateral ducts and a single renal sac.
Kidneys or excretory organs found in Molluscs (e.g., Unio); Keber's organ is also present for excretion in Unio.

topics.name: Echinoderms and Table of Excretory Organs across Phyla

subtopics.definition_content: Excretion of excess amino acids as such; seen in some molluscs (Unio, Limnae) and some echinoderms (Asterias).

Excretion of ammonia (NH₄⁺ or NH₃) as primary nitrogenous waste; ammonia is highly toxic and highly soluble in water, requiring large amounts of water for excretion; seen in most aquatic arthropods, bony and freshwater fishes, amphibian tadpoles, turtles, etc.

Excretion of urea CO(NH₂)₂ as primary nitrogenous waste; urea is less toxic and less soluble than ammonia; seen in land vertebrates (adult amphibians, mammals), elasmobranch fishes, marine bony fish, adult frog, earthworms, nematodes.

Excretion of uric acid crystals as primary nitrogenous waste; crystals are nontoxic and almost insoluble in water allowing water conservation; formation requires xanthine oxidase enzyme; seen in land gastropods, most insects, land reptiles, birds and Kangaroo rat.
Excretory product in certain marine molluscs, crustaceans and teleost fishes; formed by methylation of ammonia to trimethylamine then oxidation; soluble in water but nontoxic.
Excretory product of spiders; also formed in some Unio, penguin and birds; insoluble in water so no water is needed for its excretion.

topics.name: Waste products of nucleic acid metabolism
Some sundry excretory substances (Others excretory products)

subtopics.definition_content: Formed when benzoic acid (from food or fat metabolism of rabbits/mammals) is combined with glycine and changed into less toxic hippuric acid for excretion.
Formed in birds when benzoic acid is combined with ornithine for excretion.

Excretory product formed from creatine phosphate (high-energy storage molecules in muscle cells synthesised by glycine, arginine and methionine); excess creatine phosphate is excreted as such or after conversion to creatinine.

Dark-red, bean-shaped organs about 11 cm long, 5 cm wide and 3 cm thick, each weighing about 150 gm in adult male and 135 gm in adult female; placed retroperitoneally against the back wall of abdominal cavity below the diaphragm, opposite last thoracic and first three lumbar vertebrae.
Center of the concave inner surface of the kidney from which the ureter emerges, the renal artery enters, the renal vein exits and the renal nerves enter.
8 to 18 conical masses in the medulla of the kidney, each having a broad base towards the cortex and a narrow end called renal papilla towards the pelvis.
Extensions of the cortex into the medulla between the renal pyramids.
Innermost layer surrounding each kidney, made up of fibrous connective tissue.

Structural and functional unit of the kidney; a thin, long, much convoluted tubular unit. One human kidney contains about one million nephrons; rabbit kidney about 2 lac, frog kidney about 2 thousand.

Blind or closed, enlarged and double-walled cup forming the proximal end of each nephron in the cortex; contains the glomerulus. Named after English physiologist and histologist William Bowman. Inner wall made of podocytes with slit pores (~25 nm wide).

Network of up to 50 parallel branching and anastomosing capillaries within Bowman's capsule; covered by endothelium, basement membrane and epithelium made of podocytes; receives blood through afferent arteriole, exits through efferent arteriole; about 50 times more permeable than capillaries elsewhere.
Composite structure of Bowman's capsule and glomerulus; named after Italian microscopist Marcello Malpighi.
Tubular part of nephron opening from Bowman's capsule; made up of simple cuboidal epithelium with microvilli (brush border epithelium); most important site for selective reabsorption.
U-shaped (hair-pin turn) structure of the nephron between PCT and DCT; has distinct descending limb and ascending limb; ascending limb is impermeable to water and permeable to NaCl.
Coiled tubular part of nephron opening from ascending limb of Henle's loop; made up of cuboidal epithelium which is glandular in nature.
Formed by union of collecting ducts of one pyramid; leads into the pelvis; relatively impermeable to water.
Epithelial cells forming the inner wall of Bowman's capsule with gaps (about 25 nm wide) called slit pores that restrict passage of colloids but allow small molecules and water through.

Peritubular capillary system formed by efferent arteriole of juxta-glomerular nephron that wraps around Henle's loop; makes a U-turn at the innermost medulla and returns to venous circulation near medulla–cortex junction.

Specialized cellular apparatus located where the DCT passes close to the Bowman's capsule and afferent arteriole; contains macula densa cells with Lacis cells (Polkisson's cells) that secrete renin modulating blood pressure and thus renal blood flow and GFR.
Cells of the DCT epithelium in contact with the afferent arteriole that are denser than other epithelial cells; contain special Lacis cells (Polkisson's cells) that secrete renin.

topics.name: Types of nephron – Cortical and Juxtamedullary

subtopics.definition_content: Nephrons forming 80–85% of total nephrons; small in size; lie mainly in renal cortex; Henle's loops are very short; control plasma volume when water supply is normal; blood supply from peritubular capillaries only.

Nephrons forming 15–20% of total nephrons; large in size; Bowman's capsules near cortex–medulla junction; Henle's loops are very long extending deep into medulla; control plasma volume when water supply is short; blood supply from peritubular capillaries and vasa recta.

topics.name: Origin and types of kidneys in different vertebrates

subtopics.definition_content: Special part of mesoderm from which kidney tubules (nephrons) arise in the embryo.

Hypothetical primitive kidney of ancestral vertebrates extending entire length of coelom; tubules segmentally arranged with nephrostome present and external glomerulus; duct is archeonephric duct; e.g., larva of Myxine and some apodan amphibians.

Kidney originating from anterior part of nephrotome; 3 tubules in frog embryo, 7 in human, 12 in chick; nephrostome present; glomerulus is external (glomus); duct is pronephric duct or mullerian duct; functional in adult Myxine and embryos of all anamniotes.

Kidney originating from middle part of nephrotome; duct is mesonephric (Wolffian) duct; functional only in embryos of amniotes; functional in both embryo and adult of anamniotes (fishes and amphibians).

Kidney originating from posterior part of nephrotome; nephrostome absent; thin U-shaped loop of Henle between PCT and DCT (incomplete in reptiles and birds, well developed in mammals); duct is metanephric duct (ureter); found in all amniotes.

Kidney in fish and amphibians where tubules extend through length of coelom behind pronephros, formed from entire nephrotome left behind the pronephros; in males differentiated into anterior genital and post renal part.

Whitish tubes about 28 cm long emerging from the hilum of each kidney; wall consists of transitional epithelium surrounded by muscle fibres; carry urine by peristalsis to the urinary bladder; retroperitoneal.

Pear-shaped hollow muscular organ in pelvic cavity; made of smooth involuntary detrusor muscles; lumen lined by transitional epithelium (rugae); average capacity 700–800 ml; neck guarded by two sphincters (inner involuntary, outer voluntary).
Smooth triangular area in the urinary bladder.

Tube leading from the urinary bladder; in female 3–5 cm long carrying only urine; in male about 20 cm long carrying urine and spermatic fluid through prostate gland and penis. Male urethra has 3 regions: prostatic (3–4 cm), membranous (1 cm) and penile (15 cm).

chapters.name: Physiology of excretion

subtopics.definition_content: Oxidation of amino acids using oxygen resulting in removal of the amino group (NH₂) leaving pyruvic acid; the amino group is converted to ammonia (NH₃) during deamination.

Cycle in liver where CO₂ is activated by biotin and combines with two NH₃ molecules in presence of carbamyl phosphate synthetase (CPS) and 2 ATP to form carbamyl phosphate; carbamyl phosphate reacts with ornithine to form citrulline; citrulline combines with NH₃ to form arginine; arginine is broken into urea and ornithine by arginase enzyme.

Passive process of filtration from glomerulus into Bowman's capsule; non-colloidal part of plasma (urea, water, glucose, salts, vitamins, minerals, nitrogenous waste) is forced out by high blood pressure. Glomerular capillaries are about 50 times more permeable than capillaries elsewhere.
Hydrostatic force that blood under pressure exerts against the walls of glomerular capillaries; GBHP = +70 mmHg.
Osmotic pressure created in blood of glomerular capillaries due to plasma proteins (albumin, globulin, fibrinogen); resists filtration; BCOP = 30 mmHg (25 mmHg in other body capillaries).
Pressure caused by filtrate in Bowman's capsule that resists filtration; CHP = 20 mmHg.

subtopics.definition: Effective filtration pressure (EFP) / Net filtration pressure (NFP)
subtopics.definition_content: EFP = GBHP − (BCOP + CHP) = 70 − (30 + 20) = 20 mmHg.

Plasma fluid filtering from glomerular capillaries into Bowman's capsule; equals Plasma − Protein; contains urea, water, glucose, amino acids, vitamins, fatty acids, uric acid, creatine, creatinine, toxins, salts; isotonic to blood plasma.

Amount of filtrate formed per minute in all nephrons of paired kidneys; 120–125 ml/min in males, 110 ml/min in females; 180 litres per day filtered, only 1.5 litres excreted as urine (0.8% of total filtrate).
About 1250 ml (25% of cardiac output) blood circulates through kidneys each minute; plasma component is about 670 ml = RPF.
Ratio of GFR to RPF; GFR/RPF = 120/670 = 0.17.

Discovered by Richard and supporters; PCT reabsorbs glucose, amino acids, Na⁺, K⁺ (active transport); Cl⁻ (passive); 80% water by osmosis – obligatory water reabsorption; 80–90% filtered bicarbonate; some urea by diffusion. DCT reabsorbs 13% water (facultative) under ADH; Na⁺ under aldosterone.
Reabsorption of 80% of filtered water by passive osmosis in PCT regardless of body water status.
Reabsorption of 13% water in DCT and collecting duct under influence of ADH when plasma water level falls.

Active secretion of creatinine, hippuric acid, foreign substances (pigments, drugs including penicillin), H⁺ and NH₃ into filtrate in PCT; K⁺, H⁺, NH₄⁺ and HCO₃⁻ into DCT; urea enters filtrate by diffusion in thin ascending limb of Henle's loop.
Substances almost completely absorbed from filtrate; e.g., sugar, amino acids, vitamins, HCO₃⁻ and Na⁺.
Substances absorbed only at low concentration from filtrate; e.g., urea, phosphate, uric acid, H⁺, K⁺.
Substances not reabsorbed from filtrate; e.g., creatinine and hippuric acid.
Substances that increase the volume of urine to be excreted; e.g., tea, coffee, alcohol.

topics.name: Counter current mechanism of urine concentration

subtopics.definition_content: Mechanism by which mammals form hypertonic urine using Henle's loop and vasa recta; descending limb (permeable to water) – water moves out, Na⁺ and Cl⁻ move in → filtrate becomes hypertonic; ascending limb (impermeable to water) – Na⁺ and Cl⁻ move out by active transport → filtrate becomes hypotonic; collecting duct passes through hypertonic tissue fluid – water moves out osmotically → urine becomes hypertonic.
Pigment giving urine its yellowish colour; derived from the breakdown of haemoglobin from worn-out RBCs.

The highest concentration of a substance in the blood up to which it is fully reabsorbed from the glomerular filtrate; if blood level exceeds this, excess is excreted in urine; e.g., renal threshold of glucose is 180 mg per 100 ml blood.
Presence of glucose in urine; pathological condition when blood glucose exceeds renal threshold.
Presence of proteins (albumins) in urine; a pathological condition.
Presence of blood cells in urine; a pathological condition.
Presence of ketone bodies in urine; a pathological condition.
Presence of pus in urine; a pathological condition.

Process of passing out urine from the urinary bladder; urge starts when bladder is a little more than half full; involves relaxation of urethral sphincters and contraction of smooth muscles of bladder wall driving urine through urethra.

formulas: E.F.P. = G.B.H.P. − (B.C.O.P. + C.H.P.) = 70 mmHg − (30 mmHg + 20 mmHg) = 20 mmHg

subtopics.definition_content: Hormone produced in hypothalamus, released from pituitary gland; enhances fluid retention by making kidneys reabsorb more water from DCT and collecting duct; released when osmoreceptors detect blood osmolarity above 300 mosm L⁻¹; inhibited when drinking reduces osmolarity.

Multihormonal system operated by JGA in response to decreased blood pressure; JGA releases renin → renin converts angiotensinogen to angiotensin II → angiotensin II constricts arterioles, signals PCT to reabsorb more NaCl and water, stimulates adrenal gland to release aldosterone → aldosterone induces DCT to reabsorb more Na⁺ and water → increased blood volume and pressure.

Hormone secreted as renin by specialised cells in afferent arterioles of kidney cortex (JGA) in response to decreased blood pressure or Na⁺ level; converts plasma protein angiotensinogen into angiotensin II.
Hormone secreted by adrenal gland stimulated by angiotensin II; induces DCT to reabsorb more Na⁺ and water, increasing blood volume and pressure.
Hormone that increases blood Ca²⁺ (hypercalcaemia) and decreases PO₄; increases absorption of Ca⁺ in kidney, increases excretion of PO₄.
Hormone that increases excretion of Ca²⁺ in the kidney.
Fatty acids produced by renal pyramids; participate in blood pressure regulation.
Hormone secreted by juxtaglomerular apparatus; plays important role in erythropoiesis (RBC production).

Inflammation of renal pelvis, calyces and interstitial tissue due to local bacterial infection reaching via urethra and ureter; affects countercurrent mechanism; symptoms: pain in back, frequent and painful urination.
Inflammation of glomeruli caused by injury to kidney, bacterial toxins or drug reaction; proteins and RBCs pass into the filtrate.
Inflammation of urinary bladder caused by bacterial infection; patient has frequent, painful urination with burning sensation.

Presence of excessive amount of urea in blood resulting from decreased excretion of urea in kidney tubules due to bacterial infection (nephritis) or mechanical obstruction; urea poisons cells at high concentration.
Formed by precipitation of uric acid or oxalate; blocks kidney tubule; causes severe pain (renal colic) in back spreading to thighs.
Partial or total inability of kidneys to carry on excretory and salt-water regulatory functions; leads to uremia, salt-water imbalance and stoppage of erythropoietin secretion.

Proper maintenance of the internal environment; kidneys achieve it by continuously eliminating metabolic wastes and regulating blood composition (water balance, osmotic pressure, pH, electrolyte concentrations, RBC count, renal blood flow).

Machine used to filter blood of a person with damaged kidneys; works on principle of dialysis (diffusion of small solute molecules through a semipermeable membrane); cellophane tube suspended in salt-water solution similar to normal blood plasma but without urea; removes urea, uric acid, creatinine, excess salts and excess H⁺ ions.

Separation of small molecules (crystalloids) from large molecules (colloids) in solution by interposing a semipermeable membrane; used in artificial kidney to filter blood of patients with kidney damage.

Grafting a kidney from a compatible donor to restore kidney functions in a recipient suffering from kidney failure; first kidney transplant between identical twins in 1954 by Dr. Charles Hufnagel; India's first transplant on December 1, 1971 at Christian Medical College, Vellore.
Kidney transplant from an identical twin; always successful.
Renal transplant from a sibling, close relative or cadaver; usually successful with immunosuppressants that prevent graft rejection.
Genetic marker on surface of leucocytes used for recipient-donor matching; a person inherits 3 antigens from mother and 3 from father; higher number of matching antigens increases graft longevity.
Ideal immunosuppressant used in kidney transplantation; suppresses immunity against foreign tissue but maintains immunity against infection and cancer.

topics.name: Skin, Lungs, Liver, Large intestine, Saliva, Gills

subtopics.definition_content: Glands in mammalian skin that pass out sweat consisting of water, inorganic salts (chiefly NaCl), traces of urea and lactic acid, very small amounts of amino acids and glucose.
Oil glands in skin that pass out sebum containing lipids such as waxes, sterols, other hydrocarbons and fatty acids.
Regulation of solute movement and hence water movement (which follows solutes by osmosis); involves maintenance of osmotic conditions of body fluids.
Total solute concentration expressed as molarity or moles of solute per litre of solution; unit is milliosmole per litre (mosm L⁻¹).
Animals that do not actively control osmotic condition of body fluids but change osmolarity according to the ambient medium; e.g., all marine invertebrates and some freshwater invertebrates.

Animals that maintain internal osmolarity different from the surrounding medium; most vertebrates are strict osmoregulators; exceptions include hagfish (Myxine sp.) and elasmobranch fish (sharks and rays).

topics.name: Abnormal Constituents of Urine — Introduction

subtopics.definition_content: Not present in normal condition. Includes: (1) Protein – If protein is present in urine it may be due to infection or injury in kidney. (Mainly albumin is filtered). (2) Blood – Due to infection and injury of kidney blood may appear in urine. (3) Sugar – In diabetes mellitus sugar appear in urine. (4) Bile or bile pigment – In jaundice bile pigment appear in urine. (5) Ketone bodies – In starvation and diabetes. Ketone bodies appear in urine.
Sugar appear in urine due to hyposecretion of insulin.
Tasteless more urine passing due to hyposecretion of A.D.H.

Drugs that increase the rate of urine flow. Naturally occurring diuretics include caffeine in coffee, tea, and cola soda, which inhibit Na⁺ reabsorption and alcohol in beer, wine and mixed drinks inhibits secretion of ADH.
Stone of the bladder.
Organisms that can tolerate a wide range of salinity.
Organisms that can live within a narrow range of salinity.
Condition characterised by excretion of large amount of homogentistic acid in urine as a result of incomplete metabolism of amino acids. This causes blackening of urine on standing.

key_concepts: The most frequent protozoan seen in urine is Trichomonas vaginalis, a cause of vaginitis in females and urethritis in males.
Both kidney and ureter are retroperitoneal organs.
Urine gives a pungent smell if left for sometime. This smell is of ammonia formed by conversion of urea into ammonia by bacterial action.
A starving person will excrete more urea because during starvation proteins are broken down for energy production.

subtopics.definition_content: The study of bone structure and treatment of bone disorders.
The specialized branch of medicine that deals with preservation and restoration of skeletal system, joints.
Protein present in bones.
Protein present in cartilage.
Study of cartilage.

The hardened tissues of the body together forming the skeleton (sclero = hard). Skeleton of invertebrates is most often secreted on the surface, forming a lifeless or dead exoskeleton, whereas skeleton of vertebrates develops most often underneath the surface forming a living or growing endoskeleton.

Hard and horny keratinized derivatives of epidermal layer of skin, such as claws, most reptilian's scales, bird feathers and mammalian hairs, horns, nails and hoofs. All living amphibians lack an exoskeleton.

Dermal bony skeleton derived from the dermis of skin. Includes bony scales and plates or scutes (osteoderms), finrays and antlers of fishes, some reptiles (crocodiles, turtles and tortoises) and mammals.
Greater part of vertebrate skeleton lying more deeply, forming the endoskeleton. It develops from mesenchyme and is formed by bones in vertebrates.
Bones formed by the ossification of preexisting cartilage. e.g., vertebra, Girdles, limb bones, basioccipital, supraoccipital, sphenoid, Incus, malleus, stapes.
Bones formed by independent ossification in connective tissue. e.g., Ribs, sternum, clavicle, Nasal, vomer, palatine, maxilla.
Bones where ossification takes place on ligament e.g., cotyloid bone of Rabbit, and tendons e.g., Patella, Pisiform.
Bones with hollow spaces containing air. e.g., bones of bird, frontal, sphenoid, ethmoid, maxilla of human.
Part of skull formed by (a) brain box (cranium proper) and (b) two sense capsules - Orbit or optic capsule (eye) and auditory or otic capsule (ear).

A strong and firm bony box with a helmet-like covering over the brain, called vault of skull, and a relatively thicker and stronger floor of base upon which the brain rests. Its cavity is called cranial cavity. Size of cranial cavity averages 1475 cubic centimetres in adult men.
A large opening of the cranial cavity at about the middle of the floor of cranium through which the brain is connected to spinal cord.
Also known as facial skeleton. Includes visceral skeleton formed by pairs of visceral arches: Mandibular arch (I pair), Hyoid arch (II pair) and Branchial arches (III to VII pairs).
Skull in which suspensorium is formed by quadrate. e.g., frog skull.
Skull in which suspensorium is formed by squamosal. e.g., rabbit skull (all mammals).
Fusion bone formed by preotic, epiotic and opisthotic (I, II, III pairs of otic bones) that forms the auditory capsule in mammals.
Three small bones in middle ear: Malleus (hammer) - derived from articular; Incus (anvil) - derived from quadrate; Stapes (stirrup) - derived from hyomandibular.

topics.name: Skull of Man - Cranium and Facial Region

subtopics.definition_content: Skull with two occipital condyles on each side of foramen magnum for articulation with atlas. Found in amphibia and mammals including man.

A horse-shoe shaped bone in the neck between lower jaw and larynx; not articulate to any bone but suspended from temporal bones by ligaments. Supports tongue and provides insertion to some tongue muscles.
A depression in the sphenoid bone of the mammalian skull in which the pituitary body is lodged.
Largest bone of the face and strongest of all bones of the body; forms entire lower jaw and bears all lower jaw teeth. Only skull bone that moves; articulated with temporal bones of skull.

Backbone extending in the mid axis of the back from head to the lower extremity of trunk. Formed by pieces of bones known as vertebrae. It is a modification of notochord. Length is 71 cm in adult male and about 61 cm in adult female.
Thoracic and sacral curves which retain anterior concavity of foetus; anteriorly concave.
Cervical and lumbar curves which are modifications of the original foetal curves; anteriorly convex.
Exaggeration of thoracic curve, resulting in 'round-shouldered' appearance; also called hunch back.
An exaggeration of lumbar curve; also called sway back.
An abnormal lateral curvature in any region of spine.
Vestigeal notochord found in intervertebral disc. Inter-vertebral disc is fibro cartilaginous disc present between centrum of vertebrae.
Vertebrae in which anterior end is concave and posterior end is convex. e.g., 2nd to 7th vertebra of frog, Reptile (Lizard).
Vertebrae in which anterior end is convex and posterior end is concave. e.g., Fishes, snake and crocodile only.
Anteriorly convex from dorsal to ventral and concave from side to side; on posterior side concave from dorsal to ventral and convex from side to side (saddle shaped). e.g., Birds.
No cavity in centrum so centrum is flat. e.g., Mammals (man, Rabbit).
Cavity present on both sides of centrum. e.g., VIIIth vertebra of frog, all vertebrae of scoliodon (Dog fish).
Biconvex with condyle on both sides. e.g., IXth vertebra of frog.

topics.name: Specific Vertebrae - Atlas, Axis, Cervical, Thoracic, Lumbar, Sacrum, Coccyx

subtopics.definition_content: First cervical vertebra. Centrum is absent. Neural spine absent. Transverse process are long with transverse foramen. It supports the globe of the head.
Second cervical vertebra with centrum acoelus. Odontoid process or dens is present, which is modified centrum of Atlas. It is pivot for rotation of atlas and head around odontoid process.
Process present in axis vertebra that is the modified centrum of Atlas; serves as pivot for rotation of atlas and head.
Vertebrartereal canals present in typical cervical vertebrae.
A triangular bone formed by fusion of 5 sacral vertebrae. Fusion normally begins between 16 to 18 years of age and is completed by 30 years of age. Serves as strong foundation for pelvic girdle.
Structure in birds formed by fusion of last thoracic, lumbar, sacral and one or two caudal vertebrae.
Formed by fusion of four coccygeal vertebrae; the last section of backbone; a small triangular bone. Fusion generally occurs between 20 and 30 years of age.
Upper seven pairs of ribs (1st to 7th) attached in front directly to the sternum by hyaline cartilage.
Next three pairs (8th, 9th, 10th) of ribs whose costal cartilage attach indirectly to sternum, connecting to costal cartilage of 7th rib.
The lower two pairs (11th and 12th) of ribs that are free in front and do not even reach the sternum.
One of two facets by which a rib articulates with thoracic vertebrae (present in first nine ribs along with tuberculum).
A bone of chest made up of manubrium (presternum), mesosternum (5 sternebrae) and xiphoid process (metasternum). In male it is nearly 17 cm long. Absent in fish, Turtle.
A cartilaginous plate (hyaline) attached with xiphisternum in mammals.
Consists of Skull + Vertebral column + Sternum + Ribs.
Consists of Girdle + Limb bones.
Large, flattened and triangular cartilage bone forming the main part of each half of pectoral girdle (Os innominate). Has acromian spine, acromian process, metacromian process and coracoid process.
A deep, cup-like concavity located at the end of scapula close to coracoid process; the head of humerus (bone of upper arm) fits into this cavity.
Long and slender, rod-like membrane bone articulated with the acromian process of scapula. The other end is connected with presternum by means of an elastic ligament.
Stout hip bone formed by fusion of three bones: upper ilium, lower ischium and inner pubis.
Cup-shaped depression in innominate where head of femur articulates; formed by ilium, ischium and pubis (in mammals pubis is replaced by cotyloid bone).
Large oval gap below the acetabulum in the innominate bone.
Bowl formed by two innominate bones and sacrum together, supporting the lower abdominal viscera. Adaptation for upright posture.
Cartilaginous joint present between the two pubic bones at the ventral midline; present in mammals.

Longest and largest bone of upper limb (arm bone). Articulates proximally with scapula (glenoid cavity) and distally at the elbow with both ulna and radius. Has greater and lesser tuberosity, deltoid tuberosity and trochlea.
Groove at the distal end of humerus at the elbow joint; the base is marked by a supratrochlear foramen for passage of brachial artery and nerve.
Proximal projection of ulna which forms prominence of elbow.
Cup-like receptacle in ulna formed by olecranon process for articulation with the distal end (trochlea) of humerus.
Eight wrist bones arranged in 2 rows with 4 bones in each row, joined to one another by ligaments.
Longest and strongest bone of body; also called bone of thigh. Has greater trochanter, lesser trochanter, 3rd trochanter and patellar groove. Head has fovea capitis (depression).
Sesamoid bone forming knee cap; formed by ossification in tendon; forms the prominence of knee joint.
Thumb of foot.
Central shaft of a long bone.
Immovable joints with no joint cavity and no movement possible.
Fixed or fibrous joints found between skull bones; articulating bones held together by white fibrous tissue.
Type of fibrous joint in which cone shaped peg fits into socket. e.g., Teeth in mandibles and maxillary bones.
Type of fibrous joint with more fibrous tissue than sutures. e.g., distal articulation between tibia and fibula.

Imperfect joints in which synovial cavity is absent; permit a small amount of movement; fibrocartilage is placed between the bones. e.g., Pubic symphysis, between bodies of the vertebrae, between manubrium and body of sternum.

topics.name: Freely Movable Joints (Diarthrosis) and Types of Synovial Joints

subtopics.definition_content: Perfect freely movable joints with synovial cavity and ligaments. Articular cartilage (hyaline) covers articular surfaces; synovial membrane secretes synovial fluid that lubricates and provides nourishment to articular cartilage.
Fluid secreted by synovial membrane that lubricates the joint and provides nourishment to articular cartilage. In old age, stiffness of joints is due to decrease in synovial fluid.
Ball of one bone articulates in socket of another bone. e.g., head of humerus and glenoid cavity of pectoral girdle; femur and acetabulum of pelvic girdle; joint between incus and stapes.
Movement is possible in one direction only. e.g., Joint of malleus and incus, knee joint, elbow joint, articulation joint of lower jaw, joint of phalanges of digits.
Helps in turning movement; one bone is fixed and second articulates. e.g., Atlas and axis (atlanto-axial joint).
Limited movement in all directions. e.g., Tarsal bones of ankle, zygapophysis of vertebrae, Radius and Ulna.
Ball and socket-like joint but not fully developed. e.g., metacarpal of thumb and carpals of hand.
Biaxial joint where oval condyle of one bone fits into an elliptical cavity of another bone. e.g., joint between radius and carpals at wrist.
Joint between skull and atlas which allows nodding movement.
Injury to a joint capsule, typically involving a stretching or tearing of tendons or ligaments. Both these structures have much poorer regenerative power than bone.
Inflammation of the joints; a common disease of old age with symptoms of pain and stiffness in the joints.

Secretion of the lubricating synovial fluid between the bones at the joint stops; smooth cartilage covering the ends of the bones wears out and is replaced by uneven bony spurs; joint becomes inflamed and painful. Stiffness or fixation of a joint is called ankylosis.

Chronic painful inflammation of the synovial membranes of many joints simultaneously, usually starting in the small joints in the hand; involves erosion of joints. Usually starts at the age of 20-40 years; affects women more often than men.

Inherited disorder of purine metabolism occurring especially in men; body forms excess amounts of uric acid and crystals of sodium urate are deposited in synovial joints, giving rise to severe arthritis. Generally affects the great toe.

Reduction in bone tissue mass causing weakness of skeletal strength (G.osteon = bone, poros = pore, osis = condition). Results from excessive resorption of calcium and phosphorus from the bone; leads to vertical compression or crush fracture of vertebrae and fracture of neck of femur.
Displacement of bones from their normal positions at a joint; accompanied by pulling or even tearing of the ligaments.
Displacement of vertebrae and the intervertebral fibrocartilage disc from their normal position; may result from mechanical injury or defects of ligaments holding the vertebrae together.
Fracture in which the bone is partially bent and partially broken, as when a greenstick breaks; occurs only in children.
Bone breaks into more than two pieces (smaller fragments between two main fragments).
Bone breaks completely but a fragment pierces out through the skin.
A small piece breaks off fully from the bone but remains attached to the ligament.
Inflammation of the bursae present in the joints; caused by physical injury or constant pressure on a single joint for a long time.
Inflammation of bone due to pus producing organism; may remain localised or spread inside involving various parts including bone marrow.

topics.name: Action and Classification of Body Muscles

subtopics.definition_content: Muscles arranged in opposing pairs; one muscle moves a body part in one direction by its contraction and the other moves that part in the opposite direction. When one muscle contracts, its opposing muscle relaxes.
Muscles that act together to produce a movement.
Muscles that act most powerfully during any given movement.
Muscle that bends one part over another joint.
Antagonist of flexor; its contraction extends a joint by pulling one of the articulating bones apart from another.
Muscle whose contraction rotates the forearm to turn the palm downward or backward.
Antagonist of pronator; contracts to rotate the forearm so that palm faces upward or forward.
Muscle that contracts to draw a bone away from the body midline; abduction is elevation.
Muscle that draws a bone towards the body midline; adduction is depression.
Muscle that pulls the lower jaw, tongue and the head forward.
Antagonist of protractor; draws the lower jaw, tongue and the head backward.
Turning of feet so that the soles face one another.
Opposite of inversion; soles of the feet face laterally.

topics.name: Important Muscles of the Human Body

subtopics.definition_content: Calf muscle; shank (principal calf muscle in leg of tetrapod) muscle; also called trijection muscle (deltoid) in context of arm.
Longest muscle in the human body.
Largest muscle in the human body; buttock muscles.
Smallest muscle in the human body (also called arrector pilli).
The state of body stiffening after death due to non-separation of actin and myosin filaments caused by non-availability of ATP.
The passage of lactic acid produced in muscles into liver where 80% of it is changed to glucose/glycogen for continued supply to muscles. The remaining 20% is oxidised.

topics.name: Muscular Movement and Types of Locomotion

subtopics.definition_content: Movement of an animal as a whole from one place to another (locus = place + moveo = to move).
Study of movement (G. Kinein = to move, Logos = study).
Adaptations for running locomotion. e.g., Horse, flightless birds.
Adaptations for hopping locomotion. e.g., Frog, rabbit.
Adaptations for swimming locomotion. e.g., Fish, whale.
Adaptations for flying locomotion. e.g., Insects, birds.
The streaming of cells in the early embryo to form tissues or organs; may be considered a form of locomotion.
Movement caused by cilia; e.g., cilia in trachea propel dust particles, in vasa efferentia propel sperms, in oviducts propel eggs, flame cells of flatworms push excretory materials.

Movement caused by flagella; e.g., flagella of choanocytes of sponges maintain water current, flagella of cells of gastrodermis of Hydra help in circulation of food, sperms move by flagellar movement.
Movement by pseudopodia; e.g., leucocytes and macrophages move by pseudopodial movement.
Streaming movement of the cytoplasm (cytoplasmic streaming movement) observed in most of the cells.

topics.name: Nervous systems in invertebrates and vertebrates

subtopics.definition_content: The system meant to perceive stimuli detected by the receptors, to transmit these to various body parts, and to effect responses through effectors. In vertebrates it plays three vital roles: (1) Sensory function - senses changes within and outside the body; (2) Integrative functions - analyses sensory information, stores some aspects, and makes decisions; (3) Motor functions - responds to stimuli by initiating muscular contractions or glandular secretions.

chapters.name: Development of central nervous system in human

subtopics.definition_content: A longitudinal mid-dorsal ectodermal thickening of the embryo from which the central nervous system develops. This neural plate or neural groove is converted by fusion into a closed mid-dorsal longitudinal neural tube lying above the notochord.

The embryonic brain formed by enlargement of the anterior end of the embryonic neural tube. By differential growth and two constrictions, it is divided into three primary cerebral vesicles: forebrain (prosencephalon), midbrain (mesencephalon), and hindbrain (rhombencephalon).
Formed of cell-bodies, non-medullated nerve fibres, neuroglia, dendrites of association neurons and motor neurons.
Formed of medullated nerve fibres or myelinated axon of motor and sensory neurons, which appear white due to the presence of medullary sheath.

Connective tissue membranes which surround the brain and spinal cord of CNS. In mammals, CNS is covered by three meninges (cranial meninges) in order from superficial to deeper: duramater, arachnoid and piamater. Duramater is nonvascular, tough, fibrous connective tissue. Arachnoid mater is made up of reticular connective tissue. Innermost vascular piamater is made up of loose areolar connective tissue.

Lymph-like extracellular fluid secreted by the choroid plexuses by filtration of blood, found in ventricles of the brain and central canal of the spinal cord. It provides: (i) protection to brain from mechanical shocks; (ii) optimum physiological fluid environment for neural functions; (iii) relief mechanism for increase in intracranial pressure; (iv) sink-like facility for metabolites of brain; (v) nourishment to CNS. Rate of formation is 20 ml/h (480 ml/day). Total amount present is 80-150 ml. CSF is cell free, slightly alkaline, and isotonic to plasma.
The enlargement of head, a pathological condition characterized by an abnormal accumulation of cerebrospinal fluid resulting in headache, vomiting, pain and stiffness of the neck.

Infection and inflammation of meninges or injury of meninges. Infection may be viral, bacterial or both.
