The most common cause is infection of Streptococcus pneumoniae, Neisseria meningitidis and Haemophilus influenzae. Lumbar puncture is done for drainage of excess cerebrospinal fluid during meningitis.

topics.name: Fore brain (Prosencephalon) - Cerebrum, Basal ganglia, Limbic system

subtopics.definition_content: The peripheral portion of each cerebral hemisphere formed of grey matter. It is the highest centre for many sensations and activities, 2-4 mm thick (thickest at pre-central gyrus, 4.5 mm). Phylogenetically divided into allocortex/paleocortex, mesocortex, and neocortex/neopallium. Neocortex is most recent, having 6 layers of neurons.

A thick band of transverse nerve fibres of white matter that interconnects the two cerebral hemispheres. Present only in eutherian mammals. Has anterior part genu, middle part trunchus and last part splenium.

Several groups of nuclei in each cerebral hemisphere. Corpus striatum is the largest nucleus, consisting of caudate nucleus and lenticular nucleus (subdivided into putamen and globus pallidus). Caudate and putamen control large automatic movements of skeletal muscle; globus pallidus controls muscle tone for specific body movements.

Present on inner border of cerebrum and floor of diencephalon; also called emotional brain or animal brain. Controls emotion and animal behaviour such as chewing, licking, sniffing, docility, tameness, affection, rage, pain, pleasure, anger, sexual feelings, fear, sorrow and grooming. Structures include cingulate gyrus, hippocampal gyrus, amygdaloid body, olfactory bulb, mammillary body, dentate gyrus and anterior nucleus of thalamus.

Forms the roof of the third ventricle (diocoel). Not formed of nervous tissue; consists of piamater only. Its anterior part is vascular and folded, called anterior choroid plexus. Bears the pineal stalk and pineal body at its tip, and paired habenular nuclei.

A pair of masses of grey matter forming the superior part of lateral walls of the third ventricle. Comprises 80% of diencephalon. Principal relay station for sensory impulses reaching the cerebral cortex from spinal cord, brain stem and cerebellum. Allows crude appreciation of pain, temperature and pressure. Contains medial geniculate nucleus (hearing), lateral geniculate nucleus (vision) and ventral posterior nucleus (taste, touch, pressure, vibration, heat, cold, pain).

Forms the floor of diencephalon, visible in the ventral view of the brain. Contains many hypothalamic nuclei. Gives a nervous process called infundibulum (forms pars nervosa) which meets hypophysis to form the pituitary gland. Centre for hunger, thirst, sweating, sleep, fatigue, temperature, anger, pleasure, love and hate, and satisfaction. Controls ANS and releases factors for endocrine glands.

A pine cone-shaped gland located in the centre of brain. Innervated by sympathetic nerves. Has a photosensory role in amphibians and primitive reptiles ('Third eye'). Pinealocytes secrete melatonin which is anti-FSH and anti-LH, inhibiting reproductive function. Melatonin secretions decrease after puberty.

Four optic lobes in mammals (two superior colliculi and two inferior colliculi) present on dorsal side of mid brain (tectum). Superior colliculi are concerned with reflex action of eye, head and neck in response to visual stimulus. Inferior colliculi are concerned with movement of head and trunk in response to hearing stimulus.

A pair of thick bands of longitudinal nerve fibres present on the floor or ventral side of mid brain. The dorsal part (white matter) is called tegmentum while the most ventral part (grey matter) is called crura cerebrae. Contain ascending and descending tracts connecting upper and lower regions of brain.

topics.name: Hind brain - Cerebellum, Medulla oblongata, Pons Varolii

subtopics.definition_content: Second largest portion of brain, lies posterior to medulla and pons. Butterfly-shaped, consists of central constricted vermis, lateral cerebellar hemispheres (with anterior and posterior lobe), and flocculo-nodular lobe (sense of equilibrium). Superficial cerebellar cortex has grey matter in ridges called folia. Deep white matter is called Arbor vitae (tree of life). Receives sensory impulses from proprioceptors; coordinates skeletal muscle contractions and regulates posture and balance.

The hindmost and posterior-most part of brain. Cavity is known as IVth ventricle (metacoel). On ventral surface are ascending and descending tracts called pyramids; their crossing is called decussation of pyramids. Contains nuclei of origin of 5 pairs of cranial nerves (VIII, IX, X, XI, XII). Centre for heartbeat, respiration, digestion, blood pressure, gut peristalsis, swallowing, secretion of glands, and involuntary functions like vomiting, coughing, sneezing, hiccuping.

An oval mass of white matter lying above the medulla oblongata, consists mainly of nerve fibres interconnecting parts of brain (bridge connecting spinal cord with brain). Contains pneumotaxic area and apneustic area. Together with medullary rhythmicity area, helps in controlling respiration.
White matter tree-like structure deep to the grey matter (cerebellar cortex) in cerebellum. Also called 'tree of life'.

topics.name: Cavities or ventricles of the brain
Structure and anatomy of spinal cord

subtopics.definition_content: The last tapering end of the spinal cord. Its ciliated central canal is called the Vth ventricle.

A horse tail-like collection of roots of spinal nerves at the inferior end of the spinal cord. Spinal nerves arising from the inferior part of the cord angle inferiorly in the vertebral canal like wisps of hair.
Extension of piamater below conus medullaris up to coccyx.

The last dilation of subarachnoid space below 1st lumbar vertebra. Proper site for lumbar puncture or spinal tap for drainage of CSF (5 to 10 ml), used in diagnosing diseases of CNS like meningitis, encephalitis, intracranial pressure and meningococcal infection.

topics.name: Components of reflex action and Reflex arc

subtopics.definition_content: Fast, predictable, automatic responses to change the environment. First studied by Marshal Hall (1833). Best and Taylor defined it as 'simplest form of irritability associated with the nervous system... an immediate involuntary response to a stimulus.' Reflex actions are involuntary, not under conscious control of the brain. Central nervous system is responsible for the control of reflex action.

Formed by the neurons forming the pathway taken by the nerve impulses in reflex action. Components: (i) receptor organs; (ii) sensory neurons (afferent neurons) - in the ganglion on the dorsal side of spinal cord; (iii) association neurons (integrating centre/internuncial neurons) - in spinal cord; (iv) motor neurons - in ventral horn of spinal cord; (v) effector organs (muscles and glands).
The simplest reflex found in vertebrates. The sensory neuron synapses directly on to the motor neuron cell body. The reflex action takes place without the involvement of the brain.

An inborn, unlearned reflex to a stimulus. Mostly protective in function. Examples: knee jerk (patellar reflex), corneal reflex (blinking), rapid withdrawal of hand when burned or pricked, coughing, sneezing and yawning.

Not inborn, but acquired and dependent on past experience, training and learning. First demonstrated by Russian physiologist Ivan Petrovitch Pavlov (1846-1936) in a hungry dog. Examples: learning of dancing, cycling, swimming, singing, driving.

Arise from grey matter of spinal cord. There are 31 pairs of spinal nerves in man (37 pairs in rabbit). All spinal nerves are mixed. Divided into 5 groups: Cervical (C) - 8 pairs, Thoracic (T) - 12 pairs, Lumbar (L) - 5 pairs, Sacral (S) - 5 pairs, Coccygeal (Co) - 1 pair. Each spinal nerve arises by 2 roots: dorsal (afferent/sensory) root with dorsal root ganglion, and ventral (efferent/motor) root with no ganglion.

Automatically regulates the activities of smooth muscles, cardiac muscles and glands. Entirely motor (all autonomic axons are efferent fibres). Operates without conscious control. Discovered by Langley. Regulated by centres in the brain like cerebral cortex, hypothalamus and medulla oblongata. On the basis of transmitter produced, fibres are classified as cholinergic (release acetylcholine) or adrenergic (release norepinephrine). Consists of two divisions: sympathetic (thoracolumbar outflow) and parasympathetic (craniosacral outflow).

A typical neuron consists of a nucleated cell body (= cyton, soma or perikaryon), five to seven short, slender and branched (= arborized) dendrites, and a single, relatively thicker and longer fibrous axon. The latter is definitioninally branched into short telodendria. Each telodendron bears a definitioninal knob or button. The dendrites and cytons receive impulses; axons conduct impulses away from the receiving parts.
A wave of reversed polarity or depolarization (action potential) moving down an axon.

topics.name: Mechanism of conduction of nerve impulse

subtopics.definition_content: The potential difference across the plasma membrane in a resting nerve fibre. Sodium ions (Na+) predominate in extracellular fluid and potassium ions (K+) predominate in intracellular fluid. Na+ are 10 times more outside; K+ ions are 25 times more inside. The plasma membrane is electrically positive outside and negative inside. This potential averages -70 mV (-40 to -90 mV) in inner side of membrane. State is called polarized state.

When the nerve fibre is stimulated, the membrane becomes permeable to sodium ions, becomes negatively charged on the outside and positively charged on the inside. This wave of depolarization travelling down a nerve fibre is called action potential. The action potential of +45 mV on inner side of axolemma is also called spike potential. Most accepted mechanism is ionic theory proposed by Hodgkin and Huxley.

After depolarization, membrane becomes less permeable to sodium ions while permeability to potassium ions increases. Sodium ions are pumped out and potassium ions are pumped in (by sodium-potassium pump) until original resting state is achieved, making membrane negative inside and positive outside. The period during repolarization when the nerve cannot be stimulated again is called the refractory period.

An active transport mechanism (also called sodium potassium exchange pump or sodium pump) that expels sodium ions and draws in potassium ions against concentration and electrochemical gradient during repolarization.

An area of functional contact between one neuron and another for the purpose of transferring information. Usually found between fine definitioninal branches of the axon of one neuron and the dendrites or cell body of another (axo-dendrite synapse). definition first used by Sir Charles Sherrington (1861-1954). Consists of presynaptic knob with synaptic vesicles, presynaptic membrane, synaptic cleft (about 200 Å across), and postsynaptic membrane with receptor sites. Acts as a one-way valve - impulse can only travel from axon to dendron.

The first neurotransmitter to be isolated, obtained by Otto Loewi in 1920 from endings of parasympathetic neurons of the vagus nerve in frog heart. Released at cholinergic synapses. Hydrolysed by enzyme acetylcholinesterase (AChE) into acetate and choline which are reabsorbed into the synaptic knob and resynthesized.

Transmission of an impulse across a synapse is slower than its conduction along a neuron due to time needed for release of neurotransmitter, its diffusion through the synaptic cleft, and its action on the postsynaptic membrane. Amounts to about half a millisecond at body temperature (37°C).

A temporary suspension of impulse transmission in the region of synapse due to exhaustion of its neurotransmitter. Lasts for several seconds during which the neurotransmitter is resynthesized. The only fatigue that affects the nervous tissue.

Proposed by Keith Lucas (1905). When stimulated, the axon membrane does not respond momentarily due to its threshold. When the threshold is broken, stimulation is conducted through its whole length as a strong impulse. If stimulation is too weak to break the threshold, impulse is not established; if much more than threshold, impulse conduction remains normal.

Receptors that receive stimulation directly from the external environment. Types include: photoreceptor (light/eye), thigmoreceptor (touch), phonoreceptor (sound/ear), olfactoreceptor (smell/nose), gustoreceptor (taste/tongue), thermoreceptor (temperature), galvano receptor (electric current), rheoreceptor (water/air current), geo receptor (gravity). Receptors of vision, hearing and smell (telereceptors) receive stimuli from a distance.
Located in skeletal muscles, joints, tendons. Enable awareness of the position of arm or leg without looking at it.
Present in internal organs. Examples: receptors for hunger, thirst, pain and balancing.
Present in skin around the base of hair and feather. Sensitive to both touch and pressure.
Found in deep layer of dermis; sensitive to pressure.

An olfactoreceptor concerned with smell. First appears in amphibians and well developed in snake, lizard and sphenodon. Structure is blind sac-like and lined by olfactory epithelium (Shneiderian membrane). Not found in rabbit; vestigeal in man.
Thermoreceptor sensitive to temperature and pain (frigido receptor). Found in lips, tongue, conjunctiva of eyes and corium of finger.
Thermoreceptor sensitive to temperature and mainly related with heat.

Oudefinitionost opaque, fibrous and non-vascular layer of the eyeball, easily seen as the white of the eye. Made of dense connective tissue (collagen fibres and fibroblasts). Covers entire eyeball except cornea; gives shape to eyeball.
Transparent round window in the centre of sclerotic layer. Maximum refraction occurs here, placing the image approximately on the retina.
Highly vascular posterior portion of the middle vascular tunic (uvea) of the eye. Highly pigmented; supplies nutrients to the eye.

A circular, shelf-like diaphragm formed by inward turn of the vascular tunic beyond the ciliary body. The colour of the iris dedefinitionines the colour of the eye (brown, black, blue or green). In albinos, iris is deficient of pigments.

Colourless, transparent and fibrous crystalline structure made up of protein (alpha and beta crystalline protein), enclosed in lens membrane. Ectodermal in origin. Lodged in eyeball by suspensory ligament (Zonula of Zinn) of ciliary body. Biconvex in man; elliptical (subspherical) in frog.

Watery fluid filling the outer aqueous chamber (divided into larger anterior and smaller posterior chamber) of the eye, formed by ciliary body. Continuously absorbed into blood and replaced. Contains most diffusible substances of plasma. Refractive index 1.33 D.
Jelly-like substance filling the inner vitreous chamber. Contains 99% water, some salt, a little mucoprotein (vitrein) and hyaluronic acid. Not absorbed or replaced. Refractive index 1.34 D.

Innermost, thin and transparent layer. Purplish red due to eye pigment rhodopsin (visual purple) in rods. Made up of four layers: (a) cuboidal pigmented epithelium (towards choroid); (b) layer of rods and cones; (c) layer of bipolar neurons; (d) layer of ganglia (innermost, towards vitreous chamber).
A small shallow depression within the yellow spot (macula lutea) in the area centralis of retina. The most sensitive part of the eye and area of most acute vision. Contains only cone cells.

The region where the optic nerve turns towards the outer side, pierces through the whole thickness of the wall of eyeball, forming an optic foramen. This region has no retina, does not take part in image formation.

topics.name: Biochemistry of eye - Rod cells and Cone cells

subtopics.definition_content: A purplish pigment (scotopsin + 11-cis retinal) present in rod cells. Functions in dim light and at night. Vitamin A is needed for its formation. Bright light splits rhodopsin into scotopsin and retinal (bleaching). In the dark, rhodopsin is resynthesized (dark adaptation). There are 120 million rods in human eye.

Pigment present in cone cells made up of photopsin + retinal. Sensitive to colour. Three types of cones: erythrolabe (sensitive to red, 775 nm), cyanolabe (sensitive to blue, 430 nm) and chlorolabe (sensitive to green, 535 nm). There are 6 million cones in the human eye.

The change in strength of the lens to focus on objects at different distances. When viewing near objects: ciliary muscles contract (no tension on suspensory ligament), lens becomes thick, refraction increased. When viewing distant objects: ciliary muscles relax (maximum tension on suspensory ligament), lens becomes thin, refraction decreased. Ageing causes loss of accommodation (presbyopia).

Both eyes focused on the same object from slightly different angles, with overlapping visual fields and foveae focused on the same object. Provides depth to images (stereoscopic or 3D effect) and enables correct distance judgement. Found in man, primates, owl and cat.
Near objects are clear, far objects are not clear. Eyeball becomes longer. Image is formed before the retina. Corrected by concave lens.
Far objects are clear, near objects are not clear. Eyeball becomes short. Image is formed behind the retina. Corrected by convex lens.
Curvature of cornea becomes irregular and image is not clearly formed. Corrected by cylindrical lens.
Due to defective protein metabolism. Lens or cornea (sometimes both) become opaque. Operation is needed.
Due to increase in intraocular pressure in aqueous chamber due to blockage of Schlemm's canal. Operation is needed at early stage.
Power of accommodation of lens decreases due to age factor and defective metabolism. Also known as age-sightedness. Corrected by bifocal lens.
Present at the junction of auditory meatus and tympanic cavity. Vibrates at the same frequency as sound waves and transmits vibrations across the tympanic cavity via ear ossicles.

A chain of three small movable bones crossing the tympanic cavity: Malleus (hammer-shaped, modification of articular bone of lower jaw), Incus (anvil-shaped, modification of quadrate bone) and Stapes (stirrup-shaped, modification of hyomandibular/columella). Stapes is the smallest bone of the body. Known as H.A.S. in humans. In frog only stapes is present.

Made up of elastic cartilage, connects middle ear to nasopharynx. Maintains equilibrium of pressure on both sides of the tympanic membrane. Blocking of Eustachian tube impairs hearing due to imperfect vibrations of drum. Normally closed, opens during swallowing and yawning.

The internal ear enclosed by bony labyrinth (formed by periotic bone/petrous). Perilymphatic space (filled with perilymph) is between membranous and bony labyrinth. Endolymph is found in membranous labyrinth. Consists of two parts: vestibule (utriculus and sacculus with semicircular canals) and cochlea.

Sensory spot in each ampulla of semicircular canals for detecting rotational/angular acceleration. Contains sensory cells and a dome-shaped gelatinous cupula (without otoliths). Three cristae are present (one per semicircular canal).
Group of sensory cells present in utriculus and sacculus. Two maculae are present in man. Contains specific receptor cells with otoliths. Helps in static equilibrium and linear acceleration.

A spirally coiled tube (2-3 coiling, 2¾ turns in man) connected to sacculus. Divided into three chambers: scala vestibuli (upper, perilymph, connected to middle ear by oval window/fenestra ovalis), scala media/real cochlear duct (middle, endolymph), and scala tympani (lower, perilymph, connected to middle ear by round window/fenestra rotundus). Separated by Reissner's membrane (between SM and SV) and basilar membrane (between SM and ST).

Discovered by Italian anatomist Alfonso Corti. Present on basilar membrane. Contains inner hair cells (in one layer, ~3500) and outer hair cells (in 3-4 rows, ~20,000) as receptor cells; and supporting cells. Inner hair cells respond to velocity of movement of basilar membrane; outer hair cells are primarily concerned with displacement of basilar membrane by sound waves. Tectorial membrane is a leaf-like gelatinous structure at dorsal side of organ of Corti.

Present in endolymph of utriculus and sacculus, made up of protein and calcium carbonate. When the head position changes, otoliths press upon the sensory hairs of the maculae, stimulating sensory cells to generate nerve impulses for static equilibrium.

topics.name: Mechanism of sound perception - Hearing and Equilibrium

subtopics.definition_content: Detected by maculae in utriculus and sacculus. Detects changes in head (or body) position with respect to gravity and linear acceleration. With a change in body position, the otoliths press upon sensory hairs of the maculae stimulating sensory cells. Macula of utricle responds to vertical movements of head; macula of saccule responds to lateral (sideways) movement.

Detected by cristae in semicircular canal ampullae. Detects turning or rotational movements of the head (angular acceleration). Three semicircular ducts are arranged in three different planes so a movement in any direction stimulates at least one crista.

topics.name: Defects of ear and Tips and Tricks

subtopics.definition_content: Loss of hearing due to defect in cochlea. Nystagmus (uncontrolled oscillation of the eyes) is one of the symptoms.
Acute infection of middle ear.
These glands have ducts for discharging their secretions. Therefore, they are called as duct glands. ex – Liver, Sweat gland, Sebaceous gland, Gastric glands and some intestinal glands.
These glands lack ducts and pass secretions into the surrounding blood directly. Therefore they are called as ductless glands. ex – Thyroid, parathyroid, adrenal, pituitary, pineal body and thymus.

These glands consist of both exocrine and endocrine tissue. The exocrine discharge its secretion by a duct and the endocrine tissue discharges its secretion into the blood. Pancreas and gonads are heterocrine glands. These are also called mixed glands.
Hormones are informational molecules secreted by the endocrine cells in one part of the body and carried by blood to another part where they stimulate or inhibit specific physiological process.
First hormone discovered was secretin. It was discovered by two English physiologists: William M Bayliss and Ernest H. Starling in 1903.
definition hormone was coined by Starling (1905) from Greek word Homone means to excite. It is a misnomer because a number of hormones are known to have inhibitory effect (e.g., Somatostatin).
Some hormones secreted in inactive form called Prohormone e.g., Pro-insulin.
These are derived from tyrosine amino acid and have amino (−NH₂) group e.g., Thyroxine, Epinephrine, Nor-epinephrine.

These are fat soluble and have sterol group. These are derived from cholesterol e.g., hormones of adrenal cortex (cortisol, cortisone, corticosterone, aldosterone) testes (testosterone) and ovaries (oestrone, oestradiol, progesterone etc.).

These are formed of 3–200 amino acids interlinked by peptide bonds and are water soluble e.g., (a) Proteinaceous hormones like STH, TSH, FSH, LH etc. Out of these FSH and LH are glycoproteins. (b) Long peptide hormones like insulin and glucagon, ACTH, Parathormone. (c) Short peptide hormones like oxytocin, ADH, MSH.

Receptor always located on outer surface of plasma membrane of target cell. Hormone receptor complex activates membrane enzyme adenyl cyclase which hydrolyses ATP into cyclic AMP. c-AMP acts as secondary messenger and activates inactive enzyme system by cascade effect. Includes proteinaceous, peptide and amine hormones.

These hormones are small sized so are diffusable through the plasma membrane. Bind proteinaceous receptor in cytosol. Operate through de-novo synthesis of m-RNA by activation of certain genes. Includes steroid hormones of testes, ovary and adrenal cortex.

chapters.name: Mechanism of Hormone Action and Feedback Control

topics.name: Mechanism of Hormone Action on Cell Surface

subtopics.definition_content: The hormone is called first messenger in the cAMP mechanism of hormone action.

Mechanism of formation of cAMP was discovered by E.W. Sutherland in 1950. The hormone receptor complex causes the release of an enzyme adenylyl cyclase from the receptor site. This enzyme hydrolyse the ATP into c-AMP. The c-AMP activates the existing enzyme system of the cell. This accelerates the metabolic reactions in cell. The hormone is called first messenger and the c-AMP is definitioned the second messenger.

Receptor proteins of some hormones are large transmembrane intrinsic proteins acting as ion channels for facilitated diffusion of Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺ etc. On binding with specific hormone, receptor proteins undergo conformational changes, altering membrane permeability for ions. e.g., insulin promotes entry of glucose from blood into muscle cells by increasing permeability of sarcolemma to glucose.

subtopics.definition: Hormone action within a cell (steroid hormones)
subtopics.definition_content: Steroid hormones act within the cell. Their small, lipid soluble molecules pass through cell membrane and bind to specific receptor molecules in the cytoplasm. Receptor molecules carry them into nucleus where receptor-hormone complex binds to a specific receptor site on chromosome and activates certain repressed genes. Activated gene transcribes m-RNA which directs synthesis of enzyme (protein molecule) in cytoplasm.

Decrease and increase in the circulating amount of a hormone has a directly inverse effect on the secretion of hormone. This is known as the 'pull and push' or 'feed-back control' mechanism of hormonal secretion.

Thyroid stimulating hormone (T.S.H.) stimulates the thyroid gland to secrete thyroxine hormone. A high amount of thyroxine in the blood exerts an inhibitory effect on pituitary to secrete less T.S.H. This eventually results in decrease in thyroxine. This is called 'Direct feedback control'.

Oxytocin released by posterior pituitary gland stimulate contraction of uterus during child birth. As the contraction of uterus progresses, more and more of oxytocin is released. This is called positive feed back control.
Thomas Addison is called as father of endocrinology.
definition 'endocrine' was first used by Claude Bernard.
Walter canon stated that the hormones maintain homeostasis in the body.

topics.name: Position, Origin and Structure of Pituitary Gland

subtopics.definition_content: Pituitary gland is the smallest (about 1 to 1½ cm in diameter) endocrine gland of the body. It is pea-shaped, ovoid, reddish brown gland situated at the base of the brain in a cavity, hypophyseal fossa of the sella turcica of sphenoid bone. It is connected by a short stalk called Infundibulum, to the ventral wall (Hypothalamus) of diencephalon. It weight about 0.5 to 1 gm. It controls most of the endocrine glands. Hence, it is also called leader of endocrine orchestra or master gland.

Adenohypophysis arises as hypophyseal or Rathke's pouch from dorsal wall of embryonic stomodeum. It is the anterior lobe of pituitary. Consists of Pars distalis, Pars tuberalis and Pars indefinitionedia. Ectodermal in origin.
The neurohypophysis (Pars nervosa or Posterior lobe) forms as an outgrowth from the infundibulum of the floor of hypothalamus. Consists of Pars nervosa and Infundibulum.
Found in adenohypophysis of pituitary. These are not stained by acid and base dye. Pigment granules are absent. These are colourless and may change into chromophils.
A type of chromophil cell found in adenohypophysis. Synthesize and secrete growth hormone and prolactin.
A type of chromophil cell found in adenohypophysis. Synthesize and secrete TSH, ACTH, FSH, LH and MSH hormones.
Herring bodies are dilated definitioninal portion of Neurosecretory axon constituting hypothalamohypophyseal tract. They are hormone precursors for oxytocin and vasopressin.

Veins that drain blood from hypothalamus run into pars distalis through pars tuberalis and divide into capillaries, called portal hypophyseal veins. These constitute a hypothalamo-hypophyseal portal system. Hypothalamic hormones reach anterior pituitary by this portal system.

Molecules of this hormone are polypeptides of 191 amino acid monomers. It is the major hormone in the secretion of anterior pituitary and most important stimulant of proper normal growth of body. Promotes biosynthesis of DNA, RNA and proteins in all body cells (anabolic growth factor). Stimulates cellular growth and proliferation, growth and repair of bones, muscles and connective tissue. In liver cells promotes glycogenesis, deamination and gluconeogenesis.

Growth hormone triggers synthesis of certain special, insulin-like growth factors (IGFs) in cells of many tissues, such as liver, muscles, cartilages, bones, etc. These growth factors are called somatomedins.

GH-release hormone (GHRH), also called somatocrinin, promotes secretion of growth hormone. GH-inhibitory hormone (GHIH), also called somatostatin, retards secretion of growth hormone by the anterior pituitary.

Hyposecretion of growth hormone in childhood results into blunted growth of body. Growth of all organs is retarded. Growth of bones at their epiphyseal ends stops. The body remains a dwarf. This pituitary dwarfism is called nanism or ateliosis. The dwarfs (midgets) of circuses are pituitary dwarfs; unlike thyroid cretins, brain development is normal but they are infertile.

Hypersecretion of growth hormone during growth period (childhood and adolescence) causes excessive growth of all body parts, resulting into a symmetrically giant body. This is called proportionate gigantism.

Oversecretion of growth hormone after growth period. Long bones do not grow in length (due to closed epiphyseal plate) but bones of hands, feet, lower jaw and rib cage thicken. Eyelids, lips, tongue, nose, chin also enlarge. Soles, palms and forehead become wrinkled. Body becomes ugly like a gorilla. Common in men, rare in women.
In some cases of hypersecretion of growth hormone, the backbone bends and thickens, causing hunchback condition (kyphosis).

Secreted by lactotroph cells of anterior pituitary. Molecules are polypeptides of 198 amino acid monomers. Secretion enhanced by PRH and suppressed by PIH (dopamine) of hypothalamus. Main physiological effect: activate growth of breasts during pregnancy and secretion of milk by mammary glands after childbirth – referred to as 'maternity hormone'. In pigeons and doves, stimulates crop epithelial cells to secrete 'pigeon milk'.

A glycoprotein, polypeptide of 204 amino acid residues. Stimulates growth of seminiferous tubules and spermatogenesis in men, and growth of ovarian follicles and oogenesis in women. Also stimulates secretion of oestrogens by ovarian follicle cells. GnRH from hypothalamus stimulates FSH release. definitionination of menstruation is called menopause.

subtopics.definition: Luteinizing hormone (LH) or Interstitial cell-stimulating hormone (ICSH)
subtopics.definition_content: A glycoprotein, polypeptide of 204 amino acid residues. In men: stimulates interstitial cells of testes (cells of Leydig) to secrete male hormones (androgens). In women: stimulates last stages of oogenesis, ovulation, corpus luteum development and secretion of progesterone by corpus luteum. Secretion controlled by GnRH.

Secreted by corticotroph cells of anterior pituitary. Molecules are 39 amino acid polypeptides. Secretion prompted by corticotropin-release hormone (CRH) of hypothalamus. Intensifies synthesis of adrenal cortical hormones, particularly glucocorticoids. Hyposecretion of ACTH leads to rheumatic arthritis.

A glycoprotein secreted by thyrotroph cells of anterior pituitary. Polypeptide of 201 amino acid residues. Secretion stimulated by hypothalamic thyrotropin-release hormone (TRH). Promotes growth and function of thyroid gland. There is no thyrotropin-inhibiting hormone.

Formerly called indefinitionedin. Molecule is a small peptide of 13 amino acid residues. Secretion controlled by MRH and MIH of hypothalamus. In lower vertebrates, target cells are melanophores – MSH affects spreading of melanin granules causing skin colour darkening in fish and amphibians. In humans, MSH receptor in brain suggests it may influence brain activity.
Influences carbohydrate and fat metabolism of body. Hormone influencing carbohydrate metabolism is known as diabetogenic hormone. Hormone influencing fat metabolism is known as ketogenic hormone.

Principal role is to promote reabsorption of water from distal convoluted tubules of nephrons and collecting ducts, reducing excretion of water in urine (diuresis). Also called antidiuretic hormone (ADH). Also increases blood pressure by contracting blood vessels (vasoconstriction). Secreted by supra-optic nucleus of hypothalamus. Release controlled by osmoregulatory centre in hypothalamus.

When vasopressin is released in smaller amounts, diuresis increases. In acute diuresis, quantity of urine may increase to about 20 litres instead of normal 1 to 2 litres per day. This condition is called polyuria or diabetes insipidus (passing of watery, tasteless urine). It causes dehydration of body and thirst.

Stimulates contraction of uterine muscles, inducing labour pains for child birth (parturition) when secretion of progesterone from placenta declines. Released in increasing amounts under positive feedback regulation as labour pain increases, dilating the cervix. After childbirth, helps normalization of uterus and contracts breast muscles and lactic ducts to facilitate release of milk (lactation) – galactogogic effect. Secreted by paraventricular nucleus of hypothalamus.

Pituitary gland regulates growth, sex, general behaviour and secretory activities of other principal endocrine glands. Most appropriately referred to as 'The Master Gland' of body, or the 'Chief Executive of Endocrine System', or 'The Leader of Endocrine Orchestra'.

Hypothalamus is the floor of diencephalon. Formed of masses of grey matter, called hypothalamic nuclei, containing neurosecretory cells. Connected with anterior pituitary lobe by blood capillaries of hypophyseal portal system and with posterior pituitary lobe by axons of its neurons, both passing through pituitary stalk.

Neurosecretory cells of hypothalamus secrete neurohormones called releasing factors (RF) or inhibiting factors (IF). These are carried by hypophyseal portal system to adenohypophysis and stimulate or inhibit the release of trophic hormones. These are proteinaceous, formed of 3–20 amino acids.

Pituitary gland and hypothalamus together form hypothalamo-pituitary complex. The hypothalamic-pituitary (hypothalamo-hypophyseal) system is a direct proof of coordination between the hormonal and nervous system. It regulates most physiological activities of body and maintains homeostasis.

topics.name: Position, Origin and Structure of Thyroid Gland

subtopics.definition_content: Largest endocrine gland of the body. Located in the neck upon the ventral aspect of larynx (sound box or Adam's apple) and a few anteriomost tracheal rings. A dark brown and H-shaped/butterfly bilobed gland. Average weight 30 grams. Name 'thyroid' introduced by Thomas Wharton (1656) from Greek 'Thyreos' – a shield.

Each lobule consists of a large number of small and hollow, spherical follicles (acini) embedded in loose connective tissue (stroma). Wall of each follicle is single-layered cuboidal epithelium suspended from basal lamina; its cavity is filled with iodothyroglobulin colloid. Stroma also contains parafollicular or 'C' cells (remnants of ultimobranchial bodies).

A yellowish, jelly-like and iodinated colloid glycoprotein substance filling thyroid follicle cavities. Formed by iodination of thyroglobulin molecule's tyrosine monomers. Acts as a reservoir of thyroid hormones T₃ and T₄.
Tyrosine monomers of thyroglobulin that bind with single atoms of iodine form monoiodotyrosine (MIT or T₁).
Tyrosine monomers of thyroglobulin that bind with two atoms of iodine form diiodotyrosine (DIT or T₂).

Coupling of iodinated tyrosine monomers within colloid results in formation of tetraiodothyronine (thyroxine – T₄) and triiodothyronine (T₃). T₄ contains two tyrosine monomers and four iodine atoms; T₃ contains two tyrosine monomers and three iodine atoms. Daily output is about 80μg of T₄ and 4μg of T₃. Since T₃ is more potent, most T₄ also converts to T₃ by losing one iodine atom during diffusion.

topics.name: Hormones of Thyroid and their Functions

subtopics.definition_content: Iodine containing (6% iodine) amine hormone derived from tyrosine amino acid. Chemically thyroxine is tetraiodothyronine though also found as triiodothyronine (T₃). First isolated by Kocher (1914), first crystalized by Kendall (1919). Regulates Basal Metabolic Rate (BMR) of body. Controls physical, mental and sexual growth of body. Stimulates metamorphosis in frog tadpoles.

Thyroxine regulates the Basal Metabolic Rate (BMR) by controlling rate of cell respiration and energy production in mitochondria – called calorigenic effect. Synthesis of additional sodium-potassium pump (Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase) uses more ATP, generating more heat and raising body temperature (homeothermy in warm-blooded animals).

In hilly tracts where all iodine has been washed away, tadpoles of Ambystoma never metamorphose. These tadpoles grow to large size and attain sexual maturity (paedogenetic larvae). This phenomenon is called paedogenesis. The paedogenetic larvae of Ambystoma are called Axolotl larvae.

A long peptide hormone secreted by parafollicular cells of thyroid gland (C-cells). Secretion regulated by increased plasma level of calcium (feedback mechanism). TCT lowers calcium level in blood to normal by increasing calcium deposition in bones, checks osteoporosis, stimulates excretion of calcium in urine and decreases reabsorption of calcium from urine. Prevents hypercalcaemia.

Disease of infants (cretins) caused by hypothyroidism. Characterised by decreased BMR (50% of normal), stunted growth, retarded mental development (low IQ), delayed puberty, decreased body temperature, heart rate, pulse rate, blood pressure and cardiac output, reduced urine output, decreased blood sugar level, pigeon's chest. Can be congenital or endemic (absence of iodine). Corrected by thyroxine administration.

Occurs due to deficiency of thyroxine in adults. Low BMR (30–40%), low body temperature, reduced heart rate, pulse rate, blood pressure and cardiac output. Peculiar feature: face and hands become swollen due to deposition of albuminous myxomatous tissue. Corrected by thyroxine administration.

Occurs due to deficiency of iodine in drinking water. Non-genetic. Characterised by enlargement of thyroid gland due to increase in number and size of acinal cells. More common in people of hilly regions. Prevented by iodised table salt.

Called auto-immune thyroiditis. Occurs due to age factor, injury-surgery, wrong treatment or infection of thyroid gland causing hyposecretion of thyroxine. When thyroxine secretion falls to minimal limit, antibodies are formed which destroy the thyroid gland.

Considerable increase in glucose and oxygen consumption by cells and rate of oxidative metabolism in mitochondria; BMR may increase several folds (hypermetabolism). Symptoms: nervous tension, restlessness and anxiety, muscular weakness (thyrotoxic myopathy), fatigue and tremors, high temperature, palpitation of heart, copious sweating, diarrhoea, insomnia, weight loss, heat intolerance, warm and soft skin, increased appetite.

A hyperthyroid goitre accompanied with asymmetrical protrusion (Exophthalmos) of the eyeballs, imparting an angry, frightened, or staring look to the patient. Protrusion of eyeballs is due to accumulation of mucus in eye orbits.
A type of hyperthyroid goitre. Enlargement of the gland usually due to a diffused growth.
Due to formation of one or more hypersecretory nodules in the thyroid gland.

topics.name: Position, Origin and Structure of Parathyroid Gland

subtopics.definition_content: Four in number, wholly or partially embedded in the dorsal surface of the thyroid gland – two glands in each lobe. Each is oval shaped, small sized (5×5 mm) and yellow coloured. Histologically formed of two masses of polygonal cells arranged in cords. Cell types: principal (chief) cells and oxyphil cells. Endodermal in origin. Present in all vertebrates except fishes.

Active hormone secreted by parathyroids. Also called Collip's Hormone (Phillips Collip, 1925). A polypeptide hormone – protein of 84 amino acid monomers. Secretion stimulated by low level of calcium in blood (feedback control). Maintains optimum plasma calcium by: (i) Mobilisation from bones, (ii) Reduced urinary excretion, (iii) Increased absorption from intestine. Also stimulates excretion of Na⁺, K⁺ and HCO₃⁻ but retards excretion of Mg²⁺.

A steroid hormone. First synthesized in inactive form in skin cells from 7-dehydrocholesterol under UV rays. Liver converts it to 25-hydroxycholecalciferol. Cells of proximal convoluted tubules of nephrons convert it to 1-25-dihydroxycholecalciferol (active vitamin D₃ = calcitriol) under influence of parathormone. Stimulates absorption of Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ in intestine and plays role in bone remodelling via osteoblasts.

Osteoblasts are bone-forming cells that deposit calcium (utilising calcium under influence of vitamin D₃). Osteoclasts are bone-eating cells that dissolve unnecessary bone parts under influence of parathormone, releasing calcium and phosphate into blood.

Undersecretion of parathormone: calcium level in ECF falls (hypocalcemia) and phosphate level rises (hyperphosphatemia). Causes neuromuscular hyperexcitability, excessive perspiration, gooseflesh, cooling of hands and feet, painful muscular spasms and convulsions, and trembling. Childhood hypoparathyroidism retards growth of bones, teeth, hair and brain.

In hypoparathyroidism, some skeletal muscles (usually of hands and feet) fail to relax after contraction and remain in 'sustained contraction'. This is called Tetany. Tetany of laryngeal, thoracic and phrenic muscles causes death (asphyxia) as the patient fails to breathe.
Oversecretion of parathormone causes demineralization of bones which become soft, weak, distorted and fragile. This is called osteoporosis.
Sharp rise in calcium level in blood and ECF due to hypersecretion of parathormone, accompanied by sharp fall in phosphate level (hypophosphatemia), weakening muscles and nerves.

Calcium excreted in urine (hypercalciurea) in hyperparathyroidism; thirst increases owing to copious urination, appetite is lost, constipation and headache become common, and often kidney stones are formed.

subtopics.definition: Feedback control of parathormone and thyrocalcitonin
subtopics.definition_content: As Ca²⁺ level tends to fall, secretion of parathormone increases, but that of thyrocalcitonin decreases. Contrarily, secretion of parathormone decreases and thyrocalcitonin increases when Ca²⁺ level tends to rise in blood.

topics.name: Position, Origin and Structure of Adrenal Gland

subtopics.definition_content: Paired glands placed on the top or superior of the kidneys as cap. Hence also called suprarenal glands. Adrenal gland was first reported by Eustachius. Dual origin: cortex from mesoderm, medulla from ectoderm. Each is small (5 cm long, 3 cm broad, 1 cm thick), triangular and yellowish cap-like structure. Weight about 3.5 to 5.09 gm. At birth adrenal glands are best developed.
Outer part of adrenal cortex (15% of gland), consisting of small polyhedral cells. Secretes mineralocorticoids e.g., Aldosterone.
Middle part of adrenal cortex (50% of gland), consisting of large polyhedral cells. Secretes glucocorticoids e.g., Cortisone, Corticosterone.
Inner part of adrenal cortex (7% of gland). Parallel cell cords branch to form a loose anastomosing network. Secretes sex hormones.
Cells of adrenal medulla. Modified postganglionic cells of sympathetic nervous system. Secrete adrenalin and nor-adrenalin, collectively called catecholamines.
About 20 steroidogenic compounds (adrenocorticoids/corticosteroids) secreted from adrenal cortex. Mineralocorticoids secreted by zona glomerulosa; principal one is aldosterone.

Principal mineralocorticoid. Also called salt-retaining hormone. Promotes reabsorption of sodium ions from kidney and excretion of potassium ions in urine. Also reabsorbs Cl⁻ ions from kidney. Controls osmotic pressure of ECF. Helps maintain acid-base equilibrium and blood pH (7.35) by promoting reabsorption of HCO₃⁻ and regulating excretion of H⁺. Also promotes absorption of water and salt in intestine, mainly in colon. RAA (Renin-angiotensin-aldosterone) pathway controls its secretion.
Mineralocorticoids

chapters.name: Adrenal Cortex – Glucocorticoids and Sex Hormones

topics.name: Glucocorticoids

subtopics.definition_content: Most abundant glucocorticoid (about 95%); anti-inflammatory and immunosuppressive hormone that retards glucose consumption and protein synthesis, promotes breakdown of proteins and fats, intensifies gluconeogenesis and glycogenesis in liver cells.
Synthesis of glucose from fatty acids and amino acids in liver cells, intensified by glucocorticoids.
Synthesis of glycogen from glucose in liver cells, promoted by glucocorticoids.
Hormone secreted by the anterior lobe of the pituitary gland that regulates secretion of glucocorticoids and sex hormones by adrenal cortex.
Hormone of the hypothalamus that regulates secretion of ACTH from anterior pituitary.
Enzyme secreted by cells of afferent arterioles that supply glomeruli when blood pressure drops due to decreased salt and water in blood; part of mineralocorticoid control.

key_concepts: Cortisol is anti-inflammatory: retards migratory movements and phagocytic activities of WBCs, reduces mast cells and histamine secretion, denotes synthesis of collagen fibres; used to treat arthritis and rheumatism

Cortisol is immunosuppressive: suppresses antibody synthesis, induces atrophy of thymus gland and lymphoid tissues, inhibits lymphocyte production; used in allergy treatment and transplantation surgery
Cortisol increases RBC count, decreases WBC count, elevates blood pressure
Sex hormones (androgen and estrogen) secreted by zona reticularis in small quantity; regulate sex organs, secondary sexual characters, growth and protein metabolism
Feedback control: decrease in cortisol → stimulates hypothalamus and pituitary → ACTHRH → ACTH → glucocorticoids increase; cortisol rise reverses the mechanism
Secretion of mineralocorticoids mainly regulated by kidneys via renin mechanism based on Na+, water and K+ levels in ECF

topics.name: Catecholamines – Epinephrine and Norepinephrine

subtopics.definition_content: Hormone comprising 80% of adrenal medullary secretion; first extracted by Abel (1899), pure form by Jokichi and Takamine (1900); derived from amino acid tyrosine via dopamine and norepinephrine by methylation; has catechol ring structure.
Hormone comprising 20% of adrenal medullary secretion; discovered by Ulf von Euler (1946); formed by hydroxylation of dopamine; precursor of epinephrine; Euler and Axelrod won Nobel Prize in 1970.
Compounds (dopamine, norepinephrine, epinephrine) whose molecular structure includes a 6-carbon ring connected to two hydroxyl groups (–OH), called catechol ring.
Indefinitionediate compound formed by hydroxylation and decarboxylation of tyrosine; further hydroxylated to form norepinephrine.

key_concepts: Both hormones are proteinaceous and derived from amino acid tyrosine
Cortisol from adrenal cortex induces synthesis of enzymes needed to convert norepinephrine to epinephrine; norepinephrine lacks these converting enzymes
ANS controls chromaffin cells directly, allowing very quick hormone release
Tyrosine → (Hydroxylation + Decarboxylation) → Dopamine → (Hydroxylation) → Norepinephrine → (Methylation) → Epinephrine

topics.name: Function of epinephrine

subtopics.definition_content: Constriction of blood vessels supplying peripheral and abdominal organs (skin, digestive, excretory, reproductive systems) caused by epinephrine; increases blood pressure.
Dilation of blood vessels supplying brain, skeletal muscles, heart, lungs, liver, adipose tissues, sensory organs caused by epinephrine; increases activity of these organs.
Pale skin condition caused by reduced blood supply to peripheral areas under epinephrine effect.
Contraction of arrector pili muscles of skin due to reduced blood supply under epinephrine effect.
Breakdown of glycogen into glucose in liver and skeletal muscles; caused by glucagon whose secretion increases under epinephrine effect while insulin secretion decreases.

key_concepts: Vasoconstriction in peripheral organs → pallor, dry mouth, retarded digestion, reduced urine, possible abortion in pregnant women
Vasodilation in vital organs (brain, heart, muscles, lungs) → alarm reaction, increased activity
Pupils dilate; tear secretion increases; bronchi and bronchioles dilate – epinephrine used in asthma treatment
Cardiac contractions intensify; heart rate, pulse rate, arterial pressure and cardiac output increase
Blood clotting time reduced due to increased adhesiveness of blood platelets
Spleen contracts releasing reserve blood corpuscles increasing blood cell count
Insulin secretion decreases; glucagon secretion increases causing glycogenolysis
BMR of all body cells considerably increases
Polygraph (lie detector) can detect emotional state based on epinephrine and norepinephrine effects

formulas.description: Biosynthetic pathway of catecholamines from tyrosine

topics.name: Sympathoadrenal System

subtopics.definition_content: Collective functional definition for the sympathetic nervous system and adrenal medulla, which act as an integrated system; medullary hormones amplify and prolong sympathetic effects.
Name given to medullary hormones (epinephrine and norepinephrine) because they augment and prolong sympathetic system effects about ten-fold when circulating in blood.
Small groups of chromaffin-like cells discovered by modern scientists near thoracic and abdominal ganglia of sympathetic system.
Fibres of sympathetic system innervating visceral organs; release norepinephrine (neurotransmitter) at their definitioninals.
Fibres of sympathetic system innervating adrenal medulla; release acetylcholine at definitioninals stimulating chromaffin cells to release epinephrine and norepinephrine.

key_concepts: Involuntary activities (digestion, heartbeat, thermoregulation, etc.) controlled by autonomic nervous system (sympathetic and parasympathetic) under hypothalamic control
Parasympathetic system stimulates organs active at rest; sympathetic system stimulates organs active during wakefulness and work
Medullary hormones increase effects of sympathetic stimulation and prolong them about ten-fold
Deficiency of either the sympathetic system or adrenal medulla is compensated by the other

topics.name: Alarm or stress reaction

subtopics.definition: Alarm or stress reaction (Fight or Flight reaction)
subtopics.definition_content: Violent physical reaction prepared within seconds when hypothalamus issues motor impulses to all organs including adrenal medulla in response to stressors; also referred to as General Adaptation Syndrome (GAS).
Scientific definition for the alarm or stress reaction; also called Fight or Flight reaction because the concerned person either boldly faces the emergency or tries to escape.

Strong stimuli (fear, anger, intense pain, accident, injury, burning, intense temperature change, micro-organism invasion, poisoning, emotional upsets, surgery, etc.) that endanger homeostasis and body survival.

key_concepts: Stressors → sensory impulses reach brain → hypothalamus issues motor impulses → norepinephrine released simultaneously in all organs by sympathetic fibres + large amounts of epinephrine and norepinephrine poured into blood by adrenal medulla
Mass release of hormones prepares whole body within seconds for violent physical reaction

chapters.name: Effects of irregularities of adrenal secretion

topics.name: Hyposecretion – Addison's Disease

subtopics.definition_content: Disease caused by undersecretion of adrenocorticoids; first described by Thomas Addison in 1849, 1855; occurs in both men and women between ages 20 to 40 years; characterised by dehydration, low BP, hypoglycemia, bronzing of skin, gastro-intestinal disorders.
Low blood glucose level; caused by low cortisol in Addison's disease, reducing BMR in body cells and impairing brain, liver and cardiac muscle efficiency.
Increased K⁺ level in blood; occurs in Addison's disease due to low cortisol; impairs brain, liver, skeletal and cardiac muscle efficiency.
Increase in number of WBCs; occurs in Addison's disease.

key_concepts: Low aldosterone and glucocorticoids → sodium ions and water excreted → dehydration, low blood pressure, weakness, Addisonian anaemia
Low cortisol → hypoglycemia, reduced BMR; brain, liver, skeletal and cardiac muscle efficiency declines; heartbeat may stop causing death
Gastro-intestinal disorders: loss of appetite, nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea, abdominal pain, restlessness
Increased sensitivity to cold, heat, infection, poisoning; complete destruction of adrenals causes death mainly from excessive sodium loss
Excessive melanin deposits on exposed skin (face, hands, feet, neck) cause deep bronzing
Increased H⁺ concentration may cause acidosis

topics.name: Hypersecretion – Cushing's Disease and Adrenogenital Syndrome

subtopics.definition_content: Disease caused by oversecretion of adrenocorticoids; first described by Cushing (1932); symptoms include hyperglycemia, moon face, buffalo humps, dilated abdomen with stretch lines, severe hypertension; patients may die from brain haemorrhage, cardiac arrest, pneumonia.
Red and rounded face – symptom of Cushing's disease due to irregular fat deposits in thoracic regions and face.
Swelling of shoulders due to irregular fat deposits; symptom of Cushing's disease.
Weakening and fragility of bones due to excessive mobilization of materials from bones in hypercorticism.

Condition caused by excessive secretion of androgens in a female fetus before complete formation of ovaries; results in pseudohermaphroditism with masculinization of external genitalia; affected females are sterile.

Condition in which external genitalia are masculinized in a genetic female due to excessive androgen secretion from adrenal cortex; clitoris grows to penis size while vagina and uterus remain underdeveloped.
Potassium deficiency caused by excessive loss of potassium in urine in hypercorticism; leads to muscular weakness, convulsions, nervous disorders, tetany, paralysis, polyuria (nocturia) and thirst.
Enlarged mammary glands in adult males due to excessive secretion of female hormones from adrenal cortex.
Condition in females with excessive androgens characterised by increased facial and body hair, muscle growth and clitorial enlargement.

key_concepts: Hyperglycemia (high blood glucose) may lead to diabetes mellitus
Severe hypertension due to retention of sodium and water in ECF
Tissue wasting and osteoporosis due to excessive mobilization of proteins and minerals from all cells and bones
Excessive loss of K⁺ in urine → hypokalemia; excessive loss of H⁺ → alkalosis
Excessive androgens in female fetus (before ovary formation) → adrenogenital syndrome / pseudohermaphroditism
Excessive androgens in male children → macrogenitosomia (excessive penis development), atrophy of testes, no spermatogenesis, early erections
Excessive androgens in post-birth girls → gradual masculinization (overgrowth of clitoris, underdevelopment of mammary glands and uterus, disturbed menstruation)

topics.name: Location, Structure and Cells Types in the Pancreatic Islets

subtopics.definition_content: Approximately 1 to 2 million small (0.1 to 0.2 mm in diameter) clusters of endocrine cells scattered in the connective tissue stroma of the pancreas; discovered by Paul Langerhans (1869).
Cells constituting about 15% of pancreatic islet cells; secrete glucagon.
Cells constituting about 80% of pancreatic islet cells; secrete insulin.
Cells constituting about 5% of pancreatic islet cells; secrete somatostatin (identical to growth hormone-inhibiting hormone secreted by the hypothalamus).
Remaining pancreatic islet cells; secrete pancreatic polypeptide.

key_concepts: Pancreas is a flattened pinkish mixed gland (both exocrine and endocrine) situated in the concavity formed by duodenum just behind the stomach; measures about 15 cm in length and 4 to 5 cm in breadth
About 99% of pancreas is exocrine (hollow pancreatic acini or lobules); ~1% is endocrine (islets of Langerhans)
Pancreas forms by fusion of two bilateral endodermal processes of embryonic intestine (duodenum)

topics.name: Insulin

subtopics.definition_content: Hormone secreted by beta (B) cells of islets of Langerhans; small protein consisting of two polypeptide chains α (21 amino acid residues) and β (30 amino acid residues) joined by disulphide linkages; increases permeability of all cells for glucose several times (except brain cells and RBCs).

Disease caused by insulin deficiency (hypoinsulinism); blood glucose rises above 200 mg/100 ml; glucose appears in urine (glycosuria); characterised by polyuria, polydipsia, polyphagia, ketosis, body wasting and possible coma and death; two types – Type I (usually young, ~10%) and Type II (over 40 years or obese).
Elevated blood glucose level; occurs in diabetes mellitus due to failure of body cells to obtain glucose from blood in insulin deficiency.
Condition where glucose passes out in urine due to elevated blood glucose level beyond threshold.
Excretion of large volumes of urine; a hallmark symptom of diabetes mellitus.
Increased thirst resulting from dehydration caused by polyuria in diabetes mellitus.
Increased hunger; occurs in diabetes mellitus as cells cannot utilise glucose for energy ('starving in midst of plenty').

Formation of ketone bodies – acetone, acetoacetic acid and β-hydroxybutyrate – due to accelerated but incomplete oxidation of fatty acids especially in liver in diabetes mellitus; causes acidosis leading to possible coma and death.

Oversecretion of insulin causing persistent decrease in blood glucose level (hypoglycemia); impairs nervous efficiency, vision and fertility; causes anxiety, sweating, weakness, muscular convulsions; excess leads to insulin shock (coma) and death.

key_concepts: Normal blood glucose concentration: about 100 mg (0.1 gm) per 100 ml blood
Insulin increases permeability of all cells for glucose (except brain cells and RBCs already highly permeable)
Insulin increases BMR and RNA and protein synthesis in cells

Insulin milestones: Minkowski and Mehring (1889) linked pancreas to diabetes; Banting and Best (pure extract, 1923), Nobel Prize to Banting and Macleod; Abel (pure crystals, 1926); F. Sanger (molecular structure of bovine insulin, 1955; Nobel Prize 1958); first protein synthesized in laboratory (1964); Tsan synthesized human insulin (1965); first produced commercially by DNA recombinant technique
Insulin treatment: regular injections; balanced diet; insulin tablets (e.g. dionyl); drugs like glyburide stimulate insulin secretion
Hyperinsulinism treatment: cortisol, adrenaline, growth hormone and glucagon injections; glucose injections

topics.name: Glucagon

subtopics.definition_content: Hormone secreted by alpha cells of islets of Langerhans; discovered by Kimball and Murlin (1923); small protein with a single polypeptide chain of 29 amino acid residues; elevates blood glucose by intensifying glycogenolysis, deamination, gluconeogenesis and inhibiting glycogenesis in liver; promoter of catabolic metabolism.

key_concepts: Glucagon intensifies glycogenolysis, deamination and gluconeogenesis, and inhibits glycogenesis in liver cells
Glucagon intensifies lipolysis in adipose tissues
Limit-control ('push and pull') feedback: high blood sugar → insulin secreted + glucagon inhibited; blood sugar falls → insulin inhibited + glucagon stimulated

topics.name: Somatostatin and Pancreatic polypeptide

subtopics.definition_content: Hormone secreted by delta (δ) cells of pancreas; small peptide of 14 amino acid residues; resembles GHIH of hypothalamus; acts as paracrine hormone to retard secretory activities of α and β cells and slow food digestion, absorption and assimilation.
Hormone secreted by F (PP) cells of pancreas; acts as local paracrine hormone; retards secretion of pancreatic enzymes and somatostatin; inhibits motility of stomach, duodenum and gall bladder.

key_concepts: Somatostatin resembles growth hormone inhibitory hormone (GHIH) of hypothalamus
Somatostatin prolongs utilization of every feed by slowing digestion, absorption of digested nutrients and assimilation in cells

topics.name: Testes

subtopics.definition_content: Main androgen (masculinizing hormone) secreted by interstitial cells (Leydig cells) of testes, derived from cholesterol; secreted from puberty to about age 40 years with gradual decline thereafter; stimulates male reproductive system, spermatogenesis, male secondary sexual characters, libido and protein anabolism.
Special cells present between the seminiferous tubules of testes; secrete male hormones (androgens) derived from cholesterol; include testosterone (main), androstenedione and DHEA.

Surgical removal of testes; before puberty retards growth of genitalia, muscles, bones and sexual characteristics; after puberty causes demasculinization, reduced spermatogenesis, diminished sex urge and potency.
Deficient secretion of testosterone due to congenital defects or injury to testes; before puberty retards growth of genitalia, muscles and bones as well as development of sexual characteristics.
Less important androgen secreted by interstitial cells of testes along with testosterone and androstenedione.

key_concepts: Testes quiescent from birth to puberty; reactivated at puberty by gonadotropic hormones (FSH and ICSH) from pituitary

Testosterone stimulates: male reproductive system growth, spermatogenesis (with FSH), secondary sexual characters (beard, body hair, deep voice, larynx enlargement, broad shoulders), male sexual behaviour, protein anabolism, erythropoiesis, increased sebaceous gland secretion
Androgens also secreted in traces from adrenal glands in both boys and girls
Castration before puberty: retards genitalia, muscle, bone growth and sexual characters; castration after puberty preserves libido but diminishes efficiency
Castrated animals in husbandry: cattle → steers, horses → geldings, fowls → capons; castration makes these docile

topics.name: Ovary

subtopics.definition_content: Cessation of ovarian function and menstrual cycles in women normally at about age 45 to 55 years; results in rise of urinary excretion of pituitary gonadotropins.

Female sex hormone secreted by cells of Graffian (ovarian) follicle surrounding the maturing ovum; derived from cholesterol; stimulates female reproductive tract growth and function, oogenesis, and development of accessory sex characters.

key_concepts: Primordial ovarian follicles form in female fetuses at about 16 weeks of gestation; ovaries quiescent until age 7 or 8 years
Puberty in girls set in at about age 11 to 13 years under pituitary gonadotropins (FSH and LH); menstrual cycle begins
Ovarian hormones: estrogen, progesterone and relaxin – all derived from cholesterol
Sex hormones discovered by Adolf Butenandt in 1929 and 1931; Nobel Prize 1939 jointly with Leopold Ruzicka

topics.name: Origin, Position, Structure and Function of pineal body

subtopics.definition_content: Small, whitish, somewhat flattened ectodermal gland at the tip of a small fibrous stalk arising from dorsal wall of diencephalon (roof/epithalamus of third ventricle of brain); contains pinealocytes and interstitial (neuroglial) cells; covered by piamater-derived thin capsule with septa forming lobules.

Hormone secreted by pineal gland; follows diurnal (day-night) cycle – rises in the evening and through night, drops to low around noon; lightens skin colour, regulates gonads (testes and ovaries), inhibits ovarian function, is a potent antioxidant; causes atrophy of gonads in several animals.
Large modified nerve cells present in the lobules of the pineal gland.
Granules of calcium salts deposited in pineal gland after age of about 7 years, causing its gradual degeneration.

key_concepts: Light on the retina decreases melatonin production; darkness stimulates melatonin synthesis
Girls blind from birth attain puberty earlier than normal – no inhibitory effect of melatonin on ovarian function
Serotonin (neurotransmitter) also found in pineal gland; evidence that pineal may regulate cyclic phenomena in the body

topics.name: Origin, Position and Structure

subtopics.definition_content: Bilobed endocrine and haemopoietic gland located in the upper part of the thorax near the heart in the mediastinum; endodermal in origin from epithelium of outer part of third branchial pouches; enveloped by thin fibrous connective tissue capsule with septa dividing it into lobules.
Phagocytic cells (macrophages) present in the medullary mass of thymus lobules.

key_concepts: Each thymus lobule distinguished into cortical parenchyma (numerous lymphocytes) and medullary mass of large irregularly branched reticular cells, few lymphocytes and macrophages (Hassal's corpuscles)
Thymus grows up to puberty then gradually diminishes in size; tissue infiltrated by yellowish adipose tissue ('immunity theory of ageing')

topics.name: Function of thymus glands

subtopics.definition_content: Main hormone secreted by thymus; induces proliferation of lymphocytes and their differentiation into clones specially formed to destroy specific categories of antigens and pathogens (maturation of T-lymphocytes).
Lymphocytes that mature and differentiate in the thymus under thymosin stimulation; provide cellular immunity against specific antigens and pathogens.
One of the thymic hormones (along with thymosin, thymic factor TF, and thymopoietin) that induce lymphocyte proliferation and maturation.

key_concepts: Thymus is the 'seedbed' of T-lymphocytes; stem cells from yolk sac and liver (early embryo) or only bone marrow (late embryo) migrate into thymus and proliferate into lymphocytes
Thymosin, THF, TF and thymopoietin induce proliferation and differentiation of lymphocytes into specific clones
Thymus essential in neonatal infant and postnatal child for normal development of lymphoid organs and cellular immunity

chapters.name: Gastro-intestinal mucosa, placenta, skin, kidney and heart

subtopics.definition_content: A hormone secreted by the mucosa of the pyloric stomach near the duodenum. Presence of food in the stomach provides a stimulus for its secretion. Gastrin stimulates the gastric glands to produce the gastric juice and also stimulates stomach movements.

A hormone produced by the small intestinal mucosa. It causes the release of sodium bicarbonate solution from the pancreas for pancreatic juice and from the liver for bile. It also inhibits the secretion and movements of stomach.

A hormone secreted by the mucosa of the entire small intestine. The actions of cholecystokinin and pancreozymin were discovered independently but are now considered one hormone. It stimulates the gall bladder to release bile and stimulates the pancreas to release its enzymes.
A hormone secreted by the duodenal mucosa. It shows gastric contractions and stops the secretion of gastric juice.
A hormone secreted by the duodenal mucosa. It stimulates crypts of Lieberkuhn to secrete the enzymes in the intestinal juice.
A hormone secreted by the duodenal mucosa. It stimulates the release of viscous mucus from Brunner's glands into the intestinal juice.
A hormone secreted by the mucosa of the entire small intestine. It accelerates the movements of villi to quicken absorption of food.

A protein hormone secreted by cells of placenta. Early placenta secretes so much of it that it is excreted in mother's urine about two weeks after pregnancy. Its presence in urine is used for pregnancy test. It serves to maintain the corpus luteum and stimulate it for secretion, and maintains pregnancy by preventing contraction of uterine wall.
Formerly known as placental lactogen. Reaches into mother's body, acts as a mid growth hormone and promotes growth of milk glands.

A polypeptide obtained from corpus luteum and placenta. During pregnancy it causes relaxation of the ligaments of pubic symphysis, and towards the definitionination of pregnancy, softens and widens the opening (cervix) of uterus for easy child birth (parturition). A temporary structure with endocrine function is placenta.

The main Vitamin D synthesized in skin cells under the effect of ultraviolet (UV) rays of sunlight from cholesterol-derived compounds. Liver cells convert it into hydroxycholecalciferol (calcidiol) by hydroxylation and release back into blood.

Dihydroxycalciferol produced by cells of proximal convoluted tubules of nephrons in kidneys by further hydroxylation of calcidiol. It is an important regulator of Ca²⁺ homeostasis, promotes absorption of Ca²⁺ and phosphorus in intestine and bone-formation. Required for growth of body and bone healing.
A condition caused by deficiency of Calcitriol (active Vitamin D₃) in childhood, leading to thin, weak and curved bones.
A condition caused by deficiency of Calcitriol (active Vitamin D₃) after the growth period, resulting in weak, porous and fragile bones.

A proteolytic enzyme secreted into blood by cells of juxtamedullary complexes when the rate of ultrafiltration in kidneys decreases due to low blood pressure (BP). It acts upon angiotensinogen (a large plasma-protein formed in liver), separating a small protein from it called angiotensin-I.

Converted from angiotensin-I by angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) as blood flows in blood capillaries of liver. It accelerates heartbeat, constricts arterioles increasing blood pressure, stimulates adrenal cortex to secrete aldosterone, and enhances water and sodium reabsorption from nephrons, increasing ECF volume and blood pressure.

A hormone secreted by kidneys that controls formation of erythrocytes (RBCs) in red bone marrow. Also called renal erythropoietic factor. Secretion increases on decrease in blood volume, RBC count, or haemoglobin deficiency (anaemia).

A hormone secreted by certain cardiac muscle cells of the atria of heart when volume of ECF and blood pressure (BP) increase due to retention of more NaCl in the body. It promotes copious urination (diuresis) and excretion of NaCl (natriuresis) to normalise ECF volume and BP. It also inhibits the effect of vasoconstrictor hormones and secretion of renin, aldosterone and vasopressin.
A drug which inhibits the effects of progesterone, administered for abortion since progesterone is necessary to maintain early pregnancy.
A protein factor secreted by cells of Sertoli in seminiferous tubules that directly inhibits secretion of FSH by pituitary. Its use as a male contraceptive is being explored.

chapters.name: Ovarian Hormones – Progesterone, Relaxin and Regulation

topics.name: Progesterone, Relaxin, Regulation and Key subtopics

subtopics.definition_content: It is secreted by the corpus luteum. Its hormone suspends ovulation during pregnancy, fixes the foetus to the uterine wall, forms placenta, and controls the development of the foetus in the uterus. Also called anti-abortion hormone. Ovulation, formation of corpus luteum and secretion of progesterone are stimulated by the luteinising hormone (LH) from the anterior pituitary.
It is produced by the corpus luteum at the end of the gestation period. It relaxes the cervix of the uterus and ligaments of the pelvic girdle for easy birth of the young one.

Undersecretion of estrogens (hypogonadism) before puberty due to congenital defects or damage to ovaries causes female eunuchoidism. Accessory genitals and breasts remain underdeveloped, pelvis remains narrow and buttocks flat. Secondary sexual characteristics also do not develop. Hypogonadism in adulthood reduces fertility and disturbs menstrual cycles.
Involves testing of hCG of placenta in the urine to test the pregnancy.
Contain less oestrogens and more progesterone so called combined pills. These check ovulation and so pregnancy in female.
Also called hypothalamic eunuchoidism characterized by hypogonadism in male caused by genetic inability of hypothalamus to secrete gonadotrophin releasing hormones.

chapters.name: Table 5.7-11: List of Hormones, their Chemical Nature and Functions
Local hormones, pheromones and insect endocrine glands

subtopics.definition_content: Compounds secreted by all body cells except RBCs when stimulated, which transmit coded information of metabolic adjustments between neighbouring cells and remain in ECF instead of diffusing into blood. Short-lived due to rapid degradation by ECF enzymes. Also called parahormones or tissue hormones.
Local hormones that affect metabolism of cells located in the neighbourhood of those which secrete them.
Local hormones that affect metabolism of the very cells which secrete them.

A category of lipids derived from fatty acid arachidonic acid, synthesized in the plasma membrane of cells and released in ECF. Four categories: Prostaglandins, prostacyclins, thromboxanes and leukotrienes.

Eicosanoids discovered by Ulf von Euler in 1935 in human semen, presumably secreted by prostate gland. After semen is discharged in woman's vagina, they contract uterine muscles to facilitate the sperms to ascend into fallopian tubes and reach ova to fertilize these.
Eicosanoids found in walls of blood vessels that induce vasodilation, facilitate flow of blood in vessels and prevent thrombosis by inhibiting aggregation of platelets.

Eicosanoids secreted by blood platelets. They help in blood clotting by instigating aggregation of platelets at the place of injury and also instigate vasoconstriction at places of injury to prevent excessive loss of blood.

Eicosanoids secreted by eosinophils of blood and mast cells of connective tissues. They serve as mediators in inflammatory and allergic reactions, induce bronchoconstriction (constriction of bronchioles), constrict arterioles and induce migration of neutrophils and eosinophils towards places of inflammation. Can cause asthma, arthritis, colitis.
A category of proteins which function as paracrine hormones in nervous tissues. Classified into three categories: neurotransmitters, neuromodulators and nerve growth factors.

Paracrine proteins synthesized in nerve cells and secreted by exocytosis by axon definitioninals. They transmit nerve impulses from one neuron to neighbouring neurons, muscles or glands across synapses. About 60 discovered; most common are acetylcholine, norepinephrine, dopamine, serotonin and histamine.

Paracrine hormones secreted by neurons that modulate (increase or decrease) the excitability of neighbouring neurons. Main positive neuromodulators: glutamate, aspartate and polypeptide substance P. Main negative modulators: glycine, GABA, enkephalins, endorphins, dynorphins, tachychinins and nitric oxide (NO).

Polypeptide paracrine hormones secreted by supporting glial cells of nervous tissues and cells of muscles, salivary glands and many other tissues. They play important role in growth, development and survival of nerve cells.

Chemicals excreted or released by one animal to the exterior, but evoke a physiological or behavioral response in another animal of the same species. Most are volatile and odorous fatty acids (hydrocarbons) received through olfaction. Also called ectohormones. definition coined by Karlson and Butendant (1959).
Sex pheromones secreted by certain insects to attract their mating partners.
An insect aggregation pheromone (Geraniol – Honey bee) released to transmit information of food and source of danger to fellows.
Cells lying in the brain between the two cerebral ganglia of cockroach. They secrete a hormone called the brain hormone which activates the prothoracic glands to secrete their hormone.
A pair of rod-like bodies situated on the sides of the oesophagus just behind the brain in cockroach. They secrete a growth hormone.

A pair of small, rounded bodies lying close behind the corpora cardiaca in cockroach. They secrete a juvenile hormone in nymphal stages causing retention of nymphal characters and checking appearance of adult characters. In the adult, they secrete a gonadotropic hormone regulating egg production and development and functioning of accessory sex glands.
Fairly large, irregular glands situated in the prothorax of cockroach. They secrete ecdyson which controls moulting of the nymphs. Degenerate after metamorphosis.
A steroid hormone secreted by prothoracic glands present in the prothorax of insects like cockroach. Controls moulting or ecdysis.

Secreted by corpora allata in nymphal stages. Causes retention of the nymphal characters and checks the appearance of adult characters. In the last nymphal form, corpora allata become inactive, resulting in absence of juvenile hormone and permitting appearance of adult features.

chapters.name: Table 5.7-12: Diseases caused by Hormonal Irregularities

topics.name: Tips & Tricks – Important Facts and Mnemonics

chapters.name: Life cycle and life span

subtopics.definition_content: includes two developmental periods: embryonic period (prenatal developmental period) which extends from zygote to offspring till hatching or birth, and post embryonic period (post-natal developmental period) which includes growth, adulthood, reproduction, ageing
the maximum number of years survived by any member of a species
the number of years survived by members of a population
the age at which half the population still survives

key_concepts: embryonic period
post-natal development
aging
death

topics.name: Reproduction

subtopics.definition_content: the ability of living organism to produce a new generation of living individuals similar to themselves

key_concepts: cloning
identical offspring
mitotic division

topics.name: Basic features of reproduction
key_concepts: DNA replication
cell division
mitotic
meiotic
reproductive bodies
development

topics.name: Asexual (Non-gametic) reproduction

subtopics.definition_content: production of offspring by a single parent without the formation and fusion of gametes. The young one receives all its genes from one parent
the division of the parent into two small, nearly equalized daughter individuals. During binary fission nuclear divisions or karyokinesis is always followed by division of cytoplasm or cytokinesis
if the plane of cytoplasmic division passes through any direction, the fission is called simple fission
if the plane of cytoplasmic division coincides with the transverse axis of the individual, the fission is definitioned transverse binary division
if the plane of cytoplasmic division coincides with the longitudinal axis of the individual. This kind of fission is designated as longitudinal binary fission
the division of the parent into many small daughter individuals simultaneously
the division of a multinucleate protozoan into several small, multinucleate daughters without nuclear division. The daughters grow and regain the normal number of nuclei by nuclear divisions
formation of a daughter individual from a small projection, the bud, arising on the parent body. It is a common method of asexual reproduction. In budding new individual form by mitosis
the breaking up of an animal's body into two or more pieces, each of which grows into a new individual

key_concepts: agamogenesis
mitotic division
clone
identical offspring
encystation
sporulation
schizogony
