topics.name: Sexual (gametic) reproduction

subtopics.definition_content: the production of offspring usually by two parents, male and female. Involving four processes: (i) Formation of special haploid cells, the gametes, by meiosis (Gametogenesis) (ii) Fusion of the gametes in pairs, forming diploid cells, the zygotes (Fertilization) (iii) Repeated mitotic divisions of zygotes to form embryos (Embryogenesis) (iv) Growth of Embryos into a new individual (Development)
involves the fusion of two entire gametes to form a zygote. The fusion nucleus of zygote called synkaryon
the fusing gametes are similar morphologically. Such gametes are known as isogametes, and their fusion is definitioned isogamy
the fusing gametes are different in form, size structure and behaviour
refers to retention of a larval or embryonic trait in adult body
the blastomeres formed by division of the zygote separate in early stages of development, each producing a complete individual
some acellular protist animals (e.g., Paramecium) exhibit sexual reproduction by forming male and female gamete nuclei, which they exchange through temporary cytoplasmic bridge

key_concepts: meiosis
gamete fusion
zygote formation
diploid
haploid
genetic variation
biparental

topics.name: Parthenogenesis (Virgin birth)

subtopics.definition_content: a modification of sexual reproduction in which an egg develops into a complete offspring without fertilization. It is monoparental
haploid eggs grow to form haploid males
diploid eggs grow without fertilization into diploid individuals, generally females

key_concepts: unfertilized egg development
haploid males
diploid females
environmental triggers

topics.name: Sex organs

subtopics.definition_content: gonads which form gametes are called primary sex organs – testis in males and ovary in females. Testis produces sperms and secrets testosterone. Ovary produces ova
sex organs, glands and ducts which do not produce gametes but are otherwise essential for sexual reproduction
beginning of sexual maturity or ability to reproduce. Puberty occurs at the age of 10-14 years in girls and 13-15 years in boys

key_concepts: testis
ovary
testosterone
oestrogen
gonads

topics.name: Agamospermy and Apomixis

subtopics.definition_content: a kind of plant apomixis in which the embryos and seeds are formed by asexual reproductive methods without involving meiotic gametogenesis and sexual fusion of gametes
formation of embryo directly from the diploid sporophytic cells (nucellus or integument) of ovule without involving meiosis and sexual fusion
formation of complete embryo sac from the sporophytic cell without meiosis so that the gametophyte remains diploid

key_concepts: apomixis
sporophytic cells
embryo sac
diploid

topics.name: Vegetative propagation

subtopics.definition_content: regeneration or formation of a new individual from any vegetative part of the body
small piece of any plant organ (stem, root or leaf) used for propagation
a new variety produced by joining parts of two different plants. The rooted shoot of one plant, called stock, is joined with a piece of shoot of another plant known as scion

subtopics.definition: Propagation by plant tissue culture or Micropropagation
subtopics.definition_content: propagation of plants by culturing the cells, tissues and organs called tissue culture. Small pieces of plant organs or tissues are grown aseptically in a suitable nutrient medium

key_concepts: tubers
rhizomes
bulbs
runners
tissue culture
callus
mitotic division

topics.name: Structure of anther

subtopics.definition_content: Each pollen chamber represents a microsporangium and is filled with a large number of pollen grains or microspores. Parietal cells form wall of microsporangium.
Inner to epidermis, a single layer of radially elongated cells. Cells develop fibrous thickening which help in the dehiscence of anther.

Innermost layer of the wall. Cells are multinucleate and polyploid. Tapetal cells are nutritive, producing enzymes, IAA and food materials. Ubisch bodies present help in ornamentation of microspore walls.
Present in tapetal cells; help in the ornamentation of microspore walls.

key_concepts: sporopollenin
stomium
tapetum
fibrous thickening

topics.name: Development of anther and formation of microspores

subtopics.definition_content: Divides meiotically to form four haploid microspores or pollen grains.
Four pollen grains arranged together after meiosis. Arrangement can be tetrahedral, isobilateral, linear, T-shaped and decussate.
Typical structure formed when all the pollen grains of an anther lobe form a single unit, e.g., Calotropis and some orchids.

key_concepts: meiosis
haploid microspores
compound pollen grains

topics.name: Development of male gametophyte (Microgametogenesis)

subtopics.definition_content: Outer layer of pollen grain wall. Made up of sporopollenin. Thick and ornamented. Differentiated into inner endexine and outer ektexine.
Resistant compound making up the exine. Resistant to physical and biological decomposition; preserved for long periods in fossil deposits.
Inner thin, delicate layer of pollen grain wall; made of cellulose and pectose.
Yellowish, viscous and sticky substance covering exine in insect pollinated flowers. Made up of lipids and carotenoids.
Smaller cell formed by mitotic division of microspore nucleus; lies next to spore wall.
Much larger cell formed by mitotic division of microspore nucleus.

key_concepts: germ pores
bicelled pollen grain
precocious germination

topics.name: Post-pollination development

subtopics.definition_content: Process by which liberated pollen grains are transferred to the receptive surface of the carpel (stigma).
Extension formed from vegetative (tube) cell through apertures in pollen grain wall. Secretes exogenous pectinases and other hydrolytic enzymes.
Two non-motile male gametes formed from generative cell division; carried by pollen tube.

key_concepts: tube nucleus
pollen germination
non-motile gametes

topics.name: Structure of the flower

subtopics.definition_content: Morphologically flower is a modified shoot meant for sexual reproduction of the plant. Typically, it is a condensed branch in which internodes have become condensed, bringing nodes very close to one another, and the leaves are modified to form floral-whorl that directly or indirectly participate in the process of reproduction.
Short or long stalk on which flower is commonly borne.
Upper swollen region of the flower.

key_concepts: floral whorls
calyx
corolla
androecium
gynoecium

topics.name: Parts of a flower

subtopics.definition_content: Two upper whorls of floral appendages; fertile and considered as essential or reproductive whorls.

key_concepts: microsporophylls
megasporophylls

topics.name: Structure of ovule (Megasporangium)

subtopics.definition_content: Integumented megasporangium consisting of a stalk and body.
Stalk of the ovule; one end attached to placenta, other end to body of ovule.
Opening at the top of ovule left by integuments.
Large parenchymatous tissue enclosed by integuments; generally consumed at maturity.
Female gametophyte situated in the centre of nucellus.
Outgrowth of outer integument in micropylar region, e.g., Castor (Ricinus).

key_concepts: hilum
raphe
chalazal end
integuments
perisperm

topics.name: Kinds of ovules

subtopics.definition_content: Micropyle, chalaza and funicle in straight line. Most primitive type of ovules, e.g., Betel, Piper, Polygonum.
Body of ovule completely inverted (turn at 180° angle) so micropyle and hilum come close together. Found in ~82% of angiosperm families.
Ovule circled more or less at right angle to funicle; micropylar end bent down slightly, e.g., Leguminosae, Cruciferae, Capsella.
High curvature of ovule; embryo sac becomes curved like horse shoe, e.g., Lemna, Poppy, Alisma.

key_concepts: hemianatropous
circinotropous

topics.name: Formation of megaspore

subtopics.definition_content: Diploid cell that enlarges and divides by meiosis to form linear tetrad of four haploid megaspores.
Lowermost (chalazal) megaspore from linear tetrad that enlarges and remains alive; others degenerate. Produces embryo sac.

key_concepts: archesporium
tenuinucellate
crassinucellate
meiosis

topics.name: Development of female gametophyte (Megagametogenesis)

subtopics.definition_content: Process of development of female gametophyte or embryo sac from megaspore.
Only one megaspore towards chalazal end takes part in development; by three successive mitotic divisions gives 8-nucleate embryo sac. Most common type (~70% angiosperms).
Group of 3 cells at micropylar end: central egg cell and two synergids with filiform apparatus.
Two cells flanking egg cell in egg apparatus. Show filiform apparatus attached to upper wall to attract and guide pollen tube.
Structure on synergids' upper wall; known to attract and guide the pollen tube.
Nuclei situated in centre of embryo sac; generally fuse before fertilization to form single diploid secondary nucleus.
Three cells at chalazal end; may participate in nourishing embryo by developing haustoria; generally degenerate after fertilization.

key_concepts: bisporic type
tetrasporic type
8-nucleate embryo sac

topics.name: Self pollination

subtopics.definition_content: Transfer of pollen grains from anthers to stigma of same flower or another flower on same plant.
Kind of pollination where pollen from anthers of flower transferred to stigma of same flower.
Kind of pollination where pollen from anthers of one flower transferred to stigma of another flower on same plant. Involves two flowers but belong to same parent.
Some plants never open to ensure complete self-pollination, e.g., Commelina bengalensis, Oxalis, Viola. Flowers are bisexual, small, inconspicuous, colourless, without nectar.

key_concepts: homogamy
self pollination advantages
closed flowers

topics.name: Cross pollination

subtopics.definition_content: Transfer of pollen grains from flower of one plant to stigma of flower of another plant.
In bisexual flowers, two sexes mature at different intervals to avoid self pollination.
Stamens mature earlier than stigma; flowers called protandrous, e.g., Coriander, Jasmine, Sunflower.
Stigma matures earlier than stamens; flowers called protogynous, e.g., Rose, Tobacco, Crucifers.
Plants of some species have dimorphic flowers facilitating cross pollination; pin-eyed and thrum-eyed morphs.
When pollen grain of anther does not germinate on stigma of same flower; flower is self sterile or incompatible.

key_concepts: diclincy
monoecious
dioecious
herkogamy

topics.name: Abiotic agents (Wind and Water)

subtopics.definition_content: Flowers pollinated by wind agency. Flowers small, inconspicuous with long versatile stamens, e.g., Sugarcane, Maize, Wheat, Bamboo, Pinus, Papaya.
Pollination through water agency.
Plants pollinated inside water, e.g., Zostera, Ceratophyllum, Najas.
Plants pollinated outside water, e.g., Vallisneria (Ribbon weed).

key_concepts: light pollen
feathery stigma
aquatic pollination

topics.name: Biotic agents (Animals)

subtopics.definition_content: Pollination by insects (~80% of pollination). Flowers brightly coloured, sweet smell, produce nectar. Pollen spinous and sticky (pollenkitt).
Flowers pollinated by birds. Common pollinators: Sun bird, Humming bird, Crow, Bulbul, Parrot. Flowers brightly coloured, produce plenty nectar and pollen.
Pollination by bats. Flowers large, dull-coloured, strong scent. Open at night with fragrance, e.g., Kigelia pinnata, Adansonia.
Pollination by slugs and snails, e.g., Chrysanthemum, Lemna, Arisaema.

key_concepts: myrmecophily
pollenkitt
nectar guides

topics.name: Germination of pollen grain and pollen tube growth

subtopics.definition_content: Fusion of two dissimilar sexual reproductive units (gametes). Discovered by Strasburger (1884).
Production of single pollen tube by pollen grain (general condition).
Production of many pollen tubes by pollen grain, e.g., Cucurbitaceae.

key_concepts: pollen germination
callosic plug
pollen tube guidance

topics.name: Entry of pollen tube into ovule

subtopics.definition_content: Pollen tube enters ovule through micropyle. Most common type, e.g., Lily.
Entry of pollen tube into ovule from chalazal region. Less common, e.g., Casuarina, Juglans, Betula.
Pollen tube enters ovule through middle part via integument or funicle.

key_concepts: pollen tube path
embryo sac entry

topics.name: Entry into embryo sac and double fertilization

subtopics.definition_content: Two fusion events in embryo sac: one male gamete fuses with egg (syngamy/generative fertilization); other male gamete fuses with polar nuclei (triple fusion/vegetative fertilization). Specific to angiosperms.
Fusion of one male gamete with egg to form diploid zygote. Discovered by Strasburger (1884).
Fusion of other male gamete with two polar nuclei (or secondary nucleus) to form triploid primary endosperm nucleus.

key_concepts: zygote formation
primary endosperm nucleus
triploid endosperm

topics.name: Types of endosperm

subtopics.definition_content: Nutritive tissue for developing embryo and seedling in angiosperms. Develops from triploid (3n) primary endosperm nucleus.
Primary endosperm nucleus divides by repeated mitotic free nuclear divisions without wall formation; large central vacuole develops. Most common type, e.g., Cotton, Zea mays.
First nuclear division followed by wall formation creating twochambers. Common in gamopetalae, e.g., Petunia, Datura.
Indefinitionediate between cellular and nuclear types. First division followed by wall formation; chalazal cell functions as haustorium. Prevalent in monocotyledons.
Mature endosperm with irregularity and unevenness on surface. Found in ~32 angiosperm families, e.g., Annonaceae, Palmae.
Effect of pollen on endosperm. definition given by Focke (1881), e.g., Maize.
Effect of pollen on somatic tissue outside endosperm. definition given by Swingle (1928), e.g., Date palm.

key_concepts: mosaic endosperm
aleurone layer
food storage

topics.name: Scrotum

subtopics.definition_content: A pouch of pigmented skin arising from the lower abdominal wall and hanging between the legs

key_concepts: Gubernaculum - pad supporting testis
Inguinal canal - passage through which testes descend
Homologous to labia majora in females

topics.name: Testes

subtopics.definition_content: Primary sex organs measuring 4-5 cm long, 2.5 cm wide, 3 cm thick, weighing 10-15 gms
Convoluted tubules numbering about 750 per testis, sites of spermatogenesis
Supportive nutritive cells that secrete inhibin and oestradiol
Endocrine cells in interstitium that secrete androgen (testosterone)

key_concepts: Tunica vaginalis, tunica albuginea, tunica vasculosa - three coverings
200-300 lobules per testis
Spermatogenesis takes 63 days
Germ cells - spermatogenic cells
Rete testis - plexiform network

topics.name: Vasa efferentia

subtopics.definition_content: 12-20 fine tubules connecting rete testis to caput epididymis with ciliated epithelium

key_concepts: Ductuli efferentes - alternate name
Ciliated lining conducts sperms
Part of intertesticular genital duct system

topics.name: Epididymis

subtopics.definition_content: Series of coiled efferent ducts measuring 6m in length, lined by pseudostratified columnar epithelium

key_concepts: Three parts: Caput (head), Corpus (middle), Cauda (tail)
Site of sperm storage and maturation
Peristaltic and segmenting contractions push spermatozoa
Combined with testis forms testicle

topics.name: Vasa deferentia

subtopics.definition_content: 45 cm long ducts continuing from cauda epididymis, slightly coiled in scrotum but straight in abdomen

key_concepts: Conduct sperms from epididymis to urethra
Pseudostratified columnar epithelium with stereocilia
Vasectomy - surgical sterilization

subtopics.definition_content: Short (2 cm) straight muscular tubes formed by union of vas deferens and seminal vesicle duct

key_concepts: Two ejaculatory ducts join urethra within prostate gland
Site of ejaculate formation

topics.name: Urethra

subtopics.definition_content: 20 cm long duct passing through prostate gland, urinogenital diaphragm, and penis

key_concepts: Four regions: Urinary, Prostatic, Membranous, Penile
Penile part also called spongy urethra
Carries both urine and semen

subtopics.definition_content: Erectile copulatory organ with shaft enlarging to glans penis, covered by prepuce (foreskin)

Tunica albuginea - dense connective tissue covering
Corona - margin of glans penis
Sperm storage in vasa efferentia, epididymes, proximal vasa deferentia

topics.name: Seminal vesicles

key_concepts: 60% of semen volume
Alkaline, nutritive fluid
Fructose as respiratory substrate for sperms
Not storage organs - misnomer as uterus-masculinus
Formed from mullerian duct embryologically

subtopics.definition_content: Gland surrounding first portion of urethra, secreting slightly acidic fluid (pH 6.5)

key_concepts: 25% of semen volume
Contains citric acid, calcium, phosphate, fibrinogen, fibrinolysin
Essential for sperm motility
Combines with seminal vesicle secretion causing coagulation then liquefaction

subtopics.definition_content: Also called bulbourethral glands, pair of glands attached to urethra secreting alkaline mucus

key_concepts: Discharge into spongy part of urethra
Lubrication and acid neutralization function
Secreted before ejaculation
Carries some spermatozoa - high failure rate of withdrawal method

topics.name: Perineal/Rectal glands

subtopics.definition_content: Glands in herbivorous and carnivorous mammals secreting odoriferous liquid with pheromones

key_concepts: Absent in humans
Active during breeding season in other mammals

chapters.name: Hormonal control of male reproductive system

ICSH controls Leydig cells
Secondary organs: epididymis, vasa deferentia, accessory glands, penis

Broadening of shoulders
Body and facial hair growth
Voice deepening
Musculature and bone development
Height increase characteristic of male puberty

Difficult or painful urination
Prostate cancer common in men

subtopics.definition_content: Inability to achieve or maintain penis erection for copulation

key_concepts: Results from immobility and morphological abnormality of sperms
Low sperm count contributes

Endocrine function - oestrogen and progesterone secretion
Become small after menopause with loss of follicles
Located close to lateral pelvic walls suspended by mesovarium
Compact solid organ with cortex and medulla

Develop from mullerian duct
Supported by mesosalpinx

Very short narrow thick-walled straight part following ampulla

Empty uterus dimensions: 7.5 cm long, 5 cm broad, 2.5 cm thick
Lining - endometrium with epithelium and lamina propria

Antiflexed position bending forward on itself

Cavity expands 500 times from 10 cm³ to 5000 cm³ during pregnancy

Lower parts of two uteri fused with septa between them
Lower parts of 2 uteri fused without partition wall
Both uteri completely fused forming single structure, most developed type in humans
Multiple role including menstruations site, implantation, fetal development, and labor

Fornix - depression around cervix
Two longitudinal ridges and numerous transverse vaginal rugae

Outer larger hair-covered fleshy skin folds with sebaceous and sweat glands

Fleshy elevation above labia majora bearing pubic hair made of adipose tissue

Perineum - area between fourchette and anus
Hymen - ruptured during first copulation

key_concepts: Vestibular glands - alternate name

Homologous to Cowper's glands in males

Contain fatty, connective tissues and milk glands
Modified sweat glands

Milk ejection controlled by oxytocin
Colustrum - first premilk after parturition

key_concepts: Compressed veins inhibit blood outflow
Filling of tissue with blood

subtopics.definition_content: Sexual intercourse with lubrication from urethral, Cowper's, and vaginal glands
Release of semen into proximal part of urethra by contraction of reproductive glands
Forceful discharge of semen into vagina from rhythmic muscle contractions
Peak of sexual stimulation with pleasurable sensation lasting few seconds

Friction from rhythmic movements stimulates sensory cells

key_concepts: Takes few minutes

chapters.name: Hormonal control of female reproductive system

topics.name: Pituitary gonadotropins action

subtopics.definition_content: Follicle stimulating hormone transforms primary follicle to Graafian follicle and matures ovum
Luteinizing hormone regulates ovulation and transforms Graafian follicle to corpus luteum

key_concepts: Ovary regulated by pituitary gonadotropins or GnRH
FSH controls follicle transformation and oestrogen secretion
LH regulates ovulation
LH transforms empty Graafian follicle to corpus luteum secreting progesterone

subtopics.definition_content: Female hormones controlling growth, maintenance and function of secondary sex organs
Hormone suspending ovulation during pregnancy and promoting fetal development
Hormone secreted by corpus luteum at pregnancy end broadening pelvis for parturition

topics.name: Puberty trigger and changes

subtopics.definition_content: Attained around age 13, triggered by FSH promoting ovarian follicle growth with estrogen secretion

Secondary sex character development: breast growth, external genitalia growth, pelvic broadening
Pubic and axillary hair growth
Subcutaneous fat increase in thighs, shoulders, buttocks, face
Typical feminine contour development
Menstruation and ovulation initiation

topics.name: Oestrous cycle

subtopics.definition_content: Cyclic reproductive changes consisting of few estrus days followed by anoestrus days
Heat period when female is sexually responsive allowing copulation and egg release
Quiescence period when female becomes passive not accepting males

key_concepts: Occurs in most mammal species except primates
Found in all mammals except primates
Some mammals experience throughout year (cow, buffalo)
Many mammals reproduce breeding season only
Rabbit oestrous cycle - 7 days
Rat oestrous cycle - 5 days
Dog - one cycle per year
Wall of uterus does not dissolve (no bleeding)
Mono-oestrous animals - one cycle in breeding period (Rabbit, Hare, Dog, Fox, Bat, Deer)
Poly-oestrous animals - many cycles (Rats, Squirrels, Cow, Sheep, Pig, Horse)

topics.name: Menstrual cycle

subtopics.definition_content: Gamete formation cyclic activity taking 28 days involving reproductive system structure and function changes

key_concepts: Occurs only in primates except new world monkeys
Cycle length varies 1-2 days between cycles
Absent during pregnancy and suppressed during lactation
Permanently stops at menopause

topics.name: Four phases of menstrual cycle

subtopics.definition_content: Lasts 9-10 days (days 6-13), involves FSH increase stimulating primary follicle to Graafian follicle
Involves ovulation from Graafian follicle on day 14, controlled by increased LH level
Lasts 12-14 days (days 16-28), characterized by corpus luteum progesterone secretion peaking day 22
Lasts 3-5 days (days 1-4), unfertilized ovum with 40 ml blood and uterine epithelium discharged

key_concepts: Ovulatory pattern: spontaneous ovulation (primates), induced ovulation (rabbit), seasonal ovulation (frog)
Secondary oocyte remains in zona pellucida and corona radiata during ovulation
Ovum viable two days
Decrease in progesterone and estrogen stimulates FSH-RF release
Fertilization restores diploid condition

topics.name: Effect of fertilization on menstrual cycle

subtopics.definition_content: Trophoblast cells of developing placenta secrete human chorionic gonadotrophin maintaining corpus luteum

key_concepts: hCG maintains corpus luteum progesterone/estradiol secretion
Prevents endometrium breakdown
Absence of menstrual bleeding earliest pregnancy sign
Week 16 of pregnancy placenta produces sufficient progesterone/estradiol
Corpus luteum becomes unnecessary and shrinks
Premature corpus luteum degeneration causes miscarriage at 10-12 weeks

topics.name: Gametogenesis definition_content

subtopics.definition_content: Formation of haploid gametes from undifferentiated diploid germ cells in gonads for sexual reproduction

key_concepts: Stimulated by FSH
Vitamins A and E necessary
Results in male gamete sperm and female gamete egg

topics.name: Spermatogenesis

subtopics.definition_content: Formation of sperms in seminiferous tubules of testis in male animals

key_concepts: Continuous process in mammals from seminiferous tubules with germinal epithelium
Primordial germ cells from yolk sac endoderm
Sertoli cells provide nutrition in form of glycogen
Three sub-stages: multiplication, growth, maturation

topics.name: Three phases of spermatogenesis

subtopics.definition_content: Primordial germ cells repeatedly undergo mitosis forming spermatogonia (diploid)
Spermatogonia grow 2-3 times becoming primary spermatocytes (diploid) - longest duration
Primary spermatocytes undergo meiosis-I forming 2 haploid secondary spermatocytes (first maturation division)

key_concepts: Reductional division - meiosis I
Secondary spermatocytes undergo meiosis-II equational division forming 4 spermatids
One primary spermatocyte produces 4 spermatids
Metamorphosis of spermatids into sperms = spermiogenesis or spermatoliosis

topics.name: Spermatoliosis

subtopics.definition_content: Transformation of round non-motile haploid spermatid into thread-like motile haploid sperm

key_concepts: From nucleus and golgi body - head part formed
From mitochondria - middle part formed

topics.name: Structure of sperm

subtopics.definition_content: Flat oval part composed of large posterior nucleus and small anterior acrosome
Formed from golgi complex containing digestive enzymes hyaluronidase and proteinase
Narrow space between nucleus and acrosome
Energy-chamber of sperm with spirally arranged mitochondria providing locomotion energy
Longest fibrous part enabling sperm movement with basal granule as distal centriole

key_concepts: Galea-capatis - acrosome and its membrane
Acrosome important for ovum penetration
Sperm nucleus very small without nucleoplasm and nucleolus
Contains only chromatin
Proximal centriole in pit-like depression induce cleavage
Distal centriole gives rise to axial filament
Centrioles at right angles
Mitochondrial sheath = Nabenkern sheath
Manchett - cytoplasm thin-sheet in middle-piece
Axonema surrounded by 9 solid protein fibres
Ring centriole at posterior middle-piece end
Tail parts: Main part broad with cytoplasm and 2 solid fibres; End piece narrow without cytoplasm

topics.name: Oogenesis

subtopics.definition_content: Process of ova formation in ovaries, starts before birth but completed after fertilization

key_concepts: Three phases: multiplication, growth, maturation
Unlike sperm formation, egg formation begins before birth

topics.name: Phases of oogenesis

subtopics.definition_content: Endodermal yolk sac cells enter ovary starting oogenesis during fetal development
Prolonged slow phase where oogonia form egg nests enlarge in ovarian follicles
Involves meiosis, nucleus shifts toward animal pole for meiosis-I

Multiply by mitotic divisions
Ovigerous cords or egg tubes of pfluger formed
Oogonia form rounded masses called egg nests
Ovarian follicle formed from egg nest
Central oogonium becomes primary oocyte
Others form follicular cells providing nourishment
Vitellogenesis - yolk deposition in growth phase
Zona pellucida secreted as mucoprotein membrane
Increase in reserve food, nucleus size, mitochondria number
Meiosis-I produces haploid secondary oocyte and primary polar body
Secondary oocyte proceeds meiosis-II but stops at metaphase-II
After fertilization second meiotic division completes with unequal cytoplasmic cleavage
Forms large ootid with whole cytoplasm and small second polar body

One primary oocyte forms one haploid ootid and two/three haploid polar bodies
Ootid grows into functional haploid ovum

topics.name: Structure of ovum

subtopics.definition_content: Generally spherical nonmotile gamete with yolky cytoplasm enclosed in egg envelopes
Outer smaller transparent part of differentiated cytoplasm
Inner larger opaque part of differentiated cytoplasm

Largest egg - ostrich 170×135 mm
Human ovum microlecithal 100 micrometers
Vitelline membrane - inner thin transparent secreted by ovum
Zona pellucida - middle thick transparent non-cellular

Perivitelline space - between vitelline membrane and zona pellucida

topics.name: Comparison of spermatogenesis and oogenesis

subtopics.definition_content: Both form gametes but differ in site, timing, growth phase duration, yolk synthesis, nuclear changes, gamete numbers, polar body formation, completion site, and gamete size

key_concepts: Spermatogenesis - seminiferous tubules (in), continuous (74 days), short growth, no yolk, nucleus condensed, 4 sperms per spermatogonium, no polar bodies, started and completed in testis, much smaller than spermatogonium

Oogenesis - ovaries (in), discontinuous (12-15 years minimum), long growth, vitellogenesis, nucleus bloated, 1 ovum per oogonium, 2-3 polar bodies, started in ovary completed in oviduct, much larger than oogonium

topics.name: Menopause definition_content and characteristics

Characterized by hot flushes sensation spreading trunk to face
Psychic symptoms present
FSH secreted in urine

topics.name: Eggs based on yolk amount and distribution

subtopics.definition_content: Very small yolk amount, evenly distributed (isolecithal/homolecithal)

Large yolk amount concentrated mainly in vegetal pole
Yolk concentrated in centre of egg
Enormous yolk amount with cytoplasm confined to disc-like area called germinal disc

Largest eggs ostrich eggs

topics.name: Eggs based on fate

key_concepts: Dedefinitioninate in invertebrates except echinoderms

Not surrounded by hard shell in water-living and viviparous animals

topics.name: Fertilization definition_content

topics.name: Fusion of gametic nuclei

subtopics.definition_content: Union of sperm and egg nuclei (gametic pronuclei)
Mixing of two chromosome sets from gametes

key_concepts: Head and middle piece advance into egg rotating 180 degrees
Mitochondria and proximal centriole assume leading position
Centriole subdivides establishing achromatic spindle
Second polar body produced with female pronucleus ready for union
Male pronucleus advances toward female pronucleus on copulation path
Sometimes slight course change in later copulation path portion

subtopics.definition_content: Chemicals from egg and sperm necessary for fertilization
Sperm hormones, two types: conserve sperm activity and dissolve vitelline membrane
Egg hormones, two types: neutralize androgamones and make sperm head sticky
Gamete fusion outside female body in most bony fishes, amphibians, echinoderms, lower chordates
Gamete fusion in female genital tract near ostium in oviparous, viviparous, and ovoviviparous animals
Two fusing gametes from same parent (uniparental) in Taenia, Fasciola
Two fusing gametes from different parents (biparental) in unisexual animals
Only one sperm enters and fuses with ovum in most animals
Many sperms penetrate ovum but only one fuses with ovum

topics.name: Significance of fertilization

subtopics.definition_content: Provides stimulus for egg maturation completion and activates ovum development

key_concepts: Restores diploid chromosome number (46 in humans)
Makes egg more metabolically active
Combines parental characters introducing variations for evolution
Sex chromosomes from sperm dedefinitionine sex
Fertilization membrane checks additional sperm entry
Copulation path sets division axis

topics.name: Cleavage definition_content

subtopics.definition_content: Series of rapid mitotic divisions of zygote following fertilization forming many-celled blastula

key_concepts: Follows fertilization ending with blastula formation

subtopics.definition_content: Cleavage furrow bisects both egg poles passing through animal-vegetal axis
Cleavage furrow passes from animal pole to vegetal pole not through median axis
Cleavage furrow bisects egg at right angles to median axis halfway between poles
Transverse plane resembling equatorial but passing above/below egg equator

topics.name: Patterns of cleavage

subtopics.definition_content: Blastomeres placed in radially symmetrical fashion around polar axis
4 blastomeres by usual 2 meridional cleavages, 3rd cleavage vertical forming 8 cells in 2 rows
Diagonal to polar axis with oblique mitotic spindles forming upper tier over lower cell junctions
Blastomeres arranged establishing bilateral symmetry plane in developing embryo

key_concepts: Micromeres - smaller upper cells
Macromeres - larger lower cells

subtopics.definition_content: Cells divide into equal daughter cells; each new division plane intersects preceding plane at right angles
Nucleus and achromatic figure occupy protoplasmic density centre; mitotic spindle axis occupies longest protoplasmic mass axis
Cleavage speed inversely proportional to yolk amount in region
Mitotic spindle always elongates in least resistance direction
Rapid complete zygote division in alecithal, homolecithal, mesolecithal eggs
Approximately equal blastomeres in echinoderms, amphioxus, placental mammals
Upper 4 micromeres smaller than lower 4 macromeres with yolk in fish and amphibians
Incomplete partial cleavage in large polylecithal eggs
Cleavage restricted to small cytoplasmic cap at animal pole forming germinal disc
Cleavage restricted to peripheral cytoplasm layer around yolk in centrolecithal eggs

topics.name: Cleavage in human zygote

subtopics.definition_content: Occurs during fallopian tube passage to uterus like other mammals, holoblastic type

key_concepts: First cleavage 30 hours after fertilization - meridional
Produces 2 blastomeres, one slightly larger
Second cleavage within 60 hours
Third cleavage about 72 hours
16-32 cell solid ball = morula after 4th cleavage
Two blastomere types from unequal cleavage: outer transparent micromeres, inner larger macromeres
Morula reaches uterus 4-6 days after fertilization
Still surrounded by zona pellucida preventing uterine wall sticking

subtopics.definition_content: Outer cell layer becomes flat (trophoblast/trophoectoderm) drawing endometrial secretions

key_concepts: Fluids in blastocoel collected in new central cavity
Increasing nutritive fluid expands morula forming cyst-like blastocyst
Trophoblast cells not participating in embryo proper formation
Inner macromere mass forms knob at trophoblast side forming embryonal knob
Embryonal knob - precursor of developing embryo body

subtopics.definition_content: Hollow blastula with blastocoel surrounded by single or many layered blastoderm
Coeloblastula type surrounded by two cell types: upper micromeres and lower macromeres
Solid blastula with no blastocoel in coelentrates, annelids, molluscs
Multilayered flat disc at animal pole on top of developed yolk in reptiles, birds, prototherians, fishes
Blastula as cyst with 2 cell types: outer epithelium-like trophoblast and inner embryonal knob
Blastocoel filled with yolk surrounded by peripheral cell layer in insects

topics.name: Implantation

subtopics.definition_content: Process of blastocyst attachment on uterus endometrium

key_concepts: Period: 6-10 days after fertilization, generally 7th day
Occurs in uterus in humans

topics.name: Gastrulation

subtopics.definition_content: Dynamic process with critical embryo changes including cell differentiation and three primary germ layer establishment

key_concepts: Transforms single-walled blastula to double-walled gastrula

subtopics.definition_content: Ectodermal micromeres movement anteroposteriorly enveloping endodermal and mesodermal blastomeres
Endodermal and chorda-mesodermal blastomere inward movement from blastula surface
Endodermal cells inward sinking forming archenteron in amphioxus
Chorda-mesodermal blastomere rolling in over blastopore lips in frog gastrulation
Individual blastomeres migration into blastocoel from vegetal pole (unipolar) or all sides (multipolar)
Blastoderm splitting into layers by groove appearance forming hypoblast in birds

subtopics.definition_content: Embryonal knob bottom surface cells detach by delamination forming embryonic endoderm
Blastocyst continuous growth with embryonal knob stretching and Rauber cells dispersing
Remaining embryonic disc cells become columnar forming ectoderm

key_concepts: Embryonic endoderm later forms embryonic gut
Remaining endoderm with trophoblast forms yolk sac
Embryonic disc continuous with trophoblast
Differentiated into cephalic, embryonic, caudal regions
Mesodermal layer forms between ectoderm and endoderm
Caudal region cells rapid proliferation forming mesodermal thickening

subtopics.definition_content: Epidermis, skin, nervous system, sensory organs, glands, tooth enamel, pituitary, pineal, adrenal medulla
Dermis, muscles, connective tissue, skeleton, vascular system, kidneys, gonads, adrenal cortex, spleen
Gut, glands, thyroid, parathyroid, thymus, lungs, urinary bladder, liver, pancreas, eustachian tube

subtopics.definition_content: Process of neural plate laying forming nervous system
Germ layer cell groups differentiation and specialization forming tissues and organs

key_concepts: Differentiated cell groups separate by folding or individual migration and reaggregation
By 4 weeks: simple heart, limb buds, eye rudiments, tail, pharyngeal pouches
Earlier vertebrate ancestor vestiges disappear later
After 2 months recognizable as primate embryo (called foetus)

subtopics.definition_content: Formed below embryo containing fluid (not yolk), vestigeal organ from oviparous reptilian ancestors
Formed above embryo with outer mesoderm and inner ectoderm, amniotic cavity encloses nearly surrounding embryo
Fold of splanchnopleur from hind gut with outer mesoderm and inner endoderm
Oudefinitionost somatopleur fold surrounding embryo in primates forming placenta

key_concepts: Amniotic fluid cushions, protects from injury, prevents desiccation
Yolk sac vestigeal in humans - blood cell formation site until 6 weeks, then liver
Allantois cavity acts as urinary bladder storing protein breakdown uric acid products
Chorioallantoic membrane extraembryonic lung supplying oxygen
In primates only chorion forms placenta (chorionic placenta)
Allantois remains small with extended blood vessels to vascularize

topics.name: Placenta

subtopics.definition_content: Temporary intimate mechanical and physiological connection between fetal and maternal tissues

key_concepts: Human placenta chorionic type only
Large branching villi from vascular chorion penetrate uterine wall crypts
Uterine wall thick and highly vascular to receive villi
Fully formed by 3 months lasting throughout pregnancy
Reddish-brown disc when complete
Fetal blood closely approaches maternal blood permitting material exchange
Maternal-fetal blood not in direct contact (incompatibility, pressure, harmful material check)
Nutrients (glucose, amino acids, proteins, lipids), water, minerals, vitamins, hormones, antibodies pass maternal to fetal blood
Fetal wastes (CO2, urea), water, hormones pass to maternal blood
Continuous oxygen uptake ensured by fetal/maternal haemoglobin affinity difference

subtopics.definition_content: Amino acids, monosugars, vitamins pass through placenta
O2 from maternal blood and CO2 from fetal blood diffuse through placenta
Nitrogenous wastes like urea pass through placenta
Effective for certain toxic chemicals but AIDS, syphilis, measles viruses, nicotine, heroin, cocaine pass through

topics.name: Classification of placenta

subtopics.definition_content: Marsupials (kangaroo, opossum) placentae derived from yolk sac and chorion
Majority of eutherian mammals have allantoic placenta from allantois and chorion
Primates (man, apes) placenta formed only by chorion, allantois remains small

key_concepts: Allantoic also called allantochorionic placenta
Outside eutheria, primitive allantoic placenta only in perameles (bandicoot metatherian)
Human mesoderm and blood vessels grow to chorion whose villi enter uterine crypts

subtopics.definition_content: Most primitive simplest type with all six placental barriers in odd hoofed mammals and lemurs
Uterine epithelium absent with five barriers in even hoofed mammals
Uterine epithelium and connective tissue absent with four barriers in carnivores, tree shrew, mole
Uterine epithelium, connective tissue, endothelium absent with 3 fetal layers in primates
Fetal capillaries indirect maternal blood contact with one barrier in rat, guinea pig, rabbit

topics.name: Gestation period

subtopics.definition_content: Duration between fertilization and parturition (280 days in human females)

key_concepts: Mouse minimum 19-20 days
Elephant maximum 607-641 days

topics.name: Parturition

subtopics.definition_content: Expulsion of fully formed young from mother's uterus after gestation period (280 days)

key_concepts: Progressive hormonal changes increase uterine musculature excitability
Progressive mechanical changes
Signals from fully developed fetus and placenta induce mild contractions (fetal ejection reflex)
Oxytocin causes uterine contraction
Fetus pituitary secretes increasing oxytocin quantities possibly exciting uterus
Fetal adrenal secretes large cortisol quantities as possible uterine stimulant
Progesterone inhibits uterine contractility during pregnancy
Estrogens increase uterine contractility degree
Estrogen-to-progesterone ratio increase likely partly responsible for increased uterine contractility

key_concepts: Estrogen and progesterone essential for pregnancy breast physical development
Both inhibit actual milk secretion
Prolactin exactly opposite effect - promotion of milk secretion
Secreted by mother's pituitary gland
Concentration rises steady from 5th pregnancy week to birth

subtopics.definition_content: Fluid secreted after parturition with same protein and lactose concentration as milk but almost no fat

key_concepts: Maximum production rate 1/100 subsequent milk production rate

Two hypothalamic factors transported via hypothalamic-hypophysial portal system

key_concepts: Very less iron and vitamin C quantity
Water 88.5%

topics.name: definition_content of Reproductive Health

subtopics.definition_content: A state of physical, mental and social well-being in all matters relating to the reproductive system at all stages of life. Good reproductive health implies that people are able to have a satisfying and safe sex life, the capability to reproduce and the freedom to decide if, when and how often to do so.

key_concepts: Family Planning
RCH Programmes
Reproductive and Child Health Care

topics.name: Problems and Strategies

subtopics.definition_content: Particularly in developing countries like India, has a direct bearing on the reproductive health of the society. Fast increasing human population has greater proportion of young individuals requiring health and education, with crucial concerns about age of marriage and childbearing capacity.

Introduction of sex education in schools to give right information to young individuals about reproductive organs, accessory organs of reproduction, secondary sexual characters, and related changes, sexually transmitted diseases (STDs), and related myths.

key_concepts: RCH Programmes objectives
Awareness creation
Birth control knowledge

topics.name: definition_content and Classification of STDs

subtopics.definition_content: Diseases or infections which are transmitted through infected persons during sexual intercourse. Also called venereal diseases (VD) or Reproductive tract infections (RTI).

HIV/AIDS (Human Immuno Deficiency Virus), Syphilis (Treponema palladium), Genital herpes (Herpes simplex virus), Hepatitis-B (Hepatitis B Virus), Enterobiasis (Enterobius Vermicularis), Candidiasis (Candida albicans), Gonorrhoea (Neisseria gonorrhoeae), Chlamydiasis (Chlamydia trachomatis), Trichomoniasis (Trichomonas vaginalis), Scabies (Sarcoptes scabiei).

key_concepts: Table 6.4-1
Bacterial STDs
Viral STDs
Parasitic STDs

topics.name: Modes of Transmission

subtopics.definition_content: STDs are generally transmitted through: (i) Sexual relations with infected persons, (ii) Using infected needles and surgical instruments, (iii) By blood transfusion, (iv) Through infected mother to the newborn.

key_concepts: Sexual contact
Blood transfusion
Vertical transmission

topics.name: Prevention of STDs

subtopics.definition_content: Sexual relationship with only one uninfected person; use precautions during sexual intercourse; consult qualified doctor without delay; have regular checkups; take medications for HPV or HSV if applicable; learn common symptoms and seek medical help immediately; use clean needles if injecting; take responsibility for protecting self and partners.

key_concepts: Safe practices
Medical consultation
Monogamy

topics.name: definition_content and Classification

subtopics.definition_content: The regulation of conception by preventive methods or devices to limit the number of offspring.
Birth control methods which deliberately prevent fertilization, divided into two main types: temporary and permanent.

key_concepts: Temporary methods
Permanent methods
Fertility regulation

topics.name: Temporary Methods of Birth Control

subtopics.definition_content: A week before and a week after menses is considered the safe period for sexual intercourse, based on: (a) Ovulation occurs on about the 14th day (may be 13th to 16th day) of menstruation, (b) Ovum survives for about 1-2 days, (c) Sperms remain alive for about 3 days. May reduce chances of pregnancy by about 80 percent.

The oldest method of birth control (used over 2,000 years ago), involves withdrawal of the penis from the vagina before ejaculation so that semen is not deposited in the vagina and there is no fertilization.

Foam, tablets, jellies, pastes and creams introduced into the vagina before sexual intercourse that adhere to the mucus membrane and immobilise and kill the sperms. Contain agents such as lactic acid, citric acid, boric acid, potassium permanganate and Zinc sulphate.

A thin sheath, usually made of rubber, to cover the erect penis. Most widely used contraceptive by males in India because it is cheap and easily available. Prevents deposition of semen in the vagina and serves as safeguard against AIDS and sexual diseases.

subtopics.definition: Mechanical Means – Diaphragm and Cervical Cap
subtopics.definition_content: Dome-shaped rubber plastic covers fitted on the cervix in the female's vagina, checking entry of sperms into the uterus. Must be kept fitted for at least six hours after sexual intercourse and smeared with spermicidal jelly or cream. Counterparts of condoms in females.

Plastic or metal objects placed in the uterus by a doctor (loop, copper-T, spiral, ring, bow, shield, etc.). Prevent fertilization of egg or implantation of embryo by acting as minor irritant making egg move quickly down Fallopian tubes before fertilization or implantation. Pregnancy rates less than 10%. Drawbacks include spontaneous expulsion, haemorrhage, perforation, tubal pregnancy, and infections.

Check ovulation by inhibiting secretion of follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) and luteinizing hormone (LH) necessary for ovulation. No eggs are released in women on the pill and conception cannot occur. Pregnancy rates less than 1%. Side effects include nausea, breast tenderness, weight gain, breakthrough bleeding, and high blood pressure. Examples: Mala D (taken daily), Saheli (taken weekly).

Certain contraceptives such as progestin minipill are implanted under the skin of the upper arm, preventing pregnancy for 3 to 4 years by slowly releasing progestin. Injectable one-month contraceptives are available in various countries.

Medical definitionination of pregnancy before the foetus becomes viable. One of the most widely used methods of fertility control. Certain pills (RU-486, an analogue of progesterone) act as abortants by inducing menstruation or blocking progesterone receptors, preventing pregnancy maintenance.

The best and 100% reliable way to avoid conception is to abstain from sexual intercourse. It is an unnatural mode of birth control, seems impracticable, though some couples practice abstinence at certain times successfully.

key_concepts: Hormone contraceptives
Barrier methods
Chemical methods
Pregnancy rate

topics.name: Permanent Methods of Birth Control

subtopics.definition_content: Provides permanent and sure birth control called vasectomy in man and tubectomy in woman. Involves removal of a short segment of each vas deferens or oviduct and tying up of the remaining ends tightly with surgical thread. Operations are minor, usually under local anesthesia, give little discomfort, and do not affect sexual life.

A telescopic instrument (laparoscope) used in tubal ligation to block the fallopian tubes. Eggs continue to be produced because ovaries are intact, but they fail to pass into the uterus and sperms fail to reach eggs for fertilization.

key_concepts: Vasectomy
Tubectomy
Irreversible
Most effective birth control

topics.name: Medical definitionination of Pregnancy (MTP)

subtopics.definition_content: Medical definitionination of pregnancy before the foetus becomes capable of living independently (viable). Classified as spontaneous (occur once in every 15 pregnancies, 'Nature's method of birth control') and induced (intentionally induced, may be legal or illegal).

subtopics.definition: Indications for Medical definitionination of Pregnancy
subtopics.definition_content: Legal indications include: (i) Failure of contraception with possible hazards on physical/mental health of mother, (ii) Pregnancy caused by rape, (iii) Possible hazard to health of growing foetus.

Indian government legalized MTP in 1971 with strict conditions to avoid misuse. Limitations are important to check indiscriminate and illegal female foeticide. Legally permitted up to 20 weeks of gestation. First trimester definitionination is safer than second trimester with fewer complications. Illegal to perform MTP after dedefinitionining sex of child.

Two commonly used methods: (1) Abortion pill for pregnancies up to 9 weeks old involving two drugs - mifepristone and prostaglandin taken 48 hours apart, (2) Surgical definitionination performed up to 13 weeks under local or general anesthetic.

key_concepts: Abortion pill (RU-486)
Surgical definitionination
Safe definitionination period
Legal status

topics.name: definition_content, Aim and Procedure

subtopics.definition_content: A pre-natal diagnostic technique to dedefinitionine: (1) Sex of the developing baby, (2) Genetically controlled congenital diseases, (3) Metabolic disorders in foetus.

Involves: (1) Location of foetus dedefinitionined by sonography (using high frequency ultrasound waves) to prevent accidental damage, (2) A fine hollow needle passed through abdominal and uterine wall of pregnant female (about 14th to 15th week after conception) into amniotic cavity, (3) Small amount of amniotic fluid withdrawn containing foetal skin cells and proteins, especially enzymes, which can be cultured in vitro for examination.

key_concepts: Pre-natal diagnosis
Sonography
Amniotic fluid
Foetal cells

topics.name: Significance of Amniocentesis

subtopics.definition_content: Somatic cells of foetal skin drawn with amniotic fluid are stained to dedefinitionine presence of sex chromatin (barr body). Presence of barr body indicates developing foetus is female (with 2 X-chromosomes, one of which is heterochromatised into darkly stained barr body).
By karyotypic studies of somatic cells, abnormalities due to changes in chromosome number like Down's syndrome, Turner's syndrome, Klinefelter's syndrome etc. can be dedefinitionined.

By enzyme analysis of amniotic fluid, different types of inborn metabolic disorders like phenylketonuria, alkaptonuria etc. can be detected. These inborn errors are caused by absence or inactivity of specific enzymes due to gene mutations.

Amniocentesis is being misused for dedefinitionination of foetal sex leading to female foeticide. Government of India enforced Pre-natal Diagnostic Techniques (Regulation and Prevention of Misuse) Act, 1994, since January 1, 1994 requiring registration for genetic counselling centres and laboratories. Violation brings fine of Rs. 50,000 and imprisonment for two years with cancellation of doctor's registration.

key_concepts: Karyotyping
Barr body
Sex chromatin
Genetic counselling

topics.name: Chorionic Villus Sampling (CVS)

subtopics.definition_content: A new technique that can be performed during 8th to 10th week of pregnancy when abortion is safe for the woman, unlike amniocentesis which is possible only after sixteenth week. Cells are sucked into a catheter passed through cervix. Provides mass of rapidly dividing foetal cells, facilitating examination of chromosomal disorders.

key_concepts: Early detection
Catheter technique
Chromosomal disorders

topics.name: definition_content and Concepts

subtopics.definition_content: The condition where a large number of couples are unable to produce children due to reasons that could be physical, congenital, diseases, drugs, immunological or psychological. Specialised health care units (infertility clinics) help in diagnosis and corrective treatment.
Special techniques used to assist infertile couples to have children where medical corrections are not possible.

key_concepts: Infertility clinics
Diagnosis and treatment

topics.name: Vitro Fertilisation and Embryo Transfer

subtopics.definition: IVF (In Vitro Fertilisation) - Test Tube Baby Programme
subtopics.definition_content: Fertilisation outside the body in almost similar conditions as in the body. Ova from wife/donor (female) and sperms from husband/donor (male) are collected and induced to form zygote under simulated laboratory conditions. Zygote or early embryos (with up to 8 blastomeres) transferred to fallopian tube (ZIFT) or embryos with more than 8 blastomeres into uterus (IUT).
Transfer of an ovum collected from a donor into the fallopian tube of another female who cannot produce an ovum but can provide suitable environment for fertilisation and further development.
A specialised procedure to form an embryo in the laboratory where a sperm is directly injected into the ovum.

Semen collected from husband or healthy donor is artificially introduced either into the vagina or into the uterus (IUI - Intra-uterine insemination) of the female. Used for infertility cases due to inability to inseminate or very low sperm counts.

key_concepts: Embryo transfer
Gamete collection
In vitro culture
Surrogate mother

chapters.name: Test Tube Babies (In Vitro Fertilisation and Embryo Transfer)

topics.name: definition_content and History

subtopics.definition_content: The technique of in-vitro fertilization and in-vitro development followed by embryo-transfer in the uterus of normal female to start development and finally leading to normal birth.

First attempt made by Italian scientist Dr. Petrucci (1959 A.D.). Although human embryo survived only 29 days, experiment opened new field of biological science. First test tube baby born July 25, 1978 to Lesley and Gilbert Brown (age girl, Louise Joy Brown) in Oldham, England. Created by Dr. Patricke Steptoe and Dr. Robert Edward. India's first test tube baby born October 3, 1978 in Kolkata named Kanupriya Aggarwal, created by Dr. Subash Mukherjee.

key_concepts: IVF
Embryo transfer
Louise Joy Brown
Kanupriya Aggarwal

topics.name: Procedure of Test Tube Baby Production

subtopics.definition_content: Involves: (1) Removal of unfertilized ovum from reproductive tract of female, (2) Ovum kept under aseptic conditions, (3) Fusion of sperm and ovum in culture medium outside female body to form zygote, (4) Zygote stimulated to develop in vitro up to 32-celled stage, (5) Developing embryo implanted on endometrium of uterus at 32-celled stage starting pregnancy and further development continues in womb till birth.

key_concepts: Zygote culture
Embryo implantation
32-celled stage

topics.name: Significance and Applications

subtopics.definition: Clinical Significance of Test Tube Baby
subtopics.definition_content: (1) Boon to infertile mothers, (2) Can be used for men with Oligospermia (low sperm count), (3) Old superior cows can donate oocytes. Embryos can be frozen and preserved in embryo tank for 10 years for future use.

In rare cases, surrogate mother may be used to bring up in vitro fertilized ovum to maturity. Though biological realization of test tube baby is remarkable achievement, it has raised several ethical and legal problems like rights over the child.

key_concepts: Oligospermia
Surrogate motherhood
Embryo preservation
Ethical issues

topics.name: Pre-Mendelian view points

subtopics.definition_content: Pythagoras proposed that different body parts produce minute particles
Malpighi believed that homunculus or miniature individual is present in sperm or egg; Antony Von Leeuwenhoek was first to observe human sperm
Proposed by Charles Darwin (1868); every cell, tissue and organ of animal body produces minute invisible bodies called gemmules or pangenes which can produce offsprings
August Weismann (1889) suggested the theory of continuity of germplasm; reproductive cells are germplasm and rest of the body as somatoplasm

key_concepts: blending inheritance
Fluid theory by Empedocles
Particulate theory by Maupertuis
Encasement theory by Charles Bonnet
Theory of epigenesis by Wolff

topics.name: Evidences against blending theory
key_concepts: atavism speaks against blending
traits of sex do not blend in unisexual organisms
black and white hybrid would be uniformly grey if blending were true

topics.name: Basic features of inheritance
key_concepts: traits have two alternative forms
unit factors do not blend

dominance of one alternative over the other

topics.name: Glossary of genetics definitions

subtopics.definition_content: An inherited factor that dedefinitionines a biological character of an organism; functional unit of hereditary material (Mendel called them factors)
Alternative forms of the same gene; e.g., Tall TT and dwarf tt are alternation forms of the same gene (Bateson 1902)
The specific place on a chromosome where a gene is located
Both the genes of a character are identical; gives rise to offspring having the same character on self-breeding; e.g., TT or tt (Bateson and Saunders, 1902)
Both the genes of a character are unlike; such organisms do not breed true on self fertilization; e.g., Tt (Bateson and Saunders, 1902)
The genetic constitution of an organism (Johannsen 1909); TT, Tt and tt are genotypes with reference to particular pairs of alleles
External feature of organisms, colour and behaviour etc.
Generations of homozygous individuals which produce offsprings of only one type; they breed true for their phenotype and genotype

A cross to know whether an individual is homozygous or heterozygous for dominant character; the individual is crossed with recessive parent; ratio 50% dominant:50% recessive for heterozygous individual
Total set of genes (DNA instructions) in the haploid set of chromosomes inherited as unit from parents to offspring
All the genotypes of all organisms in a population
A cross performed between hybrid and one of its parents
A checker-board used to show the result of a cross between two organisms; devised by geneticist R.C. Punnet (1927); depicts both genotypes and phenotypes of progeny

F2 generation (Bateson 1905)
monohybrid
dihybrid

clone = group of ramets

topics.name: Types of variations

subtopics.definition_content: Variations that influence somatic or body cells; appear after birth; non-inheritable and also called acquired characters, modifications or acquired variations; formed due to environmental factors, use and disuse of organs, and conscious efforts
Inheritable variations formed mostly in germinal cells which are either already present in ancestors or develop anew due to mutations
Fluctuating variations also called recombinations; formed due to recombination of alleles as found in sexual reproduction; Darwin (1859) based his theory of evolution on continuous variations
Mutations which are the ultimate source of organic variations; caused by chromosomal aberrations, change in chromosome number and gene mutations

mutations
recombination
grey to white pea seed coat is spontaneous mutation example

adaptability
breeders improve races
inbreeding reduces variation

key_concepts: Mendel born July 22, 1822; died 1884
worked on Garden pea (Pisum sativum) 1856-1864
published 1866 in Proceedings of Brunn Natural History Society
rediscovered by Hugo de Vries, Carl Correns, Erich von Tshermak in 1900
selected annual plant with short life cycle

topics.name: Monohybrid cross

subtopics.definition_content: Experiments with garden pea for single pair of contrasting characters; F2 produces tall and dwarf in approximately 3:1 phenotypic ratio and 1:2:1 genotypic ratio; monohybrid test cross ratio is 1:1

F2 3:1 ratio
law of dominance illustrated

subtopics.definition_content: Crosses involving two pairs of contrasting traits; cross between round yellow and wrinkled green seeds; F2 produces 9:3:3:1 phenotypic ratio and 1:2:2:4:1:2:1:2:1 genotypic ratio; dihybrid test cross ratio is 1:1:1:1

subtopics.definition_content: Shows 27:9:9:9:3:3:3:1 ratio in F2; trihybrid test cross ratio is 1:1:1:1:1:1:1:1; di, tri, or polyhybrid cross is actually a combination of two, three or more monohybrid crosses
