combination of monohybrid crosses

topics.name: Inter-allelic or intra-genic gene interaction

subtopics.definition_content: F1 hybrids exhibit a blending of characters of two parents; first reported in 4-O'clock plant (Mirabilis jalapa) by Carl Correns (1903); F1 plants with pink flowers (Rr) on self-pollination develop red:pink:white in 1:2:1 ratio in F2; Snapdragon or dog flower (Antirrhinum majus) is another example

Both the genes of an allelomorphic pair express themselves equally in F1 hybrids; 1:2:1 ratio both genotypically as well as phenotypically in F2 generation; examples: coat colour in cattle, Andalusian fowl, blood alleles in man

key_concepts: 1:2:1 ratio in incomplete dominance
blending inheritance
Mirabilis jalapa
Antirrhinum majus
Andalusian fowl

topics.name: Non-allelic or inter-genic gene interaction

subtopics.definition_content: Two pairs of nonallelic dominant genes present on separate gene loci which interact to produce only one phenotypic trait; neither of them alone produces the phenotypic trait in the absence of other; gives 9:7 ratio in F2; example: flower colour in sweet pea (Lathyrus)

Two independent pairs of dominant genes which interact such that one dominant gene will produce its effect whether the other is present or not; second dominant changes expression of first only in presence of first; 9:3:4 ratio; example: coat colour in rats and guinea pigs

Interaction between nonallelic genes in which one gene masks, inhibits or suppresses the expression of other gene; the gene that suppresses is known as epistatic factor and the one prevented from exhibiting is hypostatic
Out of two pairs of genes, the dominant allele of one gene masks the activity of the other allelic pair; gives 12:3:1 or 13:3 ratio; example: coat colour in dogs, fruit colour in Cucurbita
Out of two pairs of genes, the recessive epistatic gene masks the activity of the dominant gene of the other gene locus; gives 9:3:4 ratio

Two pairs of genes located on different chromosomes that dedefinitionine the same phenotype; dominant form of either gene produces same phenotype; gives 15:1 ratio in F2; example: triangular fruit shape in Capsella bursa pastoris, coat colour of mice

Two gene pairs present on separate loci that influence the same trait and interact to produce some totally new trait that neither gene by itself could produce; example: inheritance of combs in poultry (rose + pea = walnut comb)

key_concepts: epistasis
dominant epistasis 12:3:1
recessive epistasis 9:3:4
sweet pea Lathyrus 9:7
Capsella bursa pastoris 15:1

topics.name: Law of segregation (Purity of gametes)

subtopics.definition_content: When a pair of contrasting factors or genes or allelomorphs are brought together in a heterozygote, the two members of the allelic pair remain together without being contaminated and when gametes are formed from the hybrid, the two separate out from each other and only one enters each gamete

key_concepts: purity of gametes
allelomorphs
heterozygote
gamete formation
Correns formulated two laws from Mendel's generalizations

topics.name: Law of independent assortment

subtopics.definition_content: If the inheritance of more than one pair of characters is studied simultaneously, the factors or genes for each pair of characters assort out independently of the other pairs; Mendel formulated this law from dihybrid cross results

key_concepts: two or more pairs of characters
dihybrid cross
independent segregation
sexually reproducing organisms

topics.name: Lethal genes

subtopics.definition_content: Genes known to control the manifestation of some phenotypic trait as well as affect the viability of the organism; first reported in mice body by French geneticist Cuenot; first reported in plants in snapdragons (Antirrhinum majus) by E. Baur (1907)

Lethal genes that are lethal in homozygous condition and produce defective or abnormal phenotypes in heterozygous condition; example: yellow lethal in mice by Cuenot - yellow mice never breed true; homozygous yellow (YY) dies in embryonic condition giving 2:1 live yellow:brown ratio

Lethal genes that produce lethal effect only in homozygous condition; heterozygotes are normal; remain unnoticed in population; detected only when two heterozygous persons get married; example: Tay Sach's lethal

key_concepts: Cuenot
yellow mice 2:1 ratio
Stiegleder 1917 concluded yellow mice are heterozygous
multiple effect of gene Y
sickle cell anaemia pleiotropy

topics.name: Qualitative inheritance

subtopics.definition_content: Monogenic inheritance; type of inheritance in which one dominant allele influences the complete trait, so that two such alleles do not change the phenotype; dominant allele is monogene

key_concepts: monogenic
dominant allele monogene

topics.name: Quantitative/Polygenic inheritance

subtopics.definition_content: Two or more different pairs of alleles which have cumulative effect and govern quantitative characters; due to incomplete dominance; produces multiple phenotypes in F2: 1:2:1 for one pair of alleles, 1:4:6:4:1 for two pairs; examples: ear size in maize, grain colour in wheat, white spotting in mice

key_concepts: cumulative effect
continuous variation
Nilsson-Ehle proved quantitative inheritance 1908

topics.name: Criteria for cytoplasmic inheritance

subtopics.definition_content: Inheritance through hereditary material present in cytoplasm (mitochondria, plastids or foreign organism); shows maternal influence because most cytoplasm contributed by ovum; first evidence presented by Correns in Mirabilis jalapa and Baur in Pelargonium zonale in 1908
Total self-duplicating hereditary material of cytoplasm
Cytoplasmic units of inheritance

key_concepts: maternal influence
mitochondria
plastids
shell coiling in snail
kappa particles in Paramecium
Mirabilis plastid inheritance
male sterility in maize

topics.name: Theories of linkage

subtopics.definition_content: When genes are closely present they link together in a group and transmitted as a single unit; reported in Drosophila by T.H. Morgan in 1910

Proposed by Bateson and Punnet (1906); dominant alleles tend to remain together (coupling); if dominant and recessive alleles are in different parents they tend to remain separate (repulsion); cis-arrangement forms gametes (AB) and (ab); trans-arrangement forms gametes (Ab) and (aB)

key_concepts: Sutton's hypothesis 1903
Morgan's hypothesis 1910
chromosomal hypothesis by Morgan and Castle
cis heterozygote
trans heterozygote
X chromosome carries 102 genes
Y chromosome carries 10 genes

topics.name: Types of linkage

subtopics.definition_content: Linked genes are transmitted together to offspring only in their original or parental combination for two or more generations; no new or non-parental combinations formed; very rare; e.g., males of Drosophila (Morgan, 1919)

Homologous chromosomes undergo breakage and reunion during gametogenesis; some nonparental or new combinations produced through crossing over; very common; studied in almost all organisms; first described by Hutchinson in maize seed

key_concepts: parental combination
new combination
Hutchinson incomplete linkage in maize

topics.name: Linkage groups

subtopics.definition_content: All the genes which are linked with one another; number of linkage groups equals haploid number of chromosomes; e.g., Drosophila n=4 so 4 linkage groups; Pisum sativum n=7 so 7 linkage groups; prokaryotes have one linkage group

key_concepts: haploid number equals linkage groups
Sutton's hypothesis proved by Morgan on Drosophila

topics.name: Strength of linkage
key_concepts: closely located genes show strong linkage
widely located genes show weak linkage

topics.name: Factor affected to linkage
key_concepts: distance: close = strong linkage
age: increasing age increases strength
temperature: increasing temperature decreases strength
X-rays: reduce strength of linkage

topics.name: Significance of linkage
key_concepts: maintaining valuable traits of new variety
helps locating genes on chromosome
disallows combining all desirable traits

formulas.expression: Strength of linkage ∝ 1 / (Distance between the gene)
formulas.description: Strength of linkage is inversely proportional to distance between genes

topics.name: Crossing over and chiasma

subtopics.definition_content: The recombination of linked genes brought about as a result of interchange of corresponding parts between the chromatid of a homologous pair of chromosomes, so as to produce new combination of old genes; definition given by Morgan and Cattle; Janssen (1909) observed chiasmata during meiosis-I (Prophase)
According to Janssen (1909), the act of crossing over is followed by chiasma formation; crossing over takes place at pachytene stage and chiasma appear at diplotene

According to Sharp (1934), crossing over is the result of chiasma formation; chiasma are organised at pachytene and crossing over takes place at diplotene stage; rejected based on molecular biology evidence

key_concepts: Janssen 1909 chiasmata
new combination vs parental type
recon is unit of recombination

topics.name: Mechanism of crossing over

subtopics.definition_content: According to Belling (1928), chromomeres duplicate first then interchromomeric regions; synthesis may occur such that chromomeres of one chromatid connect to chromatid of other homologue at specific location causing exchange of adjacent chromatids

According to Darlington, pairing of homologues occurs to avoid singleness of a chromosome; crossing over takes place due to torsion on chromosome created by coiling of two homologues around each other

key_concepts: chromomeres
interchromomeric regions
breakage and reunion

topics.name: Factors controlling frequency of crossing over

subtopics.definition_content: If there are two double crossovers, one crossover tries to influence the other by suppressing it; frequency of crossing over is always lower than expected due to this phenomenon
Inverse measure of interference; ratio between actual number of double crossovers and expected number of such double crossovers

key_concepts: temperature
X-ray
age
sex (negligible in Drosophila males, absent in silkmoth females)
inversions suppress crossing over
centromere effect reduces crossing over
nutritional effects
genotypic effects

topics.name: Significance of crossing over
key_concepts: evidence of linear arrangement of genes
chromosomal mapping
genetic variations
new recombinants for breeding
operational definition_content of gene as smallest heritable segment without internal crossing over

formulas.expression: Coincidence = Actual number of double cross over / Expected number of double cross over
formulas.description: Coefficient of coincidence; inverse measure of interference

topics.name: Uses of chromosomal map

subtopics.definition_content: A linkage or genetic chromosome map is a linear graphic representation of the sequence and relative distances of the various genes present in a chromosome; also called linkage map or genetic map; first prepared by Sturtevant (1911)
Unit of crossing over devised by Haldane; one unit of map distance (cM) is equivalent to 1% crossing over; when chiasma is organised between two gene loci, only 50% meiotic products are crossovers

key_concepts: Sturtevant 1911 first chromosomal map
three-point test cross
chiasma frequency = 2 × cross over %

formulas.description: Relationship between chiasma frequency and crossover frequency
Crossover percentage from chiasma count

topics.name: Discovery of chromosomes
key_concepts: Hofmeister 1848 first observed in Tradescantia
Flemming 1879 coined chromatin
Roux 1883 believed chromosomes take part in inheritance
W. Waldeyer 1888 coined chromosome
Benden and Boveri 1887 fixed number per species

topics.name: Chromosomal theory of inheritance

subtopics.definition_content: Proposed independently by Sutton and Boveri in 1902; chromosomes are vehicles of hereditary information and expression as Mendelian factors or genes

key_concepts: Sutton and Boveri 1902
chromosomes as hereditary vehicles

topics.name: Kinds of chromosomes

subtopics.definition_content: In bacteria and cyanobacteria, hereditary matter is organized into a single large, circular molecule of double stranded DNA, loosely packed in the nuclear zone; known as bacterial chromosome or nucleoid
Chromosomes of eukaryotic cells are specific individualized bodies, formed of deoxyribonucleoproteins (DNA + Proteins)

key_concepts: viral chromosomes: single molecule of DNA or RNA
prokaryotic chromosomes
eukaryotic chromosomes

topics.name: Number of chromosomes
key_concepts: least chromosomes in Ascaris megalocephala (2n=2)
maximum in Adder's tongue fern Ophioglossum reticulatum (2n=1262)
human: 2n=46
chromosome number fixed for species

topics.name: Structure of chromosome

subtopics.definition_content: A part of the chromosome marked by a constriction; narrower than remaining chromosome; microtubules of chromosomal spindle fibres are attached here; kinetochore is oudefinitionost covering of centromere

Constriction other than primary constriction in certain chromosomes; associated with nucleolus during interphase; contains genes coding for 18S and 28S ribosomal RNA; known as nucleolar organizer region (NOR); in human beings chromosomes 13, 14, 15, 20 and 21 are nucleolar organizers

The rounded and sealed tips of the chromosomes; play role in Biological clock; definitioninal part beyond secondary constriction is called satellite; chromosome with satellite is known as sat chromosome

Coiled threads forming the bulk of chromosomes; a chromosome may have one (anaphase) or two (prophase and metaphase); the coiled filament called chromonema by Vejdovsky in 1912; two types: paranemic coils (easily separable) and plectonemic coils (inter-twined)

key_concepts: pellicle
matrix
chromomeres by J. Bellings
chromatids at metaphase
sat chromosome

topics.name: Molecular organisation of chromosome

subtopics.definition_content: Chromosomes composed of very fine fibrils 2-4 nm in thickness; long DNA molecule present in folding manner; given by E.J. Dupraw (1965)

Most accepted model proposed by Kornberg and Thomas (1974); nucleosome is oblate particle of 55Å height and 110Å diameter; quasicylindrical structure of histone octamer (H2A, H2B, H3, H4) wrapped by DNA with 1¾ turns making 146 base pairs; H1 is linker histone connecting nucleosomes; linker DNA joins two nucleosomes; name given by P. Outdet et al.

Nucleosomal bead represents first degree of coiling; further coiled to form solenoid (six nucleosomes per turn, diameter 300Å); solenoid further coiled to supersolenoid (2000-4000Å); given by Finch and Klug 1976; Klug awarded Nobel Prize 1982

Laemmli (1977); each chromosome has one or two scaffolds of nonhistone chromosomal proteins with lateral loops; each lateral loop is 30nm thick fibre developed through solenoid coiling of nucleosome chain

key_concepts: multiple strand models (Steffensen, Ris)
single strand models (Taylor, Dupraw)
nucleosome = core particles = Nu-bodies
Woodcock 1973 observed nucleosome structure

topics.name: Heterochromatin and Euchromatin

subtopics.definition_content: Highly condensed chromatin; transcriptionally inactive; found in centromeres; two types: constitutive heterochromatin (permanent, genetically inactive) and facultative heterochromatin (varies in different cell types and development stages)
Relatively extended and open chromatin; at least has potential of being actively transcribed; makes up major part of genome; visible only during mitosis; named by Flemming 1880

key_concepts: Flemming 1880 named chromatin
interphase vs cell division
constitutive vs facultative heterochromatin

topics.name: Chromosome banding

subtopics.definition_content: Technique demonstrated by Casperson (1968) using fluorescent dye quinacrine mustard; each chromosome has characteristic banding pattern; types: Q (quinacrine), G (Giemsa), R (reverse), T (telomeric), C (centromere) banding

key_concepts: Q banding AT-rich
G banding late-replicating
R banding = reverse of G
T banding at telomeres
C banding centromeric heterochromatin

topics.name: Human karyotype and idiogram

subtopics.definition_content: A set of chromosomes of an individual or species; in humans 23 pairs (46) chromosomes form the karyotype; first found to be 46 by Tijo and Levan (1956) of Sweden; 22 pairs autosomes + 1 pair sex chromosomes (XX female, XY male)

Homologous pairs of identified chromosomes arranged in a series of decreasing lengths; not possible in symmetrical karyotype; human chromosomes classified by Denver conference (1960) into groups A to G by Patau (1960)

key_concepts: phytohaemaglutinin (PHA) stimulates WBC division
colchicine added at metaphase to stop division
hypotonic saline treatment
Giemsa or quinacrine dye for staining
karyotyping from blood WBCs, bone marrow, amniotic fluid

topics.name: Type of chromosomes

subtopics.definition_content: V-shaped chromosomes in which centromere lies in the middle of chromosomes so that the two arms are almost equal
J or L shaped chromosomes with centromere slightly away from the mid-point so that the two arms are unequal
Rod-shaped chromosomes having subdefinitioninal centromere; one arm is very long and the other is very small
Rod-shaped chromosomes with centromere occupying a definitioninal position; one arm is very long and the other is absent

key_concepts: monocentric
dicentric
polycentric
acentric
diffused centromere in insects and some algae

topics.name: Special types of chromosomes

subtopics.definition_content: Described by Kollar (1882) and first reported by Balbiani (1881) in salivary gland cells of chironomus larva; found in salivary glands of insects (Drosophila); formed by somatic pairing between homologous chromosomes and repeated replication (endomitosis) of chromonemata; length up to 2000μm; show bands with puffs bearing Balbiani rings producing mRNA

Very elongated special type of synapsed or diplotene chromosome bivalents; first observed by Flemming (1882); structure described by Ruckert (1892); occur at diplotene stage of meiotic prophase in primary oocytes of all animal species; show rows of lateral loops for rapid transcription of mRNA; length up to 5900μm in urodele oocyte

key_concepts: B chromosomes = supernumerary chromosomes
B chromosomes genetically unnecessary
Balbiani rings produce mRNA stored temporarily in puffs
lampbrush chromosomes in vertebrate and invertebrate oocytes
mRNA stored as informosomes

topics.name: Gene action

subtopics.definition_content: Given by Beadle and Tatum (1958) working on red mould Neurospora (Drosophila of plant kingdom); each gene produces a specific enzyme controlling a specific metabolic activity; conidia irradiated with X-rays or UV-rays; prototrophs grow on minimal medium, auxotrophs do not; replaced by one gene one polypeptide theory by Yanofsky et al. (1965)

key_concepts: Neurospora crassa
prototrophs
auxotrophs
minimal medium
conidia
ascospores

topics.name: Molecular structure of gene

subtopics.definition_content: That particular length of DNA which is capable of producing a protein molecule or polypeptide chain or enzyme molecule; functional gene; given by Benzer (1955); also called complon
That length of DNA which is capable of undergoing mutation; having one or two pairs of nucleotides; given by Seymour Benzer
That length of DNA which is capable of undergoing crossing over or capable of recombination; having one or two pairs of nucleotides; given by Seymour Benzer
Combination of operator gene and sequence of structural genes which act together as a unit; composed of several genes
Unit of replication; several replicons constitute a chromosome

key_concepts: Seymour Benzer
cistron = muton = recon at DNA level
gene = segment of DNA for one polypeptide chain
Khorana Nobel prize for synthesis of artificial gene

topics.name: Some specific definitions

subtopics.definition_content: DNA segments which can join with other DNA segments causing illegitimate pairing; definition given by Hedges and Jacob (1974) for bacteria; actual discovery by Barbara McClintock (1940) in maize who named them controlling elements or mobile genetic elements; McClintock awarded Nobel Prize (1983)
DNA segments formed from RNA by reverse transcription under influence of reverse transcriptase enzyme; about 10% of DNA in genome of primates and rodents; definition given by Rogers (1983)
Genes made of segments of exons (informational) and introns; first reported in mammalian virus and eukaryotes by R. Roberts and P. Sharp (1977)
DNA sequences present in multicellular organisms which are useless; defective copies of functional genes; reported in Drosophila, mouse and human beings

key_concepts: exons carry genetic information
introns present in split genes
reverse transcriptase = RNA dependent DNA polymerase
McClintock Nobel 1983

topics.name: Characteristics of multiple allelism

subtopics.definition_content: More than two alternative forms (alleles) of a gene in a population occupying the same locus on a chromosome or its homologue; examples: blood groups in man, skin colour, eye colour in Drosophila, coat colour in cattle

key_concepts: more than two alleles in population
only two alleles per individual
examples in blood groups and coat colour
follows Mendelian inheritance

topics.name: Blood groups in man

subtopics.definition_content: A proteinous substance found on the cell membrane of RBCs
Proteinous substance found in the plasma of blood; reacts with foreign antigens

Karl Landsteiner (1900, Nobel prize) divided human population into four blood groups (A, B, AB, O) based on presence of antigens on red blood corpuscles; blood group A has antigen A and antibody 'b'; blood group B has antigen B and antibody 'a'; blood group AB has A and B antigens with no antibodies; blood group O has no antigens with both 'a' and 'b' antibodies
AB blood group persons who have both antigens A and B and no antibodies; can receive blood of all blood groups
O- blood group persons who lack both antigens A and B; can donate blood to all blood groups

key_concepts: Landsteiner 1900
agglutination = antigen-antibody reaction
blood bank temperature 4-6°C for 21 days maximum
sodium citrate anticoagulant
M,N blood group by Landsteiner and Wiener

topics.name: Inheritance of blood groups

subtopics.definition_content: Three multiple alleles Iᴬ, Iᴮ, i (Iᴼ) on same locus on homologous chromosomes; Iᴬ produces antigen A, Iᴮ produces antigen B, i produces no antigen; Iᴬ and Iᴮ are codominant and both dominant over i; inheritance of blood groups discovered by Bernstein in 1925

key_concepts: six genotypes for four blood groups
IᴬIᴬ and Iᴬi = blood group A
IᴮIᴮ and Iᴮi = blood group B
IᴬIᴮ = blood group AB (codominant)
ii = blood group O

topics.name: Rhesus or Rh factor

subtopics.definition_content: A protein found in blood of Rhesus monkey; discovered by Landsteiner and Weiner (1940); Rh positive persons (RR or Rr genotype) have Rh antigen; Rh negative (rr) do not; about 85% Europeans and 97% Indians are Rh+

Disease causing death of fetus in womb or just after birth; occurs when Rh+ father and Rh- mother; child inherits Rh+ from father; mother builds Rh antibodies after first pregnancy; antibodies attack subsequent Rh+ fetuses causing agglutination of fetal RBCs; studied by Levine with Landsteiner and Wiener

Method of preventing erythroblastosis foetalis; Rh- mother given injections of rhogam (anti-Rh antibodies from immunized donors) after delivery of Rh+ child; coats fetal RBCs so no Rh+ antigens stimulate mother's antibody formation

key_concepts: Rh+ = RR or Rr
Rh- = rr
incompatible marriage = Rh+ boy × Rh- girl
Rh- mother builds Rh antibodies after first Rh+ child
Rh factor dedefinitionined by four pairs of multiple alleles

topics.name: Gene mutations

subtopics.definition_content: Sudden, stable discontinuous and inheritable variations which appear in organism due to permanent change in their genotype; idea first from observations of Hugo de Vries (1880) on Oenothera lamarckiana
Mutations occurring in nature without a known cause
Mutations induced using numerous physical and chemical agents to increase frequency of mutations
Substitution gene mutation in which a purine is replaced by purine or pyrimidine by pyrimidine; e.g., GC→AT or AT→GC
Substitution gene mutation in which a purine is replaced by pyrimidine or vice versa; e.g., GC→CG or TA, AT→TA or CG
Addition or deletion of single nitrogenous base; none of the codons remain in same original position; reading of genetic code is shifted laterally in forward or backward direction

key_concepts: Hugo de Vries 1880
tautomerism changed pairing qualities of bases
gene or point mutations are stable changes in DNA

topics.name: Chromosomal mutation or aberrations

subtopics.definition_content: A segment of chromosome breaks off and gets lost; definitioninal segment loss is deficiency; intercalary segment loss is deletion; generally proves lethal or semilethal; cri-du-chat syndrome in human babies due to deletion of chromosome 5 segment (baby cries like a cat, mentally retarded, small head)
Deleted chromosomal segment is attached to normal homologous chromosome; a gene or many genes are repeated twice or more in the same chromosome
A piece of chromosome is removed and rejoined in reverse order; e.g., gene order A,B,C,D,E with inversion of BCD becomes A,D,C,B,E
Mutual exchange (reciprocal) of chromosome segments between non-homologous chromosomes; simple translocation involves transfer of segment from one chromosome to another non-homologous one

key_concepts: morphological aberrations
numerical aberrations
Wolf-Hirschhorn syndrome = deletion of 4p-
cri-du-chat = deletion of chromosome 5 segment

topics.name: Numerical aberrations of chromosomes

subtopics.definition_content: Somatic chromosome number is exact multiple of basic haploid number; includes monoploidy (n) and polyploidy (3n, 4n etc.); autopolyploidy = same genome repeated (gigas effect); allopolyploidy = hybridization between species with chromosome doubling (e.g., wheat, Raphanobrassica, Triticale)

Chromosomal mutations involving only a part of a set; loss = hypoploidy (monosomy 2n-1, nullisomy 2n-2); addition = hyperploidy (trisomy 2n+1, tetrasomy 2n+2); results from non-disjunction of chromosomes during cell division

key_concepts: autopolyploidy Maize, Rice, Gram
allopolyploidy Wheat, American cotton, Nicotiana
Raphanobrassica = Raphanus × Brassica
gigas effect from autopolyploidy
trisomy in Datura studied thoroughly

topics.name: Mutagens

subtopics.definition_content: Any substance or agent inducing mutation; first used by H.J. Muller (1927) on animals and L.J. Stadler (1928) on plants

key_concepts: physical mutagens: ionizing (alpha, beta, X-rays, gamma) and nonionizing (UV)
chemical mutagens: base analogues (BrdU, 2-amino purine), alkylating agents (nitrogen mustard, EMS, MMS), intercalating agents (proflavin, acridine orange)
hydroxylamine and nitrous acid

topics.name: Genetic diseases in man

subtopics.definition_content: Genetic disease due to molecular mutation of gene Hbˢ on chromosome 11; sixth amino acid in β chain changed from glutamic acid to valine; homozygous (HbˢHbˢ) have sickle-cell disease; heterozygous (HbᴬHbˢ) have sickle-cell trait; trait confers immunity to malaria

Human anaemia due to autosomal mutant gene; thalassemia major (homozygous, severe, death in childhood); thalassemia minor = Cooley's anaemia (heterozygous); inability to produce β chain of haemoglobin; β chain replaced by δ chains like in fetus

Autosomal recessive mutation on chromosome 12 discovered by A. Folling in 1934; deficiency of liver enzyme phenylalanine hydroxylase; high phenylalanine in blood interferes with brain development; muscular and cartilage defects

First recessive human trait discovered in 1902 by Archibald Garrod (father of biochemical genetics); patients excrete large amounts of homogentistic acid in urine turning black upon exposure to light; deficiency of homogentistic acid oxidase

key_concepts: chondrodystrophic dwarfism dominant autosomal
Huntington disease dominant on chromosome 4
Albinism: autosomal recessive, tyrosinase deficiency
Marfan's syndrome: dominant, spider fingers (arachnodactyly)
Tay-Sachs: hexosaminidase deficiency chromosome 15
cystic fibrosis: recessive chromosome 7
galactosemia: GPT enzyme deficiency
Philadelphia chromosome in CML

topics.name: Autosomal trisomies

subtopics.definition_content: Also known as Mongolian idiocy or mongolism; trisomy of 21st chromosome (2n+1=47); studied by Langdon Down of England (1866); features: broad forehead, short neck, flat palms without crease, stub fingers, permanently open mouth, projecting lower jaw, low intelligence, defective heart; born to mothers aged 40 and above; survive up to 20 years under medical care
Trisomy of 18th pair of autosomes (2n+1=47); child survives only about 6 months; defective nervous system, malformed ears, receding chin
Trisomy of 13th pair of autosomal chromosome; harelip, clefted palate, cerebral, ocular and cardiovascular defects; children usually survive about 3 months only

key_concepts: trisomy 21 = Down's

topics.name: Sex chromosome syndromes

subtopics.definition_content: Monosomic for sex chromosomes (XO); 2n-1=45 chromosomes; phenotypic females but sterile; underdeveloped reproductive organs; lack female hormone estrogen; dwarf about 4 feet 10 inches; wide spread nipples; about 1 in 5,000 female births

Trisomy of sex chromosomes (XXY); 2n+1=47 chromosomes; underdeveloped testes in adulthood; develop female secondary sex characters (large breasts, loss of facial hair); sterile males and mentally retarded; about 1 in 5,000 males; known since 1942
Presence of extra X chromosomes in females (XXX, XXXX, XXXXX); 47, 48, or 49 chromosomes; abnormal sexual development and mental retardation; severity increases with more X chromosomes
Presence of extra Y chromosome in males (XYY); 2n+1=47 chromosomes; unusual height, mentally retarded, criminal bent of mind; underdeveloped genital organs; frequency 1 in 300 males

sex chromosome number dedefinitionines phenotype
XO = Turner's no Barr body
XXY = Klinefelter's one Barr body

topics.name: Chromosomal theory of sex dedefinitionination

subtopics.definition_content: Fixing the sex of an individual as it begins life; genetically controlled mechanisms classified into chromosomal, genic balance, hormonal and environmental theories

Most common type of sex dedefinitionination; first studied in milk weed bug Lygaeus turcicus by Wilson and Stevens; female homogametic (XX) and male heterogametic (XY); X-chromosome carries female-dedefinitionining genes and Y-chromosome has male-dedefinitionining genes; also in all mammals including man and various plants

McClung found in male squash bug Anasa 10 pairs + 1 unpaired chromosome (X); females have 11 pairs (22 chromosomes); evolved by loss of Y-chromosome; all eggs carry 11 chromosomes; sperms with 11 chromosomes produce female, sperms with 10 produce males; e.g., Grasshopper, Dioscorea sinuta

key_concepts: X-body first observed by Henking 1891
Wilson and Stevens 1902-1905 X and Y chromosomes
autosomes carry somatic genes
sex chromosomes = allosomes
female heterogametic in birds and moths

topics.name: Haploid-diploid mechanism of sex dedefinitionination

subtopics.definition_content: In Hymenopterous insects (bees, wasps, saw flies, ants): unfertilized egg develops into haploid male (N), fertilized egg develops into diploid female (2N); eggs formed by meiosis, sperms by mitosis; quality of food dedefinitionines queen (royal jelly) vs worker (pollen and nectar) among diploid larvae

key_concepts: honeybee
haploid male
diploid female
royal jelly for queen
environment dedefinitionines fertility not sex

topics.name: Quantitative or ratio theory of sex dedefinitionination

subtopics.definition_content: C.B. Bridges worked out ratio theory in Drosophila; ratio of X-chromosomes to autosomal sets dedefinitionines sex; X/A ratio: 1.5 = super female, 1.0 = normal female, 0.66 = intersex, 0.50 = normal male, 0.33 = super male; Y-chromosome plays no part in sex dedefinitionination but governs male fertility; XO flies are male but sterile

key_concepts: X/A ratio dedefinitionines sex
intersexes sterile and indefinitionediate
gynandromorphs = part female part male body
Goldschmidt introduced definition Gynandromorphism in 1915
SRY gene on Y chromosome dedefinitionines sex in humans

topics.name: Hormonal theory of sex dedefinitionination

subtopics.definition_content: In cattle where twins of opposite sex are born, the male is normal but the female is sterile with many male characteristics (free martin); due to effect of male sex hormones on the female; studied by LILLIE

key_concepts: hormones influence sex in higher vertebrates
gonads secrete hormones affecting sex development

topics.name: Environmental theory of sex dedefinitionination
key_concepts: Bonellia marine worm: larva alone = female; larva on female's proboscis = tiny male parasite
turtles: below 28°C = more males, above 33°C = more females
crocodile: male sex predominant at high temperature

topics.name: Barr body in sex dedefinitionination

subtopics.definition_content: Small chromatin body in nucleus of female cells staining heavily with nuclear stains; first noticed by Murray Barr (1949) in nerve cells of female cats; lies against nuclear membrane in female; used to dedefinitionine sex of unborn human embryos via amniocentesis

According to British geneticist Mary Lyon (1961), one of the two X-chromosomes of a normal female becomes heterochromatic and appears as Barr body; inactivation of one X-chromosome is dosage compensation or Lyon's hypothesis; number of Barr bodies = number of X chromosomes minus 1

key_concepts: sex chromatin = Barr body
amniocentesis technique
dosage compensation
Normal female XX = 1 Barr body
Turner's XO = 0 Barr bodies
super female XXX = 2 Barr bodies
Lyon's hypothesis

topics.name: Types of sex linked inheritance

subtopics.definition_content: Inheritance of non-sexual (somatic) characters linked with sex chromosomes; concept introduced by T.H. Morgan in 1910 working on Drosophila melanogaster; about 120 sex-linked characters in man, mostly recessive

subtopics.definition: Diandric sex linked or X linked traits
subtopics.definition_content: Genes located on non-homologous segment of X chromosome with no corresponding allele on Y chromosome; genes transferred from father to daughter to her sons (Cris-cross inheritance); examples: colour blindness and haemophilia in man, eye colour in Drosophila

Person unable to distinguish certain colours; most common is red-green colour blindness described by Horner (1876); red blindness = protanopia; green blindness = deuteranopia; recessive X-linked gene; single gene in man results in condition while woman needs two genes

Blood fails to clot when exposed to air; also called bleeder's disease; first studied by John Conrad Otto in 1803; most famous pedigree from Queen Victoria's royal families of Europe; deficient in thromboplastin; Haemophilia-A lacks antihaemophilic globulin (Factor VIII); Haemophilia-B (Christmas disease) lacks PTC (Factor IX); X-linked recessive with Cris-cross inheritance
Genes located on non-homologous segment of Y chromosome; alleles do not occur on X chromosome; inherited straight from father to son (male to male); example: hypertrichosis of ears in man
Autosomal traits in which dominant expression depends on sex hormones of the individual; examples: baldness in man, length of index finger

Traits or characters which develop only in one sex; controlled by autosomal genes expressed in only one sex; inherited according to Mendel's laws; examples: moustaches and beards in human males, breast and milk secretion in human females

key_concepts: Cris-cross inheritance
XY-linked traits (XY-linked) examples: Xeroderma pigmentosa, Nephritis
colour blindness inheritance table
haemophilia Haldane discovered pedigree
carrier female

topics.name: Twins

subtopics.definition_content: Formed when one sperm fertilizes one egg forming a single zygote; same genotype and phenotype; always same sex; differences if any are due to different environmental conditions

Like monozygotic twins originate from one zygote but daughter cells formed at first cleavage fail to separate completely; remain joined at some point; first studied in Siam; always same sex, same genotype and phenotype

Dizygotic twins formed from two eggs fertilized by two sperms separately but simultaneously; may be both males, both females, or one male and one female; may have different genotypic constitution and phenotype

key_concepts: hereditary basis of human traits established by twin studies
monozygotic
dizygotic
environmental vs genetic influence

topics.name: Intelligence quotient (IQ)

subtopics.definition_content: Ratio between mental age and actual (chronological) age multiplied by 100; mental competence in relation to chronological age in man

key_concepts: IQ 0-24 = idiot
25-49 = imbecile
50-69 = moron
90-109 = average
120-139 = most superior
140+ = genius

formulas.description: Formula for calculating Intelligence Quotient

topics.name: Eugenics

subtopics.definition_content: Branch of science dealing with improvement of human race genetically; definition coined by Sir Francis Galton in 1883 (Father of eugenics); positive eugenics = encouraging inheritance of better traits; negative eugenics = restricting transmission of poor and defective germplasm

key_concepts: Galton Father of eugenics
positive eugenics
negative eugenics

topics.name: Euthenics

subtopics.definition_content: Improvement of human race by improving environmental conditions including better nutrition, unpolluted ecological conditions, better education and medical facilities

key_concepts: environmental improvement
nutrition
education
medical facilities

topics.name: Euphenics

subtopics.definition_content: Study of born defectives and their treatment; symptomatic treatment of human genetic disease especially inborn errors of metabolism; definition given by A.C. Pai (1974)

key_concepts: born defectives
inborn errors of metabolism
symptomatic treatment
A.C. Pai 1974

topics.name: Pedigree chart and symbols

subtopics.definition_content: A systematic listing (words or symbols) of the ancestors of a given individual or 'family tree'; carried out to ward off possible disaster due to picking up of harmful genetic defects like dominant polydactyly, recessive haemophilia, colour blindness, thalassemia, alkaptonuria, PKU, sickle cell anaemia
A person who is the beginner of the family history in pedigree analysis; called propositus if male, proposita if female
A circle of large persons interconnected due to interconnected marriages forming a very large family

key_concepts: squares for males
circles for females
horizontal line for marriage
vertical line for children
siblings = sibs
solid symbols = affected individuals
twins shown by bifurcating vertical lines

chapters.name: DNA — Structure, Chemical Composition, and Properties

subtopics.definition_content: First isolated by Friedrich Miescher in 1869 from nuclei of pus cells and named nuclein. The definition nucleic acid was given by Altman (1899). Two types — DNA and RNA — are found in the cells of all living organisms.
Formed by a purine or pyrimidine nitrogenous base joined to a pentose sugar. DNA nucleosides are called deoxyribosenucleosides.
A nucleotide consists of a purine or pyrimidine nitrogenous base joined to deoxyribose pentose sugar, which is further linked with a phosphate (P) group.
Two-ring nitrogen-containing compounds present in DNA: Adenine and Guanine.
One-ring nitrogen-containing compounds: Cytosine and Thymine in DNA; Uracil replaces Thymine in RNA.

topics.name: Watson and Crick's Double Helix Model

subtopics.definition: Watson and Crick's double helix model
subtopics.definition_content: Proposed in 1953: DNA consists of two antiparallel polynucleotide chains wound in a right-handed double helix. One strand runs 5'→3' and the other 3'→5'. Nitrogenous bases are inside, linked by hydrogen bonds (A=T has 2 H-bonds; G≡C has 3). Ten base pairs per turn; spacing 3.4 Å; diameter 20 Å.
Proposed by Erwin Chargaff (1950): molar concentration of A = T and G ≡ C; the ratio (A+G)/(C+T) = 1 and (A+T)/(G+C) is constant for a species.
The sequence of nitrogenous bases in one strand of DNA dedefinitionines the sequence in the other strand; A pairs with T and G pairs with C. This is of fundamental importance in molecular genetics.
The commonest form of DNA representing the Watson-Crick model. Right-handed helix with 10 base pairs per turn and axial rise of 3.37 Å.
Left-handed form of DNA helix with 12 base pairs per turn, the only left-handed form among the five recognised forms (A, B, C, D, Z).
Special type of DNA that can move between mitochondria, chloroplast and nucleus. Discovered in 1983 at Cambridge University in maize; later reported in yeast, mungbean, spinach and peas.
Multiple copies of DNA having the same or almost the same base pair sequence. Constitutes 20%–90% of DNA in higher organisms.
Small, highly repetitive DNA sequences found in some eukaryotes that differ in base composition from the rest of the genome.

topics.name: Characteristics of DNA — Denaturation and Renaturation

subtopics.definition_content: Phenomenon of separating the two strands of a DNA molecule by breaking hydrogen bonds at approximately 90°C. G≡C-rich DNA has a higher melting point; A=T-rich DNA has a lower melting point.

Separated complementary DNA strands reunite to reform the double helix when cooled to room temperature (~25°C). This property enables hybrid formation between different DNA molecules or between DNA and RNA.
Enzyme that unwinds the DNA helix by breaking hydrogen bonds using energy from ATP, initiating replication at the origin point where the replication fork forms.
A short RNA strand (50–100 nucleotides) synthesised by DNA-directed RNA polymerase, complementary to the DNA template, that provides the 3'-OH end needed for DNA polymerase to begin synthesis.
The newly synthesised strand of DNA formed continuously in the 5'→3' direction along the 3'→5' template strand during replication.
The newly synthesised strand of DNA formed discontinuously in short segments (Okazaki fragments) in the direction opposite to replication fork movement.
Short segments of newly synthesised DNA formed discontinuously on the lagging strand template during DNA replication. Gaps between fragments are filled by DNA polymerase I, then joined by DNA ligase.

Enzyme discovered by Kornberg and colleagues (1955) in E. coli. It participates in repair and proof-reading of DNA, removes RNA primer residues via 5'→3' exonuclease activity, and fills gaps left between Okazaki fragments.
Most active DNA polymerase, discovered by T. Kornberg and M.L. Gefter (1972). Responsible for DNA chain elongation during replication.
Enzyme that joins the adjacent 5' and 3' ends of Okazaki fragments, sealing the final gaps to complete DNA replication.

topics.name: Semiconservative Replication — Meselson and Stahl's Experiment

subtopics.definition_content: The mode of DNA replication in which each daughter DNA molecule conserves one parental polynucleotide chain and one newly synthesised strand. Demonstrated by Meselson and Stahl (1958) using N¹⁵-labelled E. coli.
Circular form of DNA replication characteristic of prokaryotes, discovered by J. Cairns.

chapters.name: Evidences for DNA as Genetic Material

topics.name: Bacterial Transformation — Griffith's Experiment and Avery-MacLeod-McCarty

subtopics.definition_content: Transfer of a part of DNA from surrounding medium or dead donor bacteria to living recipient bacteria resulting in a recombinant. Demonstrated by Griffith (1928) using virulent (S-type) and nonvirulent (R-type) Diplococcus pneumoniae. The chemical responsible was identified as DNA by Avery, MacLeod, and McCarty (1944).

Waring-blender experiment (1952) confirming that DNA of bacteriophage enters the host bacterial cell and carries the information for forming new phages. Radioactive P³² (a component of DNA) was found inside infected bacteria, while S³⁵ (component of protein coat) was not, proving DNA is the genetic material.

topics.name: Structure of RNA and Genetic vs Nongenetic RNA

subtopics.definition_content: Nucleic acid found in the cytoplasm, nucleolus, mitochondria and chloroplasts. Usually single-stranded but often folded back on itself forming helices. Acts as hereditary material in some plant viruses.
RNA that acts as hereditary material in most plant viruses, some animal viruses and many bacteriophages where DNA is absent. May be single-stranded or double-stranded. Established by Conrat.

Polymer of ribonucleotides that is a complementary strand to the DNA template; carries genetic information from DNA to ribosomes in the cytoplasm for protein synthesis. Named by Jacob and Monod (1961). Constitutes about 5% of total cellular RNA.

RNA component of ribosomes constituting about 80% of total cellular RNA. Eukaryotic ribosomes contain 28S, 18S, 5.8S and 5S rRNA; prokaryotic ribosomes contain 23S, 16S and 5S rRNA. Synthesised in the nucleolus/SAT region.

Family of about 60 small ribonucleic acids (75–80 nucleotides each) that recognise mRNA codons and carry specific amino acids to the ribosome for protein synthesis. 3' end definitioninates in CCA sequence for amino acid attachment. Also called soluble RNA or adaptor RNA.
A triplet of unpaired bases in a tRNA molecule whose sequence is complementary to a codon in mRNA, enabling the tRNA to deliver the correct amino acid during translation.

The most accepted structural model of tRNA proposed by Robert Holley (1965) along with H.G. Khorana and Nirenberg for yeast alanyl tRNA; resembles a four-leaf clover with stem-loop structures for amino acid attachment, activating enzyme binding, anticodon recognition, and ribosome attachment.
Small-sized RNA present in the nucleus. Participates in splicing (U1, U2), rRNA processing (U3) and mRNA processing.
Small-sized RNA occurring free in the cytoplasm; helps in binding a ribosome to the endoplasmic reticulum for producing secretory proteins.

Large RNA molecules present in the nucleus that act as precursors of mRNA. Consists of exons (coding regions) and introns (non-coding, intervening sequences); introns are removed during processing to form mature mRNA.

topics.name: Features and Nature of the Genetic Code

subtopics.definition_content: The sequence of nitrogen bases (nucleotides) in an mRNA molecule that contains the information for the synthesis of a specific protein. Discovered by frame-shift mutation experiments by Crick. Consists of 64 codons (triplets).
A sequence of three nucleotides (triplet) in mRNA that specifies a single amino acid. Experimentally proved by Nirenberg and Matthaei (1961). The first codon discovered was UUU.
The property by which a single amino acid may be specified by more than one codon. Degeneracy is associated with the third (wobble) base of the codon. Discovered by Berrfield and Nirenberg.

Proposed by Crick: the third base of a codon (wobble base) is less critical; the first two bases dedefinitionine the amino acid while the third can vary without affecting coding. This establishes economy of tRNA molecules.
AUG (codes for methionine) or GUG (codes for valine) located at the beginning of the cistron, which initiates polypeptide chain synthesis.
UAA (Ochre), UGA (Opal), or UAG (Amber) — triplets that do not code for any amino acid and signal the definitionination of polypeptide chain synthesis.

Concept proposed by Watson and Crick: unidirectional (one-way) flow of genetic information from DNA → RNA (transcription) → Protein (translation). In retroviruses, reverse flow occurs: RNA → DNA (reverse transcription).

The formation of mRNA from a DNA template; the heterocatalytic function of DNA. The template (sense/master) strand of DNA is read by RNA polymerase-II, which synthesises a complementary mRNA. Occurs in three stages: initiation (sigma factor), elongation (core enzyme), and definitionination (rho factor in prokaryotes; poly-A tail in eukaryotes).
The functional segment of DNA involved in transcription; has a promoter region (initiation site) and a definitioninator region (end of transcription).
The strand of DNA used as a template during transcription. RNA polymerase reads this strand in 3'→5' direction to synthesise mRNA in 5'→3' direction.
Synthesis of DNA from an RNA template, occurring in retroviruses. Discovered by Temin and Baltimore in Rous sarcoma virus (1975 Nobel Prize). Also called teminism.
Enzyme (RNA-dependent DNA polymerase) that catalyses reverse transcription — synthesising complementary DNA (cDNA) from an RNA template.
The process of protein (polypeptide) synthesis directed by mRNA at ribosomes; a unidirectional process involving three stages: initiation, elongation, and definitionination.
A complex of multiple ribosomes simultaneously translating a single mRNA strand. The ribosome nearest the 5' end carries the shortest polypeptide; the one at the 3' end carries the longest.

Enzyme (amino-acid activating enzyme) specific for a particular amino acid and its tRNA; catalyses the formation of aminoacyl-AMP-enzyme complex using ATP, then transfers the activated amino acid to its specific tRNA forming aminoacyl-tRNA (charged tRNA).
Enzyme located in the large ribosomal subunit that catalyses formation of a peptide bond (–CO–NH–) between the amino acids at the P-site and A-site during translation elongation.

Movement of the ribosome along mRNA in the 5'→3' direction after each peptide bond is formed, mediated by translocase (eEF2 in eukaryotes; EF-G in prokaryotes) with GTP hydrolysis, exposing the next codon at the A-site.

topics.name: Gene Expression in Prokaryotes — Operon Model and Lac Operon

subtopics.definition_content: A segment of genetic material that functions as a regulated transcription unit, consisting of structural genes, an operator gene, a promoter gene, and a regulator gene. Can be switched on and off. Concept proposed by Jacob and Monod (1961) from studies on E. coli lactose utilisation.

An inducible operon in E. coli that remains switched off normally but becomes active in the presence of an inducer (lactose/galactoside). Contains structural genes Z (β-galactosidase), Y (galactoside permease), and A (galactoside acetylase). Operates in catabolic pathways.
Genes in an operon that produce mRNAs encoding the enzymes/proteins needed for the metabolic pathway. Lac operon has three structural genes (Z, Y, A); tryptophan operon has five (E, D, C, B, A).
A small DNA segment in an operon that gives passage to RNA polymerase when structural genes are to be expressed. Normally covered by a repressor protein. The lac operon operator is 27 base pairs.

A small protein produced by the regulator gene that binds to the operator gene and blocks RNA polymerase from reaching the structural genes, keeping the operon switched off. Has two allosteric sites: one for the operator gene and one for the inducer.

A chemical that binds to the repressor protein, changes the shape of the operator-binding site, and prevents the repressor from attaching to the operator gene, thereby switching the operon on. Lactose/galactoside is the inducer of the lac operon.

A repressible operon system that normally remains active but is switched off when the concentration of an end product (corepressor) exceeds a threshold. Operates in anabolic pathways. Has five structural genes (E, D, C, B, A) and produces tryptophan.
A proteinaceous substance formed by the regulator gene of a repressible operon; can block the operator gene only when a corepressor is also present.

The nonproteinaceous component (often the end product of the metabolic pathway, e.g. tryptophan) that combines with the aporepressor to form an active repressor, which then blocks the operator gene and switches off the operon (feedback repression).

Model proposed by Britten and Davidson (1969) for eukaryotic gene regulation. Proposes four types of genes: integrator, sensor, producer, and receptor. Histones act as repressors and non-histones as inducers of RNA synthesis.

A collection of gene clones that together contain all the DNA present in a source organism. If created from genomic DNA, it is a genomic gene library; if created from RNA via reverse transcriptase, it is a cDNA library.
A gene library that seeks to include clones of all the DNA from the living source organism, created from total genomic DNA.
DNA synthesised enzymatically by reverse transcriptase (RNA-dependent DNA polymerase) using mRNA as a template. Used in RT-PCR (reverse transcriptase PCR) to amplify RNA sequences.

A repository of clones of known DNA fragments, genes, gene maps, seeds, spores, frozen sperms, eggs, or embryos stored for possible use in genetic engineering and breeding experiments, especially for extinct or endangered species.

Individual-specific DNA identification technique developed by Alec Jeffreys et al. (1985). Based on analysis of variable-number tandem repeat (VNTR) sequences. Applications include forensic identification of criminals, paternity disputes, verifying immigration claims, and studying biological evolution.

Short, highly repeated 15-nucleotide segments scattered through human chromosomal DNA. No two people are likely to have the same number of copies of these repetitive sequences, making them the basis of DNA fingerprinting.
The complete genetic material of an organism. definition introduced by Winkler in 1920.
Subdiscipline of genetics devoted to the mapping, sequencing and functional analysis of genomes. definition coined by Thomas Rodericks in 1986.

International project to sequence the complete human genome (~3 billion base pairs, ~30,000 genes). Key scientists: Francis Collins (director) and J. Craig Venter (Celera Genomics). First human chromosome to be completely sequenced was chromosome 22 (December 1999).

Collision between particles and antiparticles in the primeval matter (Ylem) caused a tremendous explosion to form atoms of hydrogen; cosmic evolution began with fusion of hydrogen into progressively heavier elements, leading to formation of stellar systems and stars. Supported by Lemaitre (1931), Gamow (1948), Dicke (1964).

The primaeval matter — a red hot, dense, rotating gaseous cloud of cosmic dust from which the universe formed about 10 to 13 billion years ago, consisting of particles of matter (neutron, proton, electron) and antiparticles of antimatter.

Proposed by Kant (1755) and Laplace (1796): our solar system evolved about 4.5 to 5 billion years ago from a rotating red-hot gaseous cloud; the sun condensed first, then planets were thrown off from the sun, and satellites were thrown off by planets. Modified by Weizsacker (1944), Alfen (1950), Hoyle (1955).

topics.name: Ancient Theories of Origin of Life

subtopics.definition_content: Proposed by Father Saurez (1548-1617): the whole universe was created in six days by God — first Earth and heaven, then sky, dry land and vegetation, Sun and planets, fishes and birds, and finally human beings and animals. Based on supernatural power.

Theory postulating that life originated from non-living matter spontaneously from time to time; supported by Plato, Aristotle, Anaximander, Needham, Van Helmont. Abiogenesis means origin of life from non-living organisms.

Concept that life originated from pre-existing life only; proposed by Huxley (1870) and supported experimentally by Francesco Redi (1668), Spallanzani (1767), and Louis Pasteur (1860-62) using swan-necked flasks.

Proposed by Richter (1865), Arrhenius (1908), Hoyle (1950) and others: life was transferred from outer space (cosmozoa) to different planets as spores covered by thick protective coatings; does not explain how life originated in space.
Proposed by Cuvier (1769-1832): catastrophe destroys all life on earth, after which new life originates from inorganic material (Mechanistic theory).

Oparin (1924) and Haldane (1929) proposed that life could have originated from non-living organic molecules (biochemical origin of life). In the primitive reducing atmosphere (nitrogen, hydrogen, ammonia, methane, CO, water vapour), energy from electric discharges and UV rays drove synthesis of organic compounds, eventually giving rise to self-duplicating molecules and cell-like units. Oparin presented this in his book 'The Origin of Life' (1936).

Haldane's concept that simple organic compounds (sugars, amino acids, purines, pyrimidines) accumulated in water bodies forming a 'hot thin soup' or broth providing the chemical stage for synthesis of complex molecules.

Cell-like colloidal structures formed by coalescence of zwitterionic colloidal protein structures; have ability to exchange substances with surrounding water and accumulate required substances within them. Proposed by Oparin as precursors of life.

Minute cell-like structures obtained by Sidney F. Fox by heating dry amino acid mixtures to 90°C then cooling in water; surrounded by membranes, can reproduce vegetatively and show biochemical processes like glucose breakdown. Similar to coacervates.
Primitive cell-like units representing the earliest form of life; Oparin's coacervates and Fox's microspheres are similar structures also called Protobionts or Eobionts.

First cells: lacked a true nucleus and cell organelles; nucleic acid surrounded by colloidal protein mixture (protoplasm) enclosed in a thin protein-lipid membrane. The earliest organisms were chemoheterotrophs and anaerobes.

Stanley Miller (1953) filled a flask with methane, ammonia, and hydrogen (2:1:2) connected to a flask of water; passed 60,000 volts through the gases for 7 days simulating lightning; the condensed vapours contained amino acids (glycine, aspartic acid, alanine) and nitrogenous bases, proving organic compounds could form abiotically and were the basis of life.

The stepwise process by which simple atoms (C, H, O, N) in the primitive atmosphere reacted to form: (1) simple molecules (water, ammonia, methane), (2) simple organic compounds (sugars, glycerol, fatty acids, amino acids, purines, pyrimidines), (3) complex organic compounds (polysaccharides, fats, proteins), and (4) nucleic acids and nucleoproteins capable of self-replication.

topics.name: Biological Evolution from Prokaryotes to Eukaryotes

subtopics.definition_content: Nutrition using energy from chemical oxidation of inorganic compounds (anaerobic respiration) to synthesize organic substances; the first autotrophic mode evolved from heterotrophy; still seen in sulphur bacteria.
Early bacterial photosynthesis using H2S instead of H2O as hydrogen donor, producing sulfur instead of oxygen: 6CO2 + 12H2S → C6H12O6 + 6H2O + 12S.
Photosynthesis using water (H2O) as hydrogen donor, releasing O2; evolved from anoxygenic photosynthesis when bacteriochlorophyll mutated to present chlorophyll so cells used H2O instead of H2S.

Cells with membrane-bound organelles (mitochondria, chloroplasts, Golgi bodies, lysosomes) that evolved after free oxygen became available from photosynthesis, changing the atmosphere from reducing to oxidizing.

topics.name: Evidence from Comparative Morphology and Anatomy

subtopics.definition_content: Organs of same embryonic origin and essential structure but different functions; e.g., forelimbs of salamander, crocodile, bird, bat, whale, and man. They evidence divergent evolution from a common ancestor.
Organs of different origin but performing the same function; e.g., wings of insect, pterodactyle, bird and bat. Functionally similar (homoplastic); evidence convergent evolution.

Organs present in reduced form with no function in an organism but well-developed and functional in allied animals; e.g., vermiform appendix is vestigeal in man but functional in rodents and herbivores. About 90 such organs in humans.
Sudden reappearance of ancestral features that were vestigeal or absent in parents; e.g., large canines, thick body hairs, short temporary tails, ability to move pinnae.

Indefinitionediate or intergrading forms between two groups of organisms; e.g., Archaeopteryx (reptiles and birds), Peripatus (Annelida and Arthropoda), Dipnoi/lungfish (Pisces and Amphibia), Prototheria (reptiles and mammals).
Fossil forms transitional between two present-day groups (taxa) of organisms; e.g., Stegocephalus (fishes and amphibians), Archaeopteryx (reptiles and birds), Inostracevia (reptiles and mammals).
Development of different functional structures from a common ancestral form; evidenced by homologous organs.
Development of similar characteristics in distantly related groups as an adaptation to the same function; evidenced by analogous organs.
Van Baer's principle (Father of modern embryology): various body structures arise from the same germ layers in different species of animals.

Proposed by Muller and Haeckel (1864): Ontogeny (life history of an individual) repeats Phylogeny (evolutionary history of the race); structures of ancient origin develop earlier than structures of newer origin.

Remnants, models, and impressions of extinct organisms found preserved in sedimentary rocks; direct evidences of organic evolution. May be entire organisms buried in sediment or snow, small parts, or impressions of leaves/stems.
The science of discovering and studying fossil records (Gr. palaeo, ancient; logy, study of). Italian scientist Leonardo da Vinci was first to recognise the importance of fossils.

Method to dedefinitionine age of fossils based on conversion of unstable radioactive nuclei into stable nuclei at fixed rates; introduced by Boltwood (1907). Key examples: C-14 → N-14, U-238 → Pb-207, K-40 → Ar-40, Rb-87 → Sr-87.

Organisms that underwent little change during long geological periods while their relatives disappeared; e.g., Peripatus (primitive arthropod), Limulus (king crab), Neopilina (Mollusca), Sphenodon (tuatara, Reptilia), Latimeria (coelocanth fish), Echidna and platypus (Mammalia).

Alfred Russel Wallace divided the world into six major biogeographical realms: Nearctic (N. America), Palaearctic (N. Africa, N. Asia, Europe), Neotropical (Central and S. America), Oriental (Asia south of Himalayas), Ethiopian (S. Africa), Australian (Australia, New Zealand).

20 related varieties of birds (family Geospizidae) studied by Darwin in Galapagos Islands; differ in shape and size of beak due to adaptive radiation from a common ancestor. Classic example of biogeographical and adaptive radiation evidence of evolution.
Process of evolution of different species from a common ancestor, radiating to different areas/habitats; e.g., Darwin's finches of Galapagos Islands and Australian marsupials.

Evolutionary theory by Lamarck (1744-1829) in 'Philosophie Zoologique' (1809) with four laws: (a) internal forces increase organism size; (b) necessity produces new structures; (c) use and disuse — continuous use develops and disuse eliminates organs; (d) inheritance of acquired characters — all characters acquired in lifetime are inherited by offspring.

Lamarck's fourth law: characters acquired by an organism during its lifetime are inherited by offspring. Refuted by Weismann's germplasm theory — only germ cell characters are inherited, not somatic cell characters.
Proposed by Weismann: inheritance concerns only germ cells; only characters introduced in germ cells will be inherited. Directly opposed to Lamarck's law of inheritance of acquired characters.

Modification of Lamarck's theory by Cope, Haeckel, Spencer and others; stresses direct effect of changed environment on organisms rather than internal force and use/disuse; only modifications influencing germ cells are transmitted.

Darwin's mechanism of evolution: over-production of offspring + limited resources → struggle for existence; organisms with the most useful variations survive (survival of the fittest); useful variations are inherited and accumulate over generations, ultimately forming new species.
definition coined by Spencer for Darwin's natural selection: in the struggle for existence, organisms with the most favourable variations survive and reproduce.

Darwin's concept that all organisms compete for limited food, breeding places, shelter and face predators/parasites; includes inter-specific (between species), intra-specific (within species), and environmental struggle.
Darwin's theory to explain inheritance: each somatic cell produces pangenes which accumulate in gametes and transfer characters to the next generation.

Phenomenon where moths living in industrial areas develop black colour (melanin pigments) to match body to soot-covered background on bark. Observed in peppered moth Biston betularia carbonaria in Manchester; change from light variety (typica) to dark variety (carbonaria) due to mutation of a single Mendelian gene. Classic example of natural selection.

Modification of Darwin's theory incorporating genetics: replaces continuous variations with mutations and genetic variations; variations accumulate in gene pool not individuals; includes reproductive isolation as essential component of evolution.

Hugo de Vries (1901) proposed that evolution occurs by sudden discontinuous variations called mutations (saltatory variations), not by gradual small variations. Based on experiments with Oenothera lamarckiana (Evening primrose). Mutations are generally harmful and recessive.

Discontinuous variation called 'sport' by Darwin and 'saltatory variation' by Bateson; caused by changes in chromosomes, genes, or DNA; generally harmful and recessive; germinal mutations are inherited and provide raw material for organic evolution.

Dissimilarities between members of the same species in structural, physiological or psychological features; heritable variations are responsible for changes in a species to form new species; progressive factors in evolution.
Variations arising in the germplasm; occur in the gene pattern and are inheritable; transmitted from generation to generation through gametes.
Variations produced by environmental factors in somatic cells; non-heritable; not important in evolution. Examples: darkening of skin by sun, muscular body of an athlete.
Small, graded variations found in members of the same species; called fluctuating variations by Darwin; considered important for natural selection.

Variations that arise suddenly and deviate markedly from the average character without indefinitionediate stages; called 'sports' by Darwin and 'mutations' by Hugo de Vries; stable and inheritable. Examples: polydactyly (more than 5 digits), four horns in goat.

Designated by Huxley (1942), initially by Dobzhansky (1937) in 'Genetics and Origin of Species'; contributors include Fisher, Haldane, Sewall Wright, Mayr, Stebbins. Five basic factors: (1) gene mutations, (2) changes in chromosome structure and number, (3) genetic recombinations, (4) natural selection, (5) reproductive isolation. First three provide variability; last two give direction to evolution. Most accepted and recent theory of organic evolution.

G.H. Hardy and Wilhelm Weinberg (1908): gene frequencies remain constant across generations if five conditions are met: (1) mating is completely random, (2) no mutations, (3) no migration, (4) very large population, (5) all genes have equal chance of transmission. Distribution of genotypes: A² + 2Aa + a² = 1. Changing gene frequencies indicate evolution in progress.

formulas.name: Hardy-Weinberg Genotype Frequency
formulas.description: A² = frequency of homozygous dominant genotype; 2Aa = frequency of heterozygous genotype; a² = frequency of homozygous recessive genotype

topics.name: Systematic Position and Classification of Man

subtopics.definition_content: Present human species (Sapient = wise); systematically: Phylum Chordata, Subphylum Vertebrata, Class Mammalia, Order Primates, Suborder Anthropoidea, Superfamily Hominoidea, Family Hominidae, Genus Homo, Species sapiens. T.H. Huxley (1863) made first scientific attempt to explain human ancestry.

Earliest known fossil ape (25 mya, Miocene); knuckle walker with large canines and square molars; arboreal; arms and legs of equal length. Regarded as a common ancestor of man and great apes (orangutan, chimpanzee, gorilla); gave rise to Ramapithecus in late Miocene.

First hominid (15 mya, Miocene); partially upright; ground-dwelling in savannah; small canines with thicker enamel; first fossil fragment from Shivalik Hills, India (discovered by G.E. Lewis). Earliest man-like primate ancestor.

First man-ape (4.0 mya, Pliocene); walked fully erect; ~4 feet tall; brain capacity 450-600 cc (slightly larger than chimpanzee); small canines; prognathous face without chin; fossils described by Raymond Dart (1925) from South Africa. Indefinitionediate between Ramapithecus and genus Homo; considered ancestral to all hominids.

Earliest Homo (2.0 mya, Early Pleistocene); brain capacity ~700 cc; fully erect; first stone tool maker and user; began hunting for meat; major increase in brain size. Heaps of stone tools found with fossils.

1.5 mya; brain capacity ~950 cc; 5-6 feet tall; thick low forehead with brow ridges; used fire (Java man first used fire for protection and cooking); cooperative hunting; stone tools; rudimentary language. Java Man (Pithecanthropus erectus, ~940 cc, discovered by Dubois 1891) and Peking Man (Sinanthropus pekinensis, ~1075 cc) are its subspecies.

Arose ~150,000-250,000 years ago; includes Heidelberg man (large heavy jaw, no chin) and Neanderthal man (1,450 cc brain, heavy brow ridges, low slanting forehead, chinless jaw, buried dead, used flint tools). Neanderthals arose ~150,000 years ago and flourished in Europe, Asia and North Africa.

Lived last 30,000+ years in Europe; ~180 cm tall; cranial capacity ~1660 cc; broad face, rounded forehead, narrow nose, prominent chin; no eyebrow ridges; cave dwelling hunters; made stone tools and ivory ornaments. Most recent ancestor of Homo sapiens; became extinct ~10,000 years ago.
Appeared after last glacial period (~10,000 years ago); spread all over the globe; learned to cultivate plants and domesticate animals; first settlers leading a settled life.

The ability of a host's body to prevent or overcome the effects caused due to the invasion by pathogenic organisms and their toxins; considered an inherent factor acquired during life to overcome disease.
The acquired factors that help in resistance against pathogenic invasions; the resistance of the body to the occurrence of any disease.
An enzyme present in sweat, nasal secretions, and tears that destroys the cell walls of many bacteria.
The phenomenon of leucocytes squeezing out through the wall of blood capillaries into extra-vascular regions.

The body's reaction when microorganisms enter tissue through injury; involves dilation of capillaries, increased capillary permeability, and accumulation of plasma at the injured site to dilute bacterial toxins.
Proteins released by cells in response to a viral infection; do not inactivate the virus but make unattacked cells less susceptible and prevent viruses from taking over cellular machinery.

Foreign molecules that invade the body and stimulate the production of antibodies in response to infection; generally large molecules made of proteins or polysaccharides found on cell walls of bacteria or coats of viruses.
Lymphocytes that migrate to the thymus and differentiate under its influence; responsible for cellular immunity; live 4-5 years or longer.

Lymphocytes that complete differentiation in the bone marrow; produce antibodies (about 20 trillion per day) participating in humoral immunity; called B-cells because they mature in the Bursa of Fabricius in birds.
Component of the immune system resulting in production of antibodies that circulate as soluble proteins in plasma of blood and lymph; protects against bacteria and viruses entering blood and lymph.

Component of the immune system in which specialized cells carry out defensive activities against pathogens that have entered host cells, including protists and fungi; also reacts against tissue transplants and cancer cells.
Type of immunity inherited by organisms from their parents; protects from birth throughout life. Example: humans have inborn immunity against distemper (a fatal disease of dogs).

A technique to develop immunity without infection; weakened or dead pathogens (attenuated) or parts of pathogens are injected into a person to stimulate antibody formation by the host's immune system.
Additional doses of vaccine (typically 2-3) administered after the initial dose to generate adequate immunity.
Antibodies that neutralize toxins produced in the body or introduced from outside; antitoxins against snake venom are used as remedy for snake bites.
Immunity in which an organism's own cells produce antibodies; develops when a person suffers from a disease or receives vaccination for a disease.
Immunity in which antibodies produced in another organism (e.g., vertebrates) in response to an antigen are injected into the human body; provides immediate relief but is not long lasting.
The process of injecting antibodies produced in other organisms into the human body for passive immunization.
The hypersensitiveness of a person to some foreign substance coming in contact with or entering the body; involves increased release of histamine from mast cells.
Substances that cause allergic reactions; common allergens include dust, pollen, mould spores, fabrics, lipsticks, feathers, fur, certain foods, heat, cold, and sunlight.

A severe allergic reaction involving all tissues of the body, occurring within minutes after injection of an antigen (e.g., penicillin); histamine causes dilation of all arteries leading to drastic fall in blood pressure and possible unconsciousness.
Condition where the immune system loses normal regulation and attacks the body's own cells ('self'), leading to a variety of autoimmune diseases.
The body's own cells that act as antigens in the same body, triggering autoimmune responses; examples include RBC, muscle cells, and liver cells.
A serious congenital disease in which both B-cells and T-cells are absent from birth; children are highly susceptible to even minor infections.
A disorder of the cell-mediated immune system caused by HIV, characterised by reduction in the number of helper T-cells, resulting in loss of natural defence against viral infections.
First stage of HIV infection; infected person shows no symptoms for 9 months or longer but is highly infectious; standard HIV blood test for antibodies becomes positive during this stage.

Second stage of AIDS infection characterised by swollen lymph glands, severe fatigue, persistent fevers with night sweats, persistent cough, diarrhoea, and possible signs of nervous system impairment.
A diagnostic test used to detect HIV infection in the host; a positive ELISA result should be confirmed using the western blot test.
Liver inflammation caused by virus, use of drugs, chemicals, and alcohol.
Caused by Hepatitis A virus (HAV); transmitted through infected food, water, clothes, and faeces; may occur in epidemic form; does not damage liver cells.
Caused by Hepatitis B virus (HBV); transmitted by infected food and blood products or medical instruments contaminated with infected blood; results in swelling of liver cells.

Antibody-provoking agents (weakened or dead pathogens, or portions of pathogens) used against viral and bacterial diseases; unable to cause disease but sufficient to stimulate antibody formation by host's immune system.
Vaccines prepared by killing pathogenic organisms by heat, UV rays, alcohol, formalin, or phenol; e.g., Typhoid Vaccine, Cholera Vaccine.
Vaccines prepared by destroying the toxic property of bacterial toxins while retaining antigenic property; e.g., Tetanus toxoid, Antidiphtheria toxoid.
Vaccines where the pathogen is weakened (made nonvirulent); provide active lifelong immunity; e.g., Oral Polio Vaccine (OPV), BCG (Bacille Calmette-Guerin), MMR.
Diseases caused by pathogens (viruses and living organisms) that readily spread from the infected to the healthy persons

Four conditions to establish that a specific pathogen causes a particular disease: (1) pathogen present in diseased but not healthy individuals; (2) pathogen isolated and grown in pure culture; (3) culture inoculated into healthy host develops the disease with characteristic symptoms; (4) pathogen recoverable from experimental host and is same as original

Communicable diseases classified into seven types according to nature of causative agent: (1) Viral Diseases, (2) Rickettsial Diseases, (3) Bacterial Diseases, (4) Spirochaetal Diseases, (5) Protozoan Diseases, (6) Fungal Diseases, (7) Helminthes Diseases

Characterised by chronic hyperglycemia which is excessive concentration of glucose in the blood; primarily a result of relative or complete lack of insulin secretion by the β cells of islets of Langerhans in pancreas
Any inflammatory condition of the joints characterised by pain and swelling
Characterised by inflammation of the synovial membrane; a kind occurring in younger people is Still's disease
Disease common among elderly persons resulting from erosion of articular cartilage
Weakness or paralysis of both legs, often accompanied by loss of sensation; usually caused by motor vehicle accident, sports accident, fall or gunshot wounds

Results from accumulation of uric acid crystals in the synovial joints; a disease associated with an inborn error of uric acid metabolism that increases production or interferes with the excretion of uric acid
A number of diseases associated with the blood vascular system

An autoimmune disease, most common in children after a severe throat infection by certain strain of Streptococcus bacteria; antibodies against Streptococcus may react with myocardium and cause heart difficulties
Caused by hypertension (increased blood pressure); serious hypertension is a common cause of chronic heart failure particularly in older people
Characterised by impaired heart function due to inadequate blood flow to the heart
Chest pain caused most often by myocardial anoxia; attacks often related to exertion, emotional disturbance and exposure to excess cold
Commonly called coronary or heart attack
Bluish colouration of the skin and mucus membranes due to too much deoxygenated haemoglobin in the blood
Abnormal and uncontrolled division of cells (cancer cells) that invade and destroy surrounding tissues; uncontrolled proliferation of cells without any differentiation
Field of biomedicine devoted to the study and treatment of tumours (from G. onkos – mass/tumour; logos – study)
Remains confined to the site of its origin and does not spread to other parts of the body; causes limited damage; is non-cancerous
First grows slowly (latent stage), then rapidly; cancer cells enter blood and lymph and migrate to many other sites continuing to divide; only malignant tumours are properly designated as cancer
Secondary growth formed when detached cancer cells travel by blood and lymph to sites distant from the original tumour and form fresh colonies
Malignant tumours mainly derived from epithelial cells; include cervical cancer, breast cancer, skin cancer, brain cancer, lung cancers, stomach cancer
Cancers located in connective and muscular tissues derived from mesoderm; include cancers of bones, cartilages, tendons, adipose tissue, lymphoid tissue and muscles
Blood cancers characterized by abnormal increase of white blood corpuscles count due to their increased formation in the bone marrow
Cancer-causing genes present in all cells; certain factors stimulate oncogenes to replicate rapidly, causing malignant tumour
