units.unit_name: Smoking, Alcoholism, Drug Addiction, Mental Health and Community Health

topics.name: Other Harmful Components of Tobacco Smoke

subtopics.definition_content: Tobacco smoke may break down the walls of alveoli of the lungs, decreasing the surface area for gas exchange, causing emphysema.
Regular consumption of alcohol either in low concentration (beer, wine, etc.) or in high concentration (brandy, whisky, rum, gin, Vodka, etc.) causes dependency on alcohol which is called alcoholism.
The liver hardens and dries up as its cells are replaced by a fibrous tissue. This kind of liver degeneration is called cirrhosis.

topics.name: Driving and drinking do not go together

subtopics.definition_content: The prolonged use of drugs may lead to the dependence of the body on them. This is drug addiction.
A physical and mental dependence on any of the above mentioned factors to get temporary relief and feel pleasure.
The compounds used for the cure of the diseases are known as drugs.

These drugs depress the activities of the central nervous system. They give a feeling of calmness, relaxation or drowsiness in the body. Their higher doses induce sleep. Examples: barbiturates and benzodiazepines (e.g., Valium).
Synthetic drugs derived from barbituric acid and are called sleeping pills.
These drugs suppress brain activity and relieve pain. They are called painkillers. Examples: opium and its derivatives and some synthetic drugs (e.g., pethidine and methadone).
These drugs stimulate the nervous system; make a person more wakeful, alert and active; and cause excitement. The stimulants include caffeine, cocaine and amphetamines.
These drugs change thoughts, feelings and perceptions of an individual. They cause hallucinations — illusion of seeing objects or hearing sounds not actually present.
Heroin is formed from morphine by acetylation. It is highly addictive and considered most dangerous opiate. It is banned even for medical use.

Derived from Ergot fungus. It is the most powerful hallucinogen which causes horrible dreams, chronic psychosis, and severe damage to the central nervous system. LSD also brings about chromosomal and foetal abnormalities.
It is extracted from the leaves and young twigs of the South American shrub Erythroxylon coca. Cocaine is used as local anaesthetic and stimulant.

An alkaloid derived from the leaves of tea plant (Thea chinesis), seeds of coffee plant (Coffea arabica) and seeds of cocoa plant (Theobroma cocoa). It is a mild stimulant used in medicines as a cardiac stimulant.
Synthetic drugs which are strong stimulants. They do not remove fatigue but impair vision and judgement of distance.

topics.name: Effect of Drug Addition on Family, Society and Addicts

subtopics.definition_content: A state of mind in which a person is not able to think, behave and interact with others normally.
A serious type of mental illness in which the patient loses touch with reality. The patient is not aware of illness and refuses to take the treatment.
A less severe form of mental illness where the patient is aware of the problem and tries to seek help. There is abnormal anxiety, fear, sadness, vague aches and pains.

A mental illness characterised by abnormal electrical discharge in a part of brain often leading to warning cry, fits of convulsions, stiffness, tongue biting, sensory changes, loss of consciousness, falling down and sleep.
A sporadic disorder of middle and late life characterised by stooped posture, stiffness and slowness of movements, fixity of facial expression and rhythmic tremor of limbs (most pronounced in hands).
A progressive degenerative disease of brain caused by senile plaques and neurofibrillary tangles resulting in loss of choline acetyltransferase activity, leading to progressive dementia.

A type of mental illness or psychotic disorder characterised by progressive deterioration of personality, shallowness of emotional life, auditory hallucinations, delusions, illogical thinking, sense of being influenced by others and feeling of being controlled by outside forces.
Treatment involving psychological techniques like psychoanalysis, discussion, explanation, reassurance.

Founded by Freud; a method of reviving past and forgotten emotional experiences of a person so as to find out the reason for mental illusion and helping the patient to readjust attitude to causal experiences.

Shock treatment useful in curing acute depression, acute mania, stupor and some types of schizophrenia; involves placing electrodes on the patient's head and providing an electric shock for a fraction of a second.

units.unit_name: Strategies for Enhancement in Food Production

topics.name: Centres of Origin of Crop Plants

subtopics.definition_content: Nikolai Ivanovitch Vavilov (1926) proposed different centres of origin for various crop plants on two criteria: occurrence of wild relatives and occurrence of maximum variation in the crop. Major centres: South East Asia (rice, mango, sugarcane), China (onion, tea, soybean), South West Asia (wheat), Ethiopia (barley, coffee), Brazil (groundnut, pineapple, rubber), Peruvian Andes (potato, tomato, chilli), Mexico (maize, cotton).
Place of origin of a cultivated crop. e.g., South-West Asia is the natural home for wheat.

The major centre of production of a crop where wild relatives do not occur and which is far away from the centre of origin or natural home. India is secondary home for groundnut (natural home is Peru and Brazil).

A dwarfing gene Norin-10 was recorded in Japan. Norman Borlaug (1963) of Mexico produced triple dwarf wheats (Mexican wheats) with high yield, resistance to lodging, common pathogens and pests, responsiveness to inputs and smaller growth period. Key varieties: Sonora-64 and Lerma Rojo-64, later modified through mutations for Indian agriculture.

A dwarfing gene dee-geo-woo-gene was noted in Taiwan and introduced in rice varieties by IRRI, Philippines – IR-8, IR-24. Dr. Gurdev S. Khush crossed 13 rice varieties from six countries and Oryza nivara (wild rice from central India) to produce early maturing, high yielding and resistant variety IR-36.

The development of new variety of plants possessing desirable characters from the existing ones. Improvement may be in: (1) Quantitative characters (yield of seeds, grains, fibres, oil), (2) Qualitative characters (biochemical components, taste, milling, baking), (3) Resistance to diseases, insects, pests, drought, frost, (4) Earliness or lateness in maturity, (5) Adaptability to wider range of conditions.
Famous Russian plant breeder who gave centres of origin of cultivated plants.
Famous Mexican plant breeder, awarded Nobel Peace Prize (1970) for developing high yielding dwarf varieties like Sonora-64, Lerma Rojo-64. Known as father of green revolution.
Pioneer mutation breeder who produced Sharbati Sonora variety of wheat by mutation, responsible for green revolution in India. Called father of green revolution in India.

Introducing a plant having desirable characters (genetic improvement, high yield, disease resistance, vigorous growth) from a region or country where it grows naturally to a region or country where it did not occur earlier.
The adjustment of a newly introduced plant to a new or changed environment.
Plant material introduced from a foreign country.
Plant material brought from within the same country.
Introduced plants used directly for cultivation without any modification.
Introduced plants used after subjecting to selection or hybridization.

Picking up of a plant having desirable characters (high yield, disease resistant, vigorous growth) from a given population based on its phenotypic characters. Involves preserving favourable characters and gradual elimination of undesirable ones.
A natural rule resulting in evolution where the fittest survive and others are wiped out. Responsible for all local varieties of crops.
Choosing certain individual plants from a mixed population for the purpose of having better crop.

Practised in naturally cross-pollinated crops (e.g., Maize). Plants with desirable phenotypic characters are selected, seeds mixed and sown for natural cross-pollination, then the best are selected again. Simplest, easiest, and quickest method; only method for improving wild or local varieties to meet immediate farmer needs.
A population of homozygous plants raised from a single homozygous plant, coined by Johannsen.

Practised in natural self-pollinated crops (e.g., Wheat). Selected plants are selfed through several generations to attain homozygosity, then pure lines are crossed to combine desirable characters. Requires 10–12 years. No new genotypes are created.

Practised in vegetatively propagated plants (e.g., sugarcane, banana, potato, onion). Method of selecting desirable clones from the mixed population of vegetatively propagated crops based on phenotypic characters, then multiplying vegetatively. Varieties are stable and hybrid vigour is easily utilized.
All the progenies of a single plant obtained vegetatively; a homozygote produced by asexual means.

Method of producing new crop varieties in which two or more plants of unlike genetical constitution are crossed. Does not change genetic contents but produces new combinations of genes. First natural hybridization reported in maize by Cotton Mather (1716); first artificial hybrid by Thomas Fairchild (1717); first practically utilized by Kolreuter (1760).
Crosses made between plants of the same variety.
Crosses made between plants belonging to two different varieties of the same species (intraspecific hybridization).
Plants of two different species belonging to the same genus are crossed (intrageneric hybridization).
Crosses made between plants belonging to two different genera.
A type where one species is completely replaced by another in nature.
The removal of stamens from the female parent before they burst and shed their pollens, to avoid self-pollination.
Covering female and male flowers with cellophane or parchment or paper bags to avoid contamination by unwanted pollen.

The increased vigour, growth, yield or function of a hybrid over the parents, resulting from crossing of genetically unlike organisms. Mostly due to heterozygosity. definition coined by G.H. Shull (1914); first studied by Kolreuter (1763) followed by Darwin (1876). Commercially exploited in maize, sorghum, bajra, tomato, sugar beet.
Sudden phenotypic changes which are heritable (Hugo de Vries). In strict sense, refers to changes in the chemical structure of gene at the molecular level (gene mutation or point mutation).

Mutation occurring in somatic or body cells. Used in crop improvement by vegetative propagation. Examples: colour spots in apple, Bhaskar variety of banana, seedless grape, navel orange, superior shrubs in coffee.

Mutation occurring in germ cells, transferable to progeny. May or may not be expressed as mutations are generally recessive (appear only in recessive homozygous state). Examples: stiff ears in wheat, rice; spathe in maize; lint in cotton.
Radiations used to induce mutations. First used by H.J. Muller (1927) on Drosophila and L.J. Stadler (1928) on plants. Includes X-rays, β-rays, γ-rays and UV rays.
Chemicals used to induce mutations. Examples: nitrous acid, maleic hydrazide, hydrazine, methyl methane sulphonate (MMS), ethyl methane sulphonate (EMS).

Mutations artificially induced by mutagens. Examples: Sonora-64 by gamma radiation → Sharbati Sonora; Lerma Roja-64A by gamma radiation → Pusa Lerma; Reimei and Atomita-I high-yielding rice varieties in Indonesia.

Condition in organisms with more than two sets of chromosomes (e.g., triploid 3n, tetraploid 4n). Arises from fusing of one egg with two sperm or failure of mitosis. Artificially induced by colchicine (alkaloid from Colchicum autumnale, Liliaceae).
Type of polyploidy with numerical increase of the same genome (e.g., autotriploid AAA, autotetraploid AAAA). Induces gigas effect. Examples: maize, rice, gram.

Polyploidy developed through hybridization between two species followed by chromosomal doubling (AABB). Allopolyploids function as new species. Examples: wheat, American cotton, Nicotiana tabacum. Recently produced: Raphanobrassica and Triticale.
Type of allopolyploidy where one genome is in more than diploid state; commonly hexaploids (AAAABB). Example: Helianthus tuberosus.
Polyploids which possess an exact multiple of the haploid set of chromosomes.
Polyploids where numerical change in chromosome number is not an exact multiple of the haploid genome.

Breeding crops with higher levels of vitamins and minerals, or higher protein and healthier fats to improve public health. Objectives: improving (i) protein content and quality, (ii) oil content and quality, (iii) vitamin content, (iv) micronutrient and mineral content. Example: maize hybrids with twice the amino acids lysine and tryptophan (2000); wheat variety Atlas 66 with high protein content.

Separation of cells, tissues or organs (explants) of a plant and allowing them to grow in aseptic nutrient media under controlled light and temperature. Requires energy (sucrose), salts (macro and micro nutrients), vitamins and amino acid glycine. Based on totipotency. Process: Explant → Disinfection → Culture medium → Callus → Plantlet (Embryoids).

The inherent capacity of each and every plant cell to develop into a complete plant. Concept given by Haberlandt (1902); demonstrated practically by Steward (1932) by developing a complete carrot plant from a single root cell.
Undifferentiated tissue formed when a tissue from an organ is cultured. Can be differentiated into shoot, root or complete plants by manipulating concentrations of auxin and cytokinin.

Propagation through tissue culture; production of large numbers of individuals in vitro in a limited space for agriculture, horticulture and forestry. Each plant is genetically identical to the parent (somaclone). Examples: Potato, Banana, Begonia, Carnation, Chrysanthemum, Gerbera.

Somatic cells cultured in electric shakers to obtain single cell suspension. Each cell differentiates into an independent embryo (embryoid) showing globular, heart-shaped and torpedo-shaped stages. Can give rise to complete plant with normal root system. Success achieved in carrot, celery, and alfalfa.

Haploid plants raised from pollen grains by anther culture technique. First reported by Guha and Maheshwari (1964) from Datura innoxia anther culture. Haploid plants are always pure (one gene for each trait). Examples: Jinghua-I (winter wheat) and Guan-18 (rice) from China.

Spontaneous variations appearing in cells or tissues in artificial medium. Variants with desirable traits (tolerance to pests, pesticides, diseases, environmental stresses) are selected and exploited for agricultural purposes.

Cells of two plants from different varieties, species or genera treated with pectinase and cellulase to remove walls; naked protoplasts fused by electrofusion or chemofusion (sodium nitrate or PEG) to produce hybrid protoplasts. Pomato is a somatic hybrid between tomato and potato (intergeneric hybrid).
Hybrid protoplast in which one of the two nuclei degenerates, containing cytoplasm of both parents but nucleus of only one parent.

Microorganisms used directly as food or as supplement to other foods. definition coined by Prof. C.L. Wilson (1966). 250g of Methylophilus methylotrophus can produce 25 tonnes of protein. Organisms used: yeast (Candida utilis), fungi (Fusarium graminearum), bacteria (Methylophilus methylotrophus). Spirulina (blue-green alga, spiral coil shape) used as food supplement for high protein, vitamins, and essential fatty acids.

Latest method of crop improvement involving manipulation of DNA segments (genes) rather than whole chromosomal sets. Steps: (1) isolation of gene using restriction enzymes, (2) transfer via protoplast microinjection, (3) cloning using DNA ligase and vectors (bacterial plasmids, bacteriophages, plant viruses).
Specific enzymes that cut DNA at specific sites; called genetic scalpels. Nobel Prize 1978 awarded to Daniel Nathans, Hamilton Smith and Werner Arber for restriction endonuclease technology.
DNA formed by joining foreign DNA and plasmid DNA with DNA ligase enzyme, resulting in a molecule with additional (foreign) characters.

A plant in which a specific character (foreign gene) has been stably introduced and integrated. First transgenic commercial crop was tobacco (made tolerant to herbicides). Bt cotton produced by inserting DNA from Bacillus thuringiensis for insect resistance. Also produced: tomato with delayed ripening, mango with less ethylene, potato with 20–40% more starch.

topics.name: Domestication and Scope of Animal Husbandry

subtopics.definition_content: The rearing of animals for specific purposes is called domestication; such animals are called domestic animals. Domestication started during the 'hunting and gathering phase' of human civilization.
The branch of agriculture specializing in the breeding, raising, care and utilization of domestic animals is known as animal husbandry.
The word livestock refers to the domestic animals kept or dealt in for use or profit. It includes cattle, buffaloes, sheep, goats, pigs, horses, mules, donkeys and camels.

The word cattle includes cow (adult female), bull (uncastrated adult male), bullock or ox (castrated adult male) and steer (young castrated male). Cattle and buffaloes are used for agricultural operations, milk, transport, manure, leather, glue/gelatin, meat and hair.
Cattle breeds that give good milk-producing cows. Examples: Gir (Gujarat, Rajasthan), Sahiwal (Punjab, Haryana, UP), Red Sindhi (AP).
Cattle breeds which give good working bullocks. Examples: Malvi (Rajasthan, MP), Hallikar (Karnataka), Kangayam (Tamil Nadu).
Cattle breeds whose females are good milk-producers and whose bullocks are good draught animals. Examples: Haryana, Ongole, Kankrej, Tharparkar.
Roughage includes fodder, silage, hay and straw. They have a large amount of fibre contents and low nutritive value.
Concentrates used in cattle feed is a mixture of substances rich in nutrient contents such as cotton seeds, oil seeds, oil cakes and cereal grains like bajra, gram, rice polish.

The introduction of semen (sperm) in the body (vagina) of females by artificial means is called artificial insemination. This method is comparatively better and economical; several cows can be inseminated by semen of a single bull.

A technique where superovulation is induced in a pedigree cow by hormone injection, fertilization is achieved by artificial insemination, and the early embryos (4–10) are collected and each transplanted into a carrier cow for further development. A week-old embryo can be preserved by deep freezing at -196°C.

The domestic dog, Canis familiaris, was among the earliest animals domesticated by man. A large number of breeds have been produced by intensive inbreeding and artificial selection of a single ancestral dog species.
Cat (Felis domesticus) is a small, furry mammal domesticated to eradicate rats and mice. It has many breeds.
The care and management of pigs is called piggery.

Pig (also hog or swine) is an omnivorous, non-ruminant, gregarious mammal of genus Sus. All breeds descended from the European wild boar Sus scrofa or its crossbreed with Asiatic S. indicus. Pigs are the most prolific breeders and quick growers among domestic animals.
The fine soft wool called pashmina is the underfur of Kashmir and Tibet goats (Capra hircus).

topics.name: Common Diseases of Sheep and Goats

subtopics.definition_content: Single-humped camel with short hair, found from North Africa to India. Does not occur in wild form. Popularly called 'the ship of the desert'.
Two-humped camel with long hair, found in Gobi desert of central Asia. Occurs in wild form also.

Mule is the hybrid between male ass (jack) and female horse (mare). It has the stamina of ass and size of horse, but is sterile. Since they are sexually sterile, they have to be produced fresh every time.
The cross between male horse (stallion) and female ass is called hinny.

Horses are solid-hoofed, non-ruminant quadrupeds with long pendant mane and tail bearing long hair. They are intelligent, faithful pets capable of adapting to all climatic conditions. The horse was the first beast of burden.

Has small ears, high domed forehead with 2 prominences on top of skull, one process on trunk tip, convex back. Tusks in males only. Matures at 25 years (male 3000 kg). Used for timber extraction in forested areas.
Has large ears, convex sloping forehead. Matures at 25 years (male 6000 kg). Found in East, Central and West Africa. Very limited use for timber transport.

Sericulture is the breeding and management of silk worms for the production of silk. It has been practiced in India since the second century B.C. Silk is a valuable natural protein fibre produced by silk worm larvae.
Known as China silk worm or mulberry silk worm. Native of China and fully domesticated. Produces white or yellow silk. Phylum: Arthropoda, Class: Insecta, Order: Lepidoptera, Family: Bombicidae.
Belongs to family Saturnidae. Widely distributed in India. Favourite host plants are Arjun (definitioninalia arjuna) and Sal (Shorea robusta). Produces Tassar silk (kosa silk).
Volatile secretion produced by the scent gland at the definitioninal end of the abdomen of the female Bombyx mori moth to attract the male.

Well-developed paired glands in the silkworm larva. When fully developed they become five times larger than the larva's length and their weight is 2/5 of the total body weight. The silk is secreted in liquid form which solidifies on contact with air.
As the larva grows, it sheds its cuticle. This periodic shedding is called moulting.
The form of a larva between two successive moults is called an instar. The silkworm larva has five instars.
The process by which larvae come out of the egg is known as hatching.
The inner core protein of silk thread (molecular formula C₃₀H₄₆N₁₀O₁₂), constituting 75–80% of the silk thread.
The outer envelope or gum protein covering the fibroin core (molecular formula C₃₀H₄₀N₁₀O₁₂), constituting 20–25% of the silk thread.
Cutting back mulberry plants that have grown too high. Pruning helps in the production of a new flush of leaves. The plants can yield for 12 years; 6 to 8 crops of leaves per year.
The process of killing the pupae inside cocoons is definitioned stifling. Eight to ten day-old cocoons are selected and dipped in hot water to kill the pupae.
The silk threads from the killed cocoons are removed and wound round a large wheel and transferred to spools. This operation is called reeling and the silk so obtained is called reeled silk.
The most important disease of silkworms. Caused by a sporozoan Nosema bombycis. The fully grown caterpillar is attacked.
A fungal disease of silkworm caused by Beauveria bassiana, transmitted by spores carried by winds. All stages of caterpillar are attacked.
A bacterial disease of silkworm caused by Bacillus bombysepticus. Digestion in the affected caterpillar gets disturbed.
A disease of silkworm caused by nuclear polyheadrosis virus. Affected larvae become swollen and body fluid becomes thick and cloudy; larvae die.

topics.name: Introduction and Species of Honey Bees

subtopics.definition_content: Apiculture is the science of rearing honeybees for obtaining honey, wax and venom. It forms a cottage industry when carried out on a large scale.

A well-developed fertile female with immensely developed ovaries. One queen per hive. Feeds on Royal Jelly. Lays 1500–2000 eggs per day; lifespan 2–5 years. Gets mated only once in her life; sperms stored in spermatheca.
The male member of the honey bee colony. Reared from an unfertilized egg in large drone cells. Sole duty is to fertilize the virgin queen; dies after copulation. Totally dependent on workers.

Sterile female honey bees; smallest of the three castes. Development from egg to adult takes 21 days; total lifespan about 6 weeks. Possess long proboscis, strong wings, pollen baskets, wax glands and a powerful sting.
A special diet secreted by young worker bees consisting of digested honey and pollen mixed with a glandular secretion. Fed to larvae destined to become queens.
The behaviour of honey bees to come out of the hive in large numbers, occurring during spring or early summer. It relieves overcrowding and provides a means of colony reproduction.
Modified cells on the ventral surface of the last four visible abdominal segments of worker bees. Wax is produced in the form of scales through small apertures.

The sting is the modified ovipositor of the honey bee. Composed of two straight grooved stylets (lancets). A pair of filiform poison glands secretes acidic material stored in a poison sac at the base of the sting.
A concave surface on the outer surface of the metathoracic tibia, partially covered by long curved bristles. Used by worker bees to carry collected pollen back to the hive.

topics.name: Life History of Apis and Colony Behaviour

subtopics.definition_content: When the egg-laying capacity of the old queen is lost or it suddenly dies, a new young and vigorous queen takes its position in the colony. This is called supersedure.

The migration of the complete honey bee colony from one place to another due to unfavourable conditions such as destruction of comb by definitionites/wax-moths or scarcity of nectar-producing flowers.

About a week after emergence, the new virgin queen takes an aerial flight followed by drones. The last drone remaining mates with her in mid-air; sperms are stored in the spermatheca to fertilize eggs throughout the queen's life.
The artificial wooden box where the bee colony is maintained and managed is called a hive.
The place where hives are kept and managed is called an apiary.
A wire grid separating the brood chamber and the super (honey) chamber in the hive. Holes are small enough to prevent the queen from entering the super but allow other bees to pass through.

topics.name: Formation, Composition and Value of Honey

subtopics.definition_content: Honey is a viscous sugary fluid formed from nectar within the stomach of the honey bee. Nectar is regurgitated and swallowed repeatedly about 240 times; the processed nectar (unripe honey, ~80% water) is deposited in comb cells and converted to ripe honey by evaporation to less than 20% water.

Bee wax is secreted by the abdominal wax glands of worker bees and used for comb construction. It is a yellowish solid insoluble in water, used for paints, varnishes, candles, ointments, and industrial purposes.

Bee venom is secreted by the poison glands of stings. It is transparent, bitter, acidic, and contains formic acid, histamine, tryptophan, sulphur, proteins, volatile oils, enzymes (hyaluronidase, phospholipase) and magnesium phosphate. Used medicinally for rheumatism, eye diseases and skin diseases.

topics.name: Communication and Dance of Honey Bee

subtopics.definition_content: Performed by scout bee when a newly discovered food source is less than 75 metres from the hive. Conveys no directional information; only the smell of flowers brought back by the scout tells foragers what to search for.

Performed by scout bee for food sources more than 100 metres away. Scout bee moves along a straight line and makes two semicircles alternately. Direction of tail wagging relative to vertical indicates position of food source relative to the sun.
Lac is the resinous secretion produced by the lac insect (Laccifera lacca / Tachardia lacca) as a protective covering around its body. It is produced by the cutaneous glands of the insect.

topics.name: Life Cycle of the Lac Insect

subtopics.definition_content: The mass emergence of newly hatched lac larvae from the female's incubating chamber is called swarming. Larvae are boat-shaped, about 0.5 mm in length, with piercing and sucking mouthparts.
The technique of lac production involving proper care and regular pruning of host plants, propagation of insects, and collection and processing of lac.
For propagation, older branches containing lac crusts are tied with new branches. This method is called oculation.
When new crusts are formed, the old twigs (approximately 20–30 cm long) are removed. This is known as harvesting. The largest yield is obtained by harvesting while females are still living.
Lac scraped from encrusted twigs after prunning of infested twigs is known as stick lac.
Stick lac that has been ground and sieved produces granular lac called seed lac. It is washed, melted, spread in thin layers and dried to form shellac of commerce.

topics.name: Damages, Precautions and Economic Importance of Lac

subtopics.definition_content: Poultry includes the birds like chicken (hen), ducks, geese and turkey. Poultry farming deals with the rearing of them for their eggs and meat.
An egg-laying poultry bird is called hen (layers).
Poultry birds groomed for obtaining meat are called chicken or broilers.
The most common disease among fowls, caused by a virus. Affects fowls of all ages; mortality is very high (98–100 percent). Can be controlled by proper management, nutrition and timely vaccination.
Aquaculture is the production of useful aquatic plants and animals such as fishes, prawns, shrimps, lobsters, crabs and molluscs by the proper utilization of small and large bodies of water.
Pisciculture is the production and breeding of fishes by man in ponds.

topics.name: Types of Ponds in Fish Culture

subtopics.definition_content: Small and shallow ponds used for raising fry from spawn (4–5 mm to 25–30 mm).
Ponds used for rearing fry up to fingerling stage (50 mm and above).
Larger and deeper ponds (0.2 to 2.0 ha in size and 2 m to 2.5 m in depth) used for stocking fry/fingerlings to obtain table-size fish.

topics.name: Types of Breeding in Fish Culture

subtopics.definition_content: Breeding of Indian major carps (Catla catla, Labeo rohita, Cirrhinus mrigala) in artificially constructed bundh-type tanks where favourable conditions simulate river-like flow to stimulate spawning. Two types: wet bundh and dry bundh.
Inducing major carps to breed in ponds by use of pituitary hormone (pituitary extract) injections. The extract can be administered intramuscularly, intraperitoneally or intracranially.
Milt or seminal fluid of fishes is milky white, non-sticky and non-granular, consisting of innumerable microscopic spermatozoa.

A high-grade collagen produced from the air bladder (swim bladder) of certain fishes such as cat fishes and carps. Used for clarifying wines and beer; also used for making purse, honeycomb, book and ribbon isinglass products.
Prepared from wastes of fish oil or canning industry or from whole fish. Contains 55–70% proteins, 10–20% minerals, vitamins A, D and K. Used as major food of domestic animals and in human diets.
Pearl is a concretion formed by molluscs. It consists of nacre (mother of pearl). It is characterised by iridescence and translucence.
Nacre is formed of two substances: calcium carbonate (as aragonite or calcite) and an albuminoid substance called conchiolin/colchitin.
The best quality pearl obtained from marine oysters (Pinctada vulgaris).
Pearls obtained from cultivated species of pearl oysters through deliberate insertion of foreign material.
Pearls attached to the shell; they are half-spherical in shape.

When a foreign material (sand grain or parasite) enters the body of a pearl oyster, it adheres with the mantle. The mantle epithelium grows over the material as a sac and secretes concentric layers of nacre around it. The completed structure is called pearl.
Young oysters formed after egg-derived free-swimming larvae sink to the bottom of the water and develop. They grow to maximum size in four to five years.
The piece of tissue (2×2 mm square) cut from the mantle of another oyster and inserted along with the nucleus into the operated oyster during pearl culture.
A foreign material (2 mm diameter sphere) prepared from the shell of molluscs and inserted into the oyster during implantation to act as the core of the pearl.
The method of culturing pearl oysters by placing operated oysters in cages suspended from rafts in the sea.

topics.name: Cheese

subtopics.definition_content: Enzyme first obtained from calf stomach by Danish chemist Christian Hensen (1874); used for cheese formation by separating casein and fat of milk from the liquid.
Liquid remaining after curds are separated from milk during cheese making; contains lactic acid, lactose, and minerals.

Cheese that is salted, placed in brine, and allowed to ripen for 1–16 months with different strains of bacteria and fungi to develop particular consistency and flavour. Examples: Roquefort, Camembert, Limburger, Swiss Cheese.
Cheese formed by melting, blending, and emulsifying one or more types of cheese.
Cheese produced by curdling milk with cheese culture (Streptococcus lactis, S. cremoris, Leuconostoc citrovorum, etc.), then gently heating to separate curd from whey; has a short shelf life.

key_concepts: About 400 kinds of cheese are known, made from various types of milk (goat, cow, buffalo, camel) using microbes
Types: Ripened, Unripened/Cottage (pasteurised skimmed milk inoculated with cheese culture), Soft (>40% moisture), Hard (≤39% moisture), Processed, Raw
Formerly, stomach lining of sheep/goat (rennin) or sap of fig trees (ficin) or Withania coagulans fruit extract was used as cheese culture

topics.name: Butter
key_concepts: Contains 80% fat, 2–6% lactose and proteins, often 2% salt
Made by churning cream until fatty globules separate from liquid butdefinitionilk
Cream inoculated with mixed cultures of Streptococcus lactis, S. diacetylactic, Leuconostoc citrovorum, and L. dextranicum

topics.name: Fermented Beverages

subtopics.definition_content: Incomplete oxidation of complex organic compounds with the help of enzymes (Invertase and Zymase) produced by yeast; the basis of baking and brewing industries.
Selected strains of Saccharomyces cerevisiae and Torulopsis utilis grown on molasses; available in powder or cake form; ferments sugar in dough, producing CO₂ (leavening agent) that raises the dough.
Dark coloured syrup left after extraction of sugar from sugarcane; contains ~30% sucrose and ~32% invert sugar (mixture of glucose and fructose).
Raising of dough caused by CO₂ produced during yeast fermentation; both CO₂ and alcohol escape during baking, making bread porous and soft.
A form of sour milk prepared by fermentation of concentrated milk first with Lactobacillus bulgaricus and Streptococcus thermophilus at 40–46°C for 4 hours, then with yeast.

key_concepts: Louis Pasteur (mid-19th century): reported alcohol, beer, and butdefinitionilk result from yeast fermentation
Beer: from Hordeum vulgare (barley) malt; 4–8% alcohol
Wine: from grapes by fermentation; 10–20% alcohol
Brandy: by distillation of wine; 43–57% alcohol
Rum: from molasses of sugarcane; 40% alcohol
Gin: from fermented European rye (Secale cereale); Sake: from rice; Cider: from apple juice; Vodka: from potato; Whisky: from cereals; Toddy: from coconut milk
Zymomonas mobilis (bacterium): potentially useful for commercial alcohol production; ferments glucose, fructose, sucrose

topics.name: Antibiotics

subtopics.definition_content: Chemical substance secreted by one micro-organism that inhibits or checks the growth of other micro-organisms; a secondary metabolite of microbial origin. definition coined by Waksman (1942).
Antibiotics that inhibit the growth of many pathogenic species differing from one another in their structure. Examples: Chloramphenicol, Tetracyclines, Ampicillin, Rifampicin.
Antibiotics that inhibit the growth of a specific pathogenic species. Examples: Penicillin, Streptomycin, Erythromycin, Polymyxin B.

key_concepts: First antibiotic: Penicillin (wonder drug) from Penicillium notatum by Sir Alexander Fleming (1928)
Waksman (1942): coined definition 'antibiotic'; also discovered Actinomycin (1941) and Streptomycin (1942)
Main sources: fungi, bacteria, and actinomycetes; about 7,000 antibiotics known

Mode of action: (1) Inhibition of cell wall synthesis – Penicillin, Ampicillin; (2) Inhibition of nucleic acid synthesis – Streptomycin, Chloramphenicol, Tetracycline; (3) Inhibition of specific enzyme system – Sulfonamides
Streptomycin: from Streptomyces griseus; active against acid-fast and Gram-negative bacilli; cures pulmonary tuberculosis
Chloramphenicol: from S. venezuelae; broad spectrum against bacterial and rickettsial infections

topics.name: Organic Acids

subtopics.definition_content: Most important organic acid produced by two-step fermentation: (1) anaerobic yeast fermentation of sugarcane juice (glucose → ethanol + CO₂); (2) aerobic oxidation by Acetobacter aceti (ethanol + O₂ → acetic acid + H₂O).

First organic acid to be produced from microbial fermentation of lactose (milk sugar); fermented by bacteria (Streptococcus lactis, Lactobacillus) and fungi (Rhizopus); used in confectionery, fruit juices, pickles, and canned vegetables.
Organic acid obtained by fermentation of sugar syrup by Aspergillus niger and Mucor species; used in dyeing, engraving, medicines, flavouring, and food preservation.

key_concepts: Gluconic acid: produced during glucose oxidation by most Aspergillus sps. and fungi like Penicillium, Mucor, Fusarium; used in pharmaceuticals, food, and textile industries
Fumaric acid: obtained by activity of Rhizopus nigricans on sugar; used as wetting agent and in manufacture of resins
Gibberellic acid: from Gibberella fujikuroi; plant growth and flowering stimulant
Ustilagic acid: from Ustilago; used in the perfume industry

topics.name: Enzymes

subtopics.definition_content: Organic substances that enhance the rate of biochemical reactions without themselves undergoing any change; also called biological catalysts or biocatalysts. definition coined by William Kuhne in 1876.
Enzymes obtained from Aspergillus oryzae and Bacillus subtilis; used in detergents to remove proteinaceous stains on clothes.
Enzyme specifically used in heart patients to dissolve blood clots inside blood vessels.
Enzyme obtained from beef plasma; used for blood clotting in surgical operations.
Protein-digesting enzyme obtained from the latex of papaya; used for making meat tender.

key_concepts: Buchner (1897): living cells of yeast not required for alcoholic fermentation; enzyme extract also causes fermentation
Over 2200 enzymes known; only a few are used in industries, medicines, and food
Amylases (from Bacillus subtilis, Aspergillus niger, A. oryzae): used in beer, bread making, and softening starched clothes
Lactase (from Saccharomyces fragilis): converts lactose to lactic acid, causing coagulation of milk protein (casein)
Invertase (from S. cerevisiae): used to prevent sandiness in dairy products
Pectinases (from Aspergillus lucherisis): involved in destruction of penicillin action
Pig insulin converted to human insulin (humulin) by enzyme action
Immobilized enzymes: trapped in gel, cross-linked, or covalently attached to solid support; used in continuous industrial processes

topics.name: Vitamins

subtopics.definition_content: Vitamin synthesized industrially by fermentation with a filamentous yeast Ashbya gossypii; precursor of FMN and FAD (coenzymes for dehydrogenase enzymes); bitter, crystalline, yellowish-brown substance essential for growth and reproduction.

Vitamin of animal protein origin (liver, meat, fish); absent from vegetarian diet; deficiency causes pernicious anaemia; produced commercially by fermentation using Pseudomonas denitrificans, Bacillus coagulans, B. megatherium, and Propionibacteria.

Produced by complicated modification of glucose by different species of Acetobacter; Acetobacter dehydrogenates D-sorbitol converting it to L-sorbose, which is a precursor of L-ascorbic acid (vitamin C).

key_concepts: First vitamin discovered: Vitamin B₁ (thiamine) by Casimir Funk (1911); its deficiency causes beri-beri
Vitamin A: discovered by McCollum; Vitamin D: by Mellanby; Vitamin C: by Albert Van Szent Gyorgy
Vitamin C was the first vitamin produced during fermentation using a wild bacterium
Vitamin B₁₂: first isolated in 1948 from liver extract; also a by-product of fermentation during streptomycin and acetone-butanol production

topics.name: Other Industrial Products

subtopics.definition_content: Soluble polysaccharides formed by simple sugars through micro-organisms like Leuconostoc mesenteroides bacteria or its enzyme dextran sucrose; dextrans represent 6–10% solution of dextrins; used in plasma transfusions.
Antiviral proteins produced by Charles Weismann through recombinant DNA technology in E. coli (1980); used as therapeutic agents for treatment of viral diseases, cancer, and multiple sclerosis.

key_concepts: Lysine (basic amino acid): produced from diaminopimelic acid (cell wall component of bacteria) by Enterobacter aerogens
Tissue culture: used in manufacture of antibiotics, alkaloids, and dyes (e.g., Shikonin – a red dye from cultured Lithospermum root cells, used in silk industry and burns treatment)

formulas.description: Yeast fermentation of sucrose to ethyl alcohol and carbon dioxide via invertase and zymase

formulas.expression: C₆H₁₂O₆ →(Yeast, Anaerobic)→ 2C₂H₅OH + 2CO₂; C₂H₅OH + O₂ →(Acetobacter aceti, Aerobic)→ CH₃COOH + H₂O
formulas.description: Two-step fermentation producing acetic acid (vinegar) from glucose
Fermentation of lactose (milk sugar) by Streptococcus lactis to produce lactic acid

topics.name: Primary Treatment (Physical)

subtopics.definition_content: Process of removing contaminants from waste water and household sewage through physical, chemical, and biological processes; produces treated effluent suitable for discharge into rivers or use in irrigation.

key_concepts: Involves physical removal of particles – large and small – from sewage through filtration and sedimentation
Sequential filtration removes floating debris, then grit (soil and small pebbles) are removed by sedimentation
All solids that settle form the primary sludge; the supernatant forms the primary effluent

topics.name: Secondary Treatment (Biological)

subtopics.definition_content: Amount of dissolved oxygen that would be consumed if all the organic matter in one litre of water were oxidized by bacteria; represents the polluting potential of water. Higher BOD = greater pollution.

Sediment formed when bacterial flocs settle in a sedimentation tank during secondary sewage treatment; part is recycled as inoculum in aeration tanks; the remaining major part is pumped into anaerobic sludge digesters.

key_concepts: Primary effluent passed into large aeration tanks where air is pumped in; aerobic microbes grow and oxidize organic matter in sewage
BOD is reduced to 10–15% of raw sewage during secondary (aerobic) treatment
Bacterial flocs: masses of bacteria associated with fungal filaments to form mesh-like structures that consume organic matter
Anaerobic sludge digesters: produce biogas (methane, H₂S, CO₂) from digestion of organic sludge
Ganga Action Plan and Yamuna Action Plan: initiated by Ministry of Environment and Forests to build sewage treatment plants so only treated sewage is discharged into rivers

units.unit_name: Bioenergy, Biofertilizers and Biological Pest Control

topics.name: Agro-industrial Residues and Bioenergy

subtopics.definition_content: Energy available from biological sources both living and their immediate remains (e.g., wood, gobar gas); does not include fossil fuels such as coal, petroleum or natural gas.

key_concepts: Bioconversion of agricultural residues, industrial wastes, and animal wastes into energy is important in solving environmental pollution problems
About 28% of population in developing countries uses dung and crop residues as fuel for cooking
Using dung to produce biogas and residue as manure solves the fertilizer-burning problem
Aerobic fermentation of dung yields fuel as well as fertilizer

topics.name: Biogas (Gobar Gas)

subtopics.definition_content: Methane-rich fuel gas produced by anaerobic breakdown and fermentation of biomass; consists of 50–70% CH₄, 30–40% CO₂, 1% H₂S and traces of H₂, N₂, O₂ and CO; calorific value is 4,429 kcal/m³ (23–28 MJ/m³).

key_concepts: Composition of biogas: CH₄ 50–68%, CO₂ 25–35%, H₂ 1–5%, N₂ 2–7%, O₂ 0–0.1%, H₂S (rare)
Biogas generation is a three-stage anaerobic digestion of organic wastes

Stage 1: Facultative anaerobic micro-organisms break polymers into soluble monomers by enzymatic hydrolysis; cellulytic enzymes degrade cellulose and hemicellulose; proteolytic enzymes break proteins; lipolytic enzymes break lipids; lignin remains as residue with inorganic salts
Stage 2: Monomers serve as substrates for micro-organisms and are converted into organic acids
Stage 3: Soluble organic acids (acetic acid) become substrates for methanogenic anaerobic bacteria (Methanobacterium, Methanobacillus, Methanosarcina, Methanococcus) that produce methane
Water content maintained at 90% for active methanogenic bacteria

Important substrates: animal wastes (cattle dung, urine, slaughterhouse), agro-industrial wastes (oil cakes, sugar industry, fruit/vegetable processing), agricultural residues, human wastes, urban solid wastes, aquatic plants (Eichhornia/water hyacinth, Wolffia, Hydrilla, Salvinia, Azolla) and algae
Okhla plant near New Delhi generates 0.6 million cubic feet of gas per day; 1.95 lakh biogas plants set up during 1985–86 under National Biogas Development Programme

topics.name: Energy Cropping and Petroplants

subtopics.definition_content: Growing of crops from which alcohol and other energy fuels can be produced; constitutes a renewable resource of energy.

Plants with large amounts of latex containing long-chain hydrocarbons considered as good substitutes for liquid fuels or petroleum. Cultivation first done by Italians in Ethiopia ~50 years ago; identified by Dr. Melvin Calvin. Most belong to families Asclepiadaceae, Euphorbiaceae and Apocynaceae.
A fuel mixture of 85–90% petrol and 10–15% alcohol used in automobiles in the USA.

key_concepts: Energy crops: Saccharum officinarum (Sugarcane), Beta vulgaris (Beet root/Chukandar), Solanum tuberosum (Potato), Zea mays (Maize), Manihot glaziovii (Tapioca); efficient users of solar energy and easily converted to ethanol
Brazil pioneered fuel alcohol production — Pro-Alcohol Programme aimed at completely replacing petrol with 95% alcohol in automobiles

Common petrocrops: Euphorbia antisyphilitica, E. caudicifolia, E. lathyris (Gasoline tree), E. royleana, Calotropis procera, Copaifera langsdorfii (Brazilian tree — 3 litres of sap/month/tree, directly usable as diesel), Cryptostegia grandiflora, Pittosporum resiniferum (Petroleum nut)
Latex contains long-chain liquid hydrocarbons usable directly or broken into chain lengths similar to petrol; hydrocarbon content can be increased by genetic engineering

topics.name: Green Manures

subtopics.definition_content: Fast-growing herbaceous crops ploughed down and mixed with soil while still green for soil enrichment; provide both organic matter and nitrogen; check soil erosion and prevent leaching; increase yield by 30–50%.
Organisms that bring about nutrient enrichment of the soil; provide essential elements to maintain or increase soil fertility; safe economically and ecologically.

key_concepts: Important green manure crops (mostly family Leguminosae): Trifolium alexandrianum (Barseem/Egyptian clover), Melilotus parviflora (Senji/Sweet clover), Dolichos biflorus (Horse gram/Kulthi), Lens esculenta (Lentil/Masure), Cyamopsis tetragonoloba (Guar/Cluster bean), Sesbania sesban (Dhaincha), Crotalaria juncea (Sunhemp/Sunn), Vigna sinensis (Lobiya/Cowpea)

topics.name: Blue-green Algae (Cyanobacteria) as Biofertilizers

subtopics.definition_content: Cyanobacteria present symbiotically in other organisms to fix atmospheric nitrogen: Anabaena azollae (in leaves of Azolla/water fern), Anabaena cycadacae (in Cycas roots), Nostoc punctaeformae (in Anthoceros thallus).
The main biofertilizer inoculated in rice fields in South-Eastern Asia; increases rice yield up to 50%. Azolla is the aquatic water fern hosting Anabaena azollae in its leaf cavities.

key_concepts: Free-living N-fixing BGA: Nostoc, Anabaena, Aulosira, Tolypothrix, Plectonema, Cylindrospermum; fix nitrogen probably via heterocysts; photosynthesis provides energy for fixation
Aulosira is the most active nitrogen-fixing BGA in rice fields
Estimated 7–8 lakh tons of nitrogen fixed by BGA annually, saving 15–17 lakh tons of urea per year

subtopics.definition_content: Soil bacteria that independently fix atmospheric N₂ and increase soil fertility: Azotobacter (aerobic), Clostridium (anaerobic), Rhodospirillum and Chromatium (photosynthetic).
Rhizobium leguminosarum bacteria that live symbiotically in root nodules of legumes and some non-legumes, fixing atmospheric N₂ and increasing soil fertility.
Azospirillum lipoferum, a bacterium that forms loose (non-nodular) association with roots of maize and some Brazilian grasses (Dobereiner, 1975), increasing crop yield to a large extent.
Symbiotic association between roots of higher plants and fungal hyphae; two types: endotrophic and ectotrophic.
Mycorrhiza where fungal hyphae are present inside or between cells of the cortex (e.g., Coffee, Pepper, Cardamom).

A type of endotrophic mycorrhiza in many grasses and some crops where fungal hyphae penetrate cortical cells that swell into vesicles or arbuscules; has significant role in phosphate nutrition of plants.
Mycorrhiza where fungal hyphae are present on the surface of roots; increases water and nutrient absorption, growth, vigour and yield (e.g., Pinus, Oak, Eucalyptus, Peach).

topics.name: Bioherbicides and Biological Control of Weeds

subtopics.definition_content: Use of other organisms to kill pests; such organisms or their products are called biopesticides.
Organisms or their extracts that destroy weeds without harming useful plants.

A bioherbicide based on a fungus. Devine (based on Phytophthora palmivora, used since 1981 to control Morrenia odorata vine in Citrus orchards) is the first mycoherbicide. Collego is another mycoherbicide from conidia of Colletotrichum gloeosporioides fungus.

topics.name: Bioinsecticides

subtopics.definition_content: Living organisms or their products able to kill or repel specific insects.

A naturally occurring soil bacterium producing a protein (cry protein) poisonous to insects; 500–600 strains identified; affects only specific insect species without harming humans, birds, fish or beneficial insects; within 15 minutes of ingestion, poisons create ulcers in insect's stomach lining causing death. Sporeine (first commercial bioinsecticide, developed in Germany) is from Bt.

Insecticides of plant or microbial origin with little toxicity for animals; e.g., Rotenone (from roots of Derris spp. and Lonchocarpus spp.), Nicotine (from tobacco), Pyrethrum and Cinerin (from Chrysanthemum), Azadirachtin (from Neem/Azadirachta indica).

Modern biological pest control in which male insects are sterilized by irradiation and released during mating season to prevent reproduction; e.g., used for screw worm (Cochliomyia hominivorax) and red weevil on coconut (technique developed by Kiplings, 1955).

Insect sex attractant hormones that attract opposite-sex insects during breeding season; natural and synthetic pheromones are used to lure insects toward death traps; used against Oriental fruit fly and gypsy moth.

A variation of pheromone control in which hydrophobic paper containing pheromones is spread over the crop area so the characteristic smell pervades the whole field, preventing males from locating females.

key_concepts: Parasites/predators/pathogens: praying mantis (Mantis) and lady bug control aphids; Cactoblastis cactorum (Cochineal insect) controlled prickly pear cactus (Opuntia) in Australia and India; lady bird beetles control fluted scale insect (Icerya purchasi) on citrus; Gambusia fish controls mosquito larvae; coccinellid predators control sugarcane scale insects
Neem (Azadirachta indica) — most useful natural insecticide; resistant to ~200 species of insects, nematodes and mites; azadirachtin is antifeedant compound
Juvenile hormone — prevents early maturation in early growth stages; if given artificially at later stage, insect becomes giant larva (immature adult) which dies quickly
Ecdysone (moulting hormone) — associated with periodic shedding of insect cuticle; use at inappropriate time causes early death of insect
Zygogramma bicolorata (beetle) suppresses Parthenium hysterophorus; Nupserha lenita (shoot and root borer) reduces Parthenium population
Parasitoids — organisms that are parasites in early stages (egg and larval) but live freely later; e.g., parasitoid wasp of cabbage butterfly

subtopics.definition_content: Use of different ecologically sound pest control methods including: biological control; better agricultural practices such as crop rotation and sanitation; starvation method (growing target crop away from the major crop); ultra low volume spraying method (most effective low concentrations of chemicals that do not cause pollution).

topics.name: Genetic engineering

subtopics.definition_content: A technology that allows DNA to be produced via artificial means; the joining together of DNA molecules from two different species that are inserted into a host organism to produce new genetic combinations of value to science, medicine, agriculture and industry.

The latest branch in applied genetics dealing with the alteration of the genetic makeup of cells by deliberate and artificial means; involves transfer or replacement of genes; also known as recombinant DNA technology or gene splicing.

key_concepts: Two main types of cloning: therapeutic cloning and reproductive cloning
Dolly the sheep was the first mammal reproduced as an exact genetic copy (reproductive cloning)

topics.name: Tools of Recombinant DNA Technology

subtopics.definition_content: Enzymes responsible for restricting the growth of bacteriophage in Escherichia coli that cut DNA at specific recognition sequences; isolated in 1963.
The first restriction endonuclease to be isolated and characterized; always cuts DNA molecules at a particular point by recognizing a specific sequence of six base pairs (the recognition sequence).
A specific sequence of base pairs that a restriction enzyme recognizes and at which it cuts DNA; e.g., Hind II recognizes a six base pair sequence.
A sequence of base pairs that reads the same on the two strands when the orientation of reading is kept the same (e.g., 5’-GAATTC-3’ / 3’-CTTAAG-5’).

Single-stranded overhanging stretches left after restriction enzymes cut a little away from the centre of the palindrome sites but between the same two bases on opposite strands; form hydrogen bonds with complementary cut counterparts, facilitating DNA ligase action.

key_concepts: Over 900 restriction enzymes isolated from over 230 bacterial strains, each recognizing different recognition sequences
Naming convention: first letter from genus, next two letters from species, followed by strain letter and Roman numeral (e.g., EcoRI from Escherichia coli RY 13)
DNA ligase creates recombinant DNA molecules by joining sticky ends

topics.name: Separation and Isolation of DNA Fragments

subtopics.definition_content: A technique to separate DNA fragments by forcing negatively charged DNA molecules through an agarose gel matrix using an electric field; smaller fragments move farther toward the anode.
The process of cutting out separated bands of DNA from an agarose gel and extracting the DNA fragment from the gel piece for purification.

key_concepts: Agarose gel is a natural polymer extracted from seaweed; provides sieving effect for DNA separation by size
Separated DNA bands visualized by staining with ethidium bromide and exposing to UV radiation (bright orange bands)
Purified DNA fragment is used in constructing recombinant DNA

topics.name: Cloning Vectors

subtopics.definition_content: A sequence from where replication starts; any piece of DNA linked to this sequence can be made to replicate within the host cells; controls copy number of the linked DNA.

A vector component that helps in identifying and eliminating non-transformants and selectively permitting the growth of transformants; typically antibiotic resistance genes (ampicillin, chloramphenicol, tetracycline, kanamycin).
A procedure through which a piece of DNA is introduced in a host bacterium.

Inactivation of an enzyme (e.g., β-galactosidase) when recombinant DNA is inserted within its coding sequence; recombinant colonies do not produce blue colour with chromogenic substrate while non-recombinants produce blue colonies.

key_concepts: Vectors can be bacteriophages or plasmids; some plasmids: 1–2 copies/cell, others: 15–100 copies/cell
pBR322 is an E. coli cloning vector with ampicillin and tetracycline resistance genes; foreign DNA ligated at BamH I site in tetracycline resistance gene causes insertional inactivation
Recombinants grow on ampicillin medium but not on tetracycline medium; non-recombinants grow on both
Agrobacterium tumefaciens Ti plasmid (T-DNA) modified as cloning vector for dicot plant cells; retroviruses disarmed for delivery into animal cells (e.g., treating SCID patients)

topics.name: Competent host (for transformation with recombinant DNA technology)
key_concepts: Calcium ion (divalent cation) treatment increases DNA uptake through pores in bacterial cell wall; heat shock at 42°C then ice forces recombinant DNA into cells
Micro-injection method: recombinant DNA directly injected into the nucleus of an animal cell
Gene gun (biolistic) method: gold or tungsten micro-particles coated with DNA bombarded into plant cells at high velocity
Disarmed pathogen vectors (e.g., modified Agrobacterium) infect cells and transfer recombinant DNA into the host

topics.name: Process of Recombinant DNA technology

subtopics.definition_content: A technique to synthesize multiple copies of a gene (or DNA) of interest in vitro using two sets of oligonucleotide primers complementary to the regions of DNA and the enzyme DNA polymerase; developed by Kary Mullis in 1983.
A protein produced when a protein-encoding gene is expressed in a heterologous host.
A bacterium that performs gene transfer without human effort; e.g., Agrobacterium tumefaciens, a soil-inhabiting plant pathogenic bacterium.

key_concepts: Steps: (i) Isolation of genetic material — cells broken using lysozyme (bacteria), cellulase (plant cells), chitinase (fungi); RNA removed with ribonuclease; proteins removed with protease; DNA precipitated with chilled ethanol
Step (ii): Cutting DNA at specific locations by restriction enzyme digestion; agarose gel electrophoresis checks digestion; cut gene ligated into cut vector to form recombinant DNA
Step (iii): PCR amplification — three steps per cycle: denaturation, primer annealing, extension; thermostable DNA polymerase from Thermus aquaticus; can amplify to billions of copies
Step (iv): Insertion of recombinant DNA into host cell via vector, gene gun, or micro-injection; selectable marker (e.g., ampicillin resistance) identifies transformed cells
Step (v): Obtaining foreign gene product — continuous culture (used medium drained, fresh medium added to maintain log phase for higher biomass/protein) or simple fermenting vessel

topics.name: Application of recombinant DNA technology
key_concepts: Elucidating molecular events in cellular differentiation and ageing; making precise gene maps
Engineering genes to produce useful chemical compounds cheaply and efficiently in pharmaceutical and biochemical industries
Production of transgenic plants and genetically modified microorganisms

Medically useful recombinant products: human insulin (diabetes), human growth hormone (short stature), calcitonin (rickets), chronic gonadotropin (infertility), blood clotting factor VIII/IX (haemophilia A/B), tissue plasminogen activator (blood clots), erythropoietin (anaemia), platelet derived growth factor (wound healing), interferon (viral infections, cancer), interleukins (immune enhancement), vaccines (hepatitis B, herpes, influenza, pertussis, meningitis)

topics.name: Major Bioethical Concerns in Biotechnology

subtopics.definition_content: The branch of ethics, philosophy and social commentary that deals with the biological sciences and their potential impact on society.

key_concepts: Use of animals in biotechnology causes great suffering
Introduction of a transgene from one species into another violates the 'integrity of species'
Transfer of human genes into animals (and vice-versa) dilutes the concept of 'humanness'

topics.name: definition_content and Rights

subtopics.definition_content: A monopoly granted to a person who has invented a new and useful article, made an improvement of an existing article, or invented a new process of making an article.

key_concepts: Patent holder rights include: make, use, sell, offer for sale, export and license

topics.name: Scope of Biopatents

subtopics.definition_content: Patents awarded for strains of microorganism, cell lines, genetically modified strains of plants and animals, DNA sequences, proteins encoded by DNA sequences, biotechnological procedures, production processes, products and product applications.

key_concepts: In 1971, Anand Mohan Chakravarty (General Electric) applied for US patent on genetically engineered Pseudomonas
Human breast cancer gene BRCA1 was patented in USA after its base sequence was dedefinitionined
Attempts made to patent BRCA2 (second breast cancer gene)

subtopics.definition_content: The unauthorized publication or reproduction of another's material.

Intellectual Property Right (IPR) claims by the formal sector over the work of the informal sector; use of bioresources of other nations without proper authorisation from the concerned country and its people.

topics.name: Case Studies of Biopiracy
key_concepts: Neem patent: USDA and W.R. Grace sought EPO patent No. 0426257 B1 on neem oil; revoked by EPO on 10 May 2000
Turmeric: American university patented wound-healing properties of turmeric; patent cancelled in 1998
Basmati patent: USPTO granted patent No. 5663484 to Texas-based Rice Tec Inc in 1997 for 'Basmati rice line and grains'
Rice biopiracy: Syngenta attempted to secure 22,972 rice varieties from India's rice diversity collection
ConAgra atta chakki patent No. 6,098,905 granted in 2000 to Nebraska-based company
Brazzein: protein ~2000 times sweeter than sugar from African plant Pentadiplandra brazzeana, patented in USA; gene isolated, sequenced and patented; proposal to express brazzein gene in maize

topics.name: Biosafety Guidelines and Risks of GMOs

subtopics.definition_content: Measures taken to prevent any risk to plants, animals and microbes from transgenic organisms.
Microorganisms modified via genetic engineering; feared to disturb ecosystems, outcompete native microbes, or transfer virulence genes to bacterial populations.

key_concepts: Biosafety guidelines ensure safe transfer, handling and use of living modified organisms
Genetically modified plants could pose biological and ecological risk

topics.name: Overview of Industrial Biotechnology

subtopics.definition_content: Use of microbes to obtain a product or service of economic value; also known as white or grey biotechnology.

key_concepts: Industrial production of useful products began in 1875 with alcohol production
Products include: lactic acid, amylase, glycerine, citric acid, gluconic acid, acetic acid, acetone, butanol, enzymes, vitamins, amino acids, and antibiotics

topics.name: Biological Warfare Agents and Mechanisms

subtopics.definition_content: The deployment of biological weapons (viruses, bacteria, fungi or toxins from living organisms) against people, their crops and animals to produce death or disease.
The biological agent or toxin kept in a suitable container so that it remains active and virulent during delivery.

key_concepts: First reported use: 5th century BC, Assyrians poisoned enemy wells with rye ergot
Smallpox used as biological weapon; Pizarro gave variola-contaminated clothing to South American natives in 15th century
Post 9/11: Anthrax spores used against USA and allies by Al-Qaeda activists
Botulinum toxin: most potent, enters nerve definitioninals and blocks neurotransmitter release
Ricin: plant toxin that kills by blocking protein synthesis
Pathogen agents: Smallpox virus, Viral encephalitides, Viral hemorrhagic fevers, Bacillus anthracis, Brucella suis, Coxiella burnetii, Francisella tularensis, Yersinia pestis
Toxin agents: Botulinum, Ricin, Staphylococcal enterotoxin B
Anticrop agents: Rice blast, Rice stem rust, Wheat stem rust

topics.name: Transported soil

subtopics.definition_content: Soil carried by running water (rivers)
Soil carried by gravity
Soil carried by wind
Soil carried by glacier

key_concepts: Soils of plains of India are mainly alluvial
Principal residual soil types in India: reddish (Vindhyas/South), black (South West), calcareous (20% CaCO3), laterite (Fe/Al oxides), peat (90% humus), black clay

topics.name: Soil profile

subtopics.definition_content: Cross section of soil showing different layers called horizons in a fully formed soil; the sequence and nature of these layers is called the soil profile
Uppermost horizon made of organic matter; subdivided into O1/Aoo (freshly added undecomposed organic matter) and O2/Ao (organic matter in various stages of decomposition)
Rich in mineral elements; large amount of completely decomposed organic matter present
Zone of accumulation or zone of alluvialation; dark in colour due to accumulation of leached substances like clay, iron and aluminium from upper horizon
Sub soil; consists of partially weathered parental rock material
Lowermost layer consisting of bed rocks (unweathered)

key_concepts: Horizons O, A and B together are called top soil; Horizon C is sub soil
Top soil contains the most biologically active layer

topics.name: Composition of soil

subtopics.definition_content: Total organic matter in the soils; rich in N, P, K; formed from decay and decomposition of dead plant and animal matter; in colloidal state; increases water holding capacity of soil; maximum in peat soil (90%)
Formation of humus by microbial activity
All dead fresh organic matter fallen (undecomposed) recently to the ground
The layer where decomposition is just started; partially decomposed litter
The relationship between soil and living organisms

key_concepts: Sandy soils: coarser particles, lower water holding capacity, better aeration; most porous; plants grow sparsely
Clayey soils: fine particles, high water holding capacity, very poor aeration; least porous (water logged)
Loam (50% sand + 25% clay + 25% silt): best for plant growth
pH of fertile soil is 6 to 7; pH below 5 inhibits bacterial activity
Soil microorganisms grouped into: microflora, macroflora, microfauna (Amoeba, Rhabditis), macrofauna (insects, earthworms)

topics.name: Positive interactions

subtopics.definition_content: Association where both organisms are mutually benefitted and the association is obligatory (necessary for existence of both organisms); definition given by De Bary
Association between roots of higher plants and fungal hyphae; ectotrophic mycorrhiza: hyphae on surface of roots (e.g., Pinus); endotrophic mycorrhiza: hyphae inside root tissue (e.g., orchids)
Composite plants in which algal partner and fungal partner are mutually associated
Association between ants and higher plants, e.g., jamun, litchi, mango; ants get food from plants and act as body-guards in return
Relationship between two living individuals of different species in which one is benefitted while the other is neither harmed nor benefitted (except to negligible extent)
Vascular plants not rooted in soil that grow upon other plants for support (but not food); possess clinging roots, absorptive roots, and aerial roots with velamen; e.g., Vanda, Dendrobium
Vascular plants rooted in soil that get support of other plants or objects for their erectness; most common in Indian tropical forests is Bauhinia vauhilli
Interaction between two living organisms of different species in which both are mutually benefitted but they can live without each other, e.g., tick bird, ox pecker and Rhinoceros

key_concepts: Symbiotic nitrogen fixation: Rhizobium bacteria in root nodules of legumes fix atmospheric nitrogen
Zoochlorellae: Chlorella vulgaris lives in gastrodermal cavity of Hydra in mutualism
Rhizosphere: soil zone around roots where microbes are present due to sugar secretion; rhizoplane = root surface
Phyllosphere: air zone around leaves having microbial population; phylloplane = leaf surface

topics.name: Negative interactions

subtopics.definition_content: A parasite lives in constant association with host and gets its food directly or indirectly without killing the host
A predator gets its food from the host after killing it; does not live in constant association with host

Both organisms in association are harmed; occurs for basic necessities of life; intraspecific competition is more severe because organisms of same species have similar requirements; territoriality results from competition
Association in which one organism is harmed and second is not affected; two types: antibiosis and allelopathy
Micro-organisms secrete chemical substances (antibiotics) which kill or inhibit other micro-organisms
Higher plants secrete poisonous substances inhibiting growth of other plants; e.g., Parthenium argentatum (congress grass) secretes trans-cinnamic acid which checks growth of other plants

key_concepts: Four types of plant parasites: Total stem (Cuscuta), Total root (Rafflesia, Orobanche), Partial stem (Viscum, Loranthus), Partial root (Santalum, Thesium)
Carnivorous plants grow in marshy nitrogen-deficient conditions; catch insects for nitrogen, e.g., Drosera
Predaceous fungi: Dactylella, Arthobotrys, Zoophagus (in soil)

topics.name: Hydrophytes

subtopics.definition_content: Plants that live in abundance of water and require abundance of water to complete their life cycle; water as ecological factor first recognized by Warming
Production of leaves of different forms by the same plant; aerial leaves not dissected, submerged leaves dissected (e.g., Ranunculus, Limnophila)
Extensively developed air spaces in root, stem and leaves of hydrophytes; helps in buoyancy and gaseous exchange

key_concepts: Types: Rooted submerged (Hydrilla, Vallisneria), Submerged floating (Ceratophyllum, Utricularia), Rooted with floating leaves (Nelumbo, Trapa, Victoria), Free floating (Wolffia - smallest angiosperm, Lemna, Pistia), Rooted emergent/Amphibious (Typha, Ranunculus, Sagittaria)
Morphological: roots poorly developed or absent in Wolffia; root hair and root cap absent; stem narrow in submerged plants; petioles long, swollen, spongy for floating
Anatomical: cuticle absent or poorly developed; stomata absent in submerged plants; epistomatic leaves in floating (Lotus); mechanical tissues poorly developed or absent
Physiological: water and mineral nutrients absorbed through general body surface; osmotic potential equal to or slightly higher than external water

topics.name: Xerophytes

subtopics.definition_content: Plants adapted to grow in dry habitats; vegetation of Rajasthan is xerophytic
Xerophytes that complete life cycle in very short period, evade dry season by leaving seeds; drought escapers/evaders, e.g., Cassia toria, Argemone mexicana, Solanum xanthocarpum

Absorb large quantities of water during rainy season and store in body parts; drought avoiding xerophytes; common in deserts, e.g., Opuntia, Bryophyllum, Euphorbia, Mesembryanthemum (ice plant), Agave
True xerophytes; drought resistant; can withstand long drought periods, e.g., Acacia, Calotropis, Casuarina, Nerium, Capparis, Prosopis

key_concepts: Morphological: roots extensively developed, much longer than shoots; stems stunted, woody, hard, covered with thick bark; leaves thick, may be reduced to spines (Opuntia), scales (Casuarina), needle-like (Pinus), or absent (Capparis)

Anatomical: stomata sunken, mainly on lower leaf surface; epidermis thick-walled with hairs (Trichophyllous, e.g., Calotropis); multiple epidermis (Ficus, Nerium); palisade on both sides of leaf (Nerium); bulliform cells cause rolling/unrolling of leaves (Ammophila)
Physiological: high osmotic concentration/potential; resistance to desiccation; less transpiration
Subtypes: Oxylophytes (acidic soil), Psychrophytes (cold soil), Halophytes (saline soil), Lithophytes (rocks), Eremophytes (deserts)

topics.name: Halophytes

subtopics.definition_content: Special types of xerophytic plants growing on saline soils with high concentrations of salts (NaCl, MgCl₂, MgSO₄) — physiologically dry soil; salt resistant; most are succulents
Negatively geotropic roots of halophytes used for gaseous exchange
Germination of seeds inside the fruits; shown by halophytes

key_concepts: Mangroves: characteristic vegetation of swamp forests (littoral swamp forests); e.g., Rhizophora, Sonneratia, Avecenna, Heritiera, Salsola, Atriplex
In India mangroves found in: Bombay, Kerala, Andaman Nicobar Islands, West Bengal (Sunderbans)

subtopics.definition_content: Plants growing in places of moderate water supply; cannot live long in water-saturated or moisture-deficient soil; e.g., garden plants and crops; vegetation of Delhi is chiefly mesophytic

topics.name: Population Dynamics/Factor Affecting Population

subtopics.definition_content: The total number of individuals of the same species occupying a particular geographic area at a given time (Clark, 1954)
The scientific study of human population; deals with change in population, composition (age groups, sex ratio), and distribution of population in space
Official count of the people of a country with statistics on age, sex, employment, education, etc.; started in India in 1891, conducted every ten years under Census Act 1948
The number of individuals present per unit area or volume at a given time; D = N/S where N = total individuals, S = space units
Average number of young ones produced by birth, hatching or germination per unit time; expressed as births per 1000 individuals per year in humans
Maximum birth rate a species can achieve under ideal environmental conditions
Actual birth rate under existing conditions; much less than potential natality
Average number of individuals that die per unit time; expressed as deaths per 1000 persons per year
Lowest death rate for a given species in most favourable conditions
Percentage ratio of natality over mortality: (natality/mortality) × 100; dedefinitionines growth of a population
Permanent entry of additional persons into the existing population of a country or region from outside
Permanent departure of persons from existing population to a different state or foreign country
The number of females in a population per 1000 males
Percentage of individuals of different ages (young, adult, old) in a population; represented by age-sex pyramid diagrams
The relative abundance of organisms of various age groups in the population

key_concepts: Three types of survivorship curves: diagonal (equal death rate for all ages, e.g., Hydra, mice, adult birds), convex (death in old age, e.g., man, rabbit, many mammals), concave (death before potential life span, e.g., fish, oysters, invertebrates)

Three types of age distribution: rapidly growing/expanding (high birth, low death; broad-base pyramid), stationary/stable (equal birth and death; zero growth; bell-shaped pyramid), declining (higher death than birth; urn-shaped pyramid e.g. Japan)
Human population age groups: pre-reproductive, reproductive, and post-reproductive
Migration is two-way movement for seasonal adjustment (not a dedefinitioninant of population size); occurs in Anguilla, Siberian crane, fur seal

formulas.description: Population density where N = total individuals, S = units of space; space in m² for land, m³ for aquatic organisms
Sex ratio formula
Population change: B=births, I=immigration, D=deaths, E=emigration; simplified to ΔN/Δt = B − D when I and E are negligible

topics.name: Malthus Theory of Human Population Growth

subtopics.definition_content: Thomas Malthus (1778) stated population tends to increase in geometric progression while food supply increases only in arithmetic progression; environmental factors called positive checks reduce population when imbalance is extreme

key_concepts: Population growth: positive growth (vital index > 100), negative growth (vital index < 100), zero growth (vital index = 100)
Three major human population explosions: ~20,000 years ago (tools/hunting), ~6,000 years ago (farming), ~300 years ago (food production, industry, medicine)

topics.name: Natural Control of Population Growth

subtopics.definition_content: The natural capacity of a population to increase at its maximum rate under ideal environmental conditions and stable age and sex ratios; estimated at about 12 per female in humans

The sum of all inhibitory abiotic factors (non-availability of food, shelter, drought, temperature fluctuations) and biotic factors (pathogens, parasites, predators) that check biotic potential from being realized

Feeding capacity of an environment for a population of a species under provided conditions; when population reaches K, growth rate becomes zero; estimated at 8–15 billion for human population on earth

key_concepts: Three components of carrying capacity: productive systems (croplands, orchards), protective systems (ocean buffers air/water cycles), assimilative systems (waterways, wetlands that utilize waste)

Two types of population variation: population fluctuations/irruptive variations (irregular changes, e.g., birds in summer) and population cycles (regular periodic changes, e.g., lemmings 3–4 year cycles)

formulas.description: Annual average growth rate where P1 = previous census population, P2 = present census population, N = number of years between censuses
Vital index; value > 100 = population increase; value < 100 = decrease; value = 100 = stable

chapters.name: Two basic types of growth curves

topics.name: Sigmoid or S-shaped growth curve

subtopics.definition: Sigmoid or S-shaped growth curve (Logistic curve)
subtopics.definition_content: Growth curve with 5 phases: (a) lag phase (adaptation, no growth), (b) positive acceleration (slow increase), (c) logarithmic/exponential phase (rapid rise), (d) negative acceleration (slow rise as environmental resistance increases), (e) stationary/plateau phase (zero growth, birth = death); described by Verhulst (1839); K = carrying capacity

key_concepts: Shown by yeast cells in culture medium and most organisms including human population
Population stabilizes at carrying capacity K with zero growth rate
Stationary population is said to be in equilibrium or at saturation level

topics.name: J-shaped Growth curve

subtopics.definition_content: Growth curve with 2 phases: (a) lag phase (adaptation period), (b) logarithmic/exponential phase (rapid growth continues until food and space are exhausted); ends in population crash
Sudden increase in mortality following exponential population growth when food and space become exhausted; characteristic of J-shaped growth

key_concepts: Examples: Reindeer experimentally reared with no predators; lemmings of Tundra; algal blooms; annual plants
Comparison: S-shaped has 5 phases and ends with zero growth at K; J-shaped has 2 phases and ends with population crash
S-shaped curve: biotic potential balanced by environmental resistance; J-shaped: environmental resistance absent until crash

topics.name: Types of Ecological Succession
