subtopics.definition_content: A relatively definite sequence of communities over a period of time in the same area. Biotic succession is caused by adaptive ability to environmental changes. The definition was first given by Hult (1885).
Includes changes which occur when living things become established on a previously uninhabited area such as a newly exposed sea floor, lake sediments or sand dunes.
Occurs where early communities have been damaged, leaving a few organisms and considerable organic matter. Remnant species, along with some new ones, regenerate a new community.
When succession has begun, the vegetation itself is responsible for replacing itself by changing existing environmental conditions.
When in succession, other conditions (not vegetation itself) are responsible for replacing communities.
Succession beginning in fresh water, e.g., ponds, lakes, streams, etc.
Succession beginning in dry conditions. Further classified into Lithosere, Psammosere, and Halosere.
Succession that starts on bare rocks.
Succession that begins on sand.
Succession that starts in saline conditions.
Succession that begins in mesic conditions.
All the seral stages (different stages of succession) from pioneer to climax community constitute a sere.
The first group or community established in an area during ecological succession.

The last stable, self-sustaining community in ecological succession, always mesophytic, in perfect harmony with the prevailing environment. Biomass increases continuously from pioneer to climax community.

key_concepts: Ecological succession is a definite, relatively fixed process
Communities are never stable but keep on changing
Succession classified by substratum: Hydrosere, Xerosere (Lithosere, Psammosere, Halosere), and Mesarch
Retrogressive succession: continuous biotic influences have a degenerating effect, e.g., forest changed to shrubby or grassland community
Serule or microsere: succession of microorganisms within a microhabitat

topics.name: General processes of succession

subtopics.definition_content: Formation of bare area without any form of life. The cause of nudation may be climatic, topographic or biotic.
Successful establishment of a species in a bare area. It has three steps: (i) Migration, (ii) Ecesis or establishment, (iii) Aggregation.
Intraspecific competition is called coaction.
Modification of environment due to organisms in it.
The process by which the definitioninal (climax) community becomes stabilized in the prevailing conditions.

key_concepts: Steps in succession: Nudation → Invasion (migration, ecesis, aggregation) → Competition and coaction → Reaction → Stabilization
Competition may be interspecific or intraspecific; intraspecific competition is called coaction

topics.name: Biotic succession on bare rock (Lithosere, Xerosere).

subtopics.definition_content: The sequence of successional stages that occur on bare rocks. Because bare rock is deficient in water, the lithosere is also called xerosere. The seral stages proceed: Lichen stage → Moss stage → Annual grass stage → Perennial grass stage → Shrub stage → Climax community.

key_concepts: Lichen stage: Crustose lichens (e.g., Graphis) are early settlers due to tolerance to desiccation; they corrode rock and create conditions for foliose lichens and then mosses; in tropics, blue-green algae are pioneers instead of lichens.
Moss stage: Mosses are larger with gregarious habit; rhizoids penetrate deeper into rocks; shade lichens and replace them; accumulate more soil and organic matter.
Annual grass stage: Hardy grasses and herbs (e.g., Aristida, Poa) invade humus-rich moss-dominated surface; roots cause fragmentation of rock creating more soil, humus, and moisture.
Perennial grass stage: Annual grasses replaced by perennial grasses (e.g., Cymbopogon, Heteropogon) due to increased moisture and soil; runners and rhizomes spread rapidly.
Shrub stage: Shrubs (e.g., Zizyphus, Capparis, Rhus, Rubus) grow in area occupied by perennial grasses; roots reach greater depth causing further rock cracks.
Climax community: Initially hardy light-demanding small trees invade; ultimately trees, shrubs, and herbs representing the climax community begin to grow.

topics.name: Biotic succession in newly formed pond/lake (Hydrosere) :

subtopics.definition_content: Seres of biotic communities that develop in a newly formed pond or lake. It starts as soon as the muddy water becomes clear.
Relatively shallow lakes with a rich accumulation of organic products, having good quantity of minerals and hence supra-optimum growth of plants.
Water body deficient in minerals and organic growth.
Upper stratum of lake water exposed to solar radiations; warm in summer and cooler in winter; circulates.
Basal stratum of lake water which is always cool.
The zone of transition between two nearby ecosystems.

key_concepts: Plankton stage: Phytoplanktons (diatoms, flagellates, blue-green and green algae) are pioneers; immediately followed by zooplanktons feeding on phytoplanktons.
Submerged stage: Submerged plants like Hydrilla and Najas root in mud forming dense growth; sand and silt deposit around plants raising the bottom level.
Floating stage: Plants with tuberous rhizomatous stems and floating leaves (e.g., Nymphaea, Nelumbo); free-floating plants (e.g., Azolla, Wolffia, Lemna) appear.
Reed swamp stage: Amphibious plants grow where water becomes shallow (0.3–1.0 m), e.g., Phragmites, Typha, Scirpus, Sagittaria; transpire large quantities of water and accumulate silt.
Sedge/Marsh meadow stage: Carex (Sedge), Juncus, Cyperus, grasses and herbs (Themeda, Caltha, Polygonum) lower the water table and add abundant humus.
Woodland stage: Rhizome-bearing shrubs and small trees tolerating excessive light and waterlogged conditions (e.g., Cornus, Cephalanthus, Populus, Alnus) appear on edges; lower water table further.
Climax forest: New trees, shrubs, and herbs appear in perfect harmony with the climate.
Coral reef ecosystem has the highest gross primary productivity.
Estuary is the ecotone of marine habitat and fresh water habitat.
In detritus ecosystem, producers are absent.

topics.name: Importance of biotic succession
key_concepts: Sequence of biotic succession is usually fixed; ecologists can immediately recognise the seral stage of a biotic community in an area.
Helps maintain biotic seral stages (e.g., pasture of grasses and herbs) by preventing succession from proceeding further through grazing and fire.
Information used for controlled growth of one or more species by preventing their superiors from invading, e.g., maintenance of teak forest.
Dams are protected by preventing siltation and biotic succession.

topics.name: Key Aspects of Ecosystem Study (continuation)

subtopics.definition_content: The maximum number of producers and consumers which can be supported by an ecosystem.

key_concepts: Protection: Each ecosystem whether natural or man-made requires protection from pollutants and pests.
Inter-relationships: Study of ecosystems gives information about inter-relationships amongst various types of organisms as well as between organisms and their abiotic environment.
Carrying capacity: By knowing the carrying capacity of an ecosystem, it can be known as to what number of producers and consumers it can support.
Inputs: The shortage of inputs can be known and corrected.

topics.name: Introduction and Overview

subtopics.definition_content: The cyclical path of the elements from the abiotic system to the biotic system and back. (Bio = living organism; Geo = water, air, earth). Also called nutrient cycles as these chemicals form the components of food.

key_concepts: Organisms require chemicals like N₂, O₂, H₂, P, C continuously for survival
Elements tend to circulate from environment to organism and back to environment
CO₂ content of air is 0.03%

topics.name: Phases of biogeochemical cycles

subtopics.definition_content: The phase that refers to the flow of chemicals in the living organisms through food chain.
The phase that refers to the distribution and flow of chemicals in the non-living environment.

key_concepts: Each biogeochemical cycle has two phases: biotic phase (organic phase) and abiotic phase

topics.name: Types of biogeochemical cycles

subtopics.definition_content: Biogeochemical cycles in which the main reservoirs of chemicals are the atmosphere and ocean. e.g., Carbon cycle, N₂ cycle, O₂ cycle, etc.
Biogeochemical cycles in which the main reservoirs are soil and rocks. e.g., Sulphur cycle, phosphorus cycle, etc.

key_concepts: Two types: gaseous cycles (reservoir = atmosphere/ocean) and sedimentary cycles (reservoir = soil/rocks)

topics.name: Carbon Cycle

subtopics.definition_content: The cycling of carbon between biotic and abiotic systems. It is a gaseous cycle. The main source of carbon is carbon dioxide (CO₂). CO₂ is present in the air and water; air is the main reservoir with CO₂ content of 0.03%.

key_concepts: Flow of Carbon into biotic system: (a) Photosynthesis — green plants utilize CO₂ and incorporate carbon into glucose; (b) Formation of shell — marine animals (protozoans, corals, molluscs, algae) convert CO₂ into CaCO₃ for shell construction

Flow of Carbon into abiotic system: (a) Respiration — plants and animals release CO₂; (b) Decomposition — dead bodies decomposed into CO₂ by bacteria, algae; (c) Shells — after death, CaCO₃ is deposited as sedimentary rocks or dissolved to release CO₂; (d) Coal — carbon deposited as coal returns as CO₂ through combustion/weathering; (e) Forest fire — combustion releases CO₂

topics.name: Nitrogen cycle

subtopics.definition_content: The cycling of nitrogen between abiotic and biotic systems. It is a gaseous cycle. The main source of N₂ is air which contains 79% N₂.

A process by which atmospheric free N₂ is converted into soluble salts like nitrites and nitrates. Occurs by electrochemical fixation (lightning; ~35 mg/m²/year) and biological fixation (by N₂-fixing organisms; 140–700 mg/m²/year, exceeds 20000 mg/m in fertile areas).
A certain amount of free N₂ is fixed by the action of lightning. The amount of nitrate formed is about 35 mg/m²/year.

The conversion of free N₂ into soluble salts by the activity of certain organisms (N₂-fixing bacteria, blue green algae, fungi). e.g., Rhizobium, Azotobacter, Closteridium, Bacillus, Nitrosomonas, Nitrococcus, Nitrobacter, Anabaena, Nostoc.
The conversion of protein from dead bodies into ammonia by decomposition.
The conversion of nitrate into ammonia or free nitrogen done by denitrifying bacteria (e.g., Pseudomonas). These bacteria utilize the O₂ present in the nitrate for the oxidation of carbohydrate.

key_concepts: N₂ content in air = 79%; plants cannot utilize free N₂ and obtain it from ammonium salts, nitrites and nitrates
N₂ fixing organisms: Rhizobium, Azotobacter, Closteridium, Bacillus, Nitrosomonas, Nitrococcus, Nitrobacter, Anabaena, Nostoc
Flow of nitrogen into abiotic system: (a) Decomposition/ammonification; (b) Excretion (ammonia, urea, uric acid); (c) Denitrification; (d) Sedimentation
Winogradsky (1891) discovered nitrogen fixation

topics.name: Oxygen cycle

subtopics.definition_content: The cycling of O₂ between biotic and abiotic systems. It is a gaseous cycle. Air is the reservoir for O₂.

key_concepts: O₂ enters the biosphere through respiration; used for oxidation of carbohydrates, proteins and fats
Certain amount of O₂ is converted into ozone (O₃) forming an umbrella-like layer in outer atmosphere preventing ultraviolet radiations from reaching earth
Carbon monoxide released from volcanoes combines with O₂ to form CO₂
O₂ returns to air by: (i) Photosynthesis — water molecules break up into H and O₂ during photosynthesis; (ii) Photodissociation — water vapour is dissociated to release H₂ and O₂ in presence of light

topics.name: Phosphorus cycle

subtopics.definition_content: The cycling of phosphorus between biotic and abiotic system. It is a sedimentary cycle. The main source of phosphorus is rocks.
Excreta from sea birds and others which can be used as fertilizer due to being rich in phosphate and uric acid.

key_concepts: Through erosion and weathering, phosphorus is made available in the soil as ionic phosphate
Plants absorb ionic phosphate through roots; incorporated into DNA, RNA, AMP, ADP, ATP, GDP, GTP, NADP, phospholipids
Excess phosphate from animals excreted through faeces; bird guano contains large amount of phosphate
Phosphate released to soil through forest fire and combustion of grasses
Large amount of phosphate is lost in sea by sedimentation; certain amount locked in bones and teeth

topics.name: Sulphur cycle

subtopics.definition_content: The cycling of sulphur between biotic and abiotic systems. It is a sedimentary cycle. Sulphur is an important component of proteins and amino acids.

key_concepts: Sulphur exists in three important states: elemental sulphur, sulphides and sulphates
Sulphur present in rocks is made available for plants as inorganic sulphate by weathering and erosion
Dead bodies release sulphur to soil; some is released as hydrogen sulphide (H₂S) by Escherichia coli under anaerobic combustion
Incomplete combustion of fossil fuel releases SO₂ into air
Green and purple photosynthetic bacteria oxidise H₂S to sulphate: H₂S + 2O₂ → SO₄²⁻ + 2H⁺
Sulphur combines with iron in sediments to form iron sulphide: Fe + S → FeS

formulas.description: Photosynthesis (carbon incorporated into glucose, carbon cycle)
Respiration (enzymatic oxidation releasing CO₂)

formulas.expression: CO₂ + H₂O → H₂CO₃ → H⁺ + HCO₃⁻ → H⁺ + CaCO₃
formulas.description: Shell formation by marine animals (CO₂ to calcium carbonate)
Formation of ozone from oxygen
Photosynthesis releasing O₂ (oxygen cycle)
Oxidation of H₂S to sulphate by photosynthetic bacteria (sulphur cycle)
Formation of iron sulphide in sediments (sulphur cycle)

topics.name: Marine Zones

subtopics.definition_content: The sea floor from the shore to the edge or the continental shelf.
The sea floor along the continental slope and the aphotic and abyssal zone.
Constituting the water of the ocean basin.

key_concepts: Three zones of the sea: littoral (shore to continental shelf edge), benthonic (continental slope and abyssal), pelagic (open ocean water)

topics.name: Marine life

subtopics.definition_content: Passively drifting or floating organisms. Includes photosynthesizing organisms like diatoms (phytoplankton) as well as heterotrophic organisms like small crustaceans (zooplanktons).
Actively moving organisms with well developed locomotory organs.
Found along the floor of the sea bed and include creeping, crawling or sessile organisms.

key_concepts: Marine life grouped into three categories: plankton (passively drifting), nektons (actively moving), benthonic (floor-dwelling)

topics.name: Other (Lakes and Ponds)

subtopics.definition_content: Relatively shallow lakes with a rich accumulation of organic products. e.g., Dal lake of Kashmir.
Generally deep lakes, often with steep and rocky sides, that are poor in circulating nutrients like phosphates.
Lakes rich in undecomposed organic matter. e.g., marshy lakes.

key_concepts: Lakes and ponds are stagnant fresh water bodies found in every biome
Many lakes are direct or indirect result of glaciation
Brackish salts are always oligotrophic e.g., Sambhar lake of Rajasthan
Major estuaries of India: Hooghly-Malta estuarine system, Adyar estuary, Chilka lake (largest brackish water lake)

chapters.name: Key Facts, Biome Tips and Biotic Zones

topics.name: Tips and Tricks — Biome Key Facts

subtopics.definition_content: Water filled depressions in tundra biome.
Red coloured nutrient-rich highly fertile soil of tropical rain forests.
The reservoir of biogenetic nutrients from which the latter are slowly transferred to cycling pool. e.g., phosphates in rocks.
Pool of biogenetic nutrients which is being emptied and filled repeatedly by exchange between biotic and abiotic components of biosphere.

key_concepts: Number of biomes on a mountain range decreases with the increase in the latitude of the mountain
Temperate forests occur in India in Himalayas
Tropical deciduous forests are also called monsoon forests
Chapparal of winter rain areas is called machhie
Black soils of tall grass prairies are richest in nutrients and most fertile in the world
Carbon constitutes 49% of organic matter
In mature ecosystems, the amount of nutrient uptake equals the amount of recycled nutrient
In young and growing ecosystems, nutrient uptake is more so nutrients are retained by growing biomass of biota
Inverted pyramid of biomass is seen in desert

topics.name: Latitudinal Lines and Biotic Zones

subtopics.definition_content: Biotic zone between 23°N and 23°S.
Biotic zone between 23° and 40°N.
Biotic zone between 40° to 60°N.
Biotic zone between 60° and 70°N.

key_concepts: Tropic of Cancer at 23.5°N of equator
Arctic circle at 66.72°N
Tropic of Capricorn at 23.5°S of equator
Antarctic circle at 66.72°S

chapters.name: Famous national park of the world
key_concepts: First national park in the world: Yellowstone National Park, founded in 1872 in U.S.A.
About 2,000 parks have been established worldwide since 1872
Parks offer protection to thousands of endangered species in their natural habitats
Some parks created specifically for very rare endangered species (e.g., Gir for Asiatic Lion, Kaziranga for One-horned Rhinoceros, Corbett/Dachigam for Kashmir Stag)

chapters.name: Indian sanctuaries and national parks
key_concepts: 89 national parks and 202 sanctuaries scattered throughout India
Total protected area: approximately 75,000 sq. km. (~19% of reserve forest area, ~2.3% of total geographical area)
Sanctuaries and parks not only protect wildlife but safeguard varied ecosystems, prevent soil erosion, and help in recycling of wastes
Many are accessible to tourists and hence of economic value

chapters.name: Wild Life Conservation

topics.name: Ex-Situ conservation in India

subtopics.definition_content: Conservation of wild animals in artificial habitats; requires establishment of rehabilitation centres for highly endangered species.

key_concepts: National Wildlife Action Plan (NWAP) 1983 guides rehabilitation centre activities

Activities of rehabilitation centres: (a) capture individuals from natural habitats, (b) study feeding/breeding habits and diseases, (c) provide captive breeding facilities, (d) release young ones back into natural habitats
Rehabilitation centre for Musk deer at Kanchula Kharak near Chamoli, Uttar Pradesh
Rehabilitation centre at Kukrail forest near Lucknow for swamp deer, chinkara, chausingha, black deer, foxes, wild dogs, jackal, wild fowls, falcon, turtles, and crocodiles

topics.name: In-Situ conservation in India

subtopics.definition_content: Conservation requiring protection of wild animals in their natural habitats; necessitates conservation of terrestrial and aquatic natural habitats which are declared as prohibited reserves.

key_concepts: Four types of reserves in India based on degree of environmental protection: National Parks, Sanctuaries, Tiger Reserves, and Biosphere Reserves

topics.name: National Parks (N.Ps)

subtopics.definition_content: Areas maintained by government and reserved for betdefinitionent of wildlife. Cultivation, grazing, forestry and habitat manipulation are not allowed.

key_concepts: 89 national parks in India occupying nearly 1.1% of geographical area
First national park of India: Jim Corbett National Park (1936)
Some early national parks of the world: Yellowstone Park (USA) and Royal Park (near Sydney, Australia)
Notable Indian parks: Kaziranga (Assam) for Rhinoceros, Manas (Assam) for elephants, Sunderbans (West Bengal) for tigers, Gir (Gujarat) for Asiatic Lion, Kanha (M.P.) for swamp deer and tigers

topics.name: Sanctuaries

subtopics.definition_content: An area reserved for the conservation of animals only. Operations such as harvesting of timber, collection of minor forest products and private ownership rights are allowed provided they do not affect the animals adversely.

key_concepts: 492 wild life sanctuaries in India covering over one lakh sq. km. (~3% of Indian geographical area)

topics.name: Tiger Reserves
key_concepts: Tiger declared National Animal in 1972; 'Project Tiger' launched to save Royal Bengal Tiger from extinction
Project Tiger implemented with World Wildlife Fund (WWF) financial collaboration
Each reserve has a highly protected core area (~300+ sq. km.) and a peripheral buffer zone with limited conservation-oriented human activity
Originally 9 reserves declared; 10 more added later (19 total)

Reserves: Corbett (Uttarakhand), Manas (Assam), Sunderbans (West Bengal), Palamu (Bihar), Ranthambhor (Rajasthan), Melghat (Maharashtra), Kanha (M.P.), Bandipur (Karnataka), Simlipal (Bihar), Namdapha (Arunachal Pradesh), Sariska (Rajasthan), Dudhwa (U.P.), Buxa (West Bengal), Periyar (Kerala), Indravati (M.P.), Nagarjunasagar (A.P.), Pench (M.P.), Valmiki (Bihar), Kallakaddu Mundanthurai (Tamil Nadu)
Smallest tiger reserve: Ranthambhor National Park
Bombay Natural History Society (1883) — early voluntary wildlife conservation organisation in India
Indian Board for Wildlife (IBWL) constituted in 1952 by Central Government of India
Wildlife (Protection) Act enacted by Central Government in 1972 — provided legal protection, nationalisation of reserves, creation of National Parks and Sanctuaries, punishment for poachers
42nd Amendment to Indian Constitution empowered Central Government to ensure wildlife protection and acquire forests
Forest (Conservation) Bill 1980 forbade deforestation for non-forestry purpose without Central Government permission
India became signatory to IUCN (International Union for Conservation of Nature and Natural Resources), WWF (World Wildlife Fund), and FAO (Food and Agricultural Organisation)
Conservation strategies: ex situ (artificial habitats) or in situ (natural habitats)

chapters.name: Threatened Species

topics.name: Wild mammals on the verge of extinction
key_concepts: Includes: lion, tiger, wolves, jackals, foxes, bears, civets, loris, most species of monkeys, scaly anteater (manis), snow leopard, rhinoceros, wild ass, wild pigs, musk deer, Kashmir stag, several species of deers, black buck, antelopes, flying squirrels, dolphins, porcupine, gaur, wild sheep and goats, Gibbon, elephant, wild buffalo

topics.name: Wild birds on the verge of extinction
key_concepts: Includes: white-winged ducks, swans, falcon, sea eagle, bamboo partridge, mountain quail, Indian skimmer, painted spur fowls, hornbill, bustard, pheasant, Sarus crane

topics.name: Wild reptiles and amphibians on the verge of extinction
key_concepts: Reptiles: turtles, tortoises, crocodiles, gharial, monitor lizard, poisonous snakes, python
Amphibians: viviparous toad and Himalayan newt
Threatened species: species of wild animals whose normal survival upon the earth is jeopardised due to their destruction or destruction of their habitats by human beings
India: ~81 species of wild mammals, ~30 wild birds, ~15 reptiles and amphibians presently on verge of extinction
Biosphere Reserves of India: 13 established (first: Nilgiri Biosphere Reserve, 1986 in Kerala, Karnataka and Tamil Nadu); 2 proposed
Red Data Book: compilation of data on species threatened with extinction (maintained by IUCN)
Green Data Book: list of rare plants growing in protected areas like Botanical Gardens
Biological Diversity Day: 29th December
Biodiversity Act of India passed by Parliament in 2002
Endemic species: a species found in a particular natural habitat only (e.g., Ficus religiosa, Butea monosperma)
Cheetah has recently become extinct from India; only wild population found in Africa
Gharial (Gavialis gangeticus) found only in India
Dodo bird (Didus ineptus) of Mauritius extensively killed due to its beautiful features
Red Panda Project started (December 26, 1995) to protect Red Panda (Ailurus fulgens) in Himalayan region
Willian Hornday coined the definition wildlife; wrote 'Our Vanishing Wildlife'
World Conservation Day: 3rd December; World Animal Day: 3rd October; World Wild Life Week: first week of October
Environment Protection Act passed in 1986
Wildlife Institute of India: located at Dehradun, Uttaranchal
Kew London is famous for herbarium
SATYA: Save Tiger Youth Association

topics.name: Frequency

subtopics.definition_content: Frequency of sound is measured in cycles per second called Hertz or Hz. Human hearing lies within the frequency of 50–15,000 Hz.
Sound below 50 Hz. Infrasound waves cannot be listened but certain body parts resonate at this frequency and can be felt. Infrasound can damage body parts.
Sound above 15,000 Hz. Ultrasonic vibrations are employed in imaging, cleaning, drilling, cutting, welding and sealing packages.

key_concepts: Human hearing range: 50–15,000 Hz
Infrasound: below 50 Hz – cannot be heard but can be felt; can damage body parts
Ultrasound: above 15,000 Hz – used in imaging, cleaning, drilling, cutting, welding

topics.name: Loudness

subtopics.definition_content: Measured in decibels (dB). Noise is unwanted annoying sound of generally 80 dB and above. Audible sound starts at 0 dB.

key_concepts: Scooters/Cars: up to 60 dB
Aeroplanes: 80–100 dB
Jet aeroplanes: 120 dB
Delhi noise level: 90–95 dB; Kolkata: 100 dB; Mumbai: 80–90 dB; Kanpur: 90–100 dB
Recommended residence noise: 25–30 dB; Living room: 35–45 dB; Bedroom: 40 dB; Classroom: 40–50 dB; Hospital: 25–35 dB

topics.name: Sonic boom

subtopics.definition_content: A series of shock waves left behind by a supersonic jet flying at a speed of more than one Mach (speed of sound). It produces sudden rattling of windows and doors and buildings may develop cracks.

key_concepts: Sonic boom caused by jets exceeding Mach 1 (speed of sound)
Effects: rattling of windows and doors, cracks in buildings, startle reaction in humans and animals

topics.name: Effects of Noise Pollution

subtopics.definition_content: Unwanted annoying sound of generally 80 dB and above. It is a physical non-persistent pollution which affects the receiver directly. A ten-year exposure to 80 dB impairs hearing by 15 dB.

key_concepts: Damage to ear drum and impairment of hearing
Emotional disturbance, development of anxiety and stress (first effect of noise pollution)
Damage to eye sight, colour perception, night vision
Hypertension, changes in peripheral circulation and breathing pattern, decreased heart output and gastric problems

topics.name: Municipal Solid Waste (MSW)

subtopics.definition_content: Solid waste collected from urban municipal sources. Percapita MSW generated daily in India ranges from about 100g in small towns to 500g in large towns. MSW in cities is collected by respective municipalities and transported to designated disposal sites.

key_concepts: MSW generation has increased significantly due to rapid population growth and economic development
Mumbai MSW growth (1981–1991): population grew 49% but MSW generation grew 67% (from 3200 to 5355 tonnes/day)
MSW increasing due to: population growth, lifestyle changes and economic development
City MSW: Mumbai 6000 tonnes/day, Hyderabad 2000 tonnes/day, Delhi 400 tonnes/day
Open dumping facilitates breeding of disease vectors: flies, mosquitoes, cockroaches, rats
Poorly maintained landfill sites lead to ground water contamination due to leachate production

topics.name: Safety norms

subtopics.definition_content: All safety norms should be strictly followed in copy book style by all workers in radioactive waste management, including wearing of protective dresses.

key_concepts: Substances with very low radiation level can be discharged into municipal sewers
Low activity wastes should be stored to reduce their activity further before final disposal
Radioactive waste can be transformed into glass, ceramic or concrete (non-absorbable and unleachable form)
Locked waste is put in concrete filled drums sealed and buried in sea at least 1000 fathoms deep

chapters.name: Case study of remedy for plastic wastes

topics.name: Polyblend – Plastic Waste in Road Construction

subtopics.definition_content: A fine powder of recycled modified plastic developed by Ahmed Khan (Bangalore). This mixture is mixed with the bitumen used to lay roads to enhance water repellent properties and increase road life by a factor of three.

key_concepts: Polyblend was developed to address the problem of accumulating plastic waste
Polyblend blended with bitumen enhances water repellent properties of roads
Road life is increased by a factor of three when Polyblend-bitumen mix is used
By 2002, more than 40 km of road in Bangalore had been laid using this technique
Developed in collaboration with R.V. College of Engineering and Bangalore City Corporation

topics.name: Methods of soil conservation

subtopics.definition_content: Prevention of soil erosion is called as soil conservation.
Crops are arranged in bands or strips to check the flow of water.
The method of alternative sowing of leguminous and cereal crops (wheat, maize). The rotation of crops can be planned depending upon the climatic conditions, type, slope and properties of soils.
Growing of forest trees; most effective in controlling soil erosion. Also helps in prevention of floods.
Hilly slopes are divided into small flat fields called terraces to check the flow of water.
Oldest method in low rainfall areas. Field is divided into furrows and ridges. Ridges at the same level are called contours.
Practice meant for increasing soil fertility that also checks soil erosion.
A practice for cultivation of crops in low and moderate rainfall areas.
Basal plant parts are used to make a soil cover which helps in moisture conservation.

key_concepts: Legumes are useful in crop rotation because of having nodulated roots
Soil fertility is maintained by rotation of crops — minerals consumed by cereal crop in first year are supplied by leguminous crops in the second year
Van Mahotsava festival introduced by Government of India for planting trees on open waste land

topics.name: Distribution of Water on Earth
key_concepts: Three fourths of earth surface (71%) is covered by oceans which contain 97.5% of total water with about 3.5% salt content
Only 2.5% water is fresh water occurring on land
1.97% of fresh water occurs as frozen ice caps and glaciers
0.5% fresh water occurs as ground water
Rivers and lakes contain 0.02%, soil 0.01%, atmosphere 0.001% of water as vapours
Fresh water: major renewable resource in terrestrial habitats

topics.name: Problems related to water resources
key_concepts: Nearly 40% of human population resides in arid and semi-arid areas where water procurement is a major challenge
More water is withdrawn from surface and subsurface reservoirs than their recharging, causing many wetlands to dry up
Average consumption of fresh water per person in modern society is 350–700 litres per day
Excessive irrigation in arid/semi-arid areas increases soil salinity
Over-withdrawal of ground water in coastal regions results in movement of saline water into underground aquifers

topics.name: Conservation of water resources

subtopics.definition_content: Surface storage and recharging of groundwater by collecting rainwater.

key_concepts: Afforestation prevents soil erosion, reduces surface run-off, retains water and protects water sheds
Industry: wastage should be reduced; waste water can be recycled
Assured irrigation is available to only 40% area in India vs over 90% in advanced countries
Only 50% of water supplied to soil in agriculture is useful; rest goes waste
Bricklining of irrigation channels and sprinkling technique of irrigation recommended to save water

topics.name: Management of water resources

subtopics.definition_content: Conversion of sea water and saline underground water into fresh useful water.
Dredging and desilitation of water bodies to prevent decrease in storage capacity.

key_concepts: Dams and Reservoirs constructed to control floods, ensure year-round water supply, and generate electricity
Canals are made to carry water in arid and semi-arid areas

topics.name: Fresh Water Wetlands

subtopics.definition_content: Low lying, marshy, swampy or peat lands which may get inundated up to depth of six metres. Occupies about 6% of world land. Can be natural or artificial.
Poorly drained, frequently flooded low lying areas having grass-like plants.
Permanently water-logged or wet areas supporting dense covering of trees and shrubs.
Low land along streams which are periodically flooded.

key_concepts: Fresh water wet lands are of 3 types: Marshes, Swamps, Riverine

topics.name: Salt Water Wetlands

subtopics.definition_content: Most highly productive ecosystems which provide food and habitat to several marine and other organisms.

Coastal wetlands of tropical seas which contain a number of trees and shrubs in the intertidal region. Allow sediments to settle and build up soil along the shoreline which is colonised by other plants. Slowly mangrove expands into the ocean.

key_concepts: Salt water wetlands are of 2 types: Estuaries and Mangrove Swamps
Mangroves provide habitat to crabs, oysters and other marine animals

topics.name: Fisheries Management Techniques
key_concepts: Fishes are important biotic resources greatly valued by man as food
Fish fauna depleted due to overexploitation and pollution by sewage and industrial wastes
Large artificial reefs in offshore waters of oceans and freshwater lakes provide hiding places and additional food attracting fish
Spawning channels to replace vital spawning areas destroyed or inaccessible
Toxicants used to destroy undesirable fish populations and restore balance in favour of commercial fish
Fertilization and artificial enrichment of water bodies

topics.name: Important Facts

subtopics.definition_content: Central Arid Zone Research Institute (Jodhpur).
Central Pollution Control Board.
International Biological Programme.
International Union for Conservation of Nature and Natural Resources.
Man and Biosphere.
National Environmental Engineering Research Institute.
World Wild Life Fund (World Wide Fund for Nature).
United Nations Environment Programme.
Central Public Health Engineering Research Institute at Nagpur.

key_concepts: Coal reserves of the world are higher than that of petroleum
Maximum output of zinc in India is from Rajasthan
Agriculture consumes maximum water all over the world
Social forestry programme started in 1976
Van Mahotsav was started by K.M. Munshi in 1950
Rich and St. Barbibaker is known as 'tree saint in India'
In India, wind and water erosion deteriorate some 40,000 ha every year; 5–18% of global erosion
World Forestry Day is 21st March
World Habitat Day is 4th October
